reading.title: The Limits of Scientific Objectivity
reading.passage:
Science is often presented as the pursuit of pure, objective knowledge — a process in which facts are gathered, theories are tested, and personal opinion plays no part. While this image captures something important about the scientific method, many philosophers and historians of science argue that it is, at best, an oversimplification.

One difficulty is that scientists, like everyone else, approach their work with expectations. The questions they choose to ask, the experiments they design, and even the data they consider worth recording are all shaped by existing theories and assumptions. An observation that does not fit a prevailing framework may be dismissed as an error rather than recognised as a discovery. History offers many examples of findings that were ignored for years simply because they did not match what scientists expected to see.

This does not mean that science is merely a matter of opinion. The crucial feature of the scientific community is not that individual researchers are free from bias, but that the system as a whole is designed to expose and correct it. Results must be published, methods described, and experiments repeated by others. A claim that cannot be reproduced will, sooner or later, lose its authority, however eminent the scientist who first made it.

Seen in this light, objectivity is better understood not as a quality possessed by individual scientists, but as something produced collectively, through criticism and debate. It is precisely because scientists disagree, challenge one another, and demand evidence that science as a whole can move closer to reliable knowledge. The popular image of the lone genius arriving at the truth in isolation is, ironically, almost the opposite of how scientific objectivity is actually achieved.

questions.q: The writer completely accepts the popular image of science as purely objective.
The questions scientists ask are shaped by existing theories and ________.
Observations that do not fit a prevailing framework have sometimes been dismissed as errors.
According to the writer, what makes science reliable?
options: individual scientists being entirely free from bias
the system exposing and correcting bias through publication and repetition

questions.q: The writer says a claim that cannot be reproduced keeps its authority if the scientist is famous.
The writer argues that objectivity is produced ________, through criticism and debate.
What does the writer suggest about the 'lone genius arriving at the truth in isolation'?
options: it is the main way objectivity is achieved
it is almost the opposite of how objectivity actually works
it is the only reliable method
it proves science is just opinion

listening.title: Lecture: Decision-Making Under Uncertainty
listening.script:
In today's lecture we'll consider how people make decisions when they cannot be certain of the outcome. Classical economic theory assumed that individuals behave rationally: that they weigh up the probabilities and choose whatever option offers the greatest expected benefit. Decades of research, however, have shown that real human behaviour departs from this ideal in systematic ways.

Consider what is sometimes called loss aversion. Experiments consistently show that, for most people, the pain of losing a certain amount feels considerably stronger than the pleasure of gaining the same amount. As a result, people will often take greater risks to avoid a loss than they would to secure an equivalent gain. This is not random error; it is a predictable pattern.

Another well-documented tendency is our reliance on mental shortcuts. Rather than calculating probabilities precisely, we judge how likely something is by how easily examples come to mind. This usually serves us well, but it can mislead us — for instance, we may overestimate the danger of dramatic but rare events, while underestimating common ones that attract less attention. Recognising these tendencies will not make us perfectly rational, but it can help us make better-informed choices.

questions.q: Classical economic theory assumed that people behave ________.
What does 'loss aversion' describe?
options: the pain of a loss feels stronger than the pleasure of an equal gain
people only ever choose certain gains

questions.q: The speaker says departures from rational behaviour are just random errors.
People often judge how likely something is by how easily ________ come to mind.
We tend to overestimate the danger of dramatic but rare events.
The speaker claims that recognising these tendencies makes us perfectly rational.

speaking.q: Let's talk about decision-making. Some people argue that important decisions should be based on data and evidence rather than intuition. To what extent do you agree, and are there situations where intuition is more valuable? Develop your answer with reasons and examples.

writing.q: In some societies, an increasing number of important decisions are being made by algorithms and artificial intelligence rather than by humans. Do the benefits of this development outweigh the drawbacks? Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Science is the pursuit of knowledge.
The image is an oversimplification.
It did not fit the prevailing framework.
The finding was dismissed as an error.
Others must be able to reproduce the result.
However eminent the scientist may be.
Objectivity is produced collectively.
It emerges through criticism and debate.
Ironically, it is almost the opposite.
No genius finds truth in isolation.

reading.title: Language and Thought
reading.passage:
Does the language we speak shape the way we think? This question has fascinated and divided scholars for more than a century. In its strongest form, the idea — sometimes associated with the linguist Benjamin Lee Whorf — claims that the structure of a language determines the thoughts its speakers are able to have. In its weakest form, it claims merely that language influences thought to some degree. The two versions lead to very different conclusions, and much confusion has arisen from treating them as the same.

The strong version has largely been abandoned. If language truly set the boundaries of thought, it would be impossible to have an idea before one had the words for it, or to translate between languages at all — yet people coin new words for new ideas, and translation, however imperfect, plainly works. Few researchers today defend such an extreme position.

The weaker version, however, has received support from careful experiments. Studies have shown, for example, that the words a language provides for colours can affect how quickly speakers distinguish between shades, and that the way a language treats space or time can influence how its speakers describe and remember events. These effects are real, but they are typically modest, and they shape rather than imprison thought.

What emerges is a more balanced picture. Language is neither a prison that locks us into a single way of seeing the world, nor a transparent window that has no effect at all. It is better thought of as a tool that makes certain distinctions easy and others less so, gently nudging attention in particular directions. The mind remains free to range beyond the categories its language offers, but it does so, much of the time, along the paths that language has made convenient.

questions.q: The strong version is sometimes associated with the linguist Benjamin Lee ________.
The strong version of the theory is still widely defended today.
Why does the writer say the strong version has been abandoned?
options: because translation and the coining of new words show thought is not limited by language
because no one studies language any more

questions.q: Experiments suggest the words a language has for colours can affect how quickly speakers tell shades apart.
The writer says the effects of language on thought are real but typically ________.
Which image best matches the writer's final view of language?
options: a prison that locks in one way of seeing
a transparent window with no effect
a tool that makes some distinctions easy and nudges attention
a fixed set of unbreakable rules

questions.q: The writer believes the mind can think beyond the categories its language offers.

listening.title: Seminar: Sustainable Architecture
listening.script:
Good morning. In this seminar we'll examine what makes a building genuinely sustainable, because the term is used very loosely. People often assume that adding solar panels or using recycled materials is enough to call a building 'green'. In reality, sustainability has to be considered across the building's entire life.

Consider, first, the energy used in construction itself — what's known as embodied energy. A building made of materials that take enormous energy to produce and transport may start its life with a large environmental debt, even before anyone moves in. Sometimes renovating an old building is far greener than demolishing it and constructing a new 'eco' one.

Second, there's the energy a building uses while it operates: for heating, cooling and lighting. Clever design can dramatically reduce this. Orienting a building to make the most of natural light and warmth, and using good insulation, can cut energy needs more than any add-on technology.

Finally, we should think about what happens at the end of a building's life. Can its materials be reused or recycled, or will they simply end up in landfill? Truly sustainable design, then, is less about individual green features and more about thinking carefully at every stage.

questions.q: The energy used to produce and transport building materials is called ________ energy.
The speaker says adding solar panels alone is enough to make a building sustainable.
When might renovating an old building be greener than building a new one?
options: when the new building would carry a large environmental debt from construction
when solar panels are added to it

questions.q: Good design uses natural light and warmth, plus good ________, to cut energy needs.
At the end of its life, a building's materials may be reused, recycled, or sent to ________.
The speaker concludes sustainability is about careful thinking at every stage, not just green features.

speaking.q: Describe a building you find impressive. You should say: where it is, what it looks like, and what it is used for; and explain why it impresses you. You have one minute to prepare, then speak for two minutes.

writing.q: Some people think that protecting the environment is the responsibility of large companies and governments, not of individuals. To what extent do you agree or disagree? Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Language may shape thought.
Does structure determine thought?
The strong version was abandoned.
People coin new words for new ideas.
Speakers distinguish between shades.
The effects are typically modest.
Language shapes rather than imprisons thought.
It is not a transparent window.
Language nudges attention in directions.
The mind can range beyond categories.

reading.title: The Hidden Life of Sea Grass
reading.passage:
Sea grass meadows are among the most overlooked habitats in the world's coastal waters. Unlike seaweed, which is a type of algae, sea grasses are true flowering plants that evolved on land before returning to the sea around one hundred million years ago. They produce roots, leaves and even tiny flowers, and they form dense underwater lawns in shallow, sheltered bays where sunlight can reach the seabed.

These meadows perform a remarkable range of services. Their roots bind loose sediment together, which prevents coastlines from eroding during storms. Their leaves slow passing currents, allowing fine particles to settle and the water to become clearer. Perhaps most strikingly, sea grass absorbs carbon dioxide far faster than most forests on land, locking the gas away in the soil beneath it for centuries. Scientists now refer to this stored carbon as "blue carbon".

Sea grass is also a nursery. Young fish, crabs and seahorses shelter among the blades, hidden from larger predators until they are big enough to survive in open water. Many commercially important fish species spend the first months of their lives in these meadows, which means that healthy sea grass supports the fishing industry far from the coast.

Despite their value, sea grass meadows are disappearing quickly. Pollution from farms clouds the water and blocks the light the plants need, while careless anchoring tears up the roots. Researchers estimate that an area of sea grass the size of a football pitch is lost every half hour worldwide. Some conservation groups have begun replanting projects, scattering seeds collected by divers, though recovery is slow and success is far from guaranteed.

questions.q: Sea grasses are a form of algae.
Sea grass stores carbon for longer than most land forests do.
Replanting projects have restored most of the sea grass lost worldwide.
Seahorses are the most common animal found in sea grass meadows.
According to the passage, sea grass helps fishing because
options: it provides food sold directly to markets
young fish grow up sheltered in the meadows
it removes pollution created by boats
it is harvested and sold as fertiliser

questions.q: How do the leaves of sea grass improve water quality?
options: They release oxygen into the current
They slow currents so particles settle
They absorb salt from the water

questions.q: Roots of sea grass hold ________ together, protecting coasts from erosion.
An area of sea grass the size of a football pitch is lost every ________ minutes worldwide.

listening.title: Booking a Coastal Kayak Tour
listening.script:
STAFF: Good morning, Bayside Kayaks, how can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to book a guided tour for two people this Saturday.

STAFF: Lovely. We have a morning tour leaving at nine and an afternoon one at two. Which suits you?

CALLER: The morning one, please. How long does it last?

STAFF: It runs for three hours, and we paddle out to the seal colony near the old lighthouse.

CALLER: Wonderful. And what's the cost?

STAFF: It's forty pounds per person, which includes the kayak, a paddle and a life jacket.

CALLER: Do we need to bring anything ourselves?

STAFF: Just wear clothes that can get wet, and bring a water bottle. We provide a waterproof bag for your phone.

CALLER: Great. Can I pay on the day?

STAFF: We ask for a deposit of ten pounds now to hold the spot, and the rest on arrival.

CALLER: That's fine. My name is Daniel Foster.

STAFF: Thank you, Daniel. Please arrive by half past eight so we can fit your equipment.

questions.q: Which tour does the caller choose?
The tour lasts ________ hours.
The tour paddles out to a seal colony near the old ________.
The full price per person is ________ pounds.
A deposit of ________ pounds is required to hold the spot.
Customers should arrive by ________ to be fitted with equipment.

speaking.q: Let's talk about water. Do you enjoy spending time near the sea, rivers or lakes? Why or why not? How often do you do water-based activities such as swimming?

writing.q:
The table below shows the number of visitors (in thousands) to three coastal nature reserves in one country over three years. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Reserve | 2020 | 2021 | 2022
Bay Marsh | 120 | 145 | 190
Reed Point | 200 | 185 | 175
Gull Cove | 80 | 110 | 160

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: The underwater meadow stretched across the shallow bay.
These overlooked habitats are vital to marine life.
The roots trap sediment carried by the current.
Without plants, the coastline begins to erode.
Sea grass can absorb carbon dioxide quickly.
The meadow acts as a nursery for young fish.
Crabs shelter among the blades of grass.
Small fish hide from predators in the meadow.
Many commercially important fish breed here.
Pollution from farms clouds the water.
Careless anchoring tears up the roots.
Divers scatter seeds across the seabed.
Success is far from guaranteed.
The meadows provide a remarkable range of services.

reading.title: Reading the Tides
reading.passage:
Twice a day in most parts of the world, the sea rises and falls in a steady rhythm known as the tide. For coastal communities, understanding this rhythm has always been a matter of survival, since the timing of the tides governs when boats can leave harbour and when fishing grounds can be safely reached.

The tides are caused mainly by the gravitational pull of the Moon. As the Moon orbits the Earth, it tugs the oceans towards it, creating a bulge of water on the side of the planet facing the Moon. A second, smaller bulge forms on the opposite side. As the Earth rotates beneath these bulges, each coastline passes through two high tides and two low tides each day. The Sun also influences the tides, though its effect is weaker despite its enormous size, because it is so much further away.

When the Sun and Moon line up, at full and new moons, their combined pull produces unusually large tides called spring tides. The word has nothing to do with the season; it refers to the way the water seems to spring upward. When the two bodies pull at right angles, the tides are gentler and are known as neap tides.

Local geography can dramatically change tidal behaviour. In wide, funnel-shaped bays, water becomes trapped and piles up, producing some of the highest tides on the planet, occasionally exceeding fifteen metres. Engineers have long dreamed of capturing this movement as a clean source of electricity, and a handful of tidal power stations now feed energy into national grids, though the high cost of building them has slowed their spread.

questions.q: Most coastlines experience two high tides each day.
The Sun has no effect on the tides.
Spring tides occur during a particular season of the year.
Tidal power stations produce electricity more cheaply than coal stations.
Why is the Sun's effect on tides weaker than the Moon's?
options: The Sun is smaller than the Moon
The Sun is much further from Earth
The Sun does not have gravity
The Sun only affects spring tides

questions.q: Neap tides happen when the Sun and Moon
options: pull at right angles to each other

questions.q: The main cause of the tides is the gravitational pull of the ________.
In funnel-shaped bays, tides can occasionally exceed ________ metres.

listening.title: A Library Talk on Local Rivers
listening.script:
LIBRARIAN: Welcome, everyone, to this afternoon's talk on the rivers of our region. I'm Megan, and I'll be your guide for the next hour.

We'll start with the longest river in the area, the Marran, which flows for about two hundred kilometres before reaching the sea. For most of its journey it passes through farmland, and farmers rely on it heavily during the dry summer months.

Next, I want to mention water quality. Twenty years ago the river was badly polluted, but a clean-up programme launched in two thousand and five has made a huge difference. Fish have returned, and last spring volunteers counted more than three hundred birds along a single stretch.

If you'd like to get involved, we run guided walks on the first Sunday of every month. They're completely free, but you do need to register at the front desk because places are limited to twenty people.

Finally, there's a small exhibition in the side room with old photographs of the river. It's open until the end of June, so do take a look before you leave today.

questions.q: The longest river in the area is called the ________.
The Marran flows for about ________ kilometres before reaching the sea.
A river clean-up programme was launched in the year ________.
Volunteers counted more than ________ birds along one stretch.
When do the guided walks take place?
options: The first Sunday of each month
The last Friday of each month

questions.q: Places on the guided walks are limited to ________ people.

speaking.q:
Describe a place near water that you have visited and enjoyed. You should say:
- where it was
- when you went there
- what you did there
and explain why you enjoyed this place.

writing.q:
Some people believe that governments should spend money protecting natural water sources such as rivers and lakes, while others argue that the money would be better spent on more urgent human needs. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: The tide rises and falls in a steady rhythm.
The tides govern when boats can leave harbour.
The Moon's gravitational pull moves the oceans.
As the Moon orbits the Earth, it tugs the seas.
A bulge of water forms on the side facing the Moon.
The Earth rotates beneath the tidal bulges.
Their combined pull creates spring tides.
Local geography can dramatically change the tides.
Funnel-shaped bays trap rising water.
Water becomes trapped and piles up in the bay.
Engineers hope to capture tidal movement as power.
Tidal stations feed energy into the national grid.
Tides can exceed fifteen metres in some bays.
Reading the tides was a matter of survival.

reading.title: The Long Journey of a Raindrop
reading.passage:
Every drop of water on Earth is endlessly recycled through a process called the water cycle. A single drop might spend centuries frozen in a glacier, only days drifting as cloud, and a few hours rushing down a mountain stream. The same water has been moving around the planet for billions of years; the rain that falls today may once have been drunk by a dinosaur.

The cycle begins when the Sun heats the surface of oceans, lakes and rivers. Some of this water turns into an invisible gas called water vapour and rises into the sky in a process known as evaporation. Plants contribute too, releasing moisture from their leaves in a process called transpiration. As the vapour rises, the air around it cools, and the gas turns back into tiny droplets that gather to form clouds.

When these droplets grow heavy enough, they fall as rain, snow or hail. This is the stage most people picture when they think of the cycle. Yet what happens next is just as important. Some rainwater flows across the land into rivers and eventually back to the sea. A large share, however, soaks into the soil and trickles down to fill underground stores called aquifers, which supply wells and springs.

Human activity is now disturbing this ancient balance. Cutting down forests reduces transpiration, while paving over land with concrete prevents rain from soaking in, so it rushes away instead and raises the risk of flooding. Understanding the cycle has therefore become essential for managing the water supplies that every society depends upon.

questions.q: The same water has been circulating on Earth for billions of years.
Transpiration is the process by which plants release moisture from their leaves.
Most rainwater soaks into the soil rather than flowing into rivers.
Paving land with concrete increases the risk of flooding.
What happens to water vapour as it rises and the air cools?
options: It sinks back to the ocean

questions.q: According to the passage, aquifers are
Water turns into vapour and rises in a process called ________.
Underground water stores that supply wells and springs are called ________.

listening.title: Comparing Two Water Filters
listening.script:
MAN: I'm trying to decide between these two home water filters. Could you help me compare them?

ASSISTANT: Of course. The first one is the jug model. You fill it from the tap and the water filters as it passes through. It costs twenty-five dollars, and the cartridge lasts about two months.

MAN: And the second one?

ASSISTANT: That's the tap-mounted filter. It screws directly onto your faucet, so you get filtered water instantly. It's pricier at sixty dollars, but the cartridge lasts twice as long.

MAN: Which removes more contaminants?

ASSISTANT: They're actually similar for everyday tap water, but the tap-mounted model is better at removing chlorine, which improves the taste.

MAN: Is either one difficult to install?

ASSISTANT: The jug needs no installation at all. The tap-mounted one takes about ten minutes and comes with everything you need.

MAN: I think I'll take the tap-mounted one, then. Do you have it in white?

ASSISTANT: We do. I'll just grab one from the back for you.

questions.q: The jug model costs ________ dollars.
The jug cartridge lasts about ________ months.
The tap-mounted filter costs ________ dollars.
What is the tap-mounted filter better at removing?
Installing the tap-mounted filter takes about ________ minutes.
Which filter does the man finally choose?

speaking.q: Let's discuss water and society. Why do you think some regions of the world struggle with water shortages while others have plenty? What responsibilities do individuals have in reducing water waste compared with governments and companies?

writing.q:
The table below shows the average daily household water use (in litres per person) for four activities in one city, comparing 2010 and 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Activity | 2010 | 2022
Bathing/showering | 60 | 45
Toilet flushing | 40 | 25
Garden watering | 30 | 35
Drinking/cooking | 10 | 10

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Water is endlessly recycled around the planet.
A drop may drift as cloud for days.
Water rises into the sky as vapour.
Evaporation lifts water into the air.
Plants release moisture through transpiration.
Tiny droplets gather to form clouds.
Rainwater trickles down into the soil.
Wells draw water from underground aquifers.
Human activity disturbs the water cycle.
Deforestation upsets a natural balance.
Paving land with concrete blocks rainwater.
Understanding the cycle is essential for planning.
Societies depend on a steady water supply.
Rain soaks into the ground after a storm.

reading.title: Coral Reefs Under Pressure
reading.passage:
Coral reefs are often described as the rainforests of the sea, and the comparison is apt. Although they cover less than one per cent of the ocean floor, they provide a home for roughly a quarter of all known marine species. The reefs themselves are built by tiny animals called polyps, which extract minerals from seawater to construct hard skeletons. Over thousands of years, generation after generation of polyps cement these skeletons together to form vast underwater structures.

What gives a healthy reef its brilliant colour is not the coral itself but a microscopic alga that lives inside the polyps' tissues. The two organisms depend on each other completely: the alga uses sunlight to make food, sharing it with the coral, while the coral offers the alga a safe place to live. This partnership is highly sensitive to temperature. When the water becomes too warm, even by a degree or two, the coral expels the alga and turns a ghostly white, an event known as bleaching. A bleached coral is not yet dead, but if the heat persists, it will starve.

Reefs face other threats besides warming. Pollution encourages the growth of seaweed that smothers young coral, and overfishing removes the very fish that would normally keep that seaweed in check. Acid from absorbed carbon dioxide makes it harder for polyps to build their skeletons in the first place.

Not all the news is grim, however. Marine biologists have discovered that some reefs recover surprisingly quickly once local pressures are eased, and a few coral species appear naturally more resistant to heat. Breeding and replanting these hardy varieties may give damaged reefs a fighting chance, though scientists caution that such efforts cannot succeed unless the underlying warming is also addressed.

questions.q: Coral reefs cover a large proportion of the ocean floor.
The bright colour of a reef comes from the coral skeletons themselves.
A bleached coral is dead immediately after it loses its alga.
Some coral species are naturally more resistant to heat than others.
Coral reefs are home to more species than tropical rainforests.
Why is the partnership between coral and alga important?
options: The alga builds the coral skeleton
The alga makes food the coral shares
The coral protects the alga from fish

questions.q: What does overfishing do to reefs?
options: It removes fish that control seaweed
It increases the number of polyps
It adds minerals to the water

questions.q: Reefs are built by tiny animals called ________.
When coral expels its alga and turns white, the event is called ________.

listening.title: Planning a Group Beach Clean-Up
listening.script:
ORGANISER: Right, thanks for coming to plan Saturday's beach clean-up. Let's sort out the details.

VOLUNTEER: Where exactly are we meeting?

ORGANISER: At the car park by the pier, at nine o'clock sharp. From there we'll spread along the north beach.

VOLUNTEER: How many people are coming?

ORGANISER: We've had forty-two sign up so far, which is our best turnout yet.

VOLUNTEER: Do we need to bring gloves and bags?

ORGANISER: We'll provide gloves and bags, but please bring your own water and wear sturdy shoes. The rocks can be slippery.

VOLUNTEER: What do we do with the rubbish afterwards?

ORGANISER: There'll be three collection points marked with red flags. The council is sending a truck at noon to take it all away.

VOLUNTEER: And if it rains?

ORGANISER: We'll go ahead unless there's a storm warning. If we have to cancel, I'll send a text by seven that morning.

VOLUNTEER: Perfect. I'll see you on Saturday.

questions.q: The group is meeting at the car park by the ________.
The clean-up starts at ________ o'clock.
So far ________ people have signed up.
What should volunteers bring themselves?
There will be ________ collection points marked with red flags.
When will the organiser send a text if the event is cancelled?

speaking.q: Let's talk about the weather and seasons. Which season do you like best, and why? How does rainy or stormy weather affect what you do in a typical day?

writing.q:
Many tourist destinations near coral reefs and beaches are becoming damaged because of the large number of visitors. Some people think tourism in these areas should be strictly limited, while others believe this would harm local economies. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: The comparison to a rainforest is apt.
Polyps extract minerals from seawater.
Each polyp builds a hard skeleton.
The skeletons are cemented together over time.
A microscopic alga lives inside the coral.
The alga lives in the polyps' tissues.
The partnership is sensitive to temperature.
Warm water makes coral expel its alga.
If the heat persists, the coral will starve.
Seaweed can smother young coral.
Fish keep the seaweed in check.
Some corals are resistant to heat.
Not all the news about reefs is grim.

reading.title: The Slow Drift of the Ocean Conveyor
reading.passage:
Beneath the familiar surface waves driven by the wind, the ocean is stirred by a far larger and slower system of currents that links every sea on the planet. Scientists call it the global conveyor belt, and a single parcel of water may take roughly a thousand years to complete one full loop. Though it moves at little more than walking pace, this circulation carries an enormous amount of heat and shapes the climate of entire continents.

The engine of the conveyor lies in the cold seas near the poles. There, surface water becomes both very cold and unusually salty, partly because the formation of sea ice leaves the surrounding water saltier. Cold, salty water is dense, so it sinks to the ocean floor and begins a long journey toward the equator. Warmer surface currents flow in the opposite direction to replace it, redistributing heat from the tropics toward higher latitudes.

This steady transfer of heat explains why parts of north-western Europe remain comparatively mild despite lying at the same latitude as far colder regions. Without the warmth delivered by these currents, the climate of such areas would almost certainly be much harsher.

Researchers have grown concerned that the conveyor may be slowing. As ice sheets melt, large volumes of fresh water pour into the polar seas, diluting the salt and reducing the water's density. If the surface water no longer sinks as readily, the whole system could weaken. The consequences are difficult to predict precisely, but they might include sharper temperature swings and shifts in rainfall patterns thousands of kilometres away. For this reason, long-term monitoring of the deep ocean, once regarded as a minor scientific curiosity, has become a priority for climate research.

questions.q: The global conveyor belt completes a loop in roughly a thousand years.
The conveyor is driven mainly by surface winds.
Cold, salty water sinks because it is denser than warmer water.
Monitoring the deep ocean is now a priority for climate research.
The conveyor belt was first discovered in the twentieth century.
Why might melting ice sheets weaken the conveyor?
options: They warm the polar seas directly
They add fresh water that dilutes the salt
They increase the saltiness of the water

questions.q: Why does north-western Europe remain comparatively mild?
options: Warm currents deliver heat to it
It has very little sea ice

questions.q: Water that is cold and salty is described in the passage as ________, which is why it sinks.
Warm surface currents redistribute heat from the tropics toward higher ________.

listening.title: A Lecture on Desalination
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good morning. Today we're looking at desalination, the process of turning seawater into fresh, drinkable water. As populations grow and droughts become more common, more countries are turning to it.

There are two main methods. The older one is distillation, where seawater is heated until it evaporates, leaving the salt behind, and the vapour is then cooled back into pure water. It works well but uses a great deal of energy.

The more modern method is reverse osmosis. Here, seawater is forced through an extremely fine membrane that traps the salt while letting water through. It now accounts for about seventy per cent of the world's desalinated water, mainly because it uses less energy than distillation.

There are drawbacks, however. The plants are expensive to build, and they produce a very salty waste liquid called brine, which must be disposed of carefully to avoid harming sea life.

For your assignment, due in two weeks, I'd like you to compare the running costs of the two methods. Please keep it under one thousand five hundred words.

questions.q: What is the older method of desalination?
In distillation, seawater is heated until it ________.
Reverse osmosis accounts for about ________ per cent of desalinated water.
The salty waste liquid produced by the plants is called ________.
The assignment is due in ________ weeks.
The assignment should be kept under ________ words.

speaking.q:
Describe a time when you learned something interesting about nature or the environment. You should say:
- what you learned
- how or where you learned it
- why it interested you

reading.title: The Quiet Revolution of Aerogels
reading.passage:
Aerogels are among the lightest solid materials ever produced, and yet they trace their origins to a deceptively simple question. In the early twentieth century, a chemist named Samuel Kistler wondered whether the liquid inside a gel could be removed without causing the delicate solid network to collapse. Ordinary drying fails because surface tension pulls the structure inward as the liquid evaporates. Kistler's answer was to replace the liquid with a gas under carefully controlled conditions, leaving behind a skeleton of solid material riddled with tiny pores.

The result is a substance that is roughly ninety-five per cent air by volume. Because so little solid material remains, aerogels conduct heat very poorly, which makes them outstanding insulators. A thin sheet placed between a flame and a delicate object can protect that object remarkably well. This property has attracted the attention of engineers working on buildings, pipelines and even spacecraft, where every gram of weight carries a cost.

Despite these advantages, aerogels have been slow to enter everyday use. The traditional manufacturing process is expensive and requires high pressures, which limits the scale at which the material can be made. Some varieties are also brittle, crumbling under modest pressure. Researchers are now experimenting with flexible versions reinforced with polymer fibres, and with cheaper drying methods carried out at ordinary pressure. If these efforts succeed, a material once confined to laboratories and space missions may finally find a place in ordinary homes.

questions.q: Samuel Kistler aimed to remove the liquid from a gel without destroying its solid structure.
Ordinary evaporation leaves the gel network completely intact.
Kistler received a major scientific prize for his discovery.
Why are aerogels good insulators?
options: They contain a large amount of metal
They are made almost entirely of air

questions.q: What has limited the widespread use of aerogels?
options: A lack of interest from engineers
The high cost and pressure of manufacturing

reading.title: Memory in Metal
reading.passage:
Most metals, once bent out of shape, stay bent. A small family of alloys behaves differently. Known as shape-memory alloys, these materials can be deformed at a low temperature and then return to their original form when warmed. The most common example combines nickel and titanium in roughly equal proportions, a metal often called nitinol.

The secret lies in the way the atoms inside the alloy are arranged. At low temperatures the atoms settle into a soft, easily bent structure. Heating the metal causes the atoms to shift into a more rigid arrangement, and as they do so the object springs back to the shape it held before. This transformation can be repeated many thousands of times, although the precise temperature at which it occurs depends on the exact mixture of metals.

Engineers have found surprising uses for this behaviour. Eyeglass frames made from nitinol can be twisted and crushed, yet recover instantly. In medicine, thin tubes called stents are folded down, inserted into a narrowed blood vessel and allowed to expand to their full width as they warm to body temperature. Aircraft designers have studied the alloys for parts that change shape during flight, although weight and cost remain obstacles.

The main drawback is fatigue. With each cycle the internal structure suffers tiny amounts of damage, and after enough repetitions the memory effect weakens. Researchers continue to search for compositions that resist this gradual decline, hoping to widen the range of tasks these remarkable metals can perform.

questions.q: Most ordinary metals return to their original shape after being bent.
Nitinol is made from roughly equal parts nickel and titanium.
The shape-memory effect can be repeated many thousands of times.
Nitinol is more expensive than ordinary steel.
What causes the alloy to return to its original shape?
What is described as the main drawback of shape-memory alloys?

The temperature at which the change occurs depends on the exact ________ of metals.

listening.title: A Tour of the Metals Testing Lab
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to the metals testing laboratory. I'm Priya, and I'll be showing you around for the next forty minutes. Please stay behind the yellow line at all times.
VISITOR: Sorry, what was the yellow line for again?
GUIDE: It marks the safe distance from the machines. Some of them apply enormous force. Our first station is the tensile tester, which pulls a metal sample until it snaps. It can apply up to fifty kilonewtons of force.
VISITOR: And how long does a single test take?
GUIDE: Usually about twenty minutes, including setup. Over here is the fatigue rig, where we bend a sample repeatedly to see how long it lasts. We often run that overnight.
VISITOR: Do you test shape-memory alloys here?
GUIDE: We do, mainly nitinol. We measure how many cycles it can survive before the memory effect fades. The record so far is just over ten thousand cycles.
VISITOR: Impressive. Where do the samples come from?
GUIDE: Most are supplied by the engineering department, though a few come from industry partners. Right, let's move on to the microscope room.

reading.title: The Promise and Problems of Graphene
reading.passage:
When two researchers in Manchester peeled a single layer of carbon from a block of graphite using ordinary sticky tape, few imagined the excitement that would follow. The flake they isolated, just one atom thick, was graphene. In the years since, this material has been described as one of the most promising substances ever studied, and the original work earned a Nobel Prize.

Graphene's appeal lies in an unusual combination of properties. It is extraordinarily strong, far stronger than steel by weight, yet it is also flexible and nearly transparent. Electricity moves through it with remarkably little resistance, faster than through most conventional conductors. These qualities have inspired visions of bendable screens, lightning-fast electronics and filters capable of separating salt from seawater.

Turning these visions into products, however, has proved harder than expected. Producing large sheets of graphene that are free of defects remains difficult and costly. Many early demonstrations used tiny, near-perfect flakes that cannot easily be scaled up to industrial quantities. Manufacturers who require thousands of square metres of flawless material have found the gap between laboratory samples and factory output frustratingly wide.

Even so, progress is being made in narrower applications. Small amounts of graphene added to other materials can improve their strength or conductivity without the need for perfect sheets. Some sports equipment and protective coatings already contain it. Researchers caution that graphene may never be the single miracle material once predicted, but argue that its quieter, supporting role could still prove transformative across many industries.

questions.q: Graphene was first isolated using a piece of ordinary sticky tape.

options: The difficulty of making large defect-free sheets

reading.title: Building with Bacteria
reading.passage:
Concrete is the most widely used building material on Earth, but it has a quiet weakness. Over time, small cracks appear as the material is stressed by weather and load. Water seeps into these cracks, reaching the steel reinforcement inside and causing it to rust. The structure gradually weakens, and repairs are expensive and disruptive. For decades, engineers simply accepted cracking as an unavoidable cost of construction.

A more imaginative solution has emerged from biology. Certain bacteria, when supplied with the right nutrients, produce limestone as a natural by-product of their metabolism. Researchers have learned to mix dormant bacteria, together with a food source, directly into the concrete during manufacture. The bacteria can survive for years in this sleeping state, waiting.

When a crack forms and water enters, the bacteria awaken. Drawing on their stored food, they begin to produce limestone, which fills the crack from within. In favourable conditions a gap a few millimetres wide can be sealed in a matter of weeks, without any human intervention. The repaired material is not quite as strong as the original, but it prevents the water from reaching the vulnerable steel.

The approach is not without limits. The bacteria require oxygen and moisture to function, so the method works best for cracks that are exposed to the air. The added ingredients also raise the cost of the concrete, and large-scale field trials are still under way. Nevertheless, the idea that a building might one day heal its own wounds has captured the imagination of the construction industry.

questions.q: Cracks in concrete can allow water to reach and rust the steel inside.

Self-healing concrete is already cheaper than ordinary concrete.

What substance do the bacteria produce to seal cracks?
What triggers the bacteria to become active?
The bacteria can remain in a ________ state for years before being needed.
The method works best for cracks that are exposed to the ________.

listening.title: A Lecture on Self-Healing Materials
listening.script:
LECTURER: Right, today we're looking at self-healing materials, and I want to focus on three broad strategies. The first, and oldest, is the capsule approach. Here, tiny capsules of a healing agent are buried inside the material. When a crack tears through a capsule, the liquid leaks out and hardens, gluing the crack shut. The main limitation is that each spot can only heal once, because the capsule is used up.

The second strategy is vascular. Instead of separate capsules, the material contains a network of thin channels, rather like blood vessels, that can deliver healing fluid again and again. This allows repeated healing, but the channels are difficult to manufacture.

The third strategy is intrinsic. The material itself contains bonds that can reform after breaking, so no separate healing agent is needed. This is elegant, but so far it works mainly for soft polymers rather than hard structural materials.

For next week, please read chapter six and prepare a short comparison of the capsule and vascular methods. We'll discuss your findings at the start of the seminar.

reading.title: Bamboo: An Ancient Material Reconsidered
reading.passage:
For thousands of years, communities across Asia and South America have built homes, bridges and scaffolding from bamboo. To modern eyes it can seem a humble material, associated with temporary structures rather than serious engineering. Yet a growing number of architects and scientists argue that this perception undervalues one of nature's most efficient building products.

Despite being hollow and light, bamboo possesses a strength that compares favourably with timber and, in some respects, with steel. Its fibres run lengthwise along the stem, giving it great resistance to bending. Crucially, it grows astonishingly fast. Some species add nearly a metre of height in a single day, reaching full size within a few years rather than the decades required for hardwood trees. Because the plant regrows from its roots after cutting, a well-managed grove can be harvested repeatedly without replanting.

This rapid growth carries an environmental benefit. As it grows, bamboo absorbs carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and locks it into the structure of the plant. When the harvested stems are used in long-lasting buildings, that carbon remains trapped rather than returning to the air. For a construction industry under pressure to reduce its emissions, such a material is increasingly attractive.

There are obstacles, however. Untreated bamboo is vulnerable to insects and rot, and its naturally curved, variable form makes standardised manufacturing awkward. Building regulations in many countries were written with concrete and steel in mind, leaving little room for bamboo. Engineers are now developing treatments and engineered bamboo products, pressed and glued into uniform beams, that may allow this ancient material to take its place in modern construction.

questions.q: Bamboo has been used as a building material for thousands of years.

listening.title: Radio Feature on Sustainable Construction
listening.script:
PRESENTER: Welcome back. With me today is materials researcher Dr Owen Clarke, who studies sustainable building. Owen, why all the interest in bamboo lately?
DR CLARKE: Well, the construction industry produces a huge share of global emissions, and people are searching for greener options. Bamboo grows fast and stores carbon, so it ticks a lot of boxes.
PRESENTER: But surely a plant can't be as strong as steel?
DR CLARKE: It's lighter, of course, but pound for pound its strength is genuinely impressive. The trick is processing it properly into what we call engineered bamboo.
PRESENTER: And what's holding it back?
DR CLARKE: Mainly regulation. Most building codes don't yet recognise it, so getting a project approved can take years. Durability used to be an issue too, but modern treatments have largely solved that.
PRESENTER: Where is it being used most?
DR CLARKE: At the moment, mostly in parts of Asia and increasingly in Europe for low-rise buildings.
PRESENTER: Fascinating. Dr Clarke, thank you for joining us.

reading.title: The Quiet Return of the Beaver
reading.passage:
For centuries, beavers were a familiar sight across much of Europe, valued for their fur and for a substance from their bodies once used in medicine. By the early twentieth century, however, relentless hunting had driven the animal to the edge of extinction across most of the continent. In several countries it disappeared entirely, surviving only in a handful of isolated pockets.

In recent decades, attitudes have changed dramatically. Conservationists now regard the beaver not as a pest but as an engineer capable of reshaping landscapes for the better. By felling small trees and building dams across streams, beavers create ponds and wetlands that slow the flow of water. These wetlands store moisture during dry spells and absorb excess rainfall during storms, reducing the risk of flooding downstream.

The benefits extend beyond water management. The ponds created by beaver dams provide habitat for fish, insects, amphibians and birds, raising the overall diversity of life in an area. Some studies suggest that the slow-moving water behind a dam can also trap pollutants, improving water quality before it travels further downstream.

Not everyone welcomes the animal's return. Farmers sometimes complain that flooded fields and blocked drainage channels damage their land. In response, several reintroduction schemes have introduced compensation payments and simple devices that control water levels without removing the dams. Supporters argue that, with sensible management, the advantages of having beavers in the landscape far outweigh the occasional inconvenience they cause.

reading.title: Cities That Cool Themselves
reading.passage:
On a hot summer afternoon, a city can be several degrees warmer than the countryside that surrounds it. This phenomenon, known as the urban heat island effect, occurs because concrete, asphalt and brick absorb heat during the day and release it slowly at night. Cars, air conditioners and industry add further warmth, while the lack of vegetation means there is little natural cooling.

The consequences are more serious than mere discomfort. During heatwaves, the extra heat trapped in cities can place a dangerous strain on elderly residents and those with health conditions. Higher temperatures also drive up the demand for air conditioning, which in turn consumes more electricity and, in many places, produces more emissions.

Urban planners have begun to respond with a range of strategies. Planting trees along streets provides shade and, through a process called transpiration, releases moisture that cools the surrounding air. Some cities have introduced "green roofs" covered with plants, which insulate buildings and reduce the heat they radiate. Others have experimented with pale, reflective paving and roofing materials that bounce sunlight away rather than soaking it up.

Water features can help too. Fountains, ponds and restored rivers lower local temperatures as water evaporates. Yet experts caution that no single measure is sufficient on its own. The most effective approach combines many small interventions across an entire city, supported by long-term planning. As summers grow hotter in many regions, designing cities that can cool themselves is becoming not a luxury but a necessity.

questions.q: Cities are sometimes warmer than the surrounding countryside.
Why do urban surfaces such as asphalt make cities hotter?
options: They absorb heat and release it slowly

questions.q: The urban heat island effect is dangerous mainly for young children.
Trees cool the air through a process called ________.
What is the benefit of pale, reflective paving?
Green roofs are now legally required in most large cities.

Experts believe a single cooling measure is enough to solve the problem.

listening.title: A Lecture on Green Roofs
listening.script: LECTURER: Right, let's look at why green roofs are catching on in our cities. A green roof is simply a roof that's been covered with a layer of growing plants, sitting on top of waterproof material. The first benefit is temperature. In summer, the plants and soil shield the building from direct sun, and tests in our city found indoor temperatures up to five degrees lower. The second benefit is stormwater. A green roof can hold back a large share of rainfall, so the soil soaks up roughly sixty percent of the water before it reaches the drains. That eases pressure on the sewer system during heavy storms. Third, there's wildlife. Even a small roof garden can attract bees, butterflies and birds, creating tiny habitats high above the street. Now, there are costs. Installing a green roof is more expensive at the start, and the structure must be strong enough to bear the extra weight. But over twenty years, the savings on energy and repairs usually make it worthwhile. Next week we'll examine a case study from a hospital that cut its cooling bill by a third.

questions.q: A green roof sits on top of ________ material.

options: They are more expensive to install

questions.q: The case study next week is about a hospital that cut its cooling bill by a ________.

Some people believe that governments should spend money on planting trees and creating parks in cities. Others argue that this money would be better spent on public transport and housing. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: The urban heat island is a well-studied phenomenon.
Asphalt roads absorb heat throughout the day.
A lack of vegetation reduces natural cooling.
Heatwaves place a strain on elderly residents.
More air conditioning produces more emissions.
Green roofs insulate buildings from the heat.
Dark surfaces radiate heat at night.
Reflective paving bounces sunlight away.
Temperatures fall as water evaporates from ponds.

reading.title: The Hidden Life of Soil
reading.passage:
Beneath our feet lies one of the most crowded and least understood environments on Earth. A single handful of healthy soil may contain more living organisms than there are people on the planet. Bacteria, fungi, worms and countless tiny creatures work together in a complex web that keeps the land fertile and the wider ecosystem functioning.

Much of this activity centres on the breakdown of dead material. When leaves fall or plants die, soil organisms decompose them, releasing nutrients that growing plants can absorb. Without this constant recycling, the surface of the land would quickly become buried under dead matter, and the supply of nutrients available to plants would collapse.

Fungi play a particularly remarkable role. Many form partnerships with plant roots, extending a vast network of fine threads through the soil. These threads draw water and minerals from a much larger area than the roots could reach alone, and in return the plant supplies the fungi with sugars. Some scientists believe that, through these networks, plants may even exchange chemical signals, warning one another of insect attacks.

Yet soil is under threat. Intensive farming, heavy machinery and the overuse of chemicals can damage its delicate structure and reduce the number of organisms living within it. Once degraded, soil can take decades, or even centuries, to recover. A growing number of researchers argue that protecting the living world below ground deserves as much attention as protecting the plants and animals we can actually see.

questions.q: A handful of healthy soil can contain more organisms than the human population.
What would happen without the recycling carried out by soil organisms?
options: Dead matter would build up and nutrients would collapse

questions.q: Many fungi form partnerships with plant ________.
Plants give fungi sugars in exchange for water and minerals.
Scientists have proven that fungal networks carry electrical pulses like nerves.
According to the passage, what threatens soil?
Once degraded, soil can take decades or even ________ to recover.
The writer thinks soil life deserves as much attention as visible wildlife.

listening.title: Planning a Community Garden
listening.script:
MAYA: So, Tom, we've finally got the plot for the community garden. Where do we start?
TOM: Well, the first thing is the soil. The old car park left it really compacted, so we'll need to bring in some fresh compost.
MAYA: How much do you think?
TOM: For the whole area, about twenty bags should do it. The garden centre on Bridge Road sells them.
MAYA: Right. And what should we plant first?
TOM: I'd suggest beans and herbs to begin with. They're forgiving for beginners and grow quickly.
MAYA: Good idea. What about tools?
TOM: We've got spades and forks donated already, but we still need a watering system. A simple hose will do for now.
MAYA: And when's the first volunteer day?
TOM: Let's say Saturday the ninth. I'll put up a notice at the library.
MAYA: Perfect. Oh, should we charge people to join?
TOM: No, let's keep it free. We can ask for small donations to cover the compost instead.
MAYA: Agreed. I'll draft an email tonight.

questions.q: Why does the soil need fresh compost?
options: It was left compacted by an old car park

questions.q: They will need about ________ bags of compost.
Tom suggests planting beans and ________ first.
What do they still need to obtain?
The first volunteer day will be Saturday the ________.
How will they cover the cost of compost?

Region | 2010 | 2022
Northern Europe | 6% | 14%
Southern Europe | 4% | 11%
North America | 1% | 5%

Write at least 150 words.

reading.title: Rivers Without Walls
reading.passage:
For much of the twentieth century, the dominant approach to managing rivers was to control them as tightly as possible. Engineers straightened winding channels, lined banks with concrete and built high walls to keep water away from towns and farmland. The goal was simple: move floodwater downstream as fast as possible and reclaim land for development.

In recent years, this philosophy has come under serious question. Straightened rivers, it turns out, can make flooding worse rather than better. By speeding water along, they deliver enormous volumes to downstream towns all at once, overwhelming defences that were never designed for such surges. Concrete channels also strip rivers of the gravel beds, pools and bends that fish and other wildlife depend upon.

A different idea, often called "rewilding" rivers, is now gaining support. Instead of forcing water into narrow corridors, planners deliberately give rivers more room. They reconnect channels to their natural floodplains, allow streams to meander once more, and restore the marshes and woodlands that once bordered them. When heavy rain falls, these areas can flood harmlessly, storing water and releasing it gradually.

The approach is not without difficulties. Returning land to a river may mean buying out farms or moving infrastructure, which is expensive and politically sensitive. Critics also point out that results can be slow and hard to predict. Even so, a growing body of evidence suggests that working with a river's natural tendencies, rather than against them, often proves cheaper and more effective in the long run than building ever-higher walls.

questions.q: In the twentieth century, engineers tried to control rivers as much as possible.
Why can straightened rivers make flooding worse?
options: They deliver large volumes of water downstream all at once

questions.q: Concrete channels remove the gravel beds, pools and ________ that wildlife needs.
Rewilding involves giving rivers more space rather than less.
Planners reconnect rivers to their natural ________.
Rewilding rivers has now been adopted as official policy across Europe.
What is mentioned as a difficulty of the rewilding approach?
options: It can require buying farms and is politically sensitive

questions.q: The writer suggests working with rivers is always cheaper than building walls.

listening.title: A River Restoration Field Trip
listening.script: GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to the Willow Creek restoration site. Gather round and I'll explain what we've done here. Twenty years ago, this creek ran dead straight inside a concrete channel. It looked tidy, but it was lifeless, and every spring it flooded the village just below us. So in 2015 we began the restoration. The first step was breaking up the concrete and letting the creek find its own path again. Now it curves and bends naturally. Next, we planted native willows and reeds along the banks to hold the soil in place. We also dug shallow pools where fish can shelter, and within three years the trout had returned. The biggest surprise was the birds. We've recorded over forty species nesting here now. As for flooding, the village hasn't had a serious flood since the work was completed. The whole project cost less than the new flood wall the council had originally planned. If you look to your left, you'll see a beaver lodge, built entirely by the animals themselves last winter.

questions.q: What was the creek like before the restoration?
options: Straight and inside a concrete channel

questions.q: The restoration began in the year ________.
Workers planted native willows and ________ along the banks.
The ________ returned within three years.
Over ________ species of bird now nest at the site.
How did the project's cost compare to the planned flood wall?

speaking.q: Let's talk about water and weather. Does it rain a lot where you live? How does rainy weather affect what you do during the day? Do you prefer rainy days or sunny days?

writing.q:
Some people think the best way to deal with flooding is to build stronger walls and barriers, while others believe we should give rivers more natural space to flood safely. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: The Trouble with Plastic Recycling
reading.passage:
For decades, recycling has been presented to the public as the answer to the world's plastic problem. Households are urged to sort their waste carefully, and the familiar triangular symbol on a bottle or container suggests that the material will be transformed into something new. The reality, however, is considerably more complicated, and a surprisingly small proportion of plastic is ever genuinely recycled.

Part of the difficulty lies in the nature of plastic itself. There are many different types, and most cannot be mixed together when they are melted down. Sorting them is labour-intensive and costly, and some products combine several plastics in a single item, making them almost impossible to separate. Even when plastic is recycled, the resulting material is often of lower quality than the original, so a bottle rarely becomes another bottle. Instead it may become a lower-grade product that itself cannot be recycled again.

Economics adds a further obstacle. Producing new plastic from oil is frequently cheaper than collecting, cleaning and reprocessing used material, especially when oil prices are low. For years, wealthy countries avoided the problem by shipping their plastic waste abroad. When several importing nations recently restricted these imports, the limitations of the system were suddenly exposed.

Many environmental groups now argue that recycling, while useful, can never be more than part of the solution. The real priority, they insist, must be to reduce the amount of plastic produced in the first place. Refillable containers, alternative materials and simpler packaging, they suggest, will achieve far more than asking consumers to sort an ever-growing mountain of waste.

questions.q: The recycling symbol guarantees that an item will be recycled.
Why is sorting plastic difficult?
options: There is only one type of plastic
Different types cannot be melted together and are costly to separate
Plastic cannot be melted at all

questions.q: Recycled plastic is often of lower ________ than the original.
Producing new plastic from oil is often cheaper than reprocessing used plastic.
The passage states exactly what percentage of plastic is recycled worldwide.
What exposed the limitations of the recycling system?
Environmental groups say the real priority is to ________ the amount of plastic produced.
The writer believes recycling alone can solve the plastic problem.

listening.title: Radio Feature on a Refill Shop
listening.script:
PRESENTER: Today on Greenwaves we're visiting a shop that's trying to cut packaging waste altogether. I'm here with the owner, Priya. Priya, how does it work?
PRIYA: Well, the idea is simple. Customers bring their own containers, weigh them at the counter, then fill up with whatever they need, from pasta and rice to washing-up liquid. They pay only for the weight of the product.
PRESENTER: And what's been the most popular item?
PRIYA: Surprisingly, it's laundry detergent. People go through a lot of it, and refilling saves them a fortune in plastic bottles.
PRESENTER: How much plastic would you say you've saved?
PRIYA: Since we opened two years ago, we estimate around eight thousand bottles.
PRESENTER: That's impressive. Any challenges?
PRIYA: The biggest one is habit. People forget their containers, so we keep a supply of paper bags as a backup. And some products, like fresh bread, we simply can't sell loose.
PRESENTER: What's next for the shop?
PRIYA: We're launching a delivery service next month, using returnable glass jars.
PRESENTER: Wonderful. Thank you, Priya.

questions.q: How do customers pay in the shop?
options: Only for the weight of the product

questions.q: The most popular item is laundry ________.
Since opening, the shop estimates it has saved around ________ bottles.
What is the biggest challenge the shop faces?

The new delivery service will use returnable ________ jars.

speaking.q:
Describe a habit you have changed in order to be more environmentally friendly. You should say:

and explain how you feel about making this change.

reading.title: The Quiet Rise of Contactless Payment
reading.passage:
Two decades ago, paying for a coffee almost always meant handing over coins or feeding a banknote into a till. Today, in many cities, the same transaction is completed in under a second with a tap of a card or phone. Contactless payment, once treated as a novelty, has become the default for small purchases, and its spread reveals a great deal about how new technologies are adopted.

The technology itself is not particularly recent. Short-range wireless chips capable of transmitting payment data existed long before they appeared in ordinary wallets. What changed was the surrounding infrastructure. Banks had to issue compatible cards, shops had to install readers, and customers had to trust that a wave near a terminal would not lead to fraud. Each of these conditions reinforced the others: more readers encouraged more cards, and more cards persuaded reluctant retailers to upgrade.

Convenience, however, has come at a cost that is rarely discussed. Because contactless transactions feel effortless, people tend to spend more freely, particularly on minor, impulsive purchases. Studies of consumer behaviour suggest that the physical act of counting out cash creates a small psychological friction that encourages caution. When that friction disappears, so does some of the restraint.

There are also quieter consequences for those left behind. A small but persistent minority of people, often elderly or on low incomes, continue to rely on cash. As more businesses go entirely cashless, these individuals risk being excluded from everyday services. Some governments have begun to consider whether the right to pay with notes and coins should be protected by law, recognising that the convenience enjoyed by the majority is not shared by all.

questions.q: The wireless chip technology used in contactless cards was invented only shortly before cards became common.
The growth of card readers and the issuing of cards encouraged each other.
Contactless terminals are more expensive to install than traditional tills.
According to the passage, why may people spend more when paying contactlessly?
options: The terminals automatically add extra charges
The ease of payment removes a sense of caution
Cash is no longer accepted in most shops

questions.q: Which group is described as most likely to keep using cash?
options: The elderly and those on low incomes

questions.q: The physical act of counting cash creates a psychological ________ that encourages caution.
Some governments are considering whether the right to pay with notes and coins should be protected by ________.

listening.title: Opening a Student Bank Account
listening.script:
CLERK: Good morning, how can I help you today?

STUDENT: Hi, I'd like to open a student account, please.

CLERK: Of course. Can I take your full name first?

STUDENT: Yes, it's Daniel Okafor — that's O-K-A-F-O-R.

CLERK: Lovely. And are you currently enrolled at the university?

STUDENT: I am, yes. I'm in my second year studying Environmental Science.

CLERK: Great. With the student account, you'll get a contactless debit card and an interest-free overdraft of up to one thousand five hundred pounds.

STUDENT: That's helpful. Is there a monthly fee?

CLERK: No fee at all while you're a student. You'll just need two documents: proof of your address and your student ID.

STUDENT: I've got my ID, but my address proof is a bank letter dated the fourteenth of March. Is that recent enough?

CLERK: That's fine — anything within three months works. Your card should arrive in about five working days.

STUDENT: Perfect. Can I use the app straight away?

CLERK: Yes, you can set it up today before the card even arrives.

questions.q: The student's surname is spelled ________.
The student is studying ________ Science.
The interest-free overdraft is up to ________ pounds.
What is the monthly fee for the student account?
Besides the student ID, the student must provide proof of ________.
The card should arrive in about ________ working days.

speaking.q: Let's talk about money and shopping. Do you usually pay with cash or with a card or phone? Why? Have your spending habits changed in recent years?

writing.q:
The table below shows the percentage of payments made by different methods in one country in 2010 and 2024. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Payment method | 2010 | 2024
Cash | 56% | 18%
Debit/credit card (chip) | 30% | 27%
Contactless (card/phone) | 4% | 49%
Cheque | 10% | 6%

vocab.example: Online banking was once a novelty but is now routine.
Tapping a phone has become the default for small purchases.
Shops had to upgrade their infrastructure to accept the cards.
Banks issued compatible cards for the new readers.
Reluctant retailers eventually upgraded their terminals.
Contactless payment feels almost effortless.
People make more impulsive purchases when paying is easy.
Counting cash adds a small friction that slows spending.
Without friction, some spending restraint disappears.

Cashless shops may exclude people without cards.
Each condition reinforced the others.

reading.title: Working from Anywhere
reading.passage:
For most of the twentieth century, work meant a place. People travelled to an office, a factory, or a shop, and the boundary between work and home was marked by the journey between them. The rapid expansion of remote work has begun to dissolve that boundary, and the effects are proving more complicated than early enthusiasts predicted.

The immediate appeal is obvious. Without a daily commute, employees reclaim hours that can be spent sleeping, exercising, or simply with family. Companies, too, can recruit talented staff regardless of where they live, drawing on a far wider pool than any single city could provide. During periods when offices were closed, many organisations discovered that productivity did not collapse, as some managers had feared, but in many cases held steady or even rose.

Yet the picture is not uniformly positive. Spontaneous conversations — the brief exchange by a printer or the unplanned chat in a corridor — turn out to play a surprisingly important role in how ideas spread and how newcomers learn the unwritten rules of an organisation. When everyone works apart, these informal channels weaken, and junior employees in particular may struggle to build the relationships that advance a career.

There is also the matter of separation. When the kitchen table becomes the desk, work can quietly seep into every corner of the day. Some remote workers report difficulty switching off, answering messages late into the evening because the office is never truly left behind. The challenge for the coming decade may not be whether people work remotely, but how they protect the boundaries that the office once provided automatically.

questions.q: Many managers expected productivity to fall when offices closed.
Remote work allows companies to hire from a wider geographical area.
Remote workers are paid less than office-based workers.
Why are informal conversations described as important?
options: They reduce the need for formal meetings
They help ideas spread and newcomers learn unwritten rules

questions.q: Which group is said to struggle most when everyone works apart?
Without a daily ________, employees reclaim hours for other activities.
When the kitchen table becomes the desk, work can ________ into every corner of the day.
The writer believes remote work will certainly disappear within a decade.

listening.title: A Talk on Setting Up a Home Office
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone. Today I want to share some practical advice on setting up a home office that actually works for you. The first thing — and people often overlook this — is to choose a dedicated space. Even a corner of a room is fine, but it should be a spot your brain learns to associate with work and nothing else.

Second, think about your chair before your desk. A poor chair causes back pain within weeks, and a good one is honestly the best investment you'll make. Aim to spend roughly two hundred dollars if you can.

Third, lighting. Natural light is ideal, so try to position your desk near a window, but avoid having the window directly behind your screen, as that creates glare.

Fourth, and this is the rule most people break: set fixed working hours. Decide when you start and, more importantly, when you stop. I finish at six, close the laptop, and don't reopen it until morning.

Finally, take a proper break for lunch — away from the desk. A walk of even fifteen minutes resets your concentration for the afternoon.

questions.q: The speaker advises choosing a ________ space for work.
What does the speaker say you should think about before the desk?
The speaker suggests spending roughly ________ dollars on a chair.
You should avoid having the window directly behind your screen because it creates ________.
The speaker personally finishes work at ________ o'clock.
A walk of even ________ minutes resets concentration.

speaking.q:
Describe a place where you like to work or study. You should say:
- where it is
- when you usually go there
- what you do there
and explain why you find it a good place to work or study.

writing.q: Some people believe that working from home is better for both employees and employers, while others think that working in an office is more effective. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

vocab.example: Remote work has begun to dissolve the boundary between home and office.
The commute once marked the boundary between work and home.
Early enthusiasts predicted only benefits.
Without a commute, workers reclaim several hours.
Firms can recruit staff from anywhere.
The picture is not uniformly positive.
Spontaneous chats help ideas spread.
Informal channels weaken when staff work apart.
Relationships help advance a career.

Workers must protect the boundaries the office once provided.
