reading.title: The Attention Economy
reading.passage:
Every time a free app loads on a screen, a simple but profound exchange takes place. The service costs the user nothing in money, yet it is far from free. The true currency is attention, and across the digital world an entire industry has emerged whose business model depends on capturing and holding as much of it as possible.

The logic is straightforward. Most online platforms earn revenue by showing advertisements, and the longer a user stays, the more advertisements can be displayed. Engineers therefore design features whose purpose is not to help users finish a task quickly but to keep them scrolling. Endless feeds, automatically playing videos, and notifications timed to draw people back are all products of this incentive.

Critics argue that the consequences reach well beyond wasted time. When platforms compete for attention, content that provokes a strong emotional reaction — outrage, fear, or surprise — tends to spread fastest, because such material is the most engaging. Over time, this can subtly distort what people see and believe, rewarding sensation over accuracy.

Defenders of the industry respond that users are free to look away, and that the same tools can be used to educate, connect, and entertain. There is truth on both sides. What seems clear, however, is that attention has become a scarce and valuable resource, and that the contest to capture it is unlikely to fade. The question for individuals is whether they can decide, deliberately, how their own attention is spent, rather than allowing that decision to be made for them.

questions.q: Free apps cost users nothing of value.
Most online platforms earn money by displaying advertisements.
Content that provokes strong emotion tends to spread the fastest online.
Governments have introduced laws to limit how long apps can hold attention.
According to the passage, why do engineers design endless feeds and autoplay?
options: To help users complete tasks faster
To keep users on the platform longer
To reduce the number of advertisements

questions.q: What do defenders of the industry argue?
options: The same tools can educate and connect people

questions.q: The true ________ of free apps is the user's attention.
Attention has become a ________ and valuable resource.

listening.title: A Phone-In About Screen Time
listening.script:
HOST: And we're back. On the line we have Priya, who's been trying to cut down her screen time. Priya, what made you start?

PRIYA: Well, my phone told me my daily average was nearly seven hours, and I was honestly shocked.

HOST: Seven hours — that's a lot. What did you change first?

PRIYA: I turned off all notifications except calls and messages. That alone made a huge difference, because I wasn't being pulled back constantly.

HOST: Sensible. Anything else?

PRIYA: Yes, I started charging my phone in the kitchen overnight instead of by my bed. So the first thing I see in the morning isn't a screen.

HOST: And has it worked?

PRIYA: My average is down to about three hours now, and I'm sleeping much better.

HOST: That's a real improvement. Any advice for listeners?

PRIYA: Start small. Pick one app — for me it was the news app — and just delete it for a week. You'll be amazed how little you miss it.

HOST: Great advice, Priya. Thank you for calling in.

questions.q: Priya's original daily screen-time average was nearly ________ hours.
What did Priya change first?
Priya now charges her phone in the ________ overnight.
Priya's average screen time is now down to about ________ hours.
Priya advises listeners to delete one ________ for a week.
Which app did Priya delete first?

speaking.q: Let's discuss attention and technology more broadly. Why do you think people find it difficult to put down their phones? Should companies be responsible for designing technology that is less addictive, or is it up to individuals to control their own use?

writing.q:
The table below shows the average daily time (in minutes) that adults in one country spent on three online activities in 2014 and 2024. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Activity | 2014 | 2024
Social media | 45 | 132
Streaming video | 30 | 95
Online news | 25 | 18

vocab.example: A simple but profound exchange takes place.
Attention is the real currency of free apps.
The industry competes to capture attention.
Platforms earn revenue from advertisements.
Notifications are products of this incentive.
Content that provokes emotion spreads fastest.
Outrage tends to spread quickly online.
This can distort what people believe.
The system rewards sensation over accuracy.
Attention has become a scarce resource.
Individuals can choose deliberately how to spend attention.
The contest to capture attention is unlikely to fade.
The competition for attention is unlikely to fade.

reading.title: Smart Cities and Their Limits
reading.passage:
The phrase 'smart city' conjures images of seamless efficiency: traffic lights that adjust to the flow of cars, bins that signal when they are full, and energy grids that balance supply and demand in real time. Around the world, municipal authorities have invested heavily in sensors and data systems, persuaded that the careful collection of information will make urban life cleaner, safer, and more convenient. The results, however, have been mixed, and the gap between promise and delivery is instructive.

Where smart systems have worked well, they have tended to solve narrow, well-defined problems. Sensors that detect available parking spaces, for instance, can reduce the time drivers spend circling, easing congestion and lowering emissions. Because the problem is specific and the data unambiguous, the technology delivers a clear benefit.

Difficulties arise when ambitions grow larger. A truly responsive city requires vast quantities of data about its residents — where they travel, when, and how. Collecting such information raises legitimate concerns about surveillance and privacy, and citizens are increasingly wary of schemes that monitor their daily movements, however benign the stated purpose. Trust, once lost, is hard to recover.

There is a further, subtler problem. Technology tends to address the issues that can be measured, while the qualities that make a city pleasant to live in — a sense of community, beautiful public spaces, the freedom to wander — resist easy quantification. A city optimised purely for efficiency may run smoothly yet feel strangely lifeless. The most thoughtful planners now argue that data should serve a vision of urban life, rather than substitute for one.

questions.q: Smart-city projects have produced consistently successful results everywhere.
Parking-detection sensors can help reduce traffic congestion.
Smart-city sensors are usually manufactured by a small number of companies.
According to the passage, smart systems work best when they
options: collect as much data as possible

questions.q: Why are citizens increasingly wary of ambitious smart-city schemes?
options: They are too expensive for taxpayers
They raise concerns about surveillance and privacy
They reduce the number of public jobs

questions.q: The qualities that make a city pleasant to live in resist easy ________.
A city optimised purely for efficiency may run smoothly yet feel strangely ________.
Thoughtful planners believe data should serve a vision of urban life rather than replace it.

listening.title: A University Lecture on Urban Sensors
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today we'll look at how cities use sensors to manage everyday services, and I'll give you three concrete examples. Let's start with waste collection. In one pilot project, sensors were fitted inside public bins to report how full they were. Trucks then visited only the bins that needed emptying, and the city cut its collection costs by around thirty percent.

My second example is air quality. A network of low-cost monitors was spread across the city, sending readings every minute. This let officials identify pollution hotspots near schools and reroute heavy traffic during the morning.

The third example concerns water. Sensors placed along the pipe network detected leaks early by noticing unusual drops in pressure. In the first year alone, the city saved over two million litres of water.

Now, all three sound impressive, and they are. But notice the common thread: each tackles a single, measurable problem. The harder challenge, which we'll discuss next week, is what happens when cities try to connect everything together. Please read chapter four before then.

questions.q: What was the first example the lecturer gave?
The city cut waste collection costs by around ________ percent.
The air-quality monitors sent readings every ________.
Water sensors detected leaks by noticing unusual drops in ________.
In the first year the city saved over ________ million litres of water.
What should students do before next week?

speaking.q: Let's talk about the place where you live. What do you like most about your town or city? Are there any changes you would like to see in how it is run or organised?

writing.q: Many cities are using new technology to monitor traffic, energy use, and public services. Some people believe this makes cities better places to live, while others worry about the loss of privacy. Do the advantages of this kind of technology outweigh the disadvantages?

vocab.example: Smart cities promise seamless efficiency.
Municipal authorities invested in sensors.
The gap between promise and delivery is instructive.

reading.title: When Machines Recommend
reading.passage:
Few features of modern digital life are as quietly powerful as the recommendation system. Whether it suggests the next song, the next film, or the next product to buy, an automated engine is constantly guessing what a person might want and placing it conveniently in view. These systems have become so familiar that their influence is easy to overlook, yet they shape an enormous share of what people consume.

The basic principle is comparison. By analysing the behaviour of millions of users, an engine can spot patterns: people who enjoyed one thing often enjoy another. A recommendation, then, is essentially a prediction based on the choices of others who resemble you. When the predictions are accurate, the result feels almost like a personal service, saving the user the effort of searching.

The drawbacks, however, are real. Because such systems reward what is already popular, they can narrow the range of material people encounter, steadily guiding everyone towards a similar set of choices. An artist whose work does not fit existing patterns may struggle to be discovered at all. Some critics worry that recommendation engines create a comfortable but confining loop, in which users are shown more of what they already like and less of what might genuinely surprise them.

There is no obvious solution, partly because the very accuracy that makes these systems useful is also what makes them limiting. A few platforms have begun to introduce deliberate variety, occasionally suggesting material outside a user's usual taste. Whether people will welcome such interruptions, or simply skip past them in search of the familiar, remains an open question — and perhaps the most revealing test of how much we truly value the unexpected.

questions.q: Recommendation systems work mainly by comparing the behaviour of many users.
An accurate recommendation can feel like a personal service.
Recommendation engines are more accurate for music than for films.
What concern do critics raise about recommendation systems?

They narrow the range of material people see

options: Their work does not fit existing patterns

listening.title: Two Students Discuss a Music App

MAYA: Have you tried that new music app everyone's talking about?

MAYA: And? Is it any good?

TOM: Honestly, the first two weeks were brilliant. It found loads of bands I'd never heard of but loved.

MAYA: So what's the problem? You sound a bit unsure.

TOM: Well, after a while it started repeating itself. Now every playlist sounds the same — same kind of guitar, same tempo. It's a bit boring.

MAYA: That's interesting. It's learned what you like a bit too well.

TOM: Exactly. There's actually a setting called 'Discover More' that adds unfamiliar styles. I turned it on yesterday.

MAYA: And has it helped?

TOM: A little. I skipped the first few songs, but then it played some jazz I really liked. So I'm giving it another month.

MAYA: Sounds fair. Maybe I'll try it too.

reading.title: Why We Forget on Purpose
reading.passage:
Forgetting is usually treated as a failure of the mind, a sign that memory has broken down. Yet a growing body of psychological research suggests that forgetting is often an active, useful process rather than a simple loss. The brain does not record everything it encounters; instead, it constantly decides what is worth keeping and what can safely be discarded.

One reason for this selectivity is that a perfect memory would not necessarily be helpful. A person who recalled every meal, conversation and journey in full detail would struggle to find the information that actually mattered. By allowing minor details to fade, the mind makes general patterns easier to notice. In this sense, forgetting clears space for the kind of broad understanding that guides everyday decisions.

Studies of directed forgetting support this idea. When volunteers are shown a list of words and then told to forget the first half, they later remember those words less well than words they were never asked to forget. The instruction itself appears to weaken the memory, which suggests that the brain has some control over what it retains. Researchers believe this control helps people set aside outdated or distressing information.

There are limits, of course. The same mechanisms that protect us can occasionally remove things we would prefer to keep, and they cannot be switched on at will to erase a painful event. Even so, the modern view is that a healthy memory depends not only on holding on, but also on the quiet ability to let go.

questions.q: Most researchers now regard forgetting purely as a breakdown of memory.
A person with a flawless memory might find it harder to locate important information.
The passage states that directed-forgetting studies were first carried out in the 1950s.
In directed-forgetting experiments, words that participants were told to forget were:
options: remembered less well than other words

questions.q: According to the passage, one benefit of letting minor details fade is that it:
options: makes broad patterns easier to notice

questions.q: The instruction to forget appears to ________ the memory of those particular words.
Researchers think this control helps people set aside outdated or ________ information.

reading.title: The Comfort of Routine
reading.passage:
Many people describe their daily habits with a mixture of pride and embarrassment: the same breakfast each morning, the same route to work, the same chair in the evening. Such routines are sometimes dismissed as dull, yet psychologists increasingly see them as a quiet but powerful tool for managing the mind.

The central advantage of routine is that it reduces the number of decisions a person has to make. Every choice, however small, consumes a little mental energy. When a behaviour becomes automatic, that energy is freed for more demanding tasks. This is why some highly creative people deliberately keep their ordinary lives as predictable as possible: by removing trivial choices, they protect their attention for work that genuinely requires it.

Routine also offers emotional comfort. Familiar actions carry a sense of safety because their outcomes are known in advance. During periods of stress or upheaval, people often cling to small rituals — a morning walk, a particular cup of tea — precisely because these acts remain steady when everything else feels uncertain. The ritual itself may achieve little, but the predictability it provides can be genuinely calming.

There is, however, a cost. A life built entirely on routine can become rigid, leaving little room for spontaneity or new experience. The challenge, researchers suggest, is balance: enough structure to conserve energy and provide reassurance, but enough flexibility to allow growth. Used wisely, routine is not the enemy of a full life but one of its foundations.

questions.q: Psychologists now tend to view routines as a useful way of managing the mind.
According to the passage, making decisions uses up mental energy.
Most creative people surveyed said they disliked having predictable daily lives.
Why do some creative people keep their everyday lives predictable?
options: To save attention for demanding work

questions.q: During stressful times, people often value small rituals mainly because:
options: they offer a feeling of predictability

questions.q: Familiar actions carry a sense of ________ because their outcomes are known in advance.
A life built entirely on routine can become ________, leaving little room for spontaneity.
The writer concludes that routine should be avoided in order to live a full life.

listening.title: A Talk on Daily Habits
listening.script: SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone. Today I want to share a few practical ideas about building better daily habits. The first point is simple: start small. People often try to change five things at once, then give up within a week. Choosing just one new habit makes success far more likely. Second, attach the new habit to something you already do. If you always make coffee in the morning, do two minutes of stretching while it brews. We call this anchoring. Third, track your progress. A simple chart on the wall, or a tick in a notebook, gives a surprising sense of reward. Studies suggest that people who record their habits stick with them about thirty per cent longer. Fourth, be patient. Despite the popular claim that habits form in twenty-one days, research shows it usually takes closer to two months for a behaviour to feel automatic. And finally, forgive the occasional slip. Missing one day does not undo your progress; what matters is returning the next day. So remember: small, anchored, recorded, and forgiving. Those four principles will take you a long way.

reading.title: The Power of Expectation
reading.passage:
What we expect to happen can shape what actually happens, often without our awareness. Psychologists call this the expectancy effect, and it appears in settings ranging from classrooms to hospitals. The basic idea is that beliefs about an outcome can quietly influence behaviour in ways that help bring that outcome about.

A famous early study took place in a primary school. Teachers were told that certain pupils, identified by a test, were likely to make rapid progress that year. In reality the pupils had been chosen at random. Yet by the end of the year, these children really had improved more than their classmates. The most plausible explanation is that the teachers, expecting success, gave those pupils more attention, warmer encouragement and harder tasks. The expectation had become a kind of self-fulfilling prophecy.

The same effect can work in the opposite direction. If a coach quietly doubts a player's ability, that doubt may show in subtle signals — less eye contact, fewer chances to perform — which in turn discourage the player and confirm the original low opinion. In both cases the person doing the expecting is rarely aware of the influence they exert.

Understanding the expectancy effect does not mean that belief alone can guarantee any result. A confident attitude will not heal a serious illness or turn a beginner into an expert overnight. What the research does show is that expectations are not neutral. They leak into our tone, our choices and our effort, and they help to create the very conditions they predict.

questions.q: The pupils described as likely to improve had actually been selected at random.
The expectancy effect can operate in a negative as well as a positive direction.
The school study was later repeated in several other countries.
Why did the chosen pupils improve more than their classmates?
options: Teachers gave them more attention and encouragement
They studied at home for longer

questions.q: How might a coach's doubt affect a player?
options: It can discourage the player through subtle signals
It forces the player to leave the team

questions.q: The teachers' expectation became a kind of self-fulfilling ________.
The research shows that expectations are not ________.
The passage claims that a confident attitude alone can cure a serious illness.

MAYA: How many participants do we need?

MAYA: Right, twenty. And we should keep the room quiet so nothing distracts them.
TOM: Good point. We'll run it in the seminar room on Friday afternoon.

MAYA: Perfect. I'll prepare the juice and the fake labels. This could actually be fun.

reading.title: The Loneliness of the Crowd
reading.passage:
Loneliness is often imagined as physical isolation — a person living far from others, with no one to talk to. Yet psychologists point out that loneliness is better understood as a gap between the relationships a person has and the relationships they want. By this definition, someone can feel deeply lonely in a busy city, surrounded by colleagues and acquaintances, while another person living alone in the countryside may feel perfectly content.

The distinction matters because it changes how loneliness might be addressed. If the problem were simply a lack of company, the solution would be to put more people around the lonely individual. But research suggests that this rarely works on its own. What seems to matter is not the number of contacts but their quality: the sense of being understood, valued and able to confide. A single close friendship can do more to relieve loneliness than a wide circle of casual acquaintances.

Modern life may make this harder in unexpected ways. Online networks allow people to maintain hundreds of loose connections with very little effort. These connections are not worthless, but they can create an illusion of intimacy that masks a deeper shortage of close ties. Scrolling through the cheerful updates of others can even sharpen the feeling of being left out.

None of this means that solitude is harmful. Many people actively choose time alone and find it restorative. The crucial difference is choice: chosen solitude can be peaceful, while unwanted isolation, or the quiet ache of feeling unseen in a crowd, is what truly wears people down.

questions.q: According to the passage, loneliness always results from living far away from other people.
Simply increasing the number of people around a lonely person usually solves the problem on its own.
The passage states that women report higher rates of loneliness than men.
What matters most in relieving loneliness, according to the passage?
options: The number of contacts a person has

options: An illusion of intimacy that hides a shortage of close ties

questions.q: Loneliness is best understood as a gap between the relationships a person has and those they ________.

listening.title: Radio Feature on Social Connection
listening.script:
HOST: Today on the program we're looking at loneliness, and I'm joined by researcher Dr. Lena Ortiz. Welcome.
ORTIZ: Thank you for having me.
HOST: So, is loneliness really increasing?
ORTIZ: In many countries, yes. In our recent survey, about one in three adults said they often feel lonely. That's a striking figure.
HOST: One in three. And who is most affected?
ORTIZ: Interestingly, young adults reported the highest levels, higher even than the elderly, which surprises many people.
HOST: That is surprising. What can individuals actually do?
ORTIZ: The most effective step is small and regular contact. Even a five-minute phone call to a friend each day makes a measurable difference.
HOST: Just five minutes?
ORTIZ: Yes, consistency matters more than length. We also recommend joining a group built around a shared activity, like a sports club or a choir.
HOST: A choir, really?
ORTIZ: Singing together releases stress and creates a sense of belonging. Our participants who joined one reported a forty per cent drop in loneliness over three months.
HOST: Remarkable. Dr. Ortiz, thank you.

reading.title: Why First Impressions Stick
reading.passage:
Within seconds of meeting someone, most of us form a judgement about them — whether they seem warm or cold, trustworthy or suspect, competent or careless. These rapid impressions are formed on very little evidence, yet they are remarkably hard to shift afterwards. Psychologists have long been interested in why such snap judgements carry so much weight.

Part of the answer lies in efficiency. For most of human history, the ability to size up a stranger quickly had real survival value. A person who could decide in an instant whether an approaching figure was friend or threat had an advantage over one who hesitated. The mind therefore evolved to reach conclusions fast, drawing on whatever cues are immediately available, from facial expression to tone of voice.

The trouble is that speed comes at the cost of accuracy. Once a first impression has formed, it tends to act like a filter. New information that fits the impression is readily accepted, while information that contradicts it is questioned or explained away. This tendency, known as confirmation bias, means that an unfair early judgement can quietly reinforce itself over many later encounters.

There are ways to resist the pull of first impressions, though they require effort. Deliberately seeking evidence that might prove an initial judgement wrong, and reminding ourselves how little we actually knew at the start, can loosen the grip of an early conclusion. Such habits will never make us perfectly objective, but they can keep the door open to changing our minds — which, in dealings with other people, is no small thing.

questions.q: First impressions are usually formed only after gathering a great deal of evidence.
The ability to judge strangers quickly once had survival value, according to the passage.
The passage says that first impressions are more accurate in older people than in younger people.
What does the passage say happens to information that contradicts a first impression?
options: It is questioned or explained away

questions.q: What is one suggested way to resist first impressions?
options: Seeking evidence that might prove the judgement wrong

questions.q: A first impression tends to act like a ________, shaping how new information is treated.
The tendency to accept supporting information and reject contradicting information is called confirmation ________.
The writer claims these habits can make us completely objective.

listening.title: Lecture: Judging Others Quickly
listening.script: LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today we're examining how quickly we judge other people, and how reliable those judgements are. Researchers have found that we form an impression of a stranger's face in roughly one-tenth of a second. That's faster than a blink. In one classic study, participants were shown faces for just a hundred milliseconds, and their snap ratings of trustworthiness matched the ratings given by people who looked for as long as they liked. So extra time barely changed the verdict. Now, you might assume these instant judgements are accurate. Sadly, the evidence is mixed. We tend to read competence into certain features, such as a strong jaw, even though appearance tells us nothing real about ability. This matters in the real world. Studies of elections show that candidates judged as more competent from a single photograph win more often, which is rather worrying for democracy. For next week, please read chapter six and prepare a short example of a time a first impression misled you. We'll discuss them in groups.

reading.title: The Lighthouse of Alexandria
reading.passage:
Among the structures the ancient world counted as wonders, the lighthouse on the island of Pharos, off the coast of Alexandria, occupied a special place. Commissioned in the early third century BCE under the Ptolemaic rulers of Egypt, it was conceived less as a monument than as a practical aid to the busy shipping lanes that fed one of the Mediterranean's wealthiest ports. By night a fire blazed at its summit, and by day, according to several later accounts, polished bronze reflected the sun to warn approaching vessels of the treacherous shoals.

The tower was built in three diminishing tiers: a broad square base, an octagonal middle section, and a cylindrical upper stage crowned by a statue. Estimates of its height vary considerably, but most modern scholars place it somewhere between one hundred and one hundred and forty metres, which would have made it among the tallest constructions then standing. Such a scale demanded enormous quantities of dressed stone, and the limestone blocks were fitted with great precision so that the structure could withstand the salt winds that battered the coast.

For centuries the lighthouse guided ships safely into harbour, and travellers from distant lands described it with admiration. Its decline, however, was gradual rather than sudden. A series of earthquakes between the tenth and fourteenth centuries weakened the masonry, and the upper stages eventually collapsed. The remaining stones were later reused to build a fortress on the same site. Marine archaeologists have since recovered fragments from the seabed, allowing researchers to reconstruct, at least in part, a building that had once seemed indestructible.

questions.q: The lighthouse was built primarily as a decorative monument rather than for practical use.
Ancient accounts claim that mirrors were used to reflect sunlight during the day.

CALLER: Great. Does that include the special exhibition on ancient trade routes?

STAFF: Lovely. Just so you know, we ask groups to arrive fifteen minutes early to collect their tickets from the front desk.
CALLER: Understood. Thanks very much.

The structure was built to withstand strong winds.

reading.title: The Silk Road and Its Goods
reading.passage:
The phrase 'Silk Road' suggests a single highway stretching from China to the Mediterranean, but the reality was far more tangled. It was a shifting web of overland and maritime routes that linked dozens of civilisations across Asia, the Middle East and Europe. Although silk gave the network its modern name, the goods that travelled along it were remarkably varied, and few merchants made the entire journey themselves. Instead, commodities passed through many hands, each trader covering only a portion of the route before selling on to the next.

Silk was indeed prized in the Roman world, where it commanded extraordinary prices and was regarded as a mark of luxury. Yet it was only one item among many. Spices, glassware, precious stones, horses and paper all moved across these routes, and so did less tangible things. Religions, artistic styles, scientific ideas and even diseases spread along the same corridors that carried merchandise. In this sense the Silk Road functioned as a channel for cultural exchange quite as much as for trade.

The network flourished particularly when strong empires policed the routes and guaranteed a measure of safety to travellers. When central authority weakened, banditry increased and traffic fell. The eventual rise of reliable sea routes, which could carry far heavier cargoes at lower cost, gradually reduced the importance of the overland passages. By the time European sailors were charting new ocean paths, the great caravans had already lost much of their former significance, though the cultural legacy of the exchange endured for centuries afterwards.

questions.q: The Silk Road consisted of a single fixed route from China to Europe.
Most merchants travelled the whole length of the route personally.
Silk was the only valuable item traded along the network.
Roman silk was usually woven by craftsmen trained in China.
According to the passage, the Silk Road also spread which of the following?
What weakened traffic along the routes?
Trade flourished when strong empires ________ the routes.
The rise of reliable ________ routes reduced the importance of overland passages.

listening.title: A Lecture on Ancient Trade
listening.script: LECTURER: Right, let's begin. Today I want to focus on how goods actually moved across ancient Asia. Now, a common misconception is that a single merchant carried silk all the way from China to Rome. In fact, goods changed hands many times, sometimes more than twenty, before reaching their final market. This relay system meant that the price rose dramatically at each stage. By the time silk reached Rome, it could cost as much as its weight in gold. Another point worth noting is the role of oasis towns. These settlements were not just resting places; they were thriving commercial centres where traders exchanged news, currency and goods. The town of Samarkand, for example, became enormously wealthy precisely because of its position. Finally, remember that trade carried more than objects. When you study this period, pay attention to how technologies such as papermaking travelled westward, often arriving centuries after they were first invented in the east. I'll post the reading list on the portal by Friday, so please review it before our next seminar.

questions.q: What misconception does the lecturer correct?
options: That one merchant carried goods all the way to Rome
That Rome produced its own silk

reading.title: The Invention of Writing in Mesopotamia
reading.passage:
Writing was not invented in a single moment of inspiration. In Mesopotamia, the earliest known system emerged gradually out of the practical demands of an increasingly complex society. As cities grew and surplus food had to be stored, traded and taxed, administrators needed a reliable way to keep track of who owed what to whom. The first marks pressed into clay were not poetry or history but accounts: lists of grain, livestock and labour.

The script we now call cuneiform began as a series of pictures, each sign standing for an object. A drawing of an ox head meant an ox; a stylised ear of barley meant grain. Over time, however, the signs became more abstract. Scribes pressed a reed stylus into wet clay to produce wedge-shaped marks, and the original pictures were simplified into combinations of these wedges. Crucially, signs eventually came to represent sounds rather than objects, which allowed the system to record spoken language with far greater flexibility, including names and grammatical elements that no picture could easily convey.

Learning to write was difficult and time-consuming, so literacy remained the preserve of a small, trained class of scribes. These individuals enjoyed considerable status, since their skills were essential to the running of temples, palaces and businesses. The clay tablets they produced, baked hard by accident in fires or deliberately for storage, have survived in their thousands. From them, modern scholars have pieced together not only economic records but also myths, legal codes and letters, offering an unusually detailed picture of a civilisation that existed more than four thousand years ago.

questions.q: Writing in Mesopotamia developed suddenly from a single invention.
The earliest written records were mainly literary works.
Cuneiform signs eventually came to represent sounds as well as objects.
Scribes were paid more than temple priests.
What practical need first drove the development of writing?
Why did literacy remain limited to a small group?
options: Few people had ideas to record

questions.q: Scribes pressed a reed ________ into wet clay.
Some clay tablets were baked hard accidentally in ________.

listening.title: Museum Audio Guide: The Tablet Room
listening.script: GUIDE: Welcome to the Tablet Room, one of the most important galleries in our collection. The objects you see in the cases before you are clay tablets, some of them over four thousand years old. Please begin at the case on your left, numbered one. Here you will find administrative tablets recording quantities of grain. Notice how small the wedge-shaped marks are; scribes worked with great care. Moving to case two, you will see a school exercise tablet. Students copied the same signs again and again, much as children practise handwriting today. Case three contains something rather special: a fragment of an ancient poem, one of the oldest stories ever recorded. We ask visitors not to use flash photography, as bright light can damage the surfaces. If you would like to handle a replica tablet, our staff member by the exit will be happy to assist between two and four o'clock each afternoon. The full catalogue is available in the shop for nine pounds fifty.

reading.title: The Black Death and Its Aftermath
reading.passage:
When plague swept across Europe in the middle of the fourteenth century, it killed an enormous proportion of the population in a few short years. Contemporary observers, lacking any understanding of how the disease spread, attributed it variously to divine punishment, poisoned air or the alignment of the planets. The true cause, a bacterium carried by fleas on rats, would not be identified for centuries. What was undeniable, however, was the speed and scale of the mortality: in some towns more than half the inhabitants perished.

The immediate consequences were terrible, but the longer-term effects were more complicated than simple devastation. With so many labourers dead, those who survived found their work suddenly in great demand. Wages rose, and in many regions the rigid social arrangements that had bound peasants to the land for generations began to loosen. Landowners, desperate to keep workers, were sometimes forced to offer better terms, and attempts to hold wages down by law frequently failed. In this way a catastrophe contributed, however indirectly, to shifts in the balance of economic power.

Historians continue to debate just how far the plague transformed European society. Some argue that it accelerated changes already under way, hastening the decline of the old feudal order. Others caution against crediting a single event with too much, pointing out that conditions varied widely from place to place and that recovery followed different paths in different regions. What is clear is that the disease left a deep mark on the imagination of those who lived through it, an anxiety reflected in the art, literature and religious feeling of the generations that followed.

questions.q: People at the time correctly understood how the plague spread.
In some towns more than half the population died.
After the plague, wages for surviving workers tended to rise.
The plague reached Asia before it spread to Europe.
What was the actual cause of the disease?
How do some historians view the plague's effect on society?
Landowners were sometimes forced to offer better ________ to keep workers.
The anxiety it caused was reflected in the art, literature and ________ feeling of later generations.

listening.title: A Student Discussion on the Plague
listening.script:
TOM: Did you finish the reading on the Black Death for the seminar?
MAYA: Most of it. The bit about wages really surprised me. I'd always assumed the plague was just a disaster with no upside.
TOM: Same. But the argument is that fewer workers meant survivors could demand more money.
MAYA: Right, and the landowners couldn't really stop them, even when laws were passed.
TOM: Exactly. Though Professor Reed warned us not to oversimplify. She said the effects varied a lot from region to region.
MAYA: That's a fair point. Some places recovered quickly, others took decades.
TOM: For the essay, I'm thinking of focusing on the economic side rather than the religious response.
MAYA: Good idea. I might do the opposite, actually, the way fear shaped art and literature.
TOM: Between us we'll cover both. Don't forget the essay's due on the fifteenth.
MAYA: Thanks for the reminder. Shall we meet in the library on Wednesday to compare notes?
TOM: Sounds good. Two o'clock?
MAYA: Perfect.

reading.title: The Printing Press and the Spread of Ideas
reading.passage:
The development of printing with movable metal type in Europe in the middle of the fifteenth century is often described as a turning point in history. Before it, books were copied by hand, a slow and costly process that confined most written works to monasteries, universities and the wealthy. A single manuscript might take a scribe months to produce, and errors crept in with every copy. The new technology changed this situation dramatically, allowing identical copies to be produced in large numbers at a fraction of the previous cost.

The consequences extended far beyond the book trade. As printed material became cheaper and more abundant, literacy gradually spread to wider sections of society. Ideas that had once circulated slowly among a small educated elite could now reach thousands of readers within a few years. Religious reformers, scientists and political thinkers all seized upon the press as a means of broadcasting their arguments, and authorities who had previously controlled the flow of information found it increasingly difficult to suppress unwelcome views.

It would be a mistake, however, to imagine that the press caused these changes single-handedly. Demand for reading material had been growing before the press appeared, and the technology spread fastest in places where commerce, education and a degree of political freedom already existed. The press was best understood not as a lone cause but as a powerful amplifier, magnifying tendencies that were already present. What is beyond dispute is that, once established, printing transformed the speed and scale at which knowledge could travel, with effects that continue to be felt today.

questions.q: Before printing, books were mainly available only to a narrow section of society.
Hand-copied manuscripts were always free of errors.
The press made it harder for authorities to suppress unwelcome ideas.
The first printed book in Europe was a religious text.
How does the passage describe the role of the printing press?
options: The sole cause of social change
A powerful amplifier of existing tendencies

questions.q: Where did printing spread fastest?
options: Where commerce and education already existed

questions.q: A single manuscript might take a scribe ________ to produce.
Printing transformed the speed and ________ at which knowledge could travel.

First, registration: to access the reading room you'll need a reader's card, which you can obtain from the desk on the ground floor. You'll need photo identification and proof of your student status. Second, the rare books themselves. Many of our printed volumes date from the sixteenth century, and some are extremely fragile. For that reason, no pens are allowed inside; please use pencils only. Bags must be left in the lockers near the entrance, and the locker deposit is one pound, refundable. The reading room is open from nine in the morning until six in the evening on weekdays, and from ten until two on Saturdays. If you wish to request a particular item, please fill in a request slip at least one day in advance, as the books are stored off-site. Finally, our digital catalogue is searchable from any computer on campus. Any questions before we begin the tour?

reading.title: The Quiet Comeback of the House Call
reading.passage:
For much of the twentieth century, a doctor arriving at a patient's door was an ordinary sight. As clinics and hospitals grew larger and more efficient, however, the home visit was gradually pushed aside. Patients were expected to travel to the doctor, not the other way around. In recent years, that pattern has begun to shift, and the house call is making a quiet comeback in several countries.

The reasons are partly practical. An ageing population means that more people live with conditions that make leaving the house difficult. For someone who is frail or recovering from surgery, a single journey to a crowded waiting room can be exhausting and even dangerous. Sending a clinician to the home avoids these risks and, supporters argue, allows for a fuller picture of a patient's daily life. A doctor who sees an empty fridge or a flight of unsafe stairs learns something a clinic visit would never reveal.

Technology has also played a part. Portable devices now allow blood tests, heart traces and ultrasound scans to be carried out almost anywhere. Where a home visit once meant little more than a stethoscope and reassurance, a visiting team can today perform many of the checks once reserved for a hospital. Some health systems have combined these visits with video consultations, so that a specialist can advise the home team in real time.

Critics caution that the model is expensive. A clinician who spends an hour travelling between two patients could have seen several more in a clinic. Yet a number of pilot schemes report that the approach reduces costly emergency admissions, because problems are caught early. Whether the house call becomes a mainstream service or remains a niche option is likely to depend less on medicine than on how its costs are counted.

questions.q: Home visits by doctors were uncommon throughout the entire twentieth century.
An ageing population is given as one reason for the return of the house call.
Home visits are now more common in rural areas than in cities.
According to the passage, what advantage does seeing a patient at home offer a doctor?
options: It is always cheaper than a clinic visit.
It reveals details of daily life that a clinic would miss.
It removes the need for any laboratory tests.

questions.q: Portable devices now allow blood tests, heart traces and ________ scans to be done almost anywhere.
What do critics say about the home-visit model?
options: It can be expensive in clinician time.

questions.q: Some pilot schemes report that home visits cut the number of emergency admissions.
The writer suggests the future of the house call depends mainly on how its ________ are counted.

PATIENT: Great. When are nurses usually free?

PATIENT: Will do. Thank you for your help.

reading.title: Salt, Habit and the Hidden Half
reading.passage:
Most people know that eating too much salt is bad for the heart, yet few realise how little control they have over the amount they consume. Surveys in several wealthy countries suggest that only about a quarter of the salt in a typical diet is added at the table or during home cooking. The remainder, sometimes called the hidden half, is already present in processed foods before they reach the kitchen.

This matters because excess salt raises blood pressure, which in turn increases the risk of strokes and heart disease. Reducing intake is therefore a public-health goal in many nations. The difficulty is that the worst offenders are not obviously salty. Bread, breakfast cereals and ready-made sauces can contribute large amounts without tasting particularly salty at all. A single sandwich may hold more salt than a packet of crisps.

Governments have tried two broad approaches. The first relies on educating shoppers, often through labels that mark high-salt products with coloured warnings. The second works quietly with manufacturers, persuading them to lower salt gradually so that consumers barely notice. Evidence suggests the second method may be more powerful: when a whole category of bread is reformulated, every buyer benefits, including those who never read a label.

Not everyone welcomes such intervention. Some argue that adults should be free to eat as they wish and that the state has no business reshaping recipes. Others reply that because the salt is hidden, genuine free choice does not really exist. The debate, like the salt itself, tends to stay out of sight until a health crisis brings it to the surface.

questions.q: In a typical diet, most salt is added by people during cooking or at the table.
Excess salt is linked to higher blood pressure.
Why are foods like bread and cereal described as a problem?
options: They contain a lot of salt without seeming salty.
They are cheaper than other foods.

questions.q: Roughly a ________ of the salt in a typical diet is added at the table or in home cooking.
According to the passage, which approach may be more effective?

The hidden salt is already present in ________ foods before they reach the kitchen.

listening.title: Library Tour for Health Sciences Students
listening.script: GUIDE: Welcome, everyone. I'm going to show you around the Health Sciences Library, so you know where everything is before term starts. We're standing now in the main reading room, which is open from eight in the morning until midnight on weekdays. On the left you'll see the journals section. Most of our medical journals are now digital, but the printed back issues are kept here on the second floor. If you need an article from before two thousand, that's where to look. Just ahead is the help desk, staffed by subject librarians who can show you how to search the databases. I'd really recommend booking one of their free training sessions in your first week. They run every Wednesday at four o'clock and last about forty minutes. Group study rooms are at the back; you book those online, up to two weeks in advance. One quick rule: no hot food anywhere in the building, though covered drinks are fine. Finally, your student card is also your printing card, so keep it topped up. Any questions before we move on?

reading.title: When Sleep Becomes Medicine
reading.passage:
For a long time, sleep was treated as a passive state, a kind of nightly switching-off during which little of importance happened. Modern research has overturned that idea. Far from being idle, the sleeping brain is intensely busy, sorting memories, repairing tissue and clearing waste products that build up during waking hours. Doctors increasingly speak of sleep not as a luxury but as a form of medicine.

One striking discovery concerns the brain's cleaning system. During deep sleep, the spaces between brain cells appear to widen, allowing fluid to wash through and flush out harmful molecules. Some scientists suspect that long-term poor sleep may allow these molecules to accumulate, possibly contributing to certain diseases of the mind in later life. The evidence is still emerging, and researchers are careful not to overstate it.

The effects of insufficient sleep on the body are clearer. People who regularly sleep too little tend to have weaker immune responses, higher levels of stress hormones and a greater likelihood of weight gain. Reaction times slow, and the risk of accidents rises sharply. In several studies, a single bad night affected performance on memory tasks as much as mild alcohol consumption did.

Awareness of these findings has changed advice given to patients. Where doctors once focused almost entirely on diet and exercise, many now treat sleep as a third pillar of health, asking about bedtimes as routinely as they ask about meals. Yet modern life works against good sleep. Bright screens, irregular shifts and constant connectivity all push bedtimes later. The challenge for public health is not convincing people that sleep matters, but helping them protect it in a world designed to keep them awake.

questions.q: Scientists once believed little of importance occurred during sleep.
The brain's cleaning system is most active during light sleep.
What happens between brain cells during deep sleep, according to the passage?
options: The spaces widen so fluid can flush out molecules.

questions.q: Researchers have proved that poor sleep directly causes diseases of the mind.
People who sleep too little tend to have weaker ________ responses.
How is sleep now described in medical advice?
options: as a third pillar of health
as relevant only to the elderly

questions.q: Most people refuse to believe that sleep is important.
A single bad night affected memory tasks about as much as mild ________ consumption.

listening.title: A Tutorial on Sleep Research Methods
listening.script:
TUTOR: Right, before you design your sleep study, let's go over how researchers actually measure sleep. The most reliable method is something called polysomnography. Don't worry about spelling it perfectly; the key point is that it records brain activity, eye movements and muscle tone overnight, usually in a laboratory.
STUDENT: But isn't sleeping in a lab a bit artificial?
TUTOR: Good question. It is, and that's the main drawback. People often sleep worse on the first night, so we usually discard that night's data entirely. The second night is treated as the real baseline.
STUDENT: So what's the alternative for everyday studies?
TUTOR: For larger groups we use wrist devices that estimate sleep from movement. They're far cheaper and people wear them at home, but they're less accurate, especially for measuring deep sleep. For your project, with forty participants, I'd suggest the wrist devices, supported by a short daily diary.
STUDENT: How long should the study run?
TUTOR: Two weeks gives a decent picture. Anything shorter and you'll just capture random variation rather than someone's true pattern.

questions.q: What is the most reliable method of measuring sleep?
Polysomnography records brain activity, eye movements and ________ tone.
Researchers usually discard the data from the ________ night in the lab.
What is the main weakness of wrist devices?
options: They are less accurate, especially for deep sleep.
They cannot be worn at home.

questions.q: The tutor suggests supporting the wrist devices with a short daily ________.
The tutor recommends running the study for ________ weeks.

speaking.q: Let's discuss sleep and modern life. Why do you think many people in modern societies struggle to get enough sleep? To what extent should employers be responsible for their workers' rest? Do you think attitudes towards sleep will change in the future?

writing.q:
The table below shows the average hours of sleep per night reported by four age groups in a survey, comparing two decades.

Age group | 2000 | 2020
Teenagers (13-18) | 8.4 | 7.1
Young adults (19-30) | 7.6 | 6.8
Adults (31-60) | 7.2 | 6.6
Older adults (60+) | 7.0 | 6.9

Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Sleep was once seen as a passive state.
Research overturned that old idea.

Fluid flushes out harmful molecules.
Researchers are careful not to overstate the link.
Insufficient sleep weakens the immune system.

The sleeping brain is far from idle.

reading.title: The Long Shadow of Antibiotics
reading.passage:
When the first antibiotics came into widespread use in the middle of the last century, they were greeted almost as miracles. Infections that had once killed routinely could suddenly be cured in days. Within a generation, however, a shadow had begun to lengthen behind this triumph. Bacteria, it turned out, were remarkably good at adapting, and some learned to survive the very drugs designed to destroy them.

The mechanism is simple in outline. Whenever an antibiotic is used, the most vulnerable bacteria die first, while any that happen to carry a useful resistance trait are more likely to survive and multiply. Over many such cycles, resistant strains come to dominate. The more often a drug is used, the faster this process unfolds. This is why doctors are now urged to prescribe antibiotics only when they are genuinely needed.

The problem is not confined to hospitals. A large share of the world's antibiotics is given to farm animals, often not to treat illness but to make them grow faster. Critics argue that this practice breeds resistant bacteria that can eventually reach humans through food or the environment. Several countries have moved to restrict such uses, though enforcement varies and global trade complicates the picture.

Developing new antibiotics might seem the obvious answer, yet the pipeline has slowed to a trickle. The drugs are expensive to develop but, ideally, should be used sparingly once approved, which makes them unattractive to manufacturers seeking profit. Some experts therefore call for public funding to reward the creation of new drugs regardless of how much they are sold. Whether such schemes arrive in time is one of the quiet but pressing questions of modern medicine.

questions.q: Antibiotics were viewed with suspicion when they first became widely available.
According to the passage, how does resistance spread among bacteria?
options: Vulnerable bacteria pass on resistance before dying.
Resistant bacteria survive treatment and multiply.

questions.q: Using an antibiotic more often speeds up the rise of resistance.
Much of the world's antibiotics is given to ________ animals, often to make them grow faster.
All countries have now banned the use of antibiotics in farming.
Why are companies reluctant to develop new antibiotics?
options: The drugs are easy to copy.
They are costly to develop but should be used sparingly.

questions.q: Antibiotic resistance has caused more deaths than any other modern disease.
Some experts call for public ________ to reward the creation of new drugs.

listening.title: A Lecture on Vaccine Storage
listening.script: LECTURER: Today I want to talk about something that sounds dull but saves millions of lives: keeping vaccines cold. We call the system the cold chain. From the moment a vaccine is made until it reaches a patient's arm, it must be kept within a narrow temperature range, usually between two and eight degrees Celsius. Too warm, and the vaccine loses its power; too cold, and some types are damaged by freezing, which people often forget. Now, in wealthy countries the cold chain is largely invisible because the infrastructure already exists. The real challenge appears in remote regions, where electricity is unreliable and the nearest clinic may be hours away by road. One increasingly popular solution is the solar-powered refrigerator, which needs no mains power and can run for days on stored energy. Another is a special cool box that holds the right temperature for up to five days without any power at all, useful for the final stretch of a journey. The lesson is that a vaccine is only as good as the chain that delivers it.

questions.q: The system for keeping vaccines cold is called the ________ chain.
Vaccines are usually kept between two and ________ degrees Celsius.
What mistake does the lecturer say people often forget?
options: that vaccines can be damaged by freezing

questions.q: Where is the cold chain hardest to maintain?
options: in remote regions with unreliable electricity

questions.q: A ________-powered refrigerator needs no mains electricity.
A special cool box can hold the right temperature for up to ________ days without power.

speaking.q: Let's talk about medicine and remedies. When you have a minor illness, do you prefer to take medicine or to rest and recover naturally? Did your family use any traditional remedies when you were a child? Do you think people today rely too much on medicine?

writing.q: The overuse of antibiotics in both medicine and farming has led to drug-resistant bacteria. Some people think the main responsibility lies with governments, while others believe doctors and farmers must change their behaviour. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Antibiotics were an early triumph of medicine.
Bacteria adapt to survive drugs.
Drug resistance is spreading worldwide.
Vulnerable bacteria die first.
Resistant strains come to dominate.
Survivors multiply rapidly.
Doctors should prescribe antibiotics carefully.
The problem is not confined to hospitals.
Enforcement of the rules varies.

reading.title: Reading the City's Health in Its Sewers
reading.passage:
Few places seem less promising for science than a sewer, yet the wastewater flowing beneath a city has become a surprisingly valuable source of public-health information. Everything a population eats, drinks or takes eventually leaves traces in the water it flushes away. By sampling and analysing that water, researchers can build a picture of a community's health without testing a single individual.

The technique, known as wastewater epidemiology, first attracted attention as a way of estimating drug use. Because tiny chemical remnants survive the journey through the human body, scientists could compare cities by measuring these traces, sidestepping the unreliable answers that surveys often produce. The same logic was later applied to disease. During recent outbreaks, monitoring teams could detect the rise of an infection in the sewers days before it showed up in clinics, giving hospitals precious time to prepare.

The appeal is obvious. The method is cheap, fast and, crucially, anonymous: it measures a whole neighbourhood at once without singling anyone out. A single sample can represent tens of thousands of people who would never volunteer for a study. For overstretched health systems, this offers an early-warning radar that runs quietly in the background.

Still, the approach has limits. It can reveal that something is rising in a district but rarely who is affected or how severely. There are also ethical questions about analysing a community's waste without its explicit consent, even when no individual can be identified. Researchers insist the data is anonymous by design, yet they acknowledge that public trust must be earned rather than assumed. Used wisely, the humble sewer may yet become one of the more powerful instruments in the public-health toolkit.

questions.q: Wastewater analysis requires testing many individuals separately.
What was wastewater epidemiology first used to estimate?
During recent outbreaks, sewer monitoring could detect infections before clinics did.
A key advantage of the method is that it is ________, measuring a whole neighbourhood without singling anyone out.
What is described as a limit of the technique?
options: It rarely shows who is affected or how severely.
It works only in small towns.

questions.q: Most cities have already passed laws requiring wastewater testing.
Researchers believe public trust can simply be assumed.
Scientists could compare cities by measuring tiny chemical ________ that survive in the water.

listening.title: Planning a Public Health Field Project
listening.script:
SUPERVISOR: So, you're keen to base your dissertation on the wastewater project. Let's sort out the practical side.
STUDENT: Yes. I was hoping to collect samples myself.
SUPERVISOR: That's possible, but you'll need a safety briefing first; it's compulsory before anyone goes near the treatment plant. It runs on the first Monday of each month.
STUDENT: Right. And how many sites would I be sampling?
SUPERVISOR: For a project of this size, I'd keep it to three sites. More than that and the lab work becomes unmanageable on your own.
STUDENT: Understood. What about timing through the day?
SUPERVISOR: Aim for early morning, around seven, when flows are most consistent. Late samples are far harder to interpret.
STUDENT: And storage?
SUPERVISOR: Critical. Keep every sample chilled and get it to the lab within six hours, or the results won't be trustworthy. Label each bottle with the site code and the exact time.
STUDENT: Got it. I'll draft a schedule and send it over.
SUPERVISOR: Do. And remember the deadline for the ethics form is the end of this month.

The supervisor recommends sampling from ________ sites.

Diet and nutrition studies | 3 | 9

reading.title: The Quiet Revolution of the School Library
reading.passage:
For much of the twentieth century, the school library was regarded as a peripheral facility — a quiet room where pupils were sent to read silently or to copy passages from reference books. Its central purpose was custodial: to store printed material and to enforce an atmosphere of calm. Teachers rarely incorporated the library into their lessons, and the librarian was often viewed less as an educator than as a keeper of order and an issuer of borrowing cards.

This perception has shifted considerably over the past two decades. As information has become abundant and easily accessible online, the value of a library can no longer rest on the mere possession of texts. Instead, many institutions now describe their libraries as 'learning hubs', spaces designed to teach pupils how to locate, evaluate and combine information rather than simply to consume it. The contemporary school librarian is increasingly expected to deliver lessons on digital literacy, helping students distinguish reliable evidence from misleading claims.

The physical design of these spaces has changed to match their new function. Rigid rows of desks have given way to flexible furniture that can be rearranged for group projects, discussion or quiet individual study. Some schools have introduced 'maker' areas where pupils experiment with simple electronics or three-dimensional printing, blurring the boundary between the library and the workshop.

Not everyone welcomes these developments. Critics argue that the emphasis on collaboration and technology risks eroding the one thing the traditional library did well: providing a refuge of sustained concentration. A noisy, activity-filled room, they contend, may serve some learners poorly, particularly those who struggle to focus amid distraction. Defenders respond that a well-designed modern library can accommodate both modes, reserving certain zones for silence while opening others to conversation. The debate, ultimately, reflects a deeper uncertainty about what schools believe reading and learning are for.

questions.q: In the twentieth century, teachers frequently built the library into their classroom lessons.
The ease of finding information online has weakened the argument that a library's worth lies in owning texts.
Modern school librarians are expected to teach pupils how to judge whether sources are trustworthy.
Schools that introduced maker areas reported improved examination results.
What is the main objection raised by critics of the modern library?
options: It is too expensive to maintain

questions.q: How do defenders answer this objection?
options: By claiming a good design can serve both quiet and collaborative needs
By limiting access to older pupils

questions.q: Flexible furniture allows the space to be rearranged for group projects, discussion or quiet ________ study.
The article says the debate reflects uncertainty about what schools believe reading and ________ are for.

listening.title: Joining the University Library
listening.script:
LIBRARIAN: Good morning, welcome to the main library. Are you here to register?
STUDENT: Yes, I'm a new postgraduate student and I'd like to set up my account.
LIBRARIAN: Of course. Can I take your student number first?
STUDENT: It's 4 0 7 6 2.
LIBRARIAN: Lovely. And your full name?
STUDENT: Daniel Forsythe — that's F-O-R-S-Y-T-H-E.
LIBRARIAN: Thank you. As a postgraduate, you can borrow up to twenty items at a time, and the standard loan period is four weeks.
STUDENT: That's generous. What about reference books?
LIBRARIAN: Reference materials can't leave the building, I'm afraid, but you may use them in the reading room on the second floor. It's open until eleven at night during term.
STUDENT: Perfect. Is there a fee for late returns?
LIBRARIAN: Yes, twenty pence per day for ordinary loans. To avoid that, I'd recommend renewing online — you can renew an item up to three times.
STUDENT: Brilliant. And do I need anything to collect my card?
LIBRARIAN: Just bring a passport-sized photograph to the desk tomorrow, and we'll print it for you.

options: In the reading room on the second floor

The table below shows the percentage of students at one university who used different library facilities in 2010 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Facility | 2010 | 2020
Printed book loans | 68% | 41%

Group study rooms | 30% | 58%

reading.title: Learning a Language Later in Life
reading.passage:
A persistent myth holds that adults are simply incapable of mastering a new language. Children, the story goes, soak up grammar effortlessly, while their parents struggle hopelessly with verb endings and unfamiliar sounds. There is a grain of truth in this picture, but it is far less decisive than it appears. Research increasingly suggests that the disadvantages adults face are practical rather than biological.

It is true that very young children possess a remarkable ability to acquire accurate pronunciation. The muscles and neural pathways used in speech are still developing, and exposure to native speakers shapes them with little conscious effort. Beyond accent, however, the picture is more balanced. Adults bring powerful advantages: an established vocabulary in their first language, an understanding of how grammar works in the abstract, and the ability to study deliberately rather than relying on chance exposure.

What truly separates successful adult learners from unsuccessful ones is rarely talent. It is time and consistency. A learner who studies for twenty minutes every day will usually progress faster than one who crams for several hours once a week. Equally important is the willingness to make mistakes openly. Many adults, conscious of their social standing, hesitate to speak until they feel confident, and so deprive themselves of the very practice they need.

Motivation matters too, though not in the way many expect. Vague ambitions such as 'becoming fluent' tend to fade. Specific, personally meaningful goals — ordering food in a particular country, reading a favourite author in the original, talking with relatives — sustain effort far more reliably. The lesson for adult learners is encouraging: the obstacles are real, but most lie within their control, and few have anything to do with the supposed rigidity of the grown-up brain.

questions.q: The writer believes the difficulties adults face in language learning are mainly biological.
Young children acquire accurate pronunciation more easily than adults.
Adults have no advantages at all when learning a new language.
The writer states that bilingual adults learn third languages faster than monolingual adults.
According to the passage, what most distinguishes successful adult learners?
Why do many adults avoid speaking a new language?
options: They are concerned about their social standing

questions.q: The writer says specific, personally ________ goals sustain effort more reliably than vague ones.
A learner who studies twenty minutes every day usually progresses faster than one who ________ once a week.

listening.title: Signing Up for an Evening Spanish Course
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good evening, Community Learning Centre. How can I help?
CALLER: Hi, I'd like to enrol in your beginners' Spanish course.
RECEPTIONIST: Wonderful. The next one starts on the fifteenth of March and runs for ten weeks.
CALLER: Which evening is it on?
RECEPTIONIST: Classes are on Wednesdays, from seven until half past eight.
CALLER: And how much does it cost?
RECEPTIONIST: The full fee is a hundred and forty dollars, but there's a concession rate of ninety for students and pensioners.
CALLER: I'm a full-time student, so I'd qualify for that. Do I need to buy a textbook?
RECEPTIONIST: Yes, the course uses a book called Spanish Steps — the centre sells copies at reception for eighteen dollars.
CALLER: Great. Is there a limit on class size?
RECEPTIONIST: We cap each class at twelve people so everyone gets a chance to speak.
CALLER: That sounds ideal. How do I secure my place?
RECEPTIONIST: Just pay a deposit of twenty-five dollars online, and the balance is due on the first night.

The course runs for ________ weeks.

speaking.q:
Describe a skill you would like to learn in the future. You should say:
- what the skill is
- why you want to learn it
- how you would go about learning it
and explain how this skill might change your life.

writing.q:
Some people believe that children find it much easier to learn foreign languages than adults, and that language teaching should therefore begin as early as possible. Others argue that adults can learn just as effectively. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

The idea is a myth rather than a proven fact.

reading.title: Does Homework Help?
reading.passage:
Few features of schooling provoke as much disagreement as homework. To some, it is an indispensable bridge between the classroom and independent learning; to others, it is a needless burden that steals time from family life and sleep. The truth, as research repeatedly shows, depends heavily on how much is set, of what kind, and to whom.

For very young children, the evidence is surprisingly thin. Studies tracking primary pupils have generally found little or no link between time spent on homework and later academic performance. What modest benefit exists seems to come not from the tasks themselves but from the habits they encourage — sitting down at a regular hour, organising materials, and completing a job. Some researchers argue that for this age group, reading for pleasure at home is worth more than formal exercises.

The picture changes as pupils grow older. Among secondary students, a moderate amount of homework — perhaps one to two hours an evening — does appear to correlate with stronger results, particularly in subjects that reward repeated practice, such as mathematics. Yet the relationship is not limitless. Beyond a certain threshold, additional homework yields diminishing returns and may even prove counterproductive, as tired students disengage and resentment grows.

Quality, most experts agree, matters more than quantity. Repetitive worksheets that merely occupy time do little to deepen understanding. Tasks that ask pupils to apply ideas in fresh contexts, or to reflect on what they have learned, are far more valuable. There is also the question of fairness: pupils from comfortable homes, with quiet rooms and supportive adults, are better placed to complete demanding assignments than those who lack such resources. Homework, in other words, can quietly widen the very gaps that education hopes to close.

questions.q: Research has found a strong link between homework and academic success for primary-age children.
Some researchers think reading for pleasure benefits young children more than formal exercises.
For secondary students, the benefits of homework increase without any upper limit.
Schools that abolished homework saw a decline in student wellbeing.
What does the passage say about homework beyond a certain amount?
options: It produces diminishing returns and may backfire
It has no effect at all

questions.q: Which type of homework do experts consider most valuable?
options: Tasks applying ideas in new contexts

questions.q: The author warns that homework can quietly ________ the gaps that education hopes to close.
For young children, the modest benefit comes from the ________ that homework encourages.

listening.title: A Tutorial About a History Assignment
listening.script:
TUTOR: Come in, take a seat. You wanted to talk about the essay?
STUDENT: Yes, I'm a bit unsure how to narrow the topic down.
TUTOR: That's a common problem. The question on the industrial city is huge, so I'd pick a single theme — say, public health or transport.
STUDENT: Public health interests me more.
TUTOR: Good. Then focus on one city rather than several. Manchester would give you plenty of sources.
STUDENT: How long should the essay be?
TUTOR: Two thousand words, give or take ten per cent. And the deadline is the third of May.
STUDENT: Should I use the textbook for references?
TUTOR: Use it as a starting point, but I'd expect at least six academic sources, mostly journal articles. The library's database is the best place to find them.
STUDENT: Right. And how is it marked?
TUTOR: Forty per cent for argument, forty for evidence, and twenty for structure and writing. Don't leave the referencing until the last minute — that's where students lose easy marks.

The tutor suggests focusing on one city, namely ________.

Country | Ages 7-11 | Ages 12-16

Canada | 2.0 | 5.5
