reading.title: The Promise and Peril of Online Courses
reading.passage:
When the first large-scale online courses appeared, they were greeted with extraordinary optimism. Lectures by celebrated professors, once confined to a handful of elite campuses, could now be streamed to anyone with an internet connection. Commentators predicted a sweeping democratisation of education, in which a curious student in a remote village might learn alongside one at a prestigious university. The barriers of cost and geography, it seemed, were about to crumble.

Reality has been more complicated. Enrolment figures were indeed enormous, often running into the tens of thousands for a single course. Yet completion rates told a sobering story: in many cases, fewer than one in ten registered students reached the end. The freedom that made these courses attractive — study at your own pace, with no fixed timetable — also removed the gentle pressures that keep learners on track. Without deadlines, classmates or a tutor expecting their work, many quietly drifted away.

Researchers have also questioned who actually benefits. Far from reaching the underserved, online courses have tended to attract learners who already hold degrees and who possess the self-discipline that formal education instils. The technology, in other words, may have widened rather than narrowed the gap it promised to close, rewarding those already equipped to learn independently.

This is not to dismiss online learning, which has genuine strengths. It excels at delivering content flexibly and at allowing learners to revisit difficult material as often as they wish. The most promising models now combine online materials with some form of human contact — discussion groups, mentoring, or occasional face-to-face meetings. The lesson appears to be that technology can enrich education, but it cannot, on its own, supply the motivation and accountability that learning so often requires.

questions.q: Early online courses were initially met with great enthusiasm.
Completion rates for many early online courses were below ten per cent.
Online courses have mainly attracted learners who lack previous qualifications.
Universities that offered online courses lost income from their on-campus programmes.
According to the passage, why did many students fail to finish online courses?
options: There was a lack of deadlines and external pressure

questions.q: What does the writer suggest is the most promising model?
options: Combining online materials with human contact

questions.q: Commentators predicted a sweeping ________ of education.
The writer concludes that technology cannot, on its own, supply motivation and ________.

listening.title: A Talk on Effective Study Techniques
listening.script: SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone. Today I'll share three study techniques that research has shown actually work. The first is what psychologists call retrieval practice. Instead of simply rereading your notes, you close the book and try to recall the material from memory. It feels harder, but that effort is exactly what strengthens learning. The second technique is spacing. Rather than studying for five hours the night before an exam, spread that same time across several days. Memory is reinforced each time you return to the material after a gap. The third is interleaving — mixing different topics in a single session rather than blocking them. If you're studying maths, alternate between problem types instead of doing twenty of the same kind in a row. It feels less smooth, but it trains you to choose the right method. One warning: all three techniques feel less comfortable than rereading, so students often abandon them. Trust the discomfort. Finally, sleep is not optional. The brain consolidates memories overnight, so a good night's rest is itself a study strategy.

questions.q: The first technique discussed is called ________ practice.
What does the speaker recommend instead of rereading notes?
The second technique, called ________, means spreading study across several days.
The third technique, ________, means mixing different topics in one session.
Why do students often abandon these techniques?
options: They feel less comfortable than rereading

questions.q: The speaker says the brain consolidates memories during ________.

speaking.q: Let's talk about technology and study. Do you use the internet or apps to help you learn? What do you find most useful about learning online, and is there anything you dislike about it?

writing.q:
Online courses are becoming an increasingly common way of studying. Some people think they will eventually replace traditional classroom teaching, while others believe classroom learning will always be necessary. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: The courses were greeted with extraordinary optimism.
Such lectures were once confined to elite campuses.
She studied at a prestigious university.
It seemed the old barriers would crumble.
Enrolment ran into the tens of thousands.
Completion rates told a sobering story.
Without deadlines, many learners drifted away.
The courses rarely reached underserved learners.

reading.title: Rethinking the Examination
reading.passage:
The written examination, taken in silence under strict time limits, has dominated formal education for well over a century. Its appeal is easy to understand. Exams are comparatively cheap to run, they can be marked against consistent criteria, and they appear to offer a fair, impersonal measure of what a student knows. For governments and institutions managing thousands of candidates, no rival method matches their efficiency.

Yet the very features that make examinations convenient have drawn growing criticism. Because so much depends on a single performance, exams reward the ability to recall information quickly under pressure — a skill that bears little resemblance to how knowledge is used in most workplaces. A nervous but capable student may underperform badly, while a confident one with a talent for memorisation may flourish without genuine understanding. Critics argue that this measures temperament as much as competence.

There is also concern about what examinations do to teaching itself. When results carry high stakes, instruction tends to narrow towards whatever the test rewards. Teachers, understandably anxious about their pupils' prospects, may drill past papers rather than explore a subject in depth. Curiosity, creativity and the messy process of genuine inquiry can all be squeezed out by the relentless logic of preparation.

Alternatives exist, though none is without difficulty. Coursework and portfolios allow sustained, thoughtful work to be assessed, but they are harder to mark consistently and more vulnerable to outside help. Continuous assessment reduces the terror of a single day, yet it can spread pressure across an entire year. Perhaps the wisest conclusion is that no single instrument can capture something as complex as learning. A balanced system, drawing on several methods, is likely to be fairer than any one of them used alone — even if it is also more demanding to design and administer.

questions.q: Written examinations have been central to formal education for more than a hundred years.
The writer claims that no other assessment method is as efficient as exams for large numbers of candidates.
Critics believe exams may measure a student's temperament as well as their competence.
Most employers now prefer to recruit using portfolios rather than exam results.
What effect on teaching does the passage describe when exam results carry high stakes?
options: Teaching becomes broader and more creative
Teaching narrows towards what the test rewards

questions.q: What is given as a drawback of coursework and portfolios?
options: They are harder to mark consistently and open to outside help

questions.q: The writer concludes that no single ________ can capture something as complex as learning.
Teachers may ________ past papers rather than explore a subject in depth.

listening.title: A Seminar on Assessment Reform
listening.script: SPEAKER: Thank you all for coming to this session on assessment reform. Let me set out where our research stands. We surveyed two thousand secondary teachers across the region last year, and the findings were striking. Nearly seventy per cent said that exam pressure had narrowed what they were able to teach. More than half reported spending at least a quarter of class time on test preparation alone. Now, we then trialled a mixed model in twelve schools, combining a shorter final exam with coursework worth forty per cent of the grade. The results were encouraging. Student anxiety, measured by questionnaire, fell noticeably, and attendance in the trial schools improved by about eight per cent. However, teachers raised a real concern: marking the coursework fairly took far more time, roughly double the hours of marking exams. Our recommendation, therefore, is not to abolish exams but to reduce their weight and to invest in proper training for assessors. The next phase of the study begins in September, and we'll be looking for twenty further schools to take part.

questions.q: The researchers surveyed ________ secondary teachers.

What is the speaker's main recommendation?
options: Reduce the weight of exams and train assessors

speaking.q: Let's discuss assessment in education. Do you think examinations are a fair way to measure a student's ability? What other methods could schools use to judge how much someone has learned, and what might be their advantages and disadvantages?

reading.title: The Quiet Revolution of the Bauhaus
reading.passage:
When the Bauhaus school opened in the German city of Weimar in 1919, its founder Walter Gropius had an unusual ambition. He wanted to dissolve the old boundary between the fine arts and the practical crafts. For centuries, painters and sculptors had been treated as superior to carpenters and weavers. Gropius argued that this division was artificial and that a chair, a lamp or a building could be designed with the same seriousness as a painting.

The school's teaching method was deliberately unconventional. New students did not begin by copying the great masters. Instead, they spent their first months in a foundation course experimenting with materials such as paper, metal and wood. Only after this period did they choose a specialised workshop. The aim was to free young designers from inherited habits and to encourage them to discover the natural properties of each material for themselves.

Bauhaus designers became famous for objects that were simple, functional and suitable for mass production. They believed that good design should not be a luxury reserved for the wealthy. A well-made teapot or a comfortable steel chair, they argued, ought to be affordable to ordinary households. This democratic ideal was as important to the movement as its distinctive visual style.

The school was forced to close in 1933 under political pressure, having moved twice during its short life. Yet its influence spread far beyond Germany. Many of its teachers emigrated, carrying their ideas to Britain and the United States, where they shaped a generation of architects. Today the clean lines of modern offices and the plain forms of everyday products still echo the lessons first taught in those early workshops.

questions.q: Gropius believed that practical crafts deserved the same respect as the fine arts.
Students at the Bauhaus began their studies by copying famous paintings.
The foundation course lasted exactly one academic year.
According to the passage, the Bauhaus's democratic ideal was that good design should be
Why did the school's ideas spread to other countries?
options: The German government promoted them abroad

questions.q: During its existence the school changed location ________ times before closing.

listening.title: Enrolling in a Community Design Workshop
listening.script:

CALLER: Great. Can I pay on the first day?

CALLER: No problem. I'll do that online this evening.

reading.title: Colour and the Human Eye
reading.passage:
Few decisions matter more to a painter, a designer or an architect than the choice of colour. Yet colour is not a simple property of objects. A red wall does not actually contain redness; rather, its surface reflects light of a particular wavelength, and our eyes and brain interpret that signal as red. Colour, in other words, is partly a physical event and partly a creation of the mind.

The human eye contains two main types of light-sensitive cell. Rods are extremely sensitive to faint light but cannot detect colour, which is why a moonlit landscape appears almost grey. Cones, by contrast, are responsible for colour vision and work best in bright conditions. Most people have three kinds of cone, each tuned to a different range of wavelengths. The brain combines their signals to produce the enormous variety of shades we perceive.

This biological system has practical consequences for design. Because cones need good light, colours that look striking in a brightly lit shop may seem dull in a dim living room. Designers who ignore this often find that their carefully chosen schemes disappoint their clients. Lighting and colour, therefore, must be planned together rather than separately.

Culture also shapes how colour is understood. In some societies white is associated with weddings and celebration, while in others it signals mourning. A designer working for an international audience cannot assume that a colour will carry the same meaning everywhere. Successful colour design thus demands an understanding of physics, biology and human society all at once.

questions.q: A red object physically contains the quality of redness.
Rods allow us to see colour in dim light.
A moonlit landscape appears grey because
options: only rods are active in faint light

questions.q: Most people have ________ kinds of cone in their eyes.
Colours that look bright in a shop may appear dull in a dimly lit room.
The author states that men and women perceive colour differently.
What point does the passage make about the colour white?
options: It is the easiest colour to reproduce
Its meaning can vary between cultures
It is preferred by most designers

listening.title: A Tutorial on Choosing a Colour Scheme
listening.script:
TUTOR: Right, so for your interior design project, the first thing to decide is a colour scheme. Have you thought about it?

STUDENT: A bit. I was thinking of mostly blue.

TUTOR: Blue's a popular choice. It tends to feel calm, which suits a bedroom. But remember, a single colour can feel a little flat. I'd suggest picking one main colour and one accent.

STUDENT: What's an accent colour, exactly?

TUTOR: It's a second colour used in small amounts, say on cushions or a lamp, to add interest. For a blue room, a warm yellow works really well.

STUDENT: Okay. And how much of the accent should I use?

TUTOR: A good rule is about ten per cent of the room. Any more and it stops being an accent.

STUDENT: Got it. What about the lighting?

TUTOR: Crucial. Test your colours under the actual lighting of the room, not just in daylight. Colours shift a lot under artificial light.

STUDENT: Should I hand in physical samples?

TUTOR: Yes please, by Friday. Bring three painted cards so we can compare them.

reading.title: The Return of the Tall Timber Building
reading.passage:
For most of the twentieth century, the tall building was synonymous with steel and concrete. Wood was regarded as a material of the past, suitable for houses and barns but far too weak and dangerous for anything ambitious. In recent years, however, architects have begun to construct multi-storey buildings from engineered timber, and this once unlikely material is now climbing ever higher.

The change has been made possible by a product known as cross-laminated timber, or CLT. To make it, layers of wooden boards are glued together with the grain of each layer running at right angles to the next. This simple arrangement produces panels that are remarkably strong and stable, able to bear heavy loads while remaining far lighter than concrete. Whole walls and floors can be cut in a factory and assembled on site in a matter of days.

Supporters point to clear environmental benefits. Trees absorb carbon dioxide as they grow, and a timber building locks that carbon away for as long as it stands. Concrete, by contrast, is responsible for a large share of the world's industrial emissions. Provided that forests are replanted, timber construction can be part of a more sustainable approach to building.

Doubts remain, of course. The most common worry is fire, although tests show that thick timber chars on the surface and then resists burning, much as a heavy log smoulders rather than bursting into flame. Cost and unfamiliarity also slow adoption, since many builders are simply more comfortable with traditional methods. Even so, a growing number of cities now boast timber towers, and the tallest examples already rise well above ten storeys.

questions.q: For much of the twentieth century, wood was considered unsuitable for ambitious buildings.
What makes cross-laminated timber strong?
options: Its layers are crossed at right angles
It is dried for several years

questions.q: Timber panels are heavier than concrete of the same size.

A timber building continues to store carbon while it remains standing.

listening.title: A Radio Feature on a New Timber Library
listening.script:
PRESENTER: This week our architecture slot visits a building that's turning heads in the city centre: the new Oakfield Library, built almost entirely from timber. I spoke to its architect, Dana Reyes.

REYES: We wanted a public building that felt warm and welcoming, and timber does that instantly. The moment you walk in, you smell the wood.

PRESENTER: How long did construction take?

REYES: Surprisingly short. The main structure went up in just eight weeks, because the panels arrived pre-cut from the factory.

PRESENTER: And the cost?

REYES: Comparable to concrete, actually. We saved on construction time, which offset the slightly higher material price.

PRESENTER: What about the public's reaction?

REYES: Overwhelmingly positive. Visitor numbers in the first month were thirty per cent higher than at the old building.

PRESENTER: Any challenges?

REYES: Convincing the council took patience. Their main concern was fire safety, but our tests reassured them completely.

PRESENTER: Wonderful. The library is open daily from nine in the morning, and entry is free.

reading.title: How Typefaces Shape What We Read
reading.passage:
Most readers never think about the shape of the letters in front of them. The choice of typeface seems trivial compared with the meaning of the words. Yet designers have long understood that the way text looks can quietly influence how it is received, how easily it is read and even whether it is trusted.

Typefaces fall into two broad families. Serif types, such as those used in many newspapers, have small lines or 'feet' at the ends of the letters. These tiny strokes are thought to guide the eye along a line of text, which may be why serif faces have traditionally been favoured for long printed passages. Sans-serif types, which lack these strokes, look cleaner and more modern, and they tend to remain clear on screens, where fine detail can be lost.

Legibility is not the only concern. The mood of a typeface matters too. A rounded, soft typeface may make a children's book feel friendly, while a sharp, narrow one might suit a serious financial report. Choosing the wrong tone can undermine a message entirely; an invitation to a formal dinner printed in a playful, comic-style face would strike most people as a joke.

Research has even suggested that the difficulty of a typeface can affect how much readers remember. In one study, students who read material set in a slightly harder-to-read font recalled more of it later than those given a clean, easy font. The explanation, the researchers proposed, is that a small amount of effort forces the brain to engage more deeply. Such findings remain debated, but they hint that the humble typeface is more powerful than it appears.

questions.q: What distinguishes serif typefaces from sans-serif ones?
options: They have small strokes at the ends of letters
They are only used on screens

questions.q: Sans-serif typefaces tend to stay clear on screens.

options: It is too expensive to print

questions.q: A study found that a harder-to-read font helped students remember more.

listening.title: A Student Presentation on Designing a Logo
listening.script:
STUDENT: Hi everyone. Today I'll explain how I designed a logo for a small bakery called Sweet Corner. I'll cover three stages: research, sketching, and the final choice.

First, research. I visited the bakery and noticed it felt cozy and old-fashioned, so I wanted the logo to feel warm, not slick. I looked at twenty other bakery logos to see what was common.

Second, sketching. I drew about forty rough ideas by hand before choosing any on the computer. Most used a simple image of a loaf of bread, which customers recognize instantly.

For the typeface, I picked a rounded serif font because it feels friendly and traditional. A sharp modern font felt too cold for a family business.

The colors were brown and cream, which remind people of bread and baking. I avoided bright red because it felt too aggressive.

Finally, I presented three options to the owner, and she chose the one with the loaf inside a circle. She said it would look great on the paper bags. The new logo launches next month, on the third of June.

reading.title: The Lost Art of the Stained-Glass Window
reading.passage:
Walk into a medieval cathedral on a sunny morning and you may be struck by pools of coloured light falling across the stone floor. These come from stained-glass windows, some of which have survived for more than seven hundred years. For the people who first saw them, such windows were far more than decoration; they were a means of telling stories to a population that could mostly not read.

Making the glass was a demanding craft. Colour was created by adding metals to molten glass: copper could produce green, while gold was used for a rich ruby red. The glowing sheets were then cut into small pieces and joined with strips of soft lead, which could be bent around delicate shapes. Finally, fine details such as faces and folds of cloth were painted on and fixed by reheating the glass in a kiln.

The results were not merely beautiful. Because the windows depicted familiar scenes, they helped ordinary worshippers follow and remember long narratives. In an age before printing, a vivid image was one of the most effective ways of fixing a story in the memory. The window was, in effect, a book that anyone could read simply by looking up.

Much of this knowledge was nearly lost. As tastes changed and cheaper methods spread, many old workshops closed, and the precise recipes for certain colours were forgotten for generations. Today a small number of specialists have revived the craft, studying surviving fragments under microscopes to work out how the original colours were achieved. Their patient research ensures that, when an ancient window is damaged, it can once again be repaired rather than simply replaced.

questions.q: Some stained-glass windows are more than seven hundred years old.
According to the passage, what was the main purpose of the windows for early viewers?
options: To tell stories to people who could not read

questions.q: Adding ________ to molten glass could produce a green colour.
Which metal was used to make a rich ruby red?
The pieces of glass were joined together using strips of lead.
The windows took longer to make than the cathedral itself.
The recipes for certain colours were known continuously throughout history.

listening.title: A Lecture on Restoring a Cathedral Window
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today I want to walk you through how our team restored a damaged window at St Aidan's Cathedral, a project that took us almost two years.

The window dates from around the year thirteen hundred. When we began, nearly a quarter of the glass was cracked or missing, mostly in the lower section, where it had been hit by storms over the centuries.

Our first job was documentation. We photographed every single panel before touching anything, creating a record of more than four hundred images. That archive is now stored at the city museum.

Matching the old colours was the hardest part. The original blue, in particular, proved very difficult, and it took us six months of testing to reproduce it accurately.

Wherever possible we kept the original glass. We only replaced pieces that were beyond saving, and each new piece was marked discreetly so future historians can tell old from new.

The restored window was unveiled last spring, and the cathedral now welcomes visitors to view it free of charge on weekday afternoons.

reading.title: The Hidden Cost of Free Shipping
reading.passage:
When online retailers began offering free shipping more than two decades ago, the practice was treated as a temporary marketing gimmick. Today it has become an expectation. Surveys suggest that a majority of shoppers will abandon a purchase at the final stage if they are asked to pay a separate delivery charge, even a modest one. This sensitivity has forced companies to rethink how they price their goods.

The phrase "free shipping" is, of course, misleading. The cost of moving a parcel from a warehouse to a doorstep does not disappear; it is simply absorbed elsewhere. Most often, retailers raise the listed price of their products slightly, spreading the delivery expense across the entire catalogue. Customers therefore pay for shipping indirectly, whether or not they realise it. Economists describe this as a form of price bundling, in which separate charges are combined into a single, more palatable figure.

There are clear advantages to the model. A predictable total at checkout reduces hesitation, and a higher average order value often results because shoppers add extra items to feel they are getting more value. Yet the strategy carries risks. Smaller firms with thinner margins may struggle to absorb delivery costs, particularly for bulky or heavy goods sent over long distances. Some have responded by setting a minimum spend before free shipping applies, nudging customers toward larger baskets.

The environmental dimension is harder to ignore. Because the marginal cost of an additional order feels like zero to the consumer, return rates have climbed sharply, with clothing retailers reporting that as many as a third of garments are sent back. Each return generates a second journey, additional packaging and, frequently, a discarded item that is cheaper to destroy than to restock. What began as a convenience has quietly reshaped both pricing logic and consumption habits.

questions.q: Free shipping was originally seen as a lasting feature of online retail.
Many shoppers will cancel an order if a delivery fee appears at checkout.
Retailers in every country are legally required to disclose how shipping costs are recovered.
According to the passage, how do retailers most commonly cover the cost of free shipping?
What term do economists use for combining separate charges into one figure?
Some firms require a minimum ________ before offering free shipping.
Clothing retailers report that up to ________ of garments are returned.
Free shipping has contributed to higher product return rates.

listening.title: Setting Up a Market Stall
listening.script:
OFFICER: Good morning, you've reached the market office. How can I help?

VENDOR: Hi, I'd like to book a stall to sell handmade candles. What do I need to do?

OFFICER: Of course. First, the standard pitch fee is twenty-two pounds per day, or you can take a weekly rate of ninety pounds, which works out cheaper if you trade often.

VENDOR: The weekly one sounds better. How big is a standard stall?

OFFICER: Each pitch is three metres wide. If you need more space for a display table, we can offer a double pitch, but you'd have to request that in advance.

VENDOR: Right. And what time should I arrive?

OFFICER: Setting up starts at seven, and you must be ready to trade by half past eight. The market closes at five.

VENDOR: Perfect. Do I need any documents?

OFFICER: Yes, please bring proof of public liability insurance, and if you're selling food you'd need a hygiene certificate too. Candles don't require that.

VENDOR: Great. How do I pay the fee?

OFFICER: You can pay online once I've sent you a booking reference. I'll email that to you today.

reading.title: Why Some Brands Survive Recessions
reading.passage:
Economic downturns are often described as a great filter, weeding out weaker companies while allowing the strong to endure. Yet the firms that emerge intact from a recession are not always the largest or the most profitable in good times. Research into corporate behaviour suggests that survival depends less on sheer size than on how a business manages its relationship with customers during periods of strain.

One consistent finding is that brands which continue to advertise during a slump tend to recover faster afterwards. When competitors cut their marketing budgets to preserve cash, the few that maintain visibility capture a disproportionate share of attention. The cost of reaching consumers also falls, because advertising space becomes cheaper as demand for it drops. A modest budget can therefore achieve more during a downturn than it would in a booming economy.

Flexibility matters too. Companies that can quickly adjust their product range, shifting toward cheaper lines or smaller pack sizes, retain customers who are trading down rather than losing them to rivals. A supermarket that introduces a value range, for instance, keeps cost-conscious shoppers within its doors instead of sending them elsewhere. When confidence returns, many of those customers stay loyal.

There is, however, a psychological element that pure economics overlooks. Consumers reward brands that appear to understand their circumstances. A firm that raises prices aggressively during hard times may secure short-term revenue but damage trust that took years to build. Conversely, gestures of solidarity, such as freezing prices on essential goods, can generate goodwill that outlasts the recession itself. The lesson, repeated across decades of evidence, is that the businesses best placed to thrive in recovery are those that resisted the temptation to retreat entirely when conditions were at their worst.

questions.q: The companies that survive recessions are usually the biggest ones.
Advertising space tends to become cheaper during a recession.

listening.title: A Talk on Starting a Side Business
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone, to this evening's session on launching a side business while keeping your day job. I want to cover three practical points before we open up for questions.

First, money. My advice is to start with a budget you can genuinely afford to lose. For most people that's somewhere between five hundred and a thousand dollars. Don't borrow heavily at this stage; the goal is to test an idea, not to bet your savings.

Second, time. Be honest about how many hours you can spare each week. Ten hours is realistic for someone working full-time. If you promise customers more than you can deliver, your reputation suffers, and reputation is everything when you're small.

Third, paperwork. Register your business name early, and keep your business and personal finances in separate accounts from day one. It makes tax far simpler at the end of the year. Many people leave this until it's a mess.

Finally, a tip that costs nothing: talk to ten potential customers before you spend a single dollar on stock. Their answers will tell you whether anyone actually wants what you're planning to sell.

questions.q: What does the speaker advise about the starting budget?
options: Use money you can afford to lose
Wait until you have ten thousand dollars

reading.title: The Rise of the Subscription Economy
reading.passage:
Not long ago, owning a product outright was the default way of consuming. People bought music on discs, software in boxes, and razors in packs. Increasingly, however, consumers pay a recurring fee for continued access rather than a single price for ownership. This shift, often called the subscription economy, has spread from entertainment into software, transport, food and even clothing.

The appeal to businesses is obvious. A subscription converts unpredictable one-off purchases into a steady stream of revenue that is far easier to forecast. Investors reward this predictability, valuing companies with recurring income more highly than those reliant on occasional sales. For the customer, the model lowers the barrier to entry: instead of paying a large sum upfront, they spread the cost into small, manageable instalments.

Yet the convenience conceals a subtle trap. Because each individual payment is small, subscribers frequently lose track of how much they are spending in total. Studies of consumer behaviour reveal that many people continue paying for services they rarely use, simply because cancelling requires effort and the monthly charge feels trivial. Companies are well aware of this inertia and often design the cancellation process to be deliberately cumbersome.

Regulators in several countries have begun to respond. New rules increasingly require firms to make cancelling a subscription as straightforward as signing up, and to remind customers before a free trial converts into a paid plan. Critics argue these measures do not go far enough, since the underlying psychology remains unchanged. Defenders of the model counter that, used carefully, subscriptions give people access to far more than they could ever afford to own, transforming occasional luxuries into everyday conveniences. Whether the balance ultimately favours the consumer or the company may depend less on the contract than on the discipline of the individual.

questions.q: In the past, paying a recurring fee was the normal way of consuming products.
Investors tend to value companies with recurring income more highly.
Subscription services are now more profitable than traditional retail in most industries.
Why do many subscribers keep paying for services they rarely use?
options: Cancelling requires effort and the charge feels small

questions.q: What do new regulations increasingly require firms to do?
options: Make cancelling as easy as signing up

questions.q: Subscriptions turn unpredictable one-off purchases into a steady stream of ________.

listening.title: Comparing Two Phone Plans
listening.script:
ADVISOR: Hi there, you wanted to compare our two main plans, is that right?

CUSTOMER: Yes, I'm trying to work out which one suits me.

ADVISOR: Sure. The Basic plan is fifteen dollars a month and gives you ten gigabytes of data. The Plus plan is twenty-five dollars and gives you unlimited data.

CUSTOMER: I mostly use wi-fi at home, so I probably don't need unlimited.

ADVISOR: In that case Basic may be enough. Just so you know, if you go over ten gigabytes on Basic, you're charged an extra five dollars per gigabyte.

CUSTOMER: That could add up. Is there a contract?

ADVISOR: Both plans are rolling monthly, so you can cancel anytime with thirty days' notice. There's no lock-in period.

CUSTOMER: Good. And what about calls abroad?

ADVISOR: International calls aren't included in either plan, but you can add a travel pack for eight dollars a month.

CUSTOMER: I think I'll start with Basic and add the travel pack.

ADVISOR: Great choice. I'll set that up now and email you the confirmation.

reading.title: The Surprising Economics of Coffee
reading.passage:
A cup of coffee sold in a city café can cost forty or fifty times more than the raw beans it contains. To many customers this seems an unfair mark-up, evidence that the seller is profiting handsomely at their expense. The reality is more complicated, and it illustrates a principle that applies far beyond the coffee trade: the price of a finished product reflects far more than the cost of its ingredients.

Consider where the money actually goes. Rent for a prime location in a busy district can consume a quarter of every pound taken at the till. Wages for staff, who must be trained and paid even during quiet hours, account for another large share. Then there are the less visible costs: equipment, electricity, insurance, waste, taxes and the milk, cups and napkins that accompany the drink. By the time these are subtracted, the profit on a single cup is often only a few pence.

The beans themselves tell a similar story of distributed value. A farmer in a producing country may receive a tiny fraction of the final retail price, with the remainder absorbed by exporters, shippers, roasters and retailers along the chain. Campaigners have long argued that this distribution is unjust, and fair-trade schemes attempt to return a larger share to growers. Critics respond that such schemes, while well-intentioned, sometimes fail to reach the poorest farmers.

What the humble coffee cup reveals is that consumers routinely underestimate the hidden infrastructure behind everyday goods. The convenience of a warm drink served instantly depends on a long and costly chain of people and processes, most of which remain invisible at the point of sale. Understanding this does not make the price feel lower, but it does explain why the simple act of buying coffee supports a surprisingly large economy.

questions.q: A café cup of coffee can cost many times more than the beans inside it.
The price of a finished product reflects only the cost of its ingredients.
Most café customers are aware of how rent affects coffee prices.
According to the passage, what can consume about a quarter of every pound taken?
What do critics say about fair-trade schemes?
options: They always reach the poorest farmers
They sometimes fail to reach the poorest farmers

questions.q: The profit on a single cup is often only a few ________.
A farmer may receive only a tiny ________ of the final retail price.
Consumers tend to underestimate the hidden infrastructure behind everyday goods.

listening.title: Lecture: The Concept of Opportunity Cost
listening.script:
LECTURER: Today I want to introduce one of the most useful ideas in economics: opportunity cost. Put simply, the opportunity cost of any decision is the value of the next best thing you give up when you make that choice.

Let's take an example. Suppose you have a free Saturday. You could earn eighty pounds doing extra work, or you could study for an exam. If you choose to study, the opportunity cost is not zero, even though studying costs nothing directly. It is the eighty pounds you chose not to earn.

This matters because resources are limited. Every time a government spends money on, say, a new road, that same money cannot be spent on a hospital. The road's true cost includes the hospital that was never built. Economists call this the foregone alternative.

The key lesson is that the real cost of anything is what you sacrifice to obtain it, not simply the price on the label. Once you start thinking in these terms, you'll notice opportunity costs everywhere, from how you spend your time to how a company invests its capital. We'll return to this idea throughout the course.

questions.q: What is the opportunity cost of a decision?
options: The value of the next best alternative you give up

questions.q: In the example, you could earn ________ pounds doing extra work.
If you choose to study instead of working, the opportunity cost is:
options: The money you chose not to earn

questions.q: Money spent on a road cannot be spent on a ________.
Economists call the missed option the foregone ________.
What is the key lesson of the lecture?
options: The real cost is what you sacrifice to obtain something

speaking.q: Let's talk about money and spending. Do you usually plan how you spend your money, or decide on the spot? Is it important to save money? What do you think young people most often spend money on?

reading.title: Behavioural Nudges in Public Policy
reading.passage:
Traditional economics assumed that people are rational actors who weigh costs and benefits carefully before deciding. In practice, human behaviour is far messier. We procrastinate, follow the crowd, and are heavily influenced by how choices are presented to us. Over the past two decades, governments have begun to take these tendencies seriously, drawing on insights from psychology to design what are commonly called nudges.

A nudge is a small change to the environment in which people make decisions, one that steers them toward a particular outcome without removing their freedom to choose otherwise. The classic example concerns pension savings. When employees must actively opt in to a retirement scheme, participation tends to be low, because inertia keeps many from ever filling in the form. When they are instead automatically enrolled, with the option to leave, participation soars. The available choices are identical; only the default has changed.

Nudges have since been applied to a wide range of problems. Reminding taxpayers that most of their neighbours have already paid increases prompt payment. Placing healthier food at eye level in canteens shifts consumption without banning anything. Sending text messages before a medical appointment reduces the number of patients who fail to attend, saving public money. The appeal of such interventions is that they are cheap, easy to implement and respectful of individual liberty.

Nonetheless, the approach has provoked debate. Sceptics worry that nudges allow officials to manipulate citizens covertly, shaping behaviour in ways people may not notice or consent to. Others argue that nudges treat symptoms rather than underlying causes, and that genuine problems such as poverty require structural reform rather than clever defaults. Supporters reply that since choices must be presented in some way regardless, it is better to arrange them thoughtfully than to leave the outcome to chance. The debate, like human behaviour itself, shows no sign of being neatly resolved.

questions.q: Traditional economics assumed people decide carefully and rationally.
A nudge removes a person's freedom to choose a different option.
Nudges have been proven more effective than financial incentives in every case.
What happens to pension participation when employees are automatically enrolled?
Which is given as an example of a nudge?
options: Placing healthier food at eye level

questions.q: In the pension example, only the ________ has changed, not the available choices.
Sceptics worry that nudges let officials ________ citizens covertly.
Some critics believe problems like poverty need structural reform rather than nudges.

listening.title: Seminar Discussion: Choosing a Default Option
listening.script:
TUTOR: So, for today's seminar we're looking at how default settings affect behaviour. Maya, can you start us off?

MAYA: Sure. The study we read found that when organ donation was set as the default, consent rates were over ninety percent. In countries where people had to opt in, the figure was below twenty percent.

TUTOR: A striking gap. Daniel, why such a difference?

DANIEL: Mostly inertia, I think. People tend to stick with whatever option requires no action. It's not that they feel strongly either way.

TUTOR: Good. And is that ethical, Maya?

MAYA: That's the tricky part. Supporters say it saves lives, and you can still opt out. Critics say people aren't really making a conscious choice.

TUTOR: Exactly the tension in the literature. For next week, I'd like a short essay, about a thousand words, comparing two countries with different systems. Please submit it by Friday at noon, and remember to cite at least three sources.

DANIEL: Should we include our own opinion?

TUTOR: Yes, but support it with evidence, not just assertion.

reading.title: Mapping the Lunar Far Side
reading.passage:
For most of human history, the far side of the Moon remained completely hidden from view. Because the Moon rotates on its axis in almost exactly the same time it takes to orbit the Earth, the same hemisphere always faces our planet. This phenomenon, known as tidal locking, meant that the opposite hemisphere could not be observed until spacecraft were sent into orbit around the Moon in the late 1950s.

The first images returned were grainy and difficult to interpret, yet they revealed a surprising contrast. The familiar near side is marked by large, dark plains of solidified lava called maria. The far side, by comparison, is heavily cratered and has very few of these smooth plains. Scientists have proposed several explanations for this difference, with the most widely accepted being that the crust on the far side is significantly thicker, making it harder for lava to reach the surface.

Modern missions have transformed our understanding of this region. Detailed surveys have identified the South Pole-Aitken basin, one of the largest impact craters in the entire Solar System, stretching across much of the southern far side. Researchers believe that material excavated by the ancient impact could reveal information about the Moon's deep interior. For this reason, the far side has become a priority target for future robotic landers, several of which are scheduled to collect rock samples within the coming decade.

questions.q: The same side of the Moon always faces the Earth because of tidal locking.
The first images of the far side were of excellent quality.
The far side has more smooth lava plains than the near side.
The thickness of the far-side crust has been measured precisely by drilling.
According to the passage, the most widely accepted reason for the lack of maria on the far side is that
options: the far side has no volcanoes
the far side faces away from the Sun

questions.q: The South Pole-Aitken basin is described as
options: one of the largest impact craters in the Solar System

questions.q: The Moon's rotation period is almost exactly equal to the time it takes to ________ the Earth.

ORGANISER: Perfect. The membership fee is twelve pounds for the whole year, which covers all our observing sessions.

STUDENT: That's fine. When do you usually meet?

ORGANISER: Our main session is every Thursday evening at half past seven. We meet at the rooftop observatory on the science building.

STUDENT: And do I need to bring my own telescope?

ORGANISER: Not at all. We have six telescopes that members can borrow. Just bring a warm coat, because it gets very cold up there.

ORGANISER: Yes, next month we're running a trip to a dark-sky site in Wales. The coach leaves at nine in the morning, and places are limited to twenty people.

STUDENT: I'd love to come. How do I book?

ORGANISER: Just email the club secretary by the fifteenth.

reading.title: The Lives of Comets
reading.passage:
Comets are among the most spectacular objects that can be seen in the night sky, yet for much of the year they remain invisible. Most spend their existence in the cold outer regions of the Solar System, far beyond the orbit of Neptune, where temperatures are so low that their ices stay frozen solid. A comet is essentially a mixture of ice, dust and rocky fragments, often described informally as a dirty snowball.

When a comet's orbit carries it closer to the Sun, dramatic changes begin. The increasing heat causes the frozen gases to turn directly into vapour, a process called sublimation. This releases dust and gas that form a glowing cloud around the solid core, known as the coma. As the comet approaches still closer, pressure from sunlight and the solar wind pushes this material outward, creating the long, luminous tail that always points away from the Sun.

Comets follow highly elongated orbits, and some take thousands of years to complete a single circuit. Each time a comet passes near the Sun, it loses a portion of its material, so over many orbits it gradually shrinks. Eventually, some comets break apart entirely, scattering debris along their former paths. When the Earth passes through such a trail of debris, the particles burn up in the atmosphere, producing the brief streaks of light we call meteor showers.

reading.title: Why Stars Have Different Colours
reading.passage:
Anyone who looks carefully at the night sky will notice that stars are not all the same colour. Some appear faintly blue or white, while others glow with a warm reddish or orange tint. These differences are not random; they are a direct clue to the temperature of each star. Far from being a matter of mere appearance, colour is one of the most reliable tools astronomers have for studying stars they may never visit.

The basic principle is surprisingly familiar from everyday life. When a piece of metal is heated, it first glows dull red, then orange, then yellow, and finally an intense bluish white as it becomes hotter. Stars behave in exactly the same way. A cool star with a surface temperature of around three thousand degrees Celsius shines red, whereas the hottest stars, reaching tens of thousands of degrees, burn blue. Our own Sun, which appears yellow, sits comfortably between these extremes.

Colour also offers hints about a star's stage of life. Massive blue stars consume their fuel rapidly and live for only a few million years, a brief moment in cosmic terms. Smaller red stars, by contrast, burn their fuel slowly and may survive for many billions of years. By measuring colour together with brightness, astronomers can estimate not only how hot a star is but also how large it is and roughly how long it is likely to last. This single property, visible to the naked eye, therefore opens a window onto processes occurring across vast distances.

questions.q: All stars appear to be the same colour.

According to the passage, a star shining red has a surface temperature of around
The example of heated metal is used to explain

listening.title: A Lecture on Star Brightness
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good morning, everyone. Today we're going to talk about how astronomers measure the brightness of stars. Now, the first thing to understand is that there are two kinds of brightness. The first is apparent brightness, which simply means how bright a star looks from Earth. The second is intrinsic brightness, which is how much light the star actually gives off.

These two can be very different. A star may look dim simply because it is extremely far away, even though it is in fact enormously powerful. To compare stars fairly, astronomers imagine placing them all at the same standard distance. Once we do that, the true differences become clear.

The scale we use for brightness is unusual, and students often find it confusing at first. On this scale, smaller numbers mean brighter objects. So a star of magnitude one is brighter than a star of magnitude six. In fact, each step on the scale represents a brightness difference of about two and a half times. For your assignment this week, I'd like you to measure the apparent magnitude of five stars and submit your results by Friday.

reading.title: The Search for Water on Mars
reading.passage:
Few questions in planetary science have attracted as much attention as whether Mars once held liquid water. Today the planet is cold and dry, with an atmosphere far too thin to allow water to remain stable on its surface. Yet a growing body of evidence suggests that conditions were very different in the distant past.

The strongest clues come from the landscape itself. Orbiting spacecraft have photographed networks of winding channels that closely resemble dried-up riverbeds on Earth. Some regions display fan-shaped deposits of sediment, the kind that form where a river slows and spreads as it enters a lake or sea. Rovers exploring the surface have gone further still, discovering minerals that, on our own planet, form only in the presence of water. Taken together, these findings point to a warmer, wetter Mars billions of years ago.

What happened to all this water remains a matter of debate. One leading idea is that Mars gradually lost much of its atmosphere to space, and as the air thinned, the planet grew too cold for liquid water to survive. Some of the remaining water may have escaped into space as well, while a portion is thought to be locked away as ice beneath the surface and at the poles. Confirming exactly how much water still exists, and in what form, is now a central goal of current missions. The answer matters greatly, not least because the presence of water would shape any future attempt to send humans to the planet.

listening.title: Planning a Group Project on Mars
listening.script:
TUTOR: Right, let's sort out your group project on Mars exploration. Have you decided how to divide the work?

STUDENT A: One more thing. How many sources should we use?

TUTOR: At least eight, and please make sure they're academic, not just websites. Reference everything properly.

reading.title: Listening to the Universe with Radio Telescopes
reading.passage:
When most people imagine astronomy, they picture a curved glass lens trained on the heavens, gathering the faint light of distant stars. Yet a great deal of modern astronomy depends not on visible light at all, but on radio waves. Radio telescopes detect this invisible form of radiation, opening up a view of the cosmos that ordinary optical instruments can never reveal.

The advantage of radio observation lies partly in what it can see through. Visible light is easily blocked by clouds of dust that drift between the stars, hiding many of the most interesting regions of our galaxy. Radio waves, however, pass straight through such obscuring material almost untouched. As a result, radio telescopes have allowed astronomers to study the dense, dusty centre of the Milky Way and to detect cold clouds of gas where new stars are quietly being born.

Because radio waves are far longer than light waves, a single dish must be enormous to produce a sharp image, and even the largest dishes have their limits. Astronomers overcame this problem with a clever technique. By linking many separate dishes together, sometimes spread across entire continents, and combining their signals electronically, they can create a single instrument with the resolving power of a telescope thousands of kilometres wide. This approach has produced astonishingly detailed images, including the first picture ever captured of the shadow of a black hole. It is a striking reminder that some of the most powerful eyes on the universe do not, in any ordinary sense, see at all.

questions.q: All modern astronomy relies on visible light.
Radio waves can pass through clouds of dust between the stars.
A single radio dish must be large to produce a sharp image.
Radio telescopes are cheaper to build than optical telescopes.
One key advantage of radio telescopes is that they can
To overcome the limits of a single dish, astronomers
Linked dishes can create an instrument with the resolving power of a telescope thousands of ________ wide.

listening.title: A Talk on Building a Radio Observatory
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Thanks for coming along this evening. I want to tell you about the new radio observatory we're building out in the desert. People often ask why we chose such a remote location, and the answer is simple: interference. Mobile phones, radios and even microwave ovens all give off signals that can drown out the faint whispers we're trying to detect from space. So we need somewhere far from any town.

The site we picked is about four hundred kilometres inland. At the moment we have thirty-six dishes installed, and we plan to expand to over a hundred within five years. Each dish is twelve metres across, which is fairly modest, but remember, it's the combination that matters.

Construction hasn't been easy. The biggest challenge has actually been the heat, which can damage the sensitive electronics. To cope with this, every dish has its own cooling system. If you'd like to visit, we run public tours on the first Saturday of each month, and booking is essential.

reading.title: How Migratory Birds Navigate
reading.passage:
Every autumn, billions of birds undertake journeys of remarkable length, some travelling from the Arctic to the southern tip of Africa and back again within a single year. For centuries, the question of how these creatures find their way across featureless oceans and unfamiliar terrain puzzled naturalists. Today, researchers believe that birds do not rely on a single method but instead combine several overlapping cues, switching between them as conditions demand.

The most familiar of these cues is the sun. By tracking its position and compensating for the time of day, a bird can establish a reliable compass direction. At night, many species turn instead to the stars, learning the pattern of rotation around the celestial pole during their first months of life. Experiments in planetariums have shown that young birds raised under an artificial sky orient themselves according to whichever star pattern they were exposed to, suggesting that this knowledge is learned rather than inherited.

Perhaps the most intriguing mechanism is the ability to sense the Earth's magnetic field. Tiny particles of a magnetic mineral have been found in the tissue of several bird species, and some scientists argue that a light-sensitive protein in the eye allows birds to literally see magnetic lines as patterns of shading. This magnetic sense appears to act as a backup, becoming most important when the sky is overcast and neither sun nor stars are visible.

Navigation, however, involves more than simply holding a heading. A true map sense would allow a displaced bird to determine not only which way to fly but also where it currently is. Evidence for such a capacity remains contested. When experienced adults are transported far from their normal route, many correct their course and arrive at the expected destination, whereas inexperienced juveniles often continue stubbornly in their original direction. This difference hints that the finer aspects of navigation may depend heavily on memory and accumulated experience, rather than on instinct alone.

questions.q: Naturalists understood how birds navigate long before modern science.
Birds depend on one single method to find their way.
Young birds inherit knowledge of star patterns at birth.
The magnetic mineral found in birds is more abundant in females than in males.
According to the passage, the magnetic sense is most useful when
options: the sky is covered by cloud

questions.q: What does the behaviour of displaced birds suggest?
options: All birds share an identical map sense

questions.q: A light-sensitive ________ in the eye may let birds see magnetic lines as shading.

listening.title: Joining the Wildlife Survey Group
listening.script:
COORDINATOR: Good morning, thanks for your interest in our volunteer bird survey. Let me take a few details. Can I have your full name?

VOLUNTEER: Yes, it's Helen Marsh, that's M-A-R-S-H.

COORDINATOR: Lovely. And which area would you prefer to cover?

VOLUNTEER: I was hoping for the wetland site near the reservoir, if that's still available.

COORDINATOR: It is. The wetland team meets on Saturdays at seven fifteen in the morning. You'll need waterproof boots and a notebook, and we'll lend you binoculars for the first month.

VOLUNTEER: Great. How long does a typical survey last?

COORDINATOR: About three hours, finishing around half past ten. You record every species you spot and note the approximate number. Don't worry about being an expert; an experienced leader checks each sheet.

VOLUNTEER: And is there any training?

COORDINATOR: Yes, there's an introductory session on the fourteenth of March. It covers identification and how to fill in the forms correctly. The fee for materials is twelve pounds.

VOLUNTEER: Perfect, I'll sign up for that.

reading.title: The Hidden Network of Fungi
reading.passage:
Beneath the floor of almost every forest lies a vast and largely invisible network. It is woven from the threadlike filaments of fungi, structures so fine that a single teaspoon of healthy soil may contain kilometres of them. For a long time fungi were regarded as little more than agents of decay, breaking down dead leaves and timber. In recent decades, however, researchers have come to appreciate that many fungi form intimate partnerships with living plants, and that these partnerships are essential to the health of entire ecosystems.

The most important of these alliances occurs at the roots. A fungus wraps itself around, or even penetrates, the root tissue of a tree and extends its filaments far into the surrounding soil. Because these filaments are far thinner than the finest root, they can reach water and nutrients that the tree alone could never access. In exchange, the tree supplies the fungus with sugars produced through photosynthesis. Neither partner could thrive as well without the other, and the relationship is therefore described as mutually beneficial.

The consequences extend beyond a single tree. Because one fungal network may connect dozens of plants at once, resources can flow between them. Experiments using traceable forms of carbon have shown that sugars produced by a large, sunlit tree can travel through the fungal threads and reach a smaller, shaded neighbour. Some scientists suggest that warning signals, too, may pass along these connections, allowing a plant under attack from insects to prompt defensive chemistry in its neighbours before the threat arrives.

Such findings have prompted vivid descriptions of forests as cooperative communities, but caution is warranted. Critics point out that fungi are not charitable; they channel resources in ways that serve their own survival, and what looks like generosity between trees may simply be a by-product of the fungus pursuing its interests. Whatever the correct interpretation, the discovery of this underground web has transformed how ecologists picture the forest, shifting attention from individual trees to the relationships that bind them together.

questions.q: Fungi were once seen mainly as organisms that cause decay.
Fungal filaments are thicker than the finest tree roots.
The tree gives the fungus sugars in return for water and nutrients.
Fungal networks are found more often in tropical forests than in temperate ones.
What did experiments using traceable carbon demonstrate?
options: Trees produce no sugars when shaded
Sugars can move from one tree to another

questions.q: Why do critics urge caution about calling forests cooperative?
options: Fungi may act in their own interest
The networks are too small to matter

questions.q: The partnership between fungus and tree is described as mutually ________.

listening.title: Lecture: Soil and Decomposition
listening.script:
LECTURER: Right, in today's session we're looking at decomposition, the process by which dead material is broken down and returned to the soil. Now, the key players here are not the large scavengers you might imagine, but microscopic organisms, chiefly bacteria and fungi.

Let's start with the rate of decay. This depends mainly on three factors: temperature, moisture, and the type of material involved. Warm, damp conditions speed things up considerably, which is why a compost heap works best in summer. In cold or very dry environments, by contrast, decay can almost stop, and material may remain intact for decades.

Fungi are particularly important because they alone can break down a tough substance called lignin, which gives wood its strength. Without fungi, fallen trees would simply pile up. Once the lignin is broken, bacteria move in to finish the job.

For your assignment, I'd like you to set up a small experiment. Bury four leaf samples in different conditions and measure the weight loss after six weeks. Record your results in a table and submit it by the thirtieth of April. Any questions, email me before Friday.

After fungi break the lignin, ________ finish the job.

The assignment is due on the ________ of April.

reading.title: The Surprising Intelligence of the Octopus
reading.passage:
Among the many strange creatures of the ocean, few are as puzzling as the octopus. With eight flexible arms, no bones, and the ability to change colour in an instant, it seems almost designed to defy expectation. Yet what most fascinates scientists is not its appearance but its behaviour, for the octopus displays a kind of intelligence that appears to have evolved along an entirely separate path from our own.

The evolutionary distance is striking. The last common ancestor of humans and octopuses lived some six hundred million years ago and was, in all likelihood, a simple worm-like creature with little more than a scattering of nerve cells. Whatever cleverness the octopus possesses, therefore, it developed independently, without inheriting any of the structures that underlie intelligence in mammals. For this reason, studying octopuses offers a rare opportunity to ask which features of a mind are universal and which are merely accidents of our own history.

The octopus nervous system is itself unusual. Rather than concentrating processing power in a central brain, it distributes a majority of its nerve cells among the arms. Each arm can taste, touch, and react with a degree of autonomy, so that an octopus is sometimes described as a creature that thinks partly with its limbs. In laboratory tests, individuals have learned to open jars to reach food, recognised particular human keepers, and used coconut shells as portable shelters, behaviour that many researchers regard as evidence of genuine problem-solving rather than blind instinct.

There is, however, a poignant limit to this brilliance. Most octopus species live for only a year or two and die shortly after reproducing. Because they are largely solitary and short-lived, they have little opportunity to pass knowledge from one generation to the next. A human child inherits the accumulated learning of countless ancestors through culture and language; an octopus must, in effect, begin again each generation. Its intelligence, however remarkable, remains locked within a single brief life.

questions.q: The octopus's intelligence evolved separately from that of mammals.
Most of the octopus's nerve cells are located in a central brain.
Octopuses typically live for many decades.

Why is the octopus valuable for studying the mind?
options: It is closely related to humans
It lives in large social groups

options: It has too few nerve cells

listening.title: Booking an Aquarium Visit
listening.script:
STAFF: Good afternoon, City Aquarium, how can I help?

STAFF: That's fine. Wednesday morning we run a special talk on cephalopods, that's octopuses and their relatives, at ten o'clock. It lasts about forty minutes.

CALLER: That sounds perfect. How much is it for a school group?

STAFF: Students are six dollars each, and teachers go free with a group of that size. So that's one hundred and sixty-eight dollars in total.

STAFF: Yes, there's a covered picnic area near the main entrance, but please bring your own food. And one last thing, could you arrive by nine forty-five so we can check you in before the talk?

CALLER: Will do. Thank you very much.

reading.title: Coral Reefs Under Pressure
reading.passage:
Coral reefs are often called the rainforests of the sea, and the comparison is apt. Although they cover less than one per cent of the ocean floor, they provide a home for roughly a quarter of all marine species. This extraordinary richness rests on an unlikely foundation: a partnership between the coral animal and microscopic algae that live within its tissue. The algae capture sunlight and supply the coral with food, while the coral offers shelter and the raw materials the algae need. It is this collaboration that gives reefs both their brilliant colours and their capacity to build the vast limestone structures visible from space.

The arrangement, however, is delicate. The algae can only function within a narrow range of temperatures, and when the surrounding water grows too warm, even by a degree or two for a sustained period, the coral expels them. Stripped of its colourful partners, the coral turns a ghostly white, a phenomenon known as bleaching. A bleached coral is not yet dead, and if cooler conditions return quickly the algae may recolonise it. But if the stress is prolonged, the coral starves, and a once-teeming reef can be reduced to rubble within months.

Rising sea temperatures are not the only threat. As the ocean absorbs more carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, its chemistry shifts, making it harder for corals to build their skeletons. Pollution from the land, destructive fishing methods, and the physical damage caused by storms add further strain. Because these pressures often act together, a reef weakened by one may be far less able to withstand another.

The outlook is sobering, yet not without hope. Some coral populations have shown a surprising ability to tolerate higher temperatures, and scientists are studying whether such hardy varieties might be encouraged to spread. Restoration projects, in which fragments of coral are grown in nurseries and transplanted onto damaged reefs, have produced encouraging early results. Whether these efforts can keep pace with the scale of the threat remains, for now, an open question.

questions.q: Coral reefs occupy a large proportion of the ocean floor.
The algae living in coral can survive across a wide range of temperatures.
A bleached coral is always dead.
Coral restoration nurseries are more expensive than other conservation methods.
What causes coral bleaching according to the passage?
Why does the absorption of carbon dioxide harm coral?
When coral loses its algae it turns a ghostly ________.
In restoration projects, coral ________ are grown in nurseries.

listening.title: Radio Feature: A Reef Recovery Project
listening.script:
PRESENTER: This week on Coastlines, we visit a project that's trying to bring a damaged reef back to life. I spoke to marine biologist Dr Anya Roche.

ROCHE: The reef we work on lost nearly seventy per cent of its living coral after two warm summers in a row. Our approach is fairly simple in principle. We collect small fragments of coral, attach them to frames in an underwater nursery, and let them grow for about ten months before moving them onto the reef.

PRESENTER: And how successful has that been?

ROCHE: Better than we expected. Around eighty per cent of the transplanted pieces have survived the first year, which is very encouraging. But I want to be honest: this is not a cure. We can restore a patch the size of a tennis court, not an entire reef.

PRESENTER: So what's the real solution?

ROCHE: Ultimately, reducing the warming itself. Restoration buys time; it doesn't replace the need to address the underlying cause. If the water keeps heating, even our healthiest transplants will eventually fail.

speaking.q: Let's talk about the sea and water. Do you like spending time near the sea or by water? Have you ever been to a beach or coast? Is protecting the ocean something people in your country care about? Why or why not?

reading.title: The Return of the Beaver
reading.passage:
For centuries the beaver was a familiar sight across the rivers of Europe, but by the early nineteenth century it had been hunted almost to extinction. Prized for its dense fur and for a secretion once used in medicine, the animal vanished from country after country until only scattered populations survived. Its disappearance went largely unmourned; to many people the beaver was little more than a nuisance that felled trees and flooded fields. Only in recent decades, as scientists have come to understand its role in shaping landscapes, has attitudes begun to change, and the beaver is now being deliberately reintroduced to rivers from which it was long absent.

The enthusiasm rests on the recognition that the beaver is what ecologists call a keystone species, an animal whose activities support a far wider community of life. By building dams, beavers create ponds and wetlands that slow the flow of water and provide habitats for fish, amphibians, insects and birds. The pools trap sediment and filter pollutants, improving the quality of water downstream. Perhaps most valuable of all in an age of unpredictable weather, the wetlands act as natural reservoirs, holding back water during heavy rain and releasing it slowly in times of drought.

Reintroduction is not without controversy. Farmers whose land lies beside rivers worry, with some justification, that beaver dams may flood productive fields or block drainage channels. Supporters respond that such conflicts can usually be managed through careful planning and modest compensation, and that the benefits to the wider landscape far outweigh the localised costs. In several trial areas, relations between landowners and conservationists have proved more cooperative than either side initially feared.

What makes the beaver especially attractive to conservationists is that it does its work for free. Engineering schemes designed to reduce flooding or restore wetlands can cost enormous sums, yet a family of beavers achieves comparable results simply by following its instincts. In an era when funds for conservation are scarce, the prospect of an animal that improves the environment at no charge has an obvious appeal, and it is changing the way many people think about restoring the natural world.

questions.q: The beaver had almost disappeared from Europe by the early nineteenth century.
When the beaver vanished, most people were deeply saddened.
Beaver dams can help reduce flooding during heavy rain.
Beaver fur was more valuable than the fur of any other European animal.
What does the term 'keystone species' refer to in the passage?
options: An animal hunted for its fur
An animal that supports many other species
An animal that lives only in wetlands
An animal that is easy to reintroduce

questions.q: Why is the beaver especially appealing to conservationists?

listening.title: Seminar: Planning a Reintroduction
listening.script:
TUTOR: Today we're discussing how a species reintroduction is actually planned, using the beaver as our example. It's far more involved than simply releasing animals and hoping for the best.

STUDENT: Where do you even start?

TUTOR: The first stage is a feasibility study. You assess whether the habitat can support the species, check the food supply, and identify any risks to local farming. This usually takes around two years.

STUDENT: And then you release them?

TUTOR: Not yet. Next comes consultation, talking to landowners, councils, and the public. This is often the hardest part, because people have strong opinions. Only after that do you select a small founding group, typically about six animals, and release them into a fenced trial site.

STUDENT: How long before you know if it's worked?

TUTOR: You monitor closely for at least five years, tracking breeding and how the wetland develops. For your essay, due on the second of May, compare two reintroduction projects and evaluate why one succeeded and the other did not.
