Farmers worry with some justification.
The benefits outweigh the localised costs.
Beavers achieve comparable results.

reading.title: How Babies Learn to Talk
reading.passage:
Long before they utter their first recognisable word, infants are already hard at work decoding the language around them. Researchers now believe that this process begins even before birth. By the final months of pregnancy, the developing ear is mature enough to detect the rhythm and melody of the mother's speech, and newborns only a few days old show a measurable preference for the language they were exposed to in the womb.

In the first year of life, babies face a remarkable challenge. Spoken language does not arrive neatly divided into separate words; instead, it flows past as a continuous stream of sound. To make sense of it, infants must work out where one word ends and the next begins. They appear to do this by tracking statistical regularities — noticing, for instance, that certain syllables tend to follow one another far more often than others. Experiments have shown that babies are surprisingly skilled at this kind of pattern detection, even when the sounds carry no meaning at all.

There is also a curious narrowing of ability over time. Very young infants can distinguish between speech sounds used in any of the world's languages, including contrasts that adults around them never use. By around ten months, however, this universal sensitivity fades, and babies become specialists, finely tuned to the sounds of their own language but less able to hear distinctions that do not matter in it. What looks like a loss is in fact a form of efficient learning: the brain is concentrating its resources on the patterns that will prove useful.

Parents play a part too, often without realising it. The exaggerated, sing-song style of speech that adults instinctively adopt with infants — slower, higher-pitched, with stretched-out vowels — is not merely sentimental. It appears to help babies pick out individual sounds and hold their attention, providing exactly the kind of clear input that the developing language system needs.

questions.q: Newborns show no preference for any particular language in their first days of life.
Spoken language reaches infants already separated into distinct words.
How do infants appear to identify word boundaries?
options: by tracking which syllables frequently follow each other
by being explicitly taught by parents

questions.q: Babies can detect sound patterns even when the sounds have no meaning.
By around ________ months, babies lose their universal sensitivity to all speech sounds.
The narrowing of babies' ability to hear all speech sounds is described as
options: an effect that never reverses harm

questions.q: The sing-song speech adults use with babies has been proven to slow language development.
Adults instinctively speak to infants in a slower, higher-pitched style with stretched-out ________.

listening.title: Enquiry: Joining a Conversation Club
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good afternoon, Riverside Language Centre. How can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I saw a poster about your weekly conversation club and I'd like some details.

RECEPTIONIST: Of course. The club meets every Thursday evening, from half past six until eight o'clock.

CALLER: And where exactly is it held?

RECEPTIONIST: It's in the Maple Room, which is on the second floor of the main building.

CALLER: Is there a fee?

RECEPTIONIST: There's a small charge of four pounds per session, but the first visit is completely free so you can try it out.

CALLER: That sounds fair. What level is it aimed at?

RECEPTIONIST: It's designed for intermediate learners and above, so you'll need to be fairly comfortable holding a basic conversation.

CALLER: I think I'd manage. Do I need to bring anything?

RECEPTIONIST: Just a notebook, really. We provide the discussion topics, and tea and coffee are included.

CALLER: Perfect. How do I sign up?

RECEPTIONIST: Simply send an email to the address on the poster, with your name and phone number, by Tuesday at the latest.

CALLER: Brilliant, thank you very much.

questions.q: The conversation club meets every ________ evening.
Sessions run from half past six until ________ o'clock.
The club is held in the ________ Room on the second floor.
How much does a single session cost (after the first free visit)?
The club is suitable for complete beginners.
To sign up, callers must email their name and phone number by ________ at the latest.

speaking.q: Let's talk about languages. Do you enjoy learning new languages? Which language would you most like to learn, and why? Do you think it is easier to learn a language as a child or as an adult? Give reasons for your answers.

writing.q:
The table below shows the number of adults (in thousands) enrolled in evening language courses in one city over four years.

Language | 2018 | 2020 | 2022
Spanish | 12 | 15 | 21
Mandarin | 6 | 11 | 18
French | 14 | 13 | 11
Arabic | 3 | 5 | 9

Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: The study tracked how each infant responded to familiar sounds.
Babies gradually learn to decode the speech around them.
Speech reaches us as a continuous stream of sound.
The word 'banana' has three syllables.
Infants track statistical regularities in language.
They notice regularities in which sounds occur together.
Young babies can distinguish sounds adults cannot.
Some sound contrasts disappear from adult perception.
This universal sensitivity fades after the first year.
Babies become specialists in their own language.
Parents use an exaggerated, sing-song tone with babies.
Adults instinctively slow their speech for infants.
The baby-talk style is not merely sentimental.

reading.title: The Quiet Death of Languages
reading.passage:
Of the roughly seven thousand languages spoken today, linguists estimate that nearly half may disappear before the end of this century. A language is generally considered endangered when children stop learning it as their mother tongue, for once a generation grows up speaking something else, the chain of transmission is broken. The final speakers may live for decades, but the language itself is effectively no longer alive.

The causes are rarely dramatic. More often, a language fades through a slow accumulation of small choices. Parents, hoping to give their children a better start, may decide to raise them in a dominant national language that promises access to schooling, employment and the wider world. Migration to cities, where many tongues mix and one inevitably prevails, accelerates the shift. In such circumstances, abandoning a minority language can feel less like a loss than a sensible step towards opportunity.

Why should anyone be troubled by this? After all, fewer languages might seem to make communication easier. Yet many scholars argue that each language represents a unique way of organising human experience. Vocabulary and grammar can encode knowledge accumulated over centuries — about local plants, weather patterns, kinship or navigation — that may have no equivalent elsewhere. When a language vanishes, that knowledge is not automatically transferred; frequently it simply disappears with the last fluent speakers.

Efforts to reverse the trend have met with mixed success. Recording grammars and compiling dictionaries can preserve a language for study, but a documented language is not the same as a living one. The more ambitious goal of revitalisation — getting communities to speak the language again in daily life — is far harder. It depends not only on teaching materials and committed teachers, but on something less tangible: a community's conviction that the language is worth the effort. Where that conviction exists, even languages once thought beyond rescue have occasionally been brought back into everyday use.

questions.q: Linguists estimate that nearly ________ of today's languages may disappear by the end of the century.
A language is considered endangered mainly when children stop learning it as a first language.
Languages usually die out as a result of sudden, dramatic events.
According to the passage, why do parents often switch to a dominant language?
options: because they dislike their own language
because it promises better access to schooling and work
because it is easier to pronounce

questions.q: What does the writer say is lost when a language disappears?
options: knowledge encoded in its vocabulary and grammar
the ability to migrate to cities

questions.q: The writer believes that documenting a language is exactly the same as keeping it alive.
Governments have promised funding to record every endangered language in the world.
Successful revitalisation depends partly on a community's ________ that the language is worth the effort.

listening.title: Briefing: A Language Documentation Project
listening.script:
COORDINATOR: Welcome, everyone. I'll quickly outline how our community language project will run this year. Our main aim is to record stories from the last fluent speakers before that knowledge is lost.

We'll be working in three nearby villages, and we expect to interview around twenty elders over the course of the project. Each interview will be both audio and video recorded, because gestures and facial expressions often carry meaning too.

Now, a practical point about scheduling. Mornings tend to work best, as our elders prefer not to travel in the heat of the afternoon. So please arrange your sessions before noon wherever possible.

Volunteers will be paired with a local assistant who speaks the language fluently. This is essential — the assistant will check that our transcriptions are accurate. Do not attempt to transcribe alone if you are still a beginner.

Finally, all recordings must be backed up the same evening onto the project's secure drive. We've already lost material once due to a damaged memory card, and we can't risk that again. If you have any questions, the full handbook is available at the front desk.

questions.q: What is the main aim of the project?
options: to teach the language to tourists
to record stories from the last fluent speakers

questions.q: The team expects to interview around ________ elders.
Interviews will be recorded in both audio and video.
Sessions should be scheduled before ________ wherever possible.
Why must beginners not transcribe alone?
options: the local assistant must check accuracy

questions.q: All recordings must be backed up the same ________ onto a secure drive.

speaking.q:
Describe a time when you found it difficult to communicate with someone. You should say:
- who you were trying to communicate with
- what the situation was
- why communication was difficult
and explain how you eventually dealt with the situation. You will have one minute to prepare and should speak for one to two minutes.

writing.q: Some people believe that governments should spend money trying to keep minority languages alive, while others argue that this money would be better spent on more urgent needs. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Hundreds of endangered languages have very few speakers left.
When children stop learning it, the chain of transmission breaks.
A language fades through an accumulation of small choices.
Many families switch to the dominant national language.
Migration to cities accelerates the language shift.
Some families abandon a minority language for opportunity.
Grammar can encode centuries of local knowledge.
Some languages have detailed words for kinship relations.
Local knowledge can vanish with the last speaker.
Researchers compile dictionaries to preserve languages.
Revitalisation aims to bring a language back into daily use.
Success depends on something less tangible than money.
Revival needs the community's conviction that it matters.

reading.title: The Rise of Emoji
reading.passage:
When the first set of emoji appeared on Japanese mobile phones in the late 1990s, few imagined that these tiny pictures would become one of the fastest-spreading forms of communication in human history. Within two decades, billions of emoji were being sent every day, woven into messages in almost every written language on earth. To some observers this signals a worrying decline in literacy; to others, it is simply language doing what it has always done — adapting to new tools and needs.

The appeal of emoji lies partly in what plain text cannot easily convey. Written messages strip away tone of voice, facial expression and gesture, the very cues that prevent everyday speech from being misunderstood. A short remark intended as a joke can read as cold or even hostile on a screen. A well-placed emoji can restore some of that missing warmth, signalling to the reader how a sentence is meant to be taken. In this sense emoji function less as words than as substitutes for the body language absent from digital text.

It would be a mistake, however, to treat emoji as a universal language understood identically by everyone. Their meanings shift across cultures and age groups, and the same symbol can be read in strikingly different ways. A gesture considered friendly in one country may seem rude in another, and younger users frequently assign ironic meanings that older users miss entirely. Far from being transparent, emoji require their own kind of literacy.

Linguists who study emoji are careful to point out their limits. Emoji are excellent at adding emotional colour, but they struggle to express anything complex or abstract. One cannot easily argue a case, describe a process or pose a precise question using pictures alone. For this reason, most researchers doubt that emoji will ever replace written words. They are better understood as a supplement — a flexible layer that enriches text rather than a rival poised to overturn it.

questions.q: The first emoji appeared on Japanese mobile phones in the late ________.
Everyone agrees that emoji represent a decline in literacy.
According to the passage, what do emoji mainly help to restore in digital messages?
options: the tone and warmth missing from plain text

questions.q: The same emoji can be interpreted differently across cultures and age groups.
Younger users sometimes give emoji ironic meanings that older users do not notice.
Why do most researchers think emoji will not replace written words?
options: they are too expensive to use
they cannot easily express complex or abstract ideas
they are banned in many countries

questions.q: Emoji are now taught as a compulsory subject in most schools.
Researchers regard emoji as a ________ to text rather than a rival.

listening.title: Seminar: Communicating Without Words
listening.script:
PROFESSOR: Today I want to explore how much of our communication happens without words at all. Some early estimates suggested that the words we choose account for only a small fraction of the message we send, with tone of voice and body language carrying the rest. Those exact figures have since been questioned, but the underlying point still holds: nonverbal signals matter enormously.

Let's start with eye contact. In many Western settings, steady eye contact is read as a sign of confidence and honesty. Yet in several other cultures, holding someone's gaze for too long can seem disrespectful, particularly toward an older person. So the very same behavior sends opposite messages depending on where you are.

Next, consider personal space. Researchers have found that the comfortable distance between two people in conversation varies widely from culture to culture. Step too close, and your partner may feel crowded; stand too far, and you can come across as cold or distant.

Finally, a word of caution. It's tempting to think you can read someone like a book from a single gesture. In reality, isolated signals are unreliable. Crossed arms might mean defensiveness, or the person might simply be cold. Always interpret nonverbal cues as a cluster, and in context.

questions.q: The professor says the exact early figures about nonverbal communication have been questioned.
In many Western settings, steady eye contact is read as a sign of confidence and ________.
What does the professor say about eye contact across cultures?
options: it always means the same thing
the same behaviour can send opposite messages

questions.q: The comfortable conversational distance between people is called personal ________.
Why does the professor warn against reading a single gesture?
options: gestures are illegal in some places

questions.q: The professor advises interpreting nonverbal cues as a ________ and in context.

speaking.q: Let's discuss communication technology. Some people say that texting and social media have made younger generations worse at face-to-face conversation. To what extent do you agree? How do you think digital communication will change in the future? Support your answer with reasons and examples.

writing.q:
The table below shows the percentage of messages sent through different methods by one age group in a country in 2015 and 2023.

Method | 2015 | 2023
Text message | 42% | 23%
Messaging apps | 18% | 51%
Email | 28% | 19%
Voice call | 12% | 7%

Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Plain text cannot convey tone of voice.
Facial expressions are important social cues.
A joke can read as hostile on a screen.
Emoji act as a substitute for body language.
Emoji are far from transparent in meaning.
Younger users assign ironic meanings to symbols.
Emoji struggle to express abstract ideas.
Emoji are a supplement to text, not a replacement.
A flexible layer that enriches written text.
Nonverbal signals matter enormously in conversation.
Holding someone's gaze too long can seem rude.
Crossed arms may suggest defensiveness.
Read cues as a cluster, not in isolation.

reading.title: Why English Became a Global Language
reading.passage:
It is sometimes assumed that English dominates the world because it is somehow easier or more logical than other languages. Anyone who has wrestled with its irregular spelling and unpredictable pronunciation knows that this cannot be the explanation. A language becomes global not because of qualities within it, but because of the power and reach of the people who speak it. English is a case study in how historical accident, rather than linguistic merit, shapes the fortunes of a language.

The first great expansion came with the British Empire, which by the nineteenth century had carried the language to every inhabited continent. Trade, administration and education in colonised territories were often conducted in English, and a knowledge of it became a passport to advancement. Even after the empire receded, the institutions and habits it had established frequently remained, leaving English deeply embedded in law, government and schooling across much of the world.

The second wave was economic and cultural, and it was driven largely by the United States. Throughout the twentieth century, American influence in business, science, technology and popular entertainment ensured that English became the default language of international exchange. Scientists publishing their findings, companies negotiating contracts and travellers seeking a common tongue increasingly turned to English not out of preference but out of practicality.

This dominance brings obvious advantages. A shared language lowers the barriers to cooperation, allowing people from different backgrounds to study, trade and collaborate with unprecedented ease. Yet it also raises uncomfortable questions of fairness. Native speakers enjoy an effortless advantage in international settings, while others must invest years of study to compete on the same stage. Some critics warn, too, that the global spread of English can crowd out local languages and the cultures bound up with them. The story of English, then, is not simply one of triumph; it is also a reminder that the success of one language is rarely without cost to others.

questions.q: The writer agrees that English is global because it is easier than other languages.
According to the writer, what mainly determines whether a language becomes global?
options: the power and reach of its speakers
how many letters its alphabet has

questions.q: English remained influential in many former colonies even after the empire declined.
The second wave of English expansion was driven largely by the ________.
Why did people increasingly turn to English for international exchange in the twentieth century?
options: because it was legally required everywhere

questions.q: The writer says native English speakers have an advantage in international settings.
The writer states that English will be replaced by another global language within fifty years.
Critics warn that the spread of English can crowd out local languages and the ________ bound up with them.

listening.title: Discussion: Choosing a Language to Study
listening.script:
TUTOR: So, you've both decided to take up a new language next term. Have you thought about which one?

MAYA: I'm leaning towards Spanish. It's spoken in so many countries, and I'd love to travel in South America one day.

TUTOR: A sensible choice. And you, Daniel?

DANIEL: I was tempted by Japanese, honestly, but I'm worried the writing system will slow me down too much. I only have about three hours a week to study.

TUTOR: That's a fair concern. Japanese rewards patience, but progress can feel slow at first. What's drawing you to it?

DANIEL: Mostly the films and games I enjoy. But maybe I'll start with something closer to English.

TUTOR: You could consider German. It shares a lot of vocabulary with English, so the early stages feel quite manageable.

DANIEL: That's a good point. I think I'll go with German for now and keep Japanese for later.

TUTOR: Wise. And remember, whichever you choose, the key is consistency. Twenty minutes every day beats three hours crammed into one evening.

MAYA: Noted. Little and often.

TUTOR: Exactly.

questions.q: Why is Maya leaning towards Spanish?
options: it is spoken in many countries and she wants to travel
it is required for her degree

questions.q: Daniel can study for only about ________ hours a week.
Daniel was originally tempted by Japanese.
Why does the tutor suggest German for Daniel?
options: it is the easiest language overall
it shares much vocabulary with English
it is spoken where Daniel lives

questions.q: Daniel finally decides to study ________ for now.
The tutor says the key to success is ________.

speaking.q: Let's talk about travel and communication. Have you ever travelled to a place where you did not speak the local language? How did you cope? Do you think it is important to learn some of a country's language before visiting it? Give reasons for your answers.

writing.q: Some people believe that the global dominance of English brings mostly benefits, as it allows people from different countries to communicate easily. Others argue that it threatens local languages and cultures. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: English dominates international communication.
English has notoriously irregular spelling.
Global success is not about linguistic merit.
The first expansion came with the British Empire.
Administration in the colonies was conducted in English.
English became a passport to advancement.
Even after the empire receded, English remained.
English is deeply embedded in law and schooling.
English became the default language of exchange.
People used English out of practicality.
A shared language allows cooperation with unprecedented ease.
Native speakers have an effortless advantage.
Global English can crowd out local languages.

reading.title: The Puzzle of Animal Communication
reading.passage:
Animals communicate constantly. A bird's alarm call warns its flock of a circling hawk; a honeybee performs an intricate dance to reveal the direction and distance of a flower patch; a dog's posture announces whether it wishes to play or be left alone. Such signals are sophisticated, finely tuned and often astonishing. Yet most scientists hesitate to call any of them a true language, and the reasons reveal much about what makes human language so unusual.

The central difference is sometimes described as openness. The signals animals exchange, however clever, tend to be limited to a fixed set of messages tied to immediate circumstances — danger, food, territory, the readiness to mate. Human language, by contrast, is open-ended. From a finite stock of sounds and words we can generate an essentially infinite number of new sentences, including ones never spoken before. We can also talk about things that are absent, imaginary or abstract: the distant past, a hypothetical future, ideas that have no physical form at all.

This is not to dismiss the achievements of other species. Researchers have taught a handful of apes to use hundreds of symbols, and some have combined them in ways that hint at simple grammar. Certain birds and marine mammals appear to use distinct calls that function almost like names. These findings have steadily narrowed the gap once assumed to separate humans from every other creature, and they caution against arrogance.

Nonetheless, a crucial threshold remains uncrossed. No animal, however well trained, has shown the capacity to build endlessly upon its own utterances, embedding one idea within another to express thoughts of unlimited complexity. A child acquires this ability spontaneously, without formal instruction, simply by being surrounded by speech. Why human beings alone should have evolved such a faculty is among the deepest and most stubbornly unsolved questions in all of science.

questions.q: The writer claims that animal signals are simple and unimpressive.
What is described as the central difference between animal signals and human language?
Human language allows us to talk about things that are absent or imaginary.
Some apes have been taught to use hundreds of symbols.
What does the writer say recent research findings should do?
options: confirm humans are entirely unique in every way

questions.q: A child acquires language spontaneously, without formal ________.
Scientists have now fully explained why only humans evolved this language faculty.
From a finite stock of words, humans can generate an essentially ________ number of new sentences.

listening.title: Lecture: What the Bees Are Saying
listening.script:
LECTURER: This morning we'll look at one of the most famous examples of animal communication: the honeybee's waggle dance. When a foraging bee discovers a rich source of nectar, it returns to the hive and performs a dance on the vertical surface of the comb.

The remarkable thing is how much information this dance contains. The angle of the dance, relative to vertical, indicates the direction of the food in relation to the sun. And the duration of the waggling part signals the distance: roughly speaking, the longer the waggle, the further away the flowers are.

Now, this was first decoded by the researcher Karl von Frisch in the mid-twentieth century, work for which he later received a Nobel Prize. For a time, some scientists doubted that the other bees really used this information, suspecting they simply followed scent. But careful experiments, including the use of tiny robotic bees, eventually confirmed that the dance genuinely guides the hive.

Still, we should keep this in perspective. The dance conveys just two pieces of information, direction and distance, both tied to an immediate, concrete goal. It is a stunning system, but it is not a language in the human sense. That distinction, as we'll see next week, matters a great deal.

questions.q: When a bee finds nectar, it returns to the hive and performs the ________ dance.
What does the angle of the dance indicate?
options: the direction of the food relative to the sun

questions.q: A longer waggle generally signals that the flowers are further away.
The dance was first decoded by the researcher Karl von ________.
How was it confirmed that bees really use the dance information?
options: through careful experiments using robotic bees

questions.q: The dance conveys just two pieces of information: direction and ________.

speaking.q: Let's discuss communication more broadly. Some people believe that human language is what most clearly separates us from other animals. Do you agree? What do you think we can learn from studying how animals communicate? Develop your answer with reasons and examples.

writing.q:
The table below shows the estimated number of known signals used by four animal species, according to one research study.

Species | Sound signals | Visual signals
Vervet monkey | 10 | 6
Honeybee | 2 | 4
Domestic dog | 8 | 12
Bottlenose dolphin | 17 | 5

Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: The bee performs an intricate dance.
Animal signals can be remarkably sophisticated.
Scientists hesitate to call it a true language.
From a finite stock of sounds we build new sentences.
Human language is open-ended and creative.
We can discuss a hypothetical future.
This is not to dismiss other species' achievements.
These findings have narrowed the gap.
The findings caution against arrogance.
A crucial threshold remains uncrossed.
Humans embed one idea within another.
Children acquire language spontaneously.
The language faculty is unique to humans.
It remains a stubbornly unsolved question.

reading.title: The Quiet Revolution of Vertical Farming
reading.passage:
In dense urban centres where arable land is scarce and expensive, a new model of food production has begun to take root inside warehouses, disused factories and even shipping containers. Vertical farming, as it is known, stacks crops in layered trays under carefully calibrated artificial light, allowing growers to harvest leafy greens and herbs without relying on sunshine, soil or favourable weather. Proponents argue that the technique could shorten supply chains dramatically, placing fresh produce within a few kilometres of the consumers who will eat it.

The environmental case rests largely on resource efficiency. Because water in these systems is recirculated rather than lost to runoff or evaporation, a vertical farm can reportedly use up to ninety per cent less water than a conventional open field producing the same weight of lettuce. Pesticides become largely unnecessary in a sealed indoor environment, and crops can be grown all year round regardless of the season outside. For city authorities anxious about the carbon emitted when vegetables are flown in from distant countries, the appeal is obvious.

Yet the model has stubborn weaknesses. The artificial lighting that replaces the sun is voracious in its appetite for electricity, and unless that power comes from renewable sources, the climate benefits can quickly evaporate. The high cost of constructing and running such facilities also means that, for now, vertical farms tend to specialise in high-value salad crops rather than the staple grains and pulses that feed most of the world. Wheat, rice and potatoes remain stubbornly cheaper to grow in fields.

Industry analysts are cautiously divided about the future. Some predict that falling prices for energy-efficient lighting will eventually make a far wider range of crops viable indoors. Others warn that the sector has attracted more investment than its early profits can justify, and that several high-profile companies have already collapsed. What seems clear is that vertical farming will complement, rather than replace, the open fields that humanity has depended on for ten thousand years.

questions.q: Vertical farms allow crops to be grown without natural sunlight.
A vertical farm may use as little as ten per cent of the water needed by a comparable open field.
Vertical farms currently produce most of the world's wheat and rice.
The author states that vertical farming will eventually make open-field agriculture obsolete.
Most vertical farms in operation are located in former hospital buildings.
According to the passage, the main threat to the climate benefits of vertical farming is
options: the loss of water to evaporation
the heavy electricity demand of artificial lighting
the use of chemical pesticides indoors
the transport of produce over long distances

questions.q: What does the passage say about industry analysts' views?
options: They are split over the sector's prospects
They believe the sector cannot survive
They ignore the cost of lighting

questions.q: At present, vertical farms tend to concentrate on high-value ________ crops rather than staple grains.

listening.title: Booking a Community Allotment Plot
listening.script:
OFFICER: Good morning, Riverside Allotments, how can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to put my name down for a plot, please.

OFFICER: Of course. Can I take your full name?

CALLER: Yes, it's Daniel Hartley, that's H-A-R-T-L-E-Y.

OFFICER: Lovely. And a contact number?

CALLER: It's oh-seven-seven-double-one, four-three-two, nine-eight-six.

OFFICER: Thank you. Now, we have two sizes. A full plot is around two hundred and fifty square metres, and the annual fee is forty-five pounds. A half plot is twenty-five pounds.

CALLER: I think the half plot would suit me to begin with.

OFFICER: Sensible choice for a first-timer. There is a short waiting list, so you'd most likely be offered something in about three weeks. The site is open from seven in the morning until dusk.

CALLER: Is there water on site?

OFFICER: Yes, there are communal taps, but we ask members to bring their own watering cans. We also run a free composting workshop on the first Saturday of every month.

CALLER: That sounds useful. I'll come along to that.

OFFICER: Wonderful. I'll email you the welcome pack today.

questions.q: The caller's surname is spelled ________.
A full plot measures about ________ square metres.
The annual fee for a half plot is ________ pounds.
The caller is likely to be offered a plot in about ________ weeks.
What does the officer ask members to bring themselves?
A free composting workshop runs on the first ________ of every month.

speaking.q: Let's talk about food. Do you prefer cooking at home or eating out? Are there any vegetables or fruits you particularly enjoy? Has the way you eat changed since you were a child?

writing.q:
The table below shows the estimated water use (litres) needed to produce one kilogram of selected foods. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Food | Litres of water per kg
Beef | 15400
Rice | 2500
Wheat | 1800
Apples | 820
Lettuce | 240

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Much of the arable land near the city has been built over.
The lights are carefully calibrated for each type of crop.
Proponents of the scheme say it will create local jobs.
Water is recirculated through the system to reduce waste.

reading.title: Saving the World's Seeds
reading.passage:
Deep inside a mountain on a remote Arctic island lies a vault designed to outlast almost any catastrophe humanity might face. The Global Seed Vault, buried in permafrost far above the Arctic Circle, stores duplicate samples of seeds held in gene banks around the world. Its purpose is not to feed people directly but to act as an insurance policy: a backup against the loss of the genetic diversity on which future agriculture will depend.

That diversity is shrinking at an alarming rate. Over the past century, farmers have increasingly abandoned thousands of traditional local varieties in favour of a handful of high-yielding modern cultivars. While these uniform crops are productive, their genetic sameness makes them vulnerable. A single new disease or a sudden shift in climate could devastate fields planted with identical plants, much as the Irish potato famine of the nineteenth century was worsened by an over-reliance on one variety.

The seeds preserved in such vaults represent a vast library of traits that breeders may one day need. Some old varieties tolerate drought; others resist particular pests or thrive in salty soil. By crossing these with modern crops, scientists hope to produce plants resilient enough to withstand a warming and increasingly unpredictable world. Crucially, the depositors retain ownership of their seeds; the vault merely holds copies in trust.

The facility has already proved its worth. When civil conflict damaged a major gene bank in the Middle East, researchers were able to withdraw backup samples and re-establish their collection elsewhere. Yet experts caution that frozen seeds are no substitute for the living knowledge of farmers who have cultivated these crops for generations. Preserving a seed is one thing; preserving the human skill to grow it is quite another.

questions.q: The Global Seed Vault is designed mainly to provide food during emergencies.
Modern high-yielding crops are genetically more uniform than traditional varieties.
Depositors lose ownership of any seeds they place in the vault.
Backup samples from the vault were once used to rebuild a damaged gene bank.
The vault stores more seed samples than any other facility in the world.
Why does genetic uniformity make crops vulnerable, according to the passage?
options: A single disease can affect all of them
It makes them harder to harvest

questions.q: What concern do experts raise at the end of the passage?
options: The vault is too cold for some seeds
Frozen seeds cannot replace farmers' practical knowledge
The vault may run out of space

questions.q: The vault is built within ________ on a remote Arctic island.

listening.title: A Talk on Heritage Apple Varieties
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone, to today's session on heritage apple varieties. A century ago, British orchards grew well over two thousand named varieties of apple. Today, the supermarkets stock perhaps a dozen. So why does this loss matter, and what can a small grower do about it?

First, flavour. Many old varieties were prized for qualities that don't suit modern shipping. The Ribston Pippin, for example, bruises easily but has an intense, nutty taste that commercial growers gave up on decades ago.

Second, resilience. Some heritage trees carry a natural resistance to scab and mildew, which means less spraying in the orchard. That's a real advantage if you garden organically.

Now, practical advice. If you want to plant one, choose a variety suited to your local rainfall. Plant bare-root trees between November and March, while they're dormant. Space standard trees about six metres apart. And do label each tree clearly, because believe me, after three years you will not remember which is which.

Finally, do join your local orchard group. Members share grafting wood for free, and you'll find expertise that no book can match.

questions.q: A century ago, British orchards grew over ________ thousand named varieties.
The ________ Pippin bruises easily but has an intense, nutty taste.
What advantage of some heritage trees does the speaker mention?
Bare-root trees should be planted between November and ________.
Standard trees should be spaced about ________ metres apart.
According to the speaker, what do members of orchard groups share for free?

speaking.q:
Describe a traditional dish from your country that you enjoy. You should say:
- what the dish is and what ingredients it contains
- when people usually eat it
- how it is prepared
and explain why you like this dish.

writing.q:
Some people believe that governments should do more to protect traditional crop varieties and local farming methods, even if modern industrial agriculture is more efficient. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: The seeds are kept in an underground vault.
The vault is buried in permafrost to keep the seeds cold.
Each gene bank sends a duplicate of its samples.
Farmers switched to a few high-yielding cultivars.

Breeders want crops resilient enough to survive drought.
Some old varieties carry useful traits like pest resistance.
A new disease could devastate a whole region's crops.
This variety can tolerate salty soil.
The vault holds the seeds in trust for the depositors.
The famine was worsened by over-reliance on one variety.
Plant the trees while they are dormant in winter.
Members share wood to graft onto new rootstocks.

reading.title: The Return of the Pollinators
reading.passage:
Roughly three-quarters of the world's leading food crops depend, at least in part, on animal pollination. Bees, butterflies, beetles and even some birds carry pollen from flower to flower, enabling plants to set the fruits and seeds that end up on our plates. The economic value of this free service runs into hundreds of billions of dollars each year, yet in many regions the insects responsible are in worrying decline.

The causes are tangled. The intensification of agriculture has replaced the patchwork of hedgerows, wildflowers and rough margins that once fed pollinators with vast, uniform fields offering little forage outside a brief flowering period. The widespread use of certain insecticides has been linked to disorientation and death among bees, while imported parasites have spread through managed honeybee colonies. Climate change adds a further complication, sometimes causing flowers to bloom before the insects that pollinate them have emerged.

Faced with these pressures, a growing number of farmers are rethinking the way they manage their land. Some leave strips of wildflowers along the edges of their fields, providing a steady supply of nectar throughout the growing season. Others have reduced their reliance on chemical sprays, timing any necessary applications for the evening when bees are less active. A handful of orchards, unable to attract enough wild insects, now rent hives that are trucked in during the blossom period, an industry that has grown rapidly in some countries.

Researchers stress that no single measure will reverse the decline. What pollinators need is a varied landscape that offers food and shelter across the seasons, not just a flush of blossom for a few weeks in spring. Encouragingly, studies suggest that even modest changes, repeated across many farms, can make a measurable difference. The humble flower strip, dismissed by some as merely decorative, may turn out to be one of agriculture's quietest yet most important investments.

questions.q: About 75 per cent of the world's main food crops rely to some degree on animal pollination.
Intensive farming has increased the variety of forage available to pollinators.
Some farmers spray chemicals in the evening because bees are less active then.
Renting hives during blossom time has become a fast-growing industry in some countries.
Honey produced by rented hives sells for a higher price than wild honey.
How does climate change harm pollination, according to the passage?
options: Flowers may bloom before pollinating insects appear
It reduces the value of crops

questions.q: What do researchers say is needed to help pollinators?
options: A varied landscape with food and shelter all year

questions.q: Some farmers leave strips of ________ along field edges to provide nectar.

listening.title: Two Students Plan a Pollinator Project
listening.script:
MAYA: Right, Tom, we need to finalise our field project on pollinators. Have you sorted the equipment?

TOM: Mostly. I've booked two insect nets and a hand lens from the lab. We still need a clipboard and the recording sheets.

MAYA: I'll print the sheets tonight. Where are we doing the survey?

TOM: I reckon the meadow behind the science block is best. It's got the widest range of flowers.

MAYA: Agreed. What time should we go? Bees are most active when it's warm.

TOM: Late morning, around eleven, when the temperature's up but before it gets too hot.

MAYA: Good. And we should survey on three separate days to get reliable data, not just one.

TOM: Definitely. Let's do Monday, Wednesday and Friday next week.

MAYA: Perfect. Oh, and remember we have to count the insects for exactly ten minutes at each spot, then move on.

TOM: Ten minutes, got it. I'll set a timer on my phone.

MAYA: Last thing. Dr Sharma wants the draft report submitted by the twentieth of May.

TOM: Plenty of time if we start writing straight after Friday.

questions.q: Tom has booked two insect nets and a ________ from the lab.
Where will the students carry out their survey?
options: The meadow behind the science block

questions.q: They plan to start the survey at around ________ in the late morning.
To get reliable data, they will survey on ________ separate days.

speaking.q: Let's discuss the wider issue of food and the environment. Whose responsibility is it to protect insects like bees: individuals, farmers or governments? Do you think people in your country are aware of where their food comes from? How might food production change in the next fifty years?

writing.q:
The table below shows the number of managed beehives (in thousands) in four countries in 2010 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Country | 2010 | 2020
Country A | 320 | 410
Country B | 510 | 470
Country C | 180 | 260
Country D | 240 | 230

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Most fruit crops depend on insect pollination.
Uniform fields offer little forage for bees.

Hedgerows once provided shelter for wildlife.
The insecticide can cause disorientation in bees.
Imported parasites have spread through bee colonies.
A single hive may hold a colony of fifty thousand bees.

No single measure will reverse the decline.

reading.title: Feeding the City Below the Ground
reading.passage:
Thirty metres beneath the busy streets of a European capital, a former air-raid shelter has been given an unexpected second life. In tunnels once built to protect civilians from bombing, rows of pea shoots, radishes and exotic herbs now grow under banks of pink and blue light. The enterprise that runs them argues that this underground farm is a glimpse of how crowded cities might feed themselves in the decades to come, using spaces that would otherwise lie empty and forgotten.

The attractions of subterranean growing are not difficult to grasp. Underground, the temperature stays remarkably stable throughout the year, sparing growers the cost of heating in winter and cooling in summer. There are no storms, frosts or droughts to ruin a crop, and the absence of natural light, far from being a drawback, allows growers to control the plants' environment with great precision. Produce can be harvested, packed and delivered to nearby restaurants within hours, a freshness that no imported vegetable can match.

Critics, however, are quick to point out the limitations. The pink glow that nurtures the plants is generated by light-emitting diodes, and although these are far more efficient than older bulbs, the electricity bill remains substantial. Labour, too, is costly in a city centre, and the cramped tunnels make large-scale mechanisation awkward. As a result, the farm focuses on delicate, fast-growing micro-greens that command premium prices, rather than the bulky staples that fill an ordinary diet.

Whether such ventures can ever move beyond a profitable niche is hotly debated. Optimists imagine networks of abandoned tunnels, cellars and car parks quietly producing a meaningful share of a city's salad. Sceptics counter that the economics will always favour fields and greenhouses where sunlight is free. For now, the underground farm remains a curiosity that is also a serious experiment, testing just how far human ingenuity can stretch the boundaries of where food is grown.

questions.q: The underground farm is housed in a structure originally built for a different purpose.
Underground temperatures vary widely between the seasons.
The farm concentrates on bulky staple crops rather than micro-greens.
Producing micro-greens underground allows them to be sold at premium prices.
The farm exports its produce to other European countries.
Why is the lack of natural light described as an advantage?
options: It reduces the cost of seeds
It lets growers control conditions precisely

questions.q: What do sceptics argue in the final paragraph?
options: Underground farms are unsafe for workers
The economics will always favour places with free sunlight
Tunnels are too cold for any crop

questions.q: The plants are grown under light produced by light-emitting ________.

listening.title: Lecture: The Economics of Food Miles
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today we're looking at the concept of food miles, the distance a product travels from farm to plate. It's an idea that became popular in the nineteen nineties, and it's still widely misunderstood.

The basic claim is simple. The further food travels, the more fuel is burned and the more carbon is released. So buying local should always be greener, right? Well, not quite.

Here's the catch. Transport often accounts for a surprisingly small share of a food's total emissions, sometimes under ten per cent. Far more comes from how the food was produced. A tomato grown locally in a heated greenhouse can have a larger carbon footprint than one shipped from a sunny country where it grew outdoors.

The method of transport matters enormously too. Shipping by sea is remarkably efficient per kilogram, whereas air freight is the worst offender by far, producing roughly fifty times the emissions of a cargo ship over the same distance.

So the lesson is this. Don't judge food by distance alone. Ask how it was grown and how it was carried. Next week we'll examine the role of refrigeration in the cold chain.

questions.q: Food miles measure the distance a product travels from farm to ________.
The concept became popular in the ________.
What point does the lecturer make about transport emissions?
options: They are always the largest share
They are often a small share of the total

questions.q: Transport can account for under ________ per cent of a food's total emissions.
Air freight produces roughly ________ times the emissions of a cargo ship over the same distance.
What will next week's lecture examine?

speaking.q: Let's talk about shopping for food. Where do you usually buy your groceries? Do you ever buy food at local markets? Do you think about where your food comes from when you shop?

writing.q:
Many people argue that buying locally produced food is always better for the environment than buying imported food. Others claim that this is too simple a view. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: The subterranean farm grows herbs in old tunnels.
The farm offers a glimpse of future city agriculture.
The absence of light is not really a drawback here.
The pink light nurtures the young plants.
The electricity bill remains substantial.
The cramped tunnels make mechanisation awkward.
Micro-greens command premium prices in the city.
These herbs sell at a premium price.
The venture may stay a profitable niche.
Sceptics doubt the farm can ever scale up.
The project tests human ingenuity.
A heated greenhouse can have a large carbon footprint.
Air freight is the worst offender for emissions.
Air transport is the worst offender by far.

reading.title: The Promise and Peril of Lab-Grown Meat
reading.passage:
For most of human history, eating meat has meant raising and slaughtering an animal. A new technology promises to break that ancient link. Cultivated meat, sometimes called lab-grown or cell-based meat, is produced by taking a small sample of muscle cells from a living animal and coaxing them to multiply in a nutrient-rich liquid inside large steel vessels called bioreactors. The result, its developers insist, is genuine meat, biologically identical to a conventional cut, but grown without the slaughterhouse.

The potential benefits are considerable. Livestock farming occupies vast tracts of land, consumes enormous quantities of water and grain, and is responsible for a significant share of global greenhouse-gas emissions. If cultivated meat could be produced at scale, its supporters argue, it might dramatically shrink agriculture's environmental footprint while sparing billions of animals from intensive confinement. It could also reduce the risk of certain diseases that pass from farmed animals to humans.

The obstacles, however, are formidable. The growth medium that feeds the cells has historically been extraordinarily expensive, and bringing the price of a single laboratory burger down to that of a supermarket one has proved painfully slow. Scaling up from a few grams in a research lab to the tonnes required to feed a city is an engineering challenge that no company has yet fully solved. There are regulatory hurdles, too, since food-safety authorities must be satisfied that the product is safe before it can be sold.

Perhaps the greatest uncertainty is whether consumers will accept it. Surveys suggest that many people feel an instinctive unease about meat grown in a factory, however carefully it is explained to them. Marketers will need to overcome this so-called yuck factor, possibly by emphasising the technology's ethical and environmental credentials. Whether cultivated meat becomes a staple of the future diet or remains an expensive curiosity will depend not only on chemistry and engineering, but on the more unpredictable matter of human taste.

questions.q: Cultivated meat is grown from cells taken from a living animal.
Producing a lab-grown burger as cheaply as a supermarket one has been achieved quickly.
Food-safety authorities must approve cultivated meat before it can be sold.
Surveys indicate that some consumers feel uneasy about factory-grown meat.
Cultivated meat is currently cheaper to produce than conventional beef.
The first commercial cultivated-meat product was launched in Asia.
According to the passage, what is described as the 'yuck factor'?
options: The high cost of the growth medium
Consumers' instinctive unease about lab-grown meat
The difficulty of scaling up production

questions.q: The cells are multiplied inside large steel vessels called ________.

listening.title: Radio Feature: A Visit to a Cultivated-Meat Start-up
listening.script:
PRESENTER: I'm standing in a gleaming laboratory on the outskirts of Cambridge, talking to Dr Elena Voss, the founder of a cultivated-meat start-up. Elena, thanks for having me.

VOSS: My pleasure. Welcome to the future of food.

PRESENTER: So, how does this actually work?

VOSS: We begin with a tiny sample of cells, taken painlessly from a cow. We then feed those cells in a bioreactor, and over about three weeks they multiply into muscle tissue.

PRESENTER: Three weeks. And how much can one bioreactor produce?

VOSS: Our largest tank holds two thousand litres and yields roughly two hundred kilograms of meat per cycle.

PRESENTER: Impressive. What's the biggest challenge you face?

VOSS: Honestly, cost. When we started, the growth medium alone cost a fortune. We've cut that by about eighty per cent in two years, but we need to go further.

PRESENTER: And when might shoppers see this on the shelves?

VOSS: We're aiming for a limited launch in select restaurants by twenty twenty-seven, with supermarkets to follow.

PRESENTER: Fascinating stuff. Thank you, Elena.

The largest tank holds ________ litres.

speaking.q: Let's explore the future of food. Do you think people will eat more or less meat in the future? What might persuade people to try new kinds of food, such as cultivated meat or insects? Should governments encourage people to change their diets for environmental reasons?

Protein source | kg CO2 per kg

reading.title: The Slow Return of Night Trains
reading.passage:
For much of the late twentieth century, the overnight sleeper train seemed destined for extinction. The expansion of budget airlines, which offered fares that undercut almost any rail ticket, persuaded many European operators to withdraw their night services during the 1990s and early 2000s. Carriages were scrapped, depots were closed, and the specialised skills required to maintain sleeping cars were quietly lost. By 2010, a traveller wishing to cross the continent after dark had remarkably few options.

The reversal that followed surprised even the rail industry. A growing awareness of the environmental cost of short-haul flights, combined with a wave of nostalgia among younger travellers who had never experienced a sleeper, created unexpected demand. Austria's national operator was among the first to respond, acquiring abandoned rolling stock from neighbouring countries and gradually rebuilding a network that linked major cities while passengers slept. The gamble proved commercially sound, and other operators began to take notice.

Reviving the night train, however, is far from straightforward. Sleeper carriages are expensive to build and occupy track at times when freight operators also compete for space. Crossing multiple borders requires agreements on staffing, ticketing and safety standards that differ from one country to the next. Critics argue that without sustained public subsidy, the romance of the sleeper cannot survive the unforgiving arithmetic of modern transport.

Supporters counter that the night train offers something no aircraft can: the ability to travel and rest simultaneously, arriving rested at the heart of a city rather than at a distant airport. Whether this advantage is enough to secure a lasting place in the transport mix remains uncertain, but the carriages rolling once more through the darkness suggest that reports of the sleeper's death were premature.

questions.q: Budget airlines offered fares that were often cheaper than rail tickets.
The skills needed to maintain sleeping cars were carefully preserved during the decline.
Austria's operator built entirely new carriages rather than reusing old ones.
Younger travellers paid higher fares than older passengers for sleeper journeys.
What does the passage suggest revived demand for night trains?
options: Concern about flight emissions and nostalgia

questions.q: According to critics, what may the sleeper require to survive?
Night trains must compete with ________ operators for space on the tracks.
Supporters note that passengers can travel and ________ at the same time.

listening.title: Booking a Sleeper Ticket
listening.script:
AGENT: Good afternoon, Continental Rail bookings, how can I help?
CALLER: Hello, I'd like to book a sleeper from Vienna to Venice for next week.
AGENT: Certainly. Which night would you be travelling?
CALLER: The fourteenth, if there's space.
AGENT: Let me check. Yes, we have availability. Would you prefer a private compartment or a shared couchette?
CALLER: A private one, please. How much is that?
AGENT: A single private compartment is one hundred and forty euros, including breakfast.
CALLER: That's fine. What time does it depart?
AGENT: Departure is at nine fifteen in the evening, arriving in Venice at six forty the next morning.
CALLER: And can I bring a bicycle?
AGENT: You can, but there's a small charge of twelve euros and you must reserve a space in advance.
CALLER: I'll add that. Is there a power socket in the compartment?
AGENT: Yes, every compartment has a socket and a reading light. Could I take the name for the booking?
CALLER: It's Daniel Hartley, H-A-R-T-L-E-Y.
AGENT: Thank you, Mr Hartley. I'll email your confirmation shortly.

Route | 2015 | 2023

reading.title: Mapping the Hidden Rivers of Cities
reading.passage:
Beneath the pavements of many old cities lie rivers that residents have all but forgotten. As settlements expanded during the industrial era, engineers buried inconvenient streams in brick culverts, channelling them underground to free up land for building and to dispose of waste. London's Fleet and Walbrook, Toronto's Garrison Creek and dozens of similar watercourses vanished from the surface, surviving only in the names of streets and districts.

For decades these buried rivers were treated as little more than sewers. Recently, however, urban geographers and hydrologists have begun to reconsider them. Mapping their original courses has proved valuable for understanding why certain neighbourhoods flood during heavy rain: water, it turns out, remembers its ancient paths even when concrete covers them. When intense storms overwhelm drainage systems, the lowest-lying streets, often those that follow a forgotten valley, are the first to be inundated.

Some cities have gone further, attempting to bring their hidden rivers back to the surface in a process known as daylighting. Restoring a stream can reduce flood risk, lower summer temperatures and create green corridors that residents value. Seoul's celebrated transformation of a covered watercourse into a public park is frequently cited as proof of what is possible, although the scheme was expensive and required diverting traffic that had relied on an overhead motorway.

Daylighting is not always feasible. Where a buried river runs directly beneath dense development, the cost of excavation may be prohibitive, and the engineering challenges considerable. Even so, the renewed interest in these subterranean waterways reflects a broader shift in how planners think about cities, treating natural features not as obstacles to be hidden but as assets to be worked with.

questions.q: Many urban rivers were buried to create space for building.
Buried rivers have no effect on where modern cities flood.
Seoul's daylighting project was cheap to carry out.
London's Fleet now carries more water than it did before being buried.
What is meant by 'daylighting'?
options: Bringing buried rivers back to the surface

questions.q: Why may daylighting be impossible in some places?
options: Excavation under dense development is too costly

questions.q: Engineers buried streams in brick ________ to channel them underground.
Restoring a stream can lower summer ________.

listening.title: A Walking Tour of Hidden Waterways
listening.script: GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to our walking tour of the city's hidden rivers. We'll be on our feet for about two hours, so I hope you've brought comfortable shoes. Our first stop is just behind the old market, where the River Pell once flowed openly before it was covered in eighteen sixty. If you listen carefully near the drain cover, you can still hear running water. Now, a few practical points. The tour costs nine pounds per adult, and children under twelve go free. We'll have one short break at a cafe roughly halfway through, so do bring a little cash. Photography is welcome, but please don't lean over the railings at the canal section, as the path can be slippery. At the end, those who are interested can buy a detailed map of the buried rivers for four pounds; all the money goes to the local history society. Any questions before we set off? No? Then let's begin. Please stay together, because the back streets can be confusing.

The River Pell was covered over in ________.
The tour costs ________ pounds per adult.
Who goes free on the tour?

speaking.q:
Describe a place in your town or city that has changed a lot. You should say:
- what the place was like before
- how it has changed
- why the change happened
and explain how you feel about the change.

writing.q:
Some people believe that cities should restore natural features such as rivers and green spaces, even when this is very expensive. Others argue that limited public money should be spent on more urgent needs. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: The stream disappeared into a concrete culvert.
The old watercourse still shapes the valley.
