reading.title: Why Some Languages Travel Further Than Others
reading.passage:
When people migrate, they carry their languages with them, yet the fate of those languages in a new land varies enormously. Some flourish for generations, sustained by tight-knit communities and a steady flow of new arrivals; others fade within a single lifetime, abandoned by children eager to assimilate. Linguists who study this process have identified several factors that determine whether a transplanted language survives or disappears.

The size and density of the migrant community is an obvious influence. A large population concentrated in one neighbourhood can support shops, places of worship and newspapers in the heritage language, giving speakers daily reasons to use it. Dispersed communities, by contrast, struggle to maintain such institutions, and their members increasingly rely on the dominant language to conduct ordinary business.

Attitude also matters. Where a heritage language carries prestige, perhaps because it is associated with a celebrated literary tradition, families may make deliberate efforts to pass it on. Where it is stigmatised or seen as an obstacle to advancement, parents sometimes discourage its use, hoping to spare their children disadvantage. Researchers note, however, that such decisions are frequently regretted later, when grown children wish they could speak the language of their grandparents.

Government policy can tip the balance in either direction. Schools that offer bilingual instruction, or simply tolerate languages other than the official one, send a signal that diversity is acceptable. Policies that suppress minority languages, whether through outright prohibition or subtle neglect, accelerate their decline. The lesson emerging from decades of study is that language survival is rarely accidental: it reflects the combined weight of community, attitude and the wider society's willingness to make room for difference.

questions.q: All migrant languages decline at roughly the same rate in a new country.
A concentrated migrant community can more easily support heritage-language institutions.
Parents who discouraged a heritage language sometimes later regret it.
Bilingual schooling is more expensive than monolingual schooling.
According to the passage, why might children abandon a heritage language?
options: They are forced to by linguists

questions.q: What does the passage say about government policy?
options: It has no effect on language survival
It can encourage or accelerate decline

questions.q: A heritage language linked to a celebrated literary ________ may be passed on deliberately.
Language survival reflects community, attitude and the wider society's willingness to make room for ________.

listening.title: A Lecture on Heritage Languages
listening.script: LECTURER: Good morning. Today I want to look at how migrant communities keep their languages alive, and I'll focus on three practical supports. The first is community schooling. In many cities, families run weekend classes, often staffed entirely by volunteers, where children learn to read and write the heritage language. Attendance can be surprisingly high; one study in Melbourne found that nearly seventy per cent of families in a particular community sent their children to such classes. The second support is media. Where there is a local-language radio station or a printed newspaper, usage tends to remain stronger, simply because the language stays present in everyday life. The third, and perhaps most overlooked, is the role of grandparents. When elderly relatives live nearby and speak little of the dominant language, children have a genuine reason to keep using the heritage tongue. For your essay this week, I'd like you to compare two communities and assess which of these three supports matters most. Please submit it by Friday the tenth.

questions.q: Weekend community classes are often staffed entirely by ________.
In the Melbourne study, nearly ________ per cent of families sent children to classes.
What is the second support mentioned?
Why do grandparents help maintain the language?
options: Children have a real reason to use it

questions.q: Students must compare ________ communities for the essay.
The essay is due on Friday the ________.

speaking.q: Let's discuss language and identity. Why do you think some people lose their family's original language after moving abroad? What can governments or communities do to help minority languages survive? Is it important to preserve languages that very few people speak?

writing.q:
The table below shows the percentage of second-generation migrants who could speak their heritage language in four cities in 2010 and 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

City | 2010 | 2022
Melbourne | 62% | 71%
Toronto | 55% | 49%
London | 48% | 53%
Berlin | 40% | 38%

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: The community language flourished for generations.
They grew up in a tight-knit village.
Many migrants try to assimilate quickly.
A transplanted custom can change in a new land.
Population density is high in the inner city.
The community is dispersed across the suburbs.
English is the dominant language at work.
The language carries great cultural prestige.
Some accents are unfairly stigmatised.
Education is a path to advancement.
The regime tried to suppress minority languages.
The prohibition on the language lasted decades.
Neglect accelerated the language's decline.
They made a deliberate effort to teach the children.

reading.title: The Geography of Disappearing Coastlines
reading.passage:
Coastlines are among the most restless features of the planet's surface, forever advancing and retreating in response to waves, tides and the slow rhythms of the climate. For communities that live beside the sea, this natural mobility has always posed a challenge, but in recent decades the pace of change along some coasts has accelerated alarmingly, forcing difficult decisions about where, and whether, to defend the land.

The traditional response has been to build hard defences: sea walls, groynes and concrete revetments designed to hold the line against the water. Such structures can be effective, yet they are costly to maintain and often simply shift the problem elsewhere. By interrupting the natural movement of sand along the shore, a sea wall protecting one stretch of beach may starve a neighbouring stretch of the sediment it needs, hastening erosion further down the coast.

Partly for this reason, planners increasingly favour what is termed managed retreat: a deliberate decision to abandon certain areas to the sea rather than defend them indefinitely. Roads, even entire settlements, may be relocated inland, allowing the coast to find a new and more stable position. The approach is controversial, since it can require people to leave homes their families have occupied for generations, and compensation arrangements are rarely generous enough to satisfy everyone affected.

Underlying these debates is an uncomfortable truth: the resources available for coastal defence are finite, and not every threatened place can be saved. Decisions about which communities to protect inevitably involve judgements about value, both economic and cultural, that are difficult to make and harder still to justify to those who must bear the consequences. As seas continue to rise, such choices will only become more frequent and more painful.

questions.q: Coastlines naturally move back and forth over time.
Sea walls never cause problems for neighbouring stretches of coast.
Managed retreat involves defending all coastal areas indefinitely.
Compensation for relocated residents is usually higher in wealthy countries.
How can a sea wall harm a nearby beach?
options: By interrupting the movement of sand

questions.q: What is described as an 'uncomfortable truth'?
options: Defence resources are limited and not all places can be saved

questions.q: Hard defences include sea walls, groynes and concrete ________.
Managed retreat may require relocating roads and even entire ________.

listening.title: A Coastal Defence Public Meeting
listening.script: OFFICIAL: Thank you all for coming this evening. I know feelings run high on this issue, so I'll keep my introduction brief and leave plenty of time for your questions. The council has been studying the cliffs along Harbour Road for the past three years. Our engineers estimate that, at current rates, the cliff edge is retreating by about half a metre every year. Building a new sea wall along the whole stretch would cost roughly eight million pounds, and even then it would protect the road for perhaps thirty years at most. The alternative, which we are now seriously considering, is to move the coastal road forty metres inland over the next decade. I want to stress that no final decision has been made. We have set up a dedicated phone line, and a public consultation will run until the end of March. Written comments can be sent to the address on the leaflet you received at the door. Now, who would like to begin?

questions.q: The council has studied the cliffs for the past ________ years.
The cliff edge is retreating by about ________ a metre each year.
A new sea wall would cost roughly ________ million pounds.
What is the alternative the council is considering?
The road would be moved ________ metres inland.
Until when does the public consultation run?

speaking.q: Let's talk about the sea and the coast. Do you enjoy spending time near the sea? Why or why not? Are beaches popular places to visit in your country? How do you think coastal areas might change in the future?

writing.q:
As sea levels rise, some experts argue that governments should stop spending money defending coastal communities and instead help people move inland. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: The restless sea reshaped the shore overnight.
The glacier continues to retreat each year.
Wooden groynes trapped sand along the beach.
A concrete revetment shielded the cliff base.
The river deposits sediment at its mouth.
Coastal erosion threatens the village.
Pollution hastened the reef's decline.
The defences cannot be maintained indefinitely.
The factory was relocated to the coast.
The plan proved deeply controversial.
Residents received compensation for their losses.
Public funds are finite.
It is hard to justify such spending.
They must live with the consequences.

reading.title: The Unexpected Geography of Remote Work
reading.passage:
For most of the modern era, where people worked determined where they lived. Jobs clustered in cities, and workers followed, accepting long commutes and high rents as the price of opportunity. The sudden, widespread adoption of remote working has begun to loosen that ancient link, and geographers are watching with interest to see how patterns of settlement might be redrawn.

The most visible early effect was a flow of professionals away from the largest, most expensive cities towards smaller towns and rural areas. Freed from the daily journey to an office, some workers sought cheaper housing, more space and easier access to the countryside. Estate agents in once-overlooked regions reported sudden surges of interest, and a handful of small towns experienced a degree of revival they had not seen in decades.

The consequences, however, have not been uniformly welcome. In some desirable destinations, an influx of relatively well-paid newcomers has driven up house prices, pricing out local residents whose wages are tied to the regional economy. Long-standing tensions between incomers and established communities, familiar from earlier waves of migration, have resurfaced in new forms. What looks like rejuvenation to one group can feel like displacement to another.

Whether these shifts prove lasting remains genuinely uncertain. Some employers, having initially embraced remote work, are now urging staff back to the office, fearing a loss of collaboration and company culture. Others have committed to flexible arrangements permanently. Much will depend on which model prevails, and on whether the infrastructure of smaller places, from broadband to schools, can support a larger and more demanding population. The geography of work, settled for over a century, has entered a period of genuine flux.

questions.q: Historically, the location of work strongly influenced where people lived.
Every community has welcomed the arrival of remote workers.
All employers have permanently committed to remote working.
Remote workers tend to be younger than the local residents they move near.
What was the most visible early effect of remote work?
options: A move from rural areas into big cities
A flow of professionals out of expensive cities
A fall in house prices everywhere

questions.q: Why are some employers urging staff back to the office?
options: Fear of losing collaboration and culture

questions.q: An influx of well-paid newcomers has driven up house ________ in some places.
The future depends partly on whether smaller places have adequate ________, from broadband to schools.

listening.title: Interview with a Relocated Worker
listening.script:
HOST: So, Maya, you left the city two years ago to work remotely from a small coastal town. How has it worked out?
MAYA: Honestly, better than I expected. The first thing I noticed was the cost of housing. My rent dropped by almost forty per cent compared to what I paid downtown.
HOST: That's a big difference. What about the practical side, like internet?
MAYA: That was my main worry, but the town had fiber installed the year before I arrived, so my connection is actually faster than it was in the city.
HOST: And do you miss the office?
MAYA: Sometimes. I drive in to meet my team once a month, which is about a two-hour trip each way. I think that balance works well for me.
HOST: Has the local community been welcoming?
MAYA: Mostly, yes, though I'm aware that people like me have pushed prices up, and not everyone is happy about that. I try to support local businesses to give something back.
HOST: Thanks, Maya, that's a really honest picture.

questions.q: Maya left the city ________ years ago.
Her rent dropped by almost ________ per cent.
What was Maya's main worry before moving?
The town had ________ installed the year before she arrived.
Maya drives to meet her team once a ________.
What concern does Maya acknowledge about newcomers?

speaking.q:
Describe a place you would like to live in the future. You should say:
- where it is
- what it is like
- what you would do there
and explain why you would like to live in this place.

writing.q:
The table below shows the change in population (in thousands) of three small towns and one large city in a country between 2018 and 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Place | 2018 | 2023
Riverton (town) | 12 | 19
Hillcrest (town) | 9 | 15
Oakdale (town) | 14 | 13
Capital City | 880 | 845

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Tech firms cluster around the university.
Her daily commute took two hours.
Remote work has loosened the link to the office.
There was a surge of interest in rural homes.
The town enjoyed an economic revival.
An influx of tourists strained the roads.
Rising rents priced out long-term tenants.
Old tensions resurfaced during the dispute.
The grant funded the area's rejuvenation.
Gentrification can cause displacement.
Many firms embraced flexible working.
The flexible model may prevail in the end.
Rural infrastructure must be improved.
The industry is in a state of flux.

reading.title: The Quiet Death of the Evening Newspaper
reading.passage:
For much of the twentieth century, the evening newspaper occupied a particular niche in urban life. Printed in the early afternoon and sold at railway stations and street corners, it caught commuters returning home and offered them a digest of the day's events, sports results and stock prices. In many cities the evening edition outsold its morning rival, precisely because it arrived when readers finally had the leisure to absorb it.

The decline of this format was neither sudden nor simple. The most obvious culprit was television. By the late 1960s, early-evening news bulletins delivered headlines hours before a printed paper could reach the doorstep, and they did so with moving images that no column of type could match. Yet broadcasters did not finish off the evening press on their own. Changing patterns of work and commuting also played a part: as suburbs spread and journeys lengthened, the window in which a freshly printed afternoon paper remained current grew uncomfortably narrow.

Proprietors responded in various ways. Some merged their morning and evening titles to cut costs; others repositioned the evening paper as a lighter, more feature-driven product, full of human-interest stories rather than breaking news. A handful experimented with multiple daily editions, each updated with the latest results, but the printing and distribution costs of such an approach proved ruinous. None of these strategies reversed the underlying trend.

By the time the internet arrived, the evening paper was already a diminished thing, and the web simply administered the final blow. Rolling online coverage made the very idea of a fixed afternoon deadline seem quaint. Today only a scattering of evening titles survives, and most of those are distributed free, funded entirely by advertising rather than by the coins once dropped into a vendor's hand. Historians of the press tend to view the format's disappearance less as a failure than as a casualty of a changing rhythm of daily life.

questions.q: Evening newspapers were typically printed before noon.
In some cities the evening edition sold more copies than the morning edition.
Television was the sole cause of the evening newspaper's decline.
The journalists who worked on evening papers were paid less than those on morning papers.
Why did running multiple daily editions fail as a strategy?
options: The costs of printing and distribution were too high

questions.q: How are most surviving evening titles funded today?
Some proprietors repositioned the evening paper as a lighter product full of ________ stories.
Historians regard the format's disappearance as a casualty of a changing ________ of daily life.

listening.title: Joining the Local History Newspaper Archive
listening.script:
LIBRARIAN: Good morning, you've reached the City Library archive desk. How can I help?
VISITOR: Hello, I'd like to use the old newspaper collection for a research project.
LIBRARIAN: Of course. Are you a registered member already?
VISITOR: Not yet. What do I need to sign up?
LIBRARIAN: Just a form of photo identification and a proof of address. The membership itself is free for residents.
VISITOR: Great. And how far back does the collection go?
LIBRARIAN: The earliest editions we hold are from eighteen forty-two, though anything before nineteen hundred is on microfilm rather than paper.
VISITOR: That's fine. Can I book a reader machine in advance?
LIBRARIAN: Yes, you can reserve one online. Each session lasts ninety minutes, and you may book up to two sessions per day.
VISITOR: Perfect. Is there a charge for printing pages?
LIBRARIAN: Printing is twenty pence per sheet. Scanning to a memory stick, however, is completely free.
VISITOR: And where exactly is the archive room?
LIBRARIAN: It's on the second floor, just past the reference section. Ask at the desk and someone will show you in.

questions.q: To register, the visitor needs photo identification and a proof of ________.
The earliest editions held date from the year ________.
Material from before 1900 is available only on microfilm.
Each reader-machine session lasts ________ minutes.
What is the cost of scanning pages to a memory stick?
The archive room is located on the ________ floor.

speaking.q: Let's talk about news. How do you usually keep up with the news? Do you prefer to read it, watch it, or listen to it, and why?

writing.q:
The table below shows the percentage of adults in one country who obtained their news from four sources in 2005 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

News source | 2005 | 2020
Printed newspapers | 48% | 14%
Television | 71% | 52%
Radio | 33% | 21%
Online / social media | 19% | 68%

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: The evening paper occupied a particular niche in urban life.
Commuters wanted a quick digest of the day's events.
Readers had the leisure to absorb the news at home.
Television was the most obvious culprit behind the decline.
Early-evening news bulletins delivered headlines first.
Proprietors merged their titles to cut costs.
They repositioned the paper as a lighter product.
The distribution costs proved ruinous.
Fixed deadlines began to seem quaint.
Only a scattering of evening titles survives.
The format was a casualty of a changing way of life.
By then the paper was a diminished thing.
The paper remained current only for a short window.
No strategy reversed the underlying trend.

reading.title: How Search Engines Decide What You See
reading.passage:
When a person types a question into a search engine, the list of results that appears within a fraction of a second feels almost magical. In reality it is the product of a long chain of decisions made by software, much of it invisible to the user. Understanding even the outline of this process helps explain why two people searching for the same words may not see exactly the same thing.

The first stage is crawling. Automated programs, often called spiders, follow links from page to page across the web, copying what they find. The copies are then organised in an index, a vast catalogue that records which words appear on which pages. Without this index, a search would have to scan the entire web in real time, which would be impossibly slow.

The decisive stage is ranking. When a query arrives, the engine consults its index and assembles a list of candidate pages, then orders them according to hundreds of signals. Some signals concern the page itself: how often the search terms appear, whether they feature in the title, how recently the page was updated. Others are external, the best known being the number and quality of links pointing to a page from elsewhere. A link, in this model, functions rather like a recommendation.

In recent years a further layer has been added: personalisation. The engine may take account of a user's location, previous searches and even the device being used, adjusting results accordingly. A query for "football" might return very different pages in different countries. Critics argue that this tailoring can trap users inside a "filter bubble", quietly narrowing the range of information they encounter without their knowledge.

Engineers counter that some degree of personalisation is unavoidable and often helpful, since a result that suits one user may be useless to another. What few dispute is that the criteria themselves are commercially sensitive and rarely disclosed in full. The exact weighting of the signals remains a closely guarded secret, which means that the public, and indeed the businesses that depend on high rankings, must largely take the system on trust.

questions.q: Two people entering identical search terms always receive identical results.
An index allows an engine to avoid scanning the whole web for every query.
In the ranking model, a link to a page acts most like:
What do critics fear personalisation may create?
Search engines publish the full weighting of their ranking signals.
Most search engineers have publicly apologised for using personalisation.
The automated programs that follow links across the web are often called ________.
Engineers argue that some personalisation is ________, since results that suit one user may not suit another.

listening.title: A Tutorial on Evaluating Online Sources
listening.script: TUTOR: Okay everyone, today's short session is about judging whether a website is trustworthy. There are four quick checks I want you to remember. First, authorship. Always look for who wrote the page. If no author or organization is named anywhere, treat the content with caution. Second, the date. A page on, say, medical advice might be ten years out of date, so find when it was last updated. Third, the purpose. Ask yourself whether the site exists to inform you or to sell you something, because a commercial motive can colour the facts. And fourth, the evidence. A reliable page links to its sources so you can check them yourself. Now, a useful shortcut is the web address itself. Endings like dot gov or dot edu usually signal a government or educational body, which tends to be more reliable than an anonymous blog. But don't rely on that alone. The single most powerful technique is what researchers call lateral reading: instead of staying on the page, open a new tab and see what other independent sites say about it. I'll put the checklist on the shared drive after class.

questions.q: What is the first check the tutor recommends?
A site with a commercial motive may ________ the facts.
The tutor says a reliable page links to its sources.
Web address endings like dot gov or dot ________ usually signal a more reliable body.
What does the tutor call the most powerful technique?
The checklist will be placed on the ________ drive after class.

speaking.q:
Describe a website or app that you find particularly useful. You should say:
- what the website or app is
- how you discovered it
- how often and why you use it
and explain why you find it so useful.

writing.q:
Some people believe that search engines and social media should be responsible for removing false information from their platforms. Others argue that this gives such companies too much power over what people can read.

Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: The first stage of indexing is crawling the web.
The copies are organised in a vast index.
The engine assembles a list of candidate pages.
Pages are ordered according to hundreds of signals.
Some ranking signals are external to the page.
Personalisation adjusts results to the individual user.
Critics worry about the tailoring of search results.
Few dispute that the criteria are secret.
The criteria are rarely disclosed in full.
The weighting of the signals is a secret.
Ranking is the decisive stage of the process.
Understanding the outline of the process helps.
Lateral reading means checking other sites.
The criteria are commercially sensitive.

reading.title: The Rise and Limits of the Public Opinion Poll
reading.passage:
Few inventions have shaped modern political reporting as profoundly as the opinion poll. Before its arrival, editors and politicians could only guess at the public mood from letters, crowd sizes and the impressions of party workers. The poll promised something apparently scientific: a numerical snapshot of what an entire population thought, derived from interviewing only a small fraction of it.

The mathematics that makes this possible is the theory of sampling. Provided a sample is genuinely random, so that every member of the population has an equal chance of being chosen, the views of a few thousand people can mirror those of millions with a calculable margin of error. The difficulty, as practitioners quickly learned, lies in the word "random". A sample drawn only from telephone owners, or from people willing to answer the door, may systematically exclude certain groups and so distort the result.

The history of polling is therefore punctuated by famous failures. One celebrated case from the 1930s saw a magazine confidently predict the wrong winner of an election after polling its own readers and car owners, a group far wealthier than the electorate as a whole. The lesson, that a large but biased sample is worse than a small but representative one, has had to be relearned in every generation.

Modern pollsters deploy elaborate techniques to correct for such distortions, weighting their raw figures so that the proportions of age, gender and region match those of the wider population. Yet new problems have emerged. As fewer people answer unsolicited calls, response rates have fallen sharply, raising the worry that those who do reply differ in some hidden way from those who do not. Online panels bring their own biases.

None of this means that polls are worthless; over many contests they remain more accurate than any rival method. But the confident single figure splashed across a front page conceals a great deal of uncertainty. A responsible report would always accompany it with the margin of error and the size of the sample, details that headlines almost invariably omit.

questions.q: Before opinion polls, the public mood could only be estimated from indirect signs.
A genuinely random sample gives every population member an equal chance of selection.
What does the 1930s example chiefly illustrate?
options: A large but biased sample is worse than a small representative one

questions.q: Why have falling response rates worried pollsters?
options: Those who reply may differ in hidden ways from those who do not

questions.q: Modern pollsters adjust raw figures so demographic proportions match the wider population.
Online panel polls are now banned in several countries.
The mathematics that makes polling possible is the theory of ________.
A responsible report would state the margin of error and the size of the ________.

listening.title: Planning a Student Survey Project
listening.script:
STUDENT A: So, for our media studies project we have to survey students about their news habits. Where do we start?
STUDENT B: Well, first we need to decide on our sample size. The tutor suggested at least one hundred and fifty respondents to be meaningful.
STUDENT A: That's a lot. How do we reach them?
STUDENT B: I think an online questionnaire is easiest. We can share the link through the student portal.
STUDENT A: Good idea, but won't that only reach people who check the portal regularly?
STUDENT B: True, that's a bias we should mention in the report. Maybe we also hand out paper copies in the cafeteria to balance it.
STUDENT A: Okay. How many questions should we ask?
STUDENT B: Let's keep it short, no more than twelve, or people won't finish.
STUDENT A: Agreed. And when's the deadline for collecting responses?
STUDENT B: We need everything in by the fourteenth of March, so the analysis is due the week after.
STUDENT A: Right. I'll draft the questions tonight and send them to you for checking.
STUDENT B: Perfect. Don't forget to keep them neutral so we don't lead people toward an answer.

questions.q: The tutor suggested a minimum of ________ respondents.
How will they mainly distribute the questionnaire?
They plan to hand out paper copies in the cafeteria to reduce bias.
They agree to ask no more than ________ questions.
Responses must be collected by the ________ of March.
What does Student B warn about when wording the questions?
options: They should be neutral, not leading

speaking.q: Let's discuss public opinion. Why do you think people are sometimes influenced by opinion polls before an election? Should the results of such polls be published in the days before people vote?

writing.q:
The table below shows the average daily time (in minutes) that people in three age groups spent on different media activities in one country in 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Activity | Age 16-24 | Age 35-44 | Age 65+
Watching TV | 65 | 110 | 180
Social media | 145 | 80 | 25
Reading news online | 20 | 45 | 55
Listening to radio | 15 | 40 | 70

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: A poll offers a numerical snapshot of opinion.
Polling rests on the theory of sampling.
Every poll has a calculable margin of error.
A biased sample can distort the result.
Polling history is punctuated by failures.
A large but biased sample is unreliable.
A small but representative sample is better.
Pollsters correct figures by weighting them.
Fewer people answer unsolicited calls now.
A single figure conceals much uncertainty.
Headlines invariably omit the sample size.
Pollsters deploy elaborate techniques.
They use elaborate weighting methods.
The figure is splashed across the front page.

reading.title: The Long Shadow of the Photograph
reading.passage:
When photography emerged in the middle of the nineteenth century, it was widely greeted as a machine for telling the truth. Unlike a painting, filtered through the imagination of an artist, a photograph appeared to record reality directly, light falling on a sensitive surface to leave an objective trace of whatever stood before the lens. This faith in the camera's honesty would prove remarkably durable, and remarkably misplaced.

Even in its earliest decades, photography was anything but neutral. The photographer chose where to point the camera, what to include and, crucially, what to leave out. A scene of poverty could be framed to arouse pity or, with a small shift of angle, to suggest squalor and blame. Long exposure times meant that early subjects had to hold rigid poses, producing the stiff, solemn portraits we associate with the period; these were not candid records but careful constructions.

The arrival of the darkroom multiplied the possibilities for manipulation. Skilled technicians could combine negatives, remove unwanted figures or lighten a complexion, all long before the digital age. Famous historical photographs were quietly altered for political ends, with inconvenient individuals made to vanish from official group portraits. The image that millions trusted was, in such cases, a fabrication.

Digital technology has not so much created this problem as accelerated it. Software now allows alterations once requiring hours of expert labour to be performed in seconds, and to a standard that defeats the unaided eye. More troubling still is the emergence of wholly synthetic images, generated by algorithms from no original scene at all. The very category of the photograph as evidence is thereby thrown into question.

Some respond to this with alarm, predicting an era in which no image can be believed. Others point out that scepticism toward pictures is hardly new and may even be healthy. What seems clear is that the nineteenth-century faith in the camera as an impartial witness can no longer be sustained. A photograph, like any other text, must be read with attention to who made it, how, and why.

questions.q: Early viewers believed photographs recorded reality more honestly than paintings.
The stiff poses in early portraits were caused by long exposure times.
According to the passage, photo manipulation:
options: Existed long before the digital age

questions.q: What does the writer say digital technology has done to the problem of manipulation?
options: Created it for the first time

questions.q: The writer believes the old faith in the camera as an impartial witness can still be sustained.
Most photographers today receive formal training in detecting fake images.
Images generated by algorithms from no original scene are described as wholly ________.
A photograph, like any text, must be read with attention to who made it, how, and ________.

listening.title: A Talk on the History of News Photography
listening.script: LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today I want to trace how photographs came to dominate the news. In the earliest illustrated papers, photographs could not be printed directly. Instead, engravers copied them by hand onto wooden blocks, a process that could take an entire day for a single image. The breakthrough came in eighteen eighty with the invention of the halftone process, which broke a picture into tiny dots and allowed it to be printed alongside the type. Suddenly, real photographs could appear in a daily paper. By the nineteen thirties, the picture magazine had become a powerful cultural force, and photographers were sent across the globe to capture events as they unfolded. One thing I want to stress is the role of the caption. A photograph rarely speaks for itself; the words placed beneath it tell the reader what to see and how to feel. Change the caption and you can change the meaning entirely, without altering a single pixel. That is something I want you to keep in mind for next week's seminar, when we will analyse three famous war photographs and their captions.

questions.q: How were images reproduced in the earliest illustrated papers?
options: Copied by hand onto wooden blocks

questions.q: The halftone process was invented in ________.
The halftone process broke a picture into tiny ________.
By the 1930s the picture magazine was a powerful cultural force.
What does the lecturer stress about a photograph's meaning?
options: The caption can change its meaning

questions.q: Next week's seminar will analyse three famous ________ photographs and their captions.

speaking.q: Let's talk about photographs. Do you take many photos? What kinds of things do you most like to photograph, and what do you usually do with the pictures afterwards?

writing.q:
Because images can now be edited or even artificially generated so convincingly, some people argue that we should no longer trust photographs as evidence of real events.

To what extent do you agree or disagree with this view?

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: A photograph seemed to leave an objective trace.
Faith in the camera proved remarkably durable.
The faith was durable but misplaced.
These were not candid records but constructions.
An angle could suggest squalor and blame.
The darkroom multiplied possibilities for manipulation.
The trusted image was a fabrication.
Wholly synthetic images come from algorithms.
Digital tools accelerated the problem.
The camera was seen as an impartial witness.
Scepticism toward pictures is not new.
Subjects had to hold rigid poses.
Individuals were made to vanish from photos.
The old faith can no longer be sustained.

reading.title: Why Rumours Travel Faster Than Corrections
reading.passage:
It is a familiar frustration for anyone who follows the news. A dramatic claim spreads across social networks within hours, reaching millions; the careful correction that follows, days later, reaches only a fraction of the original audience. This asymmetry is not accidental. It arises from the way human attention works and from the way the platforms that now carry much of our information are designed.

Psychologists have long noted that novel and surprising information commands disproportionate attention. A claim that overturns expectations feels worth sharing, whereas a measured statement that nothing unusual has happened does not. Falsehoods, being unconstrained by reality, can be tailored precisely to provoke surprise, outrage or fear, the very emotions that prompt people to pass a story on. The truth, by contrast, must remain within the bounds of what actually occurred, and so often appears comparatively dull.

The architecture of social platforms amplifies this tendency. Their recommendation systems are tuned to maximise engagement, promoting whatever keeps users clicking, commenting and sharing. Since emotionally charged content reliably generates such activity, it is precisely the most inflammatory material, true or false, that the system pushes hardest. A correction, sober by design, rarely triggers the same reaction and is therefore quietly buried.

Timing compounds the problem. A false story enjoys a clear run during the hours or days before it is checked, and many who encounter it never return to learn that it was wrong. Even those who do may find the original impression stubbornly persistent; researchers describe a "continued influence effect", whereby a discredited claim continues to shape people's beliefs after they have consciously accepted that it is false.

None of this should breed despair. Some platforms now attach warnings to disputed posts, and there is evidence that prompting users to pause and consider accuracy before sharing can measurably reduce the spread of falsehood. But such measures swim against a powerful current. As long as attention remains the currency of the information economy, the spectacular lie will retain an advantage over the unspectacular truth, and combating it will demand effort rather than complacency.

questions.q: The gap between the reach of a rumour and its correction is purely accidental.
Novel and surprising information tends to attract disproportionate attention.

listening.title: A Workshop on Stopping the Spread of Misinformation
listening.script: FACILITATOR: Welcome to today's workshop on misinformation. I'll keep this practical. Our research team studied how false posts spread on one platform over six months, and I want to share three findings before we move to the exercises. First, speed. A false story reached its peak audience in just six hours, while the average correction took almost three days to circulate. Second, emotion. Posts that made readers feel angry were shared roughly twice as often as neutral ones. That's a striking gap. Third, and more hopefully, intervention. When we showed a simple pop-up asking, are you sure this is accurate, before someone reshared a post, sharing of false content dropped by around fifteen percent. Now, that figure may sound modest, but at the scale of millions of users it matters enormously. The key lesson is that small design changes can have large effects. After the break, you'll work in groups to design your own warning message, and we'll vote on the most persuasive one. Please remember to keep your message short, because long warnings are simply ignored.

reading.title: The Slow Reform of Jury Trials
reading.passage:
The jury trial occupies a curious place in modern justice systems. Praised as a safeguard against state overreach, it is simultaneously criticised for being slow, costly and unpredictable. In several common-law countries, the proportion of criminal cases that actually reach a jury has fallen below five per cent, with the overwhelming majority resolved through negotiated pleas. Reformers disagree sharply about whether this decline should be welcomed or resisted.

Supporters of the jury argue that ordinary citizens bring a moral sensibility that professional judges, however learned, may lack. A panel drawn from the community, they contend, is less likely to be swayed by procedural habit and more likely to reflect prevailing notions of fairness. Critics counter that jurors are vulnerable to rhetoric, struggle with complex financial or scientific evidence, and may import their own prejudices into the deliberation room. Studies in one jurisdiction found that fraud trials involving intricate accounting frequently confused lay panels, prompting calls for specialist tribunals.

A further tension concerns transparency. Because jury deliberations are conducted in secret and verdicts require no reasons, the public cannot easily scrutinise how a conclusion was reached. Judges, by contrast, must usually publish written justifications that can be appealed. Some scholars regard the jury's silence as a virtue that protects independence; others see it as an obstacle to accountability.

Proposed reforms range from the modest to the radical. The mildest would simply give jurors clearer written guidance and permit them to ask questions during testimony. More ambitious schemes would replace juries in certain categories of case with panels of trained assessors, or allow defendants to waive a jury entirely in exchange for a faster hearing. What unites these proposals is a recognition that the institution, though venerable, cannot remain wholly untouched by the pressures of cost and complexity that now define the courts.

questions.q: In some common-law countries, fewer than five per cent of criminal cases now reach a jury.
Critics believe jurors handle complex scientific evidence more easily than judges do.
Jury verdicts must normally be accompanied by written reasons.
The proposed specialist tribunals have already been adopted in most jurisdictions.
According to the passage, supporters of the jury value it mainly because
options: it is cheaper than a judge-only trial
ordinary citizens bring a moral sensibility

questions.q: The 'tension concerning transparency' refers to the fact that
options: deliberations are secret and verdicts unexplained

questions.q: The mildest reform would give jurors clearer written ________ and let them ask questions.

reading.title: Whistleblowers and the Price of Disclosure
reading.passage:
A whistleblower is an individual who reveals wrongdoing within an organisation, usually in the belief that the public interest demands it. Such disclosures have exposed financial fraud, environmental damage and threats to safety that might otherwise have remained hidden. Yet those who speak out frequently pay a heavy personal price, ranging from dismissal and reputational harm to prolonged legal battles.

Lawmakers in many countries have attempted to encourage disclosure by offering legal protection. Statutes typically shield employees from retaliation provided they report through approved channels and act in good faith. In practice, however, the protection often proves fragile. Employers may find indirect ways to penalise a worker, and the burden of proving that a dismissal was retaliatory commonly rests with the whistleblower. Critics argue that this imbalance discourages all but the most determined.

A related controversy concerns the use of financial rewards. In one model, an informant who supplies evidence leading to a successful prosecution may receive a percentage of any penalty recovered. Advocates claim this incentivises people to come forward despite the risks; sceptics worry that rewards attract opportunists and may taint the credibility of testimony. The debate has no easy resolution, since the same payment that motivates an honest insider could equally tempt a dishonest one.

Beyond the law lies a cultural dimension. In organisations where loyalty is prized above candour, the whistleblower is often cast as a traitor rather than a guardian. Changing this perception, some argue, matters more than any statute. Training that frames responsible disclosure as a professional duty, together with confidential reporting lines, may do more to surface misconduct than the promise of either protection or reward. The most effective systems, research suggests, combine clear legal safeguards with a workplace culture that treats speaking up as ordinary rather than heroic.

Sceptics of financial rewards are mainly concerned that rewards may

listening.title: A Talk on Reporting Misconduct at Work
listening.script:
TRAINER: Welcome, everyone, to this short session on raising concerns at work. I'm going to keep it practical. First, if you witness something that worries you, the golden rule is to write it down straight away. Note the date, the people involved and exactly what happened. Memory fades, and a clear record is your strongest support later.

Second, use the right channel. Our organisation has a confidential hotline, and you can reach it on extension 4-0-5. Calls are not recorded, and you may stay anonymous if you wish.

Third, don't try to investigate yourself. It's tempting to gather more evidence, but that can damage a later inquiry. Simply report what you've observed and let the compliance team take it forward.

Finally, remember your legal position. Staff who report in good faith are protected from dismissal under our policy. If you ever feel you're being treated unfairly afterwards, contact the staff representative, Mr Okafor, whose office is on the second floor. Any questions? Good — let's move to the case studies.

reading.title: Restorative Justice in Practice
reading.passage:
Restorative justice rests on a simple but unconventional premise: that crime is best understood as harm done to people and relationships rather than merely a breach of state rules. Instead of focusing primarily on punishment, the approach brings offenders, victims and sometimes the wider community together to discuss what happened and how the damage might be repaired. Proponents argue that this dialogue can deliver something a courtroom rarely offers, namely a sense of closure for those affected.

The method takes several forms. In a victim-offender conference, the two parties meet, usually with a trained facilitator, to talk through the offence and agree on a way forward, which may include an apology, financial compensation or community work. Other schemes convene larger circles involving family members and local representatives. What unites them is an emphasis on accountability through understanding rather than through the imposition of a sentence alone.

Evidence on effectiveness is encouraging but qualified. Several evaluations report that victims who take part feel more satisfied with the process than those who go through conventional courts, and some studies record modest reductions in reoffending. Yet the gains are not universal. The approach appears to work best for less serious offences and where the offender genuinely accepts responsibility. Critics caution that it can place an unfair emotional burden on victims, who may feel pressured to forgive, and warn that it is unsuitable for crimes involving a severe imbalance of power.

Despite these reservations, restorative practices have spread well beyond the criminal courts. Schools use them to resolve bullying, and some workplaces have adopted similar conferences to address disputes. Whether the model can be scaled to handle more serious offences without losing its essential character remains, for now, an open question. Its advocates insist that even limited adoption marks a meaningful shift away from a purely punitive conception of justice.

options: the two parties and a trained facilitator

reading.title: The Ethics of Predictive Policing
reading.passage:
Predictive policing refers to the use of statistical models to forecast where crimes are likely to occur or, more controversially, who is likely to commit them. Drawing on historical records of offences, arrests and calls for service, these systems generate maps and risk scores intended to help commanders allocate scarce resources. To their supporters, the appeal is obvious: a force that anticipates trouble can prevent harm rather than merely respond to it.

The ethical difficulties, however, run deep. The most frequently cited concern is feedback. If a model is trained on data reflecting where police have historically concentrated their efforts, it will tend to direct officers back to those same neighbourhoods, generating fresh arrests that appear to confirm the original prediction. Communities already subject to heavy attention may thus be locked into a cycle that the data itself perpetuates, irrespective of the actual distribution of crime.

A second problem concerns transparency. Many predictive tools are supplied by private vendors who treat their algorithms as commercial secrets. Defendants and oversight bodies may therefore be unable to examine how a score was produced, undermining the principle that those affected by official decisions should be able to contest them. Some jurisdictions have responded by demanding that any system used in public administration be open to independent audit.

Proponents respond that human judgement is hardly free of bias either, and that a well-designed model, regularly tested against outcomes, could be fairer than the intuitions it replaces. The crucial issue, on this view, is not whether to use data but how. Safeguards such as excluding arrest data in favour of victim reports, publishing accuracy statistics and subjecting tools to regular review might preserve the benefits while limiting the harms. Whether such safeguards can be enforced consistently, particularly where budgets are tight and vendors resistant, is the question on which the legitimacy of the whole enterprise may ultimately rest.

questions.q: Predictive policing systems are designed to help allocate limited police resources.

The 'feedback' problem arises because models trained on past policing data tend to
options: send officers back to heavily policed neighbourhoods

options: use victim reports rather than arrest data

listening.title: A Lecture on Data and Fairness
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today I want to unpack one idea that sits at the heart of debates about algorithms in justice, and that's the notion of bias. People often assume a machine must be neutral. After all, it doesn't have feelings or prejudices. But that assumption misses the point. A model only knows what we feed it, and if the data carries our past mistakes, the model will faithfully reproduce them.

Consider a simple example. Suppose a city has, for years, sent more patrols to one district. Naturally, more offences are recorded there, not necessarily because more crime occurs, but because more officers are watching. Feed that record into a prediction tool, and it will recommend even more patrols for the same district. The bias becomes self-reinforcing.

So what can we do? Three things, I'd suggest. First, scrutinize the input data before trusting any output. Second, test the system against independent measures, such as surveys of victims. And third, insist on transparency, so that decisions can be challenged. Without those steps, we risk dressing up old prejudice in the language of mathematics. We'll look at a case study after the break.

data is never useful in justice

reading.title: Intellectual Property in a Digital Age
reading.passage:
Intellectual property law grants creators a temporary monopoly over their work, on the reasoning that the prospect of reward will encourage invention and artistry that benefit everyone. For most of its history this bargain functioned reasonably well, but the digital age has placed it under unusual strain. When a song, a film or a textbook can be copied perfectly and shared globally at almost no cost, the very scarcity on which the system depends begins to dissolve.

Rights holders have responded with a combination of technology and litigation. Digital locks restrict how files may be used, while lawsuits target those who distribute copies without permission. Critics contend that such measures are both ineffective and disproportionate. Determined infringers, they note, routinely circumvent technical protections, while ordinary users find themselves unable to make perfectly reasonable copies of material they have lawfully purchased. The law, in this account, punishes the cooperative majority in pursuit of an uncooperative few.

A more fundamental debate concerns the appropriate length of protection. Copyright now commonly extends for decades beyond the creator's death, a duration that few economists believe is necessary to encourage creativity. Such terms, opponents argue, mainly enrich corporate holders of old catalogues while keeping cultural works out of the public domain, where they could be freely adapted and built upon. Defenders reply that long protection rewards investment and provides for an author's descendants.

Meanwhile, new forms of creativity strain the old categories altogether. When software generates images or text by recombining vast quantities of existing material, it becomes genuinely difficult to say who, if anyone, is the author, and whether the underlying works have been used fairly. Legislators accustomed to thinking in terms of discrete human creators face a landscape in which the boundaries of originality are increasingly blurred. The law, designed for an age of physical copies and identifiable authors, must now be reimagined for one in which neither assumption reliably holds.

options: enrich corporate holders of old catalogues

LIBRARIAN: Hello there. You wanted advice about using images in your dissertation?

STUDENT: Yes, exactly. I've found some great photographs online, but I'm worried about copyright.

LIBRARIAN: Sensible to check. The first rule is that finding something online doesn't make it free to use. You need to look at the licence.

STUDENT: How do I find that?

LIBRARIAN: Often it's stated near the image. Look for a Creative Commons label. Some allow reuse if you give credit; others forbid commercial use entirely.

STUDENT: My dissertation isn't commercial, though.

LIBRARIAN: True, but you should still cite the source properly. And if you want to be completely safe, use our subscription database, which is free for students and clears the rights for academic use.

STUDENT: That sounds ideal. What's it called?

LIBRARIAN: It's the ImageVault collection. You log in with your student number. There's a short guide on the second floor, near the help desk.

STUDENT: Brilliant. And is there a limit on how many I can download?

LIBRARIAN: Fifty per month, which should be more than enough.

STUDENT: Perfect, thank you.

reading.title: The Quiet Revolution in Grid Storage
reading.passage:
For decades the central weakness of renewable electricity was not the cost of generation but the impossibility of storing it economically. Wind farms produce surplus power on gusty nights when demand is low, while solar arrays peak at midday rather than during the evening rush. Without a way to bank this energy, grid operators were forced to keep fossil-fuelled plants idling as a backup, eroding much of the climate benefit that renewables promised to deliver.

Lithium-ion batteries, originally refined for consumer electronics and electric vehicles, have begun to change this calculus. Costs per kilowatt-hour have fallen by roughly ninety per cent since 2010, making it feasible to install battery banks the size of warehouses alongside solar parks. These installations can absorb cheap midday electricity and release it after sunset, smoothing the notorious evening surge. In several regions, such systems now respond to demand spikes within milliseconds, a speed that conventional gas turbines cannot match.

Yet lithium is not the only contender. Engineers are reviving older ideas, such as pumping water uphill into reservoirs when power is abundant and letting it flow back through turbines when it is scarce. Newer concepts include compressing air into underground caverns and storing heat in molten salt or even in stacked bricks. Each approach has a different sweet spot: lithium excels at short, rapid bursts, whereas pumped hydro and thermal stores are better suited to holding energy for many hours or days.

The remaining obstacle is duration. Most commercial batteries discharge fully within four hours, which is adequate for daily cycles but useless during a week-long lull in both wind and sunshine. Bridging these longer gaps will probably require a portfolio of technologies rather than a single winner. Policymakers, meanwhile, are slowly redesigning electricity markets so that storage operators are paid not merely for the energy they sell but for the flexibility and reliability they provide to the system as a whole.

questions.q: The main barrier to renewable electricity was historically the expense of generating it.
Grid operators kept fossil-fuelled plants running as a precaution before storage became viable.
Lithium-ion battery costs per kilowatt-hour have dropped substantially since 2010.
Pumped hydro storage was invented more recently than lithium-ion batteries.
Most regions have already abolished payments to fossil-fuel plants entirely.
According to the passage, lithium-ion systems are particularly good at:
options: responding to demand spikes very quickly

questions.q: The author suggests that bridging week-long lulls will most likely require:
options: a mix of different storage technologies

OFFICER: Lovely. Can I take your full name first?
CALLER: Yes, it's Helen Marsden, M-A-R-S-D-E-N.
OFFICER: Thank you. And which scheme are you interested in, the shared panel option or the home install?
CALLER: The shared panel one, please. I rent, so I can't put anything on my own roof.
OFFICER: That's fine, the shared scheme is ideal for renters. The minimum buy-in is one panel, which costs ninety pounds, and you earn credits on your monthly bill.
CALLER: How much do I save roughly?
OFFICER: A single panel saves the average household about thirty pounds a year.
CALLER: And how long is the contract?
OFFICER: It runs for five years, though you can sell your share back at any time.
CALLER: Great. What do I need to bring to register?
OFFICER: Just a recent electricity bill and some photo identification. Our office on Bridge Street is open until half past four on weekdays.

reading.title: Cooling Cities Without Air Conditioning
reading.passage:
As summers grow hotter, the world's appetite for air conditioning is climbing steeply, and with it the demand for electricity. The irony is sharp: the machines that keep buildings cool burn so much power that they intensify the very warming they are meant to relieve. Researchers are therefore turning to passive cooling, a collection of design strategies that lower indoor temperatures without consuming significant energy.

Many of these ideas are ancient. Thick walls, narrow streets and shaded courtyards kept Mediterranean and Middle Eastern dwellings tolerable long before electricity existed. Wind towers, tall shafts that catch the breeze and funnel it downward, ventilated houses in the Persian Gulf for centuries. Modern architects are rediscovering such features and combining them with contemporary materials. Light-coloured roofs, for instance, reflect sunlight rather than absorbing it, and can cut the temperature of a building's interior by several degrees.

A more exotic technique exploits a phenomenon called radiative cooling. Certain specially engineered surfaces emit heat at wavelengths that pass straight through the atmosphere and escape into the cold of outer space. In effect, a roof painted with such a coating can become cooler than the surrounding air even under direct sun. Early field trials suggest the effect is real, though manufacturing the coatings cheaply at scale remains an unsolved problem.

Urban planners are also reconsidering the city itself. Trees, parks and water features create cooler microclimates, while reflective pavements reduce the heat that streets store and radiate at night. None of these measures alone can replace mechanical cooling during a severe heatwave, but together they can shrink the load placed on the grid. Critically, passive methods tend to keep working during power cuts, precisely the moments when vulnerable residents are most at risk. For policymakers, the appeal is twofold: lower emissions and greater resilience when the electricity supply falters.

questions.q: Air conditioning can worsen the warming it is intended to counteract.
Passive cooling strategies are entirely modern inventions.
Wind towers were used in the Persian Gulf region.
Radiative cooling coatings are currently cheap to manufacture at scale.
Light-coloured roofs are now legally required in most hot countries.
Radiative cooling works by:
options: emitting heat that escapes into space

questions.q: One key advantage of passive cooling during power cuts is that it:
Light-coloured roofs reflect sunlight and can cut interior temperature by several ________.

listening.title: Lecture: Heat Islands
listening.script: LECTURER: Right, today we're looking at the urban heat island effect. In simple terms, cities are noticeably warmer than the countryside around them, sometimes by as much as seven degrees at night. Why? Three main reasons. First, dark surfaces such as asphalt and tar roofs absorb sunlight during the day and release it slowly after dark. Second, cities have very little vegetation, so they lose the cooling that trees provide through evaporation. Third, waste heat pours out of vehicles, factories and, ironically, air conditioners. Now, this matters for health. Heatwaves are far more dangerous in dense urban cores, and the elderly are especially vulnerable. So what can we do? The cheapest single measure, according to a recent study, is simply planting more street trees, which can lower local temperatures by up to two degrees. Reflective paving helps too. For your assignment this week, I'd like you to measure the surface temperature of three different materials in direct sun and submit your readings by Friday.

questions.q: Cities can be warmer than the countryside by as much as ________ degrees at night.
Which of these is given as a reason cities are warmer?
Cities lose cooling because trees provide it through ________.
Who does the lecturer say is especially vulnerable?
Planting street trees can lower local temperatures by up to ________ degrees.
Students must submit their temperature readings by ________.

speaking.q: Describe a building you think is well designed for its climate. You should say: what the building is and where it is; what features help it cope with the local weather; how people use it; and explain why you think it is well designed.

writing.q: Some people believe that governments should focus their spending on preventing climate change, while others argue that money is better spent helping communities adapt to changes that are already happening. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: The Hidden Cost of Green Metals
reading.passage:
The transition to clean energy is often imagined as a clean process, yet it depends heavily on digging metals out of the ground. Electric cars, wind turbines and solar panels require large quantities of copper, lithium, cobalt and rare-earth elements. As demand for these materials accelerates, attention is shifting to the environmental and social toll of extracting them, a toll that the green narrative has tended to overlook.

Lithium illustrates the dilemma well. Much of the world's supply is pumped as brine from beneath arid salt flats in South America, where water is already painfully scarce. Evaporating the brine to concentrate the lithium consumes enormous volumes of that water, sometimes leaving local farmers and indigenous communities with too little for their crops and livestock. Cobalt, meanwhile, is concentrated in a single country, and a significant share is mined by hand in dangerous, poorly regulated conditions.

Recyclers argue that these pressures can be eased by recovering metals from old batteries and devices rather than mining fresh ore. In principle, a battery contains the same valuable elements at the end of its life as at the beginning, so a mature recycling industry could supply a growing fraction of future demand. The difficulty is that today's batteries were not designed to be taken apart, and dismantling them safely is laborious and expensive. Until recycling becomes routine, new mines will keep opening.

Some researchers are pursuing a different escape route: redesigning technologies so that they need less of the troublesome metals in the first place. Battery chemistries that rely on abundant sodium rather than lithium are already reaching the market, though they store less energy by weight. The broader lesson is that no source of energy is entirely without consequences. A genuinely sustainable system will require not only cleaner generation but also a frank reckoning with the materials it consumes and discards.

questions.q: The clean energy transition relies on the extraction of metals.
Evaporating lithium brine uses very little water.
Cobalt production is spread evenly across many countries.
A battery contains valuable elements at the end of its life.
Sodium batteries are now cheaper than lithium batteries to manufacture.
According to the passage, a major obstacle to battery recycling is that batteries:
options: were not designed to be taken apart

questions.q: Sodium-based batteries are described as:
Lithium is often pumped as ________ from beneath arid salt flats.

listening.title: Radio Feature: Recycling Old Batteries
listening.script:
HOST: Welcome back. Today we're talking battery recycling with engineer Dana Pruitt. Dana, why is this suddenly urgent?
DANA: Well, the first big wave of electric cars sold ten years ago is now reaching the scrapyard. Millions of batteries need somewhere to go.
HOST: And can we actually recover the metals?
DANA: We can. With modern methods we recover around ninety-five percent of the cobalt and nickel. Lithium recovery is harder, closer to fifty percent today, but it's improving fast.
HOST: What's holding the industry back?
DANA: Mostly collection. Right now only about one in five household batteries is returned. People just toss them in the trash. So our first goal is convenient drop-off points, ideally in every supermarket.
HOST: Is it profitable?
DANA: It's becoming so, especially as raw metal prices rise. A single recycling plant can process forty thousand tonnes a year and still turn a profit.
HOST: Fascinating. Where can listeners learn more?
DANA: Visit our site and search for the free guide called Closing the Loop.

reading.title: Reading the Wind: Forecasting for the Grid
reading.passage:
When a power grid drew almost all of its electricity from coal and gas, planning was relatively straightforward: operators decided how much fuel to burn and the output followed. The rise of wind and solar has upended that comfortable predictability. These sources are not dispatchable, meaning their output cannot simply be turned up on command; it depends on the weather. Consequently, the accuracy of weather forecasting has quietly become one of the most valuable assets in a modern energy system.

Forecasting wind generation is deceptively difficult. The power a turbine produces rises with the cube of wind speed, so a modest error in predicting the breeze translates into a large error in predicted electricity. A forecast that is wrong by even a few hours can be costly. If operators expect plentiful wind that fails to arrive, they must scramble to buy expensive backup power; if they underestimate it, turbines may have to be switched off and clean energy wasted.

To sharpen their predictions, grid operators now blend conventional meteorology with machine learning. Algorithms are trained on years of records linking atmospheric conditions to the actual output of specific wind farms, allowing them to capture local quirks that general weather models miss. Some systems also draw on data from sensors mounted on the turbines themselves, feeding real-time observations back into the forecast. The improvements have been striking, though forecasters are quick to stress that uncertainty can be reduced but never eliminated.

Better forecasts ripple outward through the whole system. They allow storage to be charged at the optimal moment, let traders price electricity more rationally, and reduce the amount of idling fossil capacity kept on standby. In this sense, an investment in forecasting can displace an investment in physical infrastructure. As renewables continue to expand, the ability to anticipate the weather a day or even a week ahead may prove as important as the turbines and panels themselves.

questions.q: Wind and solar output can be increased on command like coal plants.
The power produced by a turbine rises with the cube of wind speed.
Underestimating wind can lead to clean energy being wasted.
Machine learning models can capture local conditions that general models miss.
Forecasters claim uncertainty in wind prediction can be eliminated completely.
Solar forecasting has proven more accurate than wind forecasting in recent trials.
According to the passage, better forecasting can:
options: displace some investment in physical infrastructure

questions.q: Renewable sources are described as not ________, because their output cannot be turned up on command.

listening.title: Tour: A Wind Farm Visit
listening.script: GUIDE: Welcome to Hillcrest Wind Farm, everyone. Before we walk out, a few safety points. Please stay on the marked path and keep your hard hats on at all times. Now, some figures. This site has twenty-four turbines, each standing one hundred and twenty metres tall to the tip of the blade. Together they generate enough electricity for roughly forty thousand homes. People often ask about the noise. Standing here at the base, the sound is about forty-five decibels, no louder than a quiet office. Birds are another common concern; our latest survey recorded fewer collisions than expected, partly because we paint one blade black, which helps birds see them. The turbines start turning at wind speeds of around twelve kilometres an hour and shut down automatically in storms above ninety. If you'd like a souvenir, the visitor shop by the car park is open until five. Right, hard hats on, and follow me.

reading.title: Powering the Poor: Off-Grid Energy
reading.passage:
Roughly seven hundred million people still live without access to electricity, the great majority of them in rural Africa and parts of South Asia. For decades the assumed solution was to extend the national grid outward, wire by wire, until every village was connected. In practice, stringing cables across vast and sparsely populated regions proved ruinously expensive, and progress stalled. A different model has since emerged, one that bypasses the central grid altogether.

The enabling technologies are small solar panels paired with cheap, efficient lighting and appliances. A single panel on a roof, coupled with a modest battery, can power lamps, a fan and a mobile-phone charger for an entire household. Where one home cannot justify the cost, a slightly larger installation can serve a cluster of buildings through a so-called mini-grid, supplying a school, a clinic and a handful of small businesses from one shared source.

What made these systems spread was less the hardware than the payment model. Many households cannot afford the upfront price of a solar kit, yet they routinely spend money on kerosene and on charging phones at distant kiosks. Companies realised that customers could instead pay in tiny instalments by mobile money, unlocking the system day by day. When a payment is missed, the unit simply switches off until the account is topped up, which sharply reduces the lender's risk.

The consequences reach well beyond light after dark. Children study in the evening, clinics refrigerate vaccines, and shopkeepers stay open later. Critics caution that these small systems cannot power the heavy machinery on which industrial development ultimately depends, and that they should not become an excuse to abandon larger ambitions. Even so, for families who once measured their day by the setting of the sun, a few watts of reliable power can be genuinely transformative.

questions.q: Most people without electricity live in rural Africa and parts of South Asia.
Extending the national grid to remote villages turned out to be inexpensive.
A mini-grid can supply several buildings from one shared source.
The payment model allowed customers to pay in small instalments by mobile money.
Solar companies report higher profits than kerosene sellers in the same regions.
According to the passage, what happens when a customer misses a payment?
options: The unit switches off until topped up

questions.q: Critics warn that small solar systems:
Roughly ________ million people still live without access to electricity.

listening.title: Seminar: Mini-Grids in Practice
listening.script: SPEAKER: Thanks for coming. I want to share what we learned from installing mini-grids in twelve villages last year. First, the technical side worked well. Each mini-grid combined solar panels with a battery bank, and average uptime was ninety-eight percent, far better than the regional grid. But the real lessons were social. We found that demand grew much faster than predicted once people trusted the supply. Within six months, families bought refrigerators and even small welding tools, which we hadn't planned for. That meant some systems were undersized. My advice to future projects: build in spare capacity from the start. Second, pricing matters enormously. We initially charged a flat monthly fee, but switched to pay-as-you-go because seasonal incomes vary. Collection rates then rose to over ninety percent. Finally, train a local technician in every village. When something breaks, waiting a week for an engineer from the city erodes trust very quickly. Questions are welcome after the break.
