reading.title: Reading the Soil: The Rise of Geoarchaeology
reading.passage:
For much of the twentieth century, archaeology was preoccupied with objects: pottery, tools, ornaments and the human remains that lay beside them. The earth in which these things were buried was treated largely as an obstacle, something to be dug through as quickly as possible in order to reach the finds. Over the past few decades, however, a quieter revolution has changed that attitude. A field known as geoarchaeology now insists that the soil itself is one of the richest documents an excavation can offer.

The central idea is straightforward. Every layer of earth at a site records the conditions under which it formed. A band of fine, wind-blown silt may indicate a long period of abandonment, when no one disturbed the ground. A dark, greasy layer rich in charcoal and burnt bone points to repeated cooking or industrial activity. By examining the texture, chemistry and microscopic contents of sediments, geoarchaeologists can reconstruct events that left behind no artefacts at all.

One of the most powerful techniques is micromorphology, in which a block of undisturbed soil is hardened with resin, sliced into a wafer-thin section and studied under a microscope. At this scale, features invisible to the naked eye become legible: the trampled floor of a vanished house, the faint outline of a decayed wooden post, or the layered droppings that betray where animals were once penned. Such evidence can confirm how a building was used long after its walls have crumbled to dust.

Chemical analysis adds a further dimension. Elevated levels of phosphorus, for instance, often mark places where organic waste accumulated, while concentrations of certain metals can reveal workshops devoted to particular crafts. None of this replaces the study of artefacts; rather, it sets them in context, allowing researchers to ask not merely what people made, but how they actually lived from day to day. The most ordinary handful of dirt, it turns out, may speak more eloquently than the finest golden cup.

questions.q: In the twentieth century, soil at excavations was often regarded as something to be removed quickly.
Geoarchaeology can reconstruct events that produced no artefacts.
What does a dark layer rich in charcoal and burnt bone suggest?
In micromorphology, a block of soil is hardened with ________ before being sliced.
According to the passage, elevated phosphorus levels often indicate
options: places where organic waste built up
the outline of a wooden post

questions.q: Geoarchaeology has completely replaced the traditional study of artefacts.
Micromorphology was first developed in the field of medical research.
Concentrations of certain ________ can reveal workshops devoted to particular crafts.

listening.title: Enrolling on a Community Excavation
listening.script:
VOLUNTEER: Hello, I'd like to sign up for the community dig you're running this summer.

COORDINATOR: Wonderful. Let me take a few details. Can I have your full name?

VOLUNTEER: Yes, it's Daniel Marsh, M-A-R-S-H.

COORDINATOR: Thank you. And which session would you prefer? We run two: the morning group starts at nine, and the afternoon group at half past one.

VOLUNTEER: The afternoon one suits me better.

COORDINATOR: Lovely. The fee for the whole week is forty pounds, which covers your training and equipment. Do note that lunch isn't included, so bring your own.

VOLUNTEER: That's fine. Do I need any experience?

COORDINATOR: None at all. On the first day you'll have a safety briefing, and then you'll be shown how to use a trowel properly. The most important rule is to work slowly. We're excavating a Roman villa, so the floor mosaics are quite fragile.

VOLUNTEER: Sounds fascinating. Where exactly do we meet?

COORDINATOR: At the site entrance on Mill Lane. Wear sturdy boots and bring a hat, because there's very little shade.

VOLUNTEER: Brilliant. I'll see you on Monday.

reading.title: The Lost Script of the Indus
reading.passage:
Among the great civilisations of the ancient world, that of the Indus Valley remains the most enigmatic. Flourishing some four and a half thousand years ago across what is now Pakistan and north-western India, it built cities of remarkable order, with straight streets, standardised bricks and elaborate systems of drainage. Yet despite a century of excavation, scholars cannot read a single sentence its people wrote. The Indus script, preserved on thousands of small seals and tablets, has resisted every attempt at decipherment.

The obstacles are formidable. Most inscriptions are extremely short, averaging only five signs, and the longest known example contains fewer than thirty. With so little text, the statistical patterns that helped crack other ancient scripts are hard to detect. Worse still, no bilingual inscription has ever been found — no equivalent of the Rosetta Stone, which unlocked Egyptian hieroglyphs by pairing them with a known language.

Scholars even disagree about what kind of writing it is. Some argue that the symbols encode a spoken language, perhaps an early form of Dravidian. Others suggest that the seals carry no language at all, functioning instead as emblems of clan, trade or religious affiliation, rather like a modern logo. If the second view were correct, the dream of one day 'reading' the Indus would be an illusion, for there would be nothing linguistic to read.

Computers have recently entered the debate. By analysing how frequently signs occur and which tend to follow others, researchers have shown that the sequences are far from random and behave statistically much like genuine writing systems. This does not prove that the script records speech, but it weakens the claim that the symbols are mere decoration. For now, the seals keep their secret. The very qualities that make the Indus civilisation so admirable — its uniformity and its quiet efficiency — have left us tantalisingly little to go on.

questions.q: The Indus civilisation built cities with planned streets and drainage systems.
Why is the Indus script so difficult to decipher?
options: the inscriptions are very long and complex
the inscriptions are very short and no bilingual text exists
the language is known but the signs are missing

questions.q: Most inscriptions average only about ________ signs.
A bilingual inscription comparable to the Rosetta Stone has been discovered for the Indus script.
What do some scholars believe the seals might be, if they do not record language?
options: emblems of clan, trade or religion

questions.q: Computer analysis has proved beyond doubt that the script records spoken language.
The Indus people traded extensively with ancient Egypt.
Computer studies show that the sign sequences are far from ________.

listening.title: Tour Briefing at a Museum
listening.script:
GUIDE: Good afternoon everyone, and welcome to the National Museum. Before we begin, a few practical points. Our tour today lasts about ninety minutes, and we'll be visiting three galleries. Please keep your bags in front of you in the crowded rooms.

We'll start upstairs in the Bronze Age gallery, which holds our most famous object: a ceremonial helmet found in a riverbed in nineteen sixty-two. Photography is allowed, but please switch off your flash, as it can damage the delicate pigments on some pieces.

From there we'll move to the gallery of ancient writing. The highlight is a collection of clay tablets covered in tiny wedge-shaped marks. Don't worry if you can't read them; almost nobody can. Our final stop is the coin room, where you can even handle a few replica coins yourselves.

The gift shop is on the ground floor, next to the cafe, and it closes at five o'clock sharp. If anyone gets separated from the group, just make your way to the main desk in the entrance hall, and we'll find you. Right then, if you'd all follow me towards the staircase, we'll make a start.

Visitors are asked to switch off the flash on their cameras.
The clay tablets are covered in tiny ________ marks.
What can visitors do in the coin room?
The gift shop closes at ________ o'clock.

speaking.q:
Describe an old object or artefact you have seen, either in a museum or somewhere else. You should say:
- what the object was
- where and when you saw it
- what it looked like
and explain why it interested you.

writing.q: Some people believe that valuable historical artefacts should be returned to the countries where they were originally made, even if they have been displayed for many years in foreign museums. Others argue that such objects are safer and seen by more people in major international museums. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: Underwater Time Capsules
reading.passage:
When a ship sinks, it can become an unexpected gift to history. On land, the remains of past societies are constantly disturbed: fields are ploughed, buildings are demolished and rebuilt, and metal is melted down for reuse. The sea, by contrast, can act as a remarkably faithful preserver. A vessel that goes down in deep, cold, oxygen-poor water may settle on the seabed and remain almost untouched for centuries, its cargo frozen at the precise moment of disaster.

This is what makes shipwrecks so valuable to archaeologists. A settlement accumulates over generations, mixing objects from many different periods, but a wreck captures a single instant. Everything aboard was in use, or being traded, on the same day. From the pottery in the hold to the personal possessions of the crew, a wreck offers a snapshot of daily life and commerce that is almost impossible to obtain on land.

The conditions of the deep sea are central to this preservation. Where there is little oxygen, the bacteria that normally consume wood and other organic materials cannot survive. Timbers, ropes and even foodstuffs that would rot away within months on a beach can endure for thousands of years in the cold darkness. Some of the oldest wrecks ever studied still contained the remnants of their last cargo, sealed inside intact jars.

Recovering such evidence, however, is fraught with difficulty. Diving to great depths is dangerous and expensive, and objects that have been waterlogged for centuries can disintegrate rapidly once exposed to air. Conservators must treat fragile timbers for years before they can safely be displayed. There is also the persistent threat of looting, for a cargo of ancient coins or fine ceramics may be worth a fortune. Underwater archaeology, then, is a race not only against decay but also against those who would plunder the past for profit.

questions.q: Why can the sea preserve a wreck so well?
options: because seawater is rich in oxygen
because deep, cold, oxygen-poor water slows decay
because ships are made of stone
because the seabed is constantly ploughed

questions.q: A shipwreck captures objects from many different historical periods mixed together.
Low oxygen levels prevent the bacteria that consume wood from surviving.
Some of the oldest wrecks still contained remnants of cargo sealed inside intact ________.
What happens to waterlogged objects when exposed to air?
Conservators can usually display recovered timbers immediately after recovery.
Underwater archaeology is a race against decay and against those who ________ the past for profit.
Most underwater archaeologists prefer working on land sites because they are safer.

listening.title: Discussing a Research Proposal
listening.script:
TUTOR: So, Priya, you wanted to talk through your dissertation proposal on the harbour excavation.

STUDENT: Yes. I'd like to focus on the trade goods recovered from the wreck found off the south coast last year.

TUTOR: A good topic. What's your main research question?

STUDENT: Essentially, where the cargo came from. I think the amphorae were produced locally, but my supervisor suspects they were imported.

TUTOR: How will you test that?

STUDENT: Through clay analysis. By comparing the mineral composition of the pottery with known clay sources, I can identify the region of origin.

TUTOR: Sensible. Just be aware that the lab work takes time; book your slot at least three weeks in advance.

STUDENT: I will. I'm also planning to interview two of the divers who recovered the material.

TUTOR: Useful, but remember that personal recollection can be unreliable, so treat it as background, not hard evidence. When is your first draft due?

STUDENT: The fourteenth of March.

TUTOR: Right. Send me your reading list by the end of this week, and we'll meet again next Tuesday.

reading.title: The Diet Written in Bones
reading.passage:
For most of human history, the food people ate left no written record. Yet the human body itself keeps an unexpectedly detailed account of diet, locked away in the chemistry of bones and teeth. By analysing this chemical signature, modern researchers can reconstruct what individuals who died thousands of years ago habitually consumed, and in doing so they have overturned a number of long-held assumptions about ancient life.

The technique relies on stable isotopes — different forms of the same chemical element that vary slightly in weight. The proportions of certain carbon and nitrogen isotopes in bone collagen reflect the kinds of plants and animals a person ate over many years. Diets dominated by particular grasses, by seafood, or by the meat of grazing animals each leave a distinctive isotopic fingerprint. Because bone is constantly rebuilt during life, it records an average of the last decade or so before death, while tooth enamel, which forms in childhood and never changes, preserves a snapshot of a person's early years.

The results have sometimes been startling. At one prehistoric coastal site, archaeologists had assumed from the abundance of fish bones that the inhabitants lived chiefly on seafood. Isotopic analysis told a different story: the people had in fact relied heavily on farmed crops and livestock, and the fish, though plentiful, formed only a minor part of their diet. The discarded bones, it turned out, were a misleading guide to what was actually eaten.

Such findings illustrate a wider lesson. Objects that survive in the ground are not a neutral sample of the past; they are biased towards whatever happens to preserve well or be thrown away in noticeable quantities. By reading the chemistry of the people themselves, rather than the rubbish they left behind, researchers gain a more direct, and often more surprising, picture of ancient lives. The dead, it seems, can still tell us what they had for dinner.

questions.q: Bone chemistry can reveal what ancient individuals habitually ate.
What does the analysis rely on?
options: stable isotopes of carbon and nitrogen

questions.q: Bone records an average of roughly the last ________ before death.
What does tooth enamel preserve a record of?
At the coastal site, isotopic analysis confirmed that the people lived mainly on seafood.
The people at the coastal site relied heavily on farmed crops and ________.
The isotopic technique is now used routinely in modern criminal investigations.
The writer suggests that surviving objects give a biased picture of the past.

listening.title: Lecture: The Origins of Farming
listening.script:
LECTURER: Today I want to challenge a comfortable myth: the idea that the invention of farming was an obvious step forward that everyone eagerly embraced. The evidence suggests it was far messier than that.

First, consider health. Skeletons from early farming communities are often shorter and show more signs of disease than those of the hunter-gatherers who came before them. A diet based heavily on a single crop, such as wheat, can lack important nutrients. So in narrow terms of physical wellbeing, early farmers may actually have been worse off.

Why, then, did farming spread? One answer is simply numbers. Although individual farmers may have been less healthy, farming produced more food per acre, so populations grew faster. Over many generations, sheer population pressure allowed farming communities to expand and displace their neighbours.

There's also the question of storage. Grain can be stored for long periods, which means surplus can be accumulated. That surplus made possible something new: a class of people who didn't farm at all, such as priests, soldiers and rulers. In other words, farming didn't just change what people ate; it reshaped the entire structure of society. We'll explore that transformation in detail next week.

questions.q: What myth does the lecturer challenge?
options: that farming was an obvious step forward everyone welcomed

questions.q: Skeletons from early farming communities often show more signs of disease than earlier hunter-gatherers.
A diet based heavily on a single ________ can lack important nutrients.
Why did farming spread despite making individuals less healthy?
options: it produced more food per acre, so populations grew

questions.q: Because grain can be stored, communities could accumulate a ________.
The lecturer says farming reshaped the structure of society.

speaking.q: Let's talk about food and meals. What kinds of food do you usually eat in a typical week? Do you prefer cooking at home or eating out? Has the food people eat in your country changed since you were a child?

writing.q: Some people think that studying the distant past, such as ancient diets and early civilisations, is of little practical value in the modern world and that resources would be better spent on present-day problems. To what extent do you agree or disagree? Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Researchers learn what people habitually ate.
The findings overturned old assumptions.
The method relies on stable isotopes.
The proportions of isotopes reflect diet.
Each diet leaves a distinctive fingerprint.
The results were sometimes startling.
The abundance of fish bones was misleading.
The discarded bones misled archaeologists.
The bones were a misleading guide.
Surviving objects are not a neutral sample.
The record is biased towards durable objects.
They reconstruct ancient diets.
Farmers came to displace their neighbours.

reading.title: Dating the Deep Past
reading.passage:
Few innovations have transformed archaeology as profoundly as the development of reliable dating methods. Before the middle of the twentieth century, archaeologists could usually place finds only in a relative sequence, knowing that one layer was older than another but unable to attach firm calendar dates to either. The arrival of techniques that could assign an absolute age to organic remains changed the discipline at a stroke, allowing distant cultures to be compared on a single, common timescale.

The most celebrated of these methods is radiocarbon dating. All living things absorb a steady proportion of a radioactive form of carbon while they are alive. When an organism dies, it stops taking in this carbon, and the radioactive form begins to decay at a known and constant rate. By measuring how much remains in a sample of bone, wood or charcoal, scientists can estimate how long ago the organism died. The method is remarkably powerful, but it has limits: it works only on materials that were once alive, and it loses accuracy beyond about fifty thousand years, by which point too little of the radioactive carbon survives to measure.

For older or non-organic materials, other techniques are required. One ingenious method measures the faint light released by certain minerals when they are heated, a glow that reflects how long they have been buried in darkness since they were last exposed to sunlight or fire. This allows archaeologists to date ancient pottery, or even the sediment surrounding a tool, where no organic material survives at all.

No single method is sufficient on its own. Each has its own range, its own materials and its own sources of error, and a careless application can produce dates that are confidently wrong. The most convincing chronologies are therefore built by combining several independent methods, cross-checking one against another. When techniques that rely on entirely different physical principles converge on the same answer, archaeologists can at last speak about the deep past with genuine confidence.

questions.q: Before reliable dating methods, archaeologists could usually only
options: place finds in a relative sequence
ignore the age of finds entirely
date objects to the nearest day

questions.q: Living things absorb a steady proportion of radioactive carbon while alive.
Radiocarbon dating loses accuracy beyond about ________ thousand years.
Radiocarbon dating can be used on materials that were never alive.
The method involving light released from heated minerals can date
options: pottery and surrounding sediment with no organic material
only objects under fifty years old

questions.q: The most convincing chronologies combine several ________ methods.
Radiocarbon dating was invented by a single named scientist working alone.
The writer says a careless application of dating can produce confident but wrong results.

listening.title: Lecture: How Tree Rings Tell Time
listening.script:
LECTURER: In this final session we'll look at one of the most elegant dating methods available to archaeologists: dendrochronology, or the study of tree rings.

The principle is beautifully simple. Each year, a tree adds a new ring of growth. In a good year, with plenty of rain and sunshine, the ring is wide; in a poor year, it's narrow. Because every tree in a region experiences the same sequence of good and bad years, they all share the same distinctive pattern of wide and narrow rings.

Now, here's the clever part. By matching the rings in a piece of ancient timber, perhaps a beam from an old building, with the rings in older and older samples, researchers have built up an unbroken record stretching back thousands of years. This lets them date a wooden object not just to a century, but to the exact year it was felled.

There is one important limitation. The method works best in regions with clear seasons. In the tropics, where growth is steady all year round, the rings are far less distinct, so the technique is much harder to apply. Even so, where it can be used, dendrochronology offers a precision that almost no other method can match. Right, let's turn to your assignments.

questions.q: The study of tree rings is called ________.
What does a wide tree ring indicate?
options: a poor year with little rain
a good year with plenty of rain and sunshine

questions.q: Trees in the same region share the same pattern of wide and narrow rings.
What can the method date a wooden object to?
options: the exact year it was felled

questions.q: The method works best in regions with clear ________.
The technique is easy to apply in tropical regions.

speaking.q:
Describe a time when you learned something interesting about the past. You should say:
- what you learned
- how you came to learn it
- who or what helped you
and explain why it stayed in your memory.

writing.q:

Samples processed (thousands)
Laboratory | 2019 | 2020 | 2021 | 2022
Lab A | 3.2 | 3.5 | 4.1 | 4.8
Lab B | 1.0 | 1.4 | 2.6 | 3.9
Lab C | 5.5 | 5.2 | 5.0 | 4.7

vocab.example: Dating methods transformed archaeology profoundly.
Finds were placed in a relative sequence.
The method assigns an absolute age.
The radioactive carbon begins to decay.
Scientists estimate how long ago it died.
One ingenious method measures faint light.
They can date the surrounding sediment.
No single method is sufficient on its own.
Convincing chronologies combine many methods.
Different methods converge on the same answer.
Radiocarbon dating is the most celebrated method.
Tropical tree rings are less distinct.
Dendrochronology is an elegant method.

reading.title: The Rise of the Conductor
reading.passage:
For much of musical history, the orchestra had no single figure standing at its head, baton in hand. In the Baroque era, ensembles were typically directed from within, often by the harpsichordist or the leader of the first violins, who would signal entrances with a nod or a sweep of the bow. The notion of a separate, non-playing director who shaped the entire performance through gesture alone emerged only gradually during the nineteenth century.

Several factors drove this transformation. As orchestras swelled in size and compositions grew more rhythmically intricate, coordinating dozens of players from the keyboard became impractical. Composers such as Berlioz and Wagner wrote scores of unprecedented complexity, demanding precise control over tempo, balance and dynamics. A dedicated conductor, facing the players and free of an instrument, could communicate these subtleties far more effectively. Wagner himself argued passionately that interpretation, not mere time-keeping, was the conductor's true responsibility.

The modern conductor also became a public personality. Figures like Hans von Bulow and, later, Arturo Toscanini cultivated reputations for fierce discipline and exacting standards, transforming the podium into a position of considerable authority. Audiences came to associate particular orchestras with the temperament of their leaders. Yet this authority was not universally welcomed; some musicians resented the concentration of artistic power in a single pair of hands, and debates about the conductor's necessity have never entirely disappeared.

Today the role continues to evolve. A handful of ensembles deliberately perform without a conductor, relying on close communication among players, while others have embraced conductors who see themselves less as autocrats than as collaborators. What remains constant is the recognition that a large body of musicians, however skilled, benefits from some shared sense of direction.

questions.q: In the Baroque period, orchestras were sometimes led by a violinist.
Wagner believed the conductor's main job was simply to keep time.
Toscanini conducted orchestras on several different continents.
According to the passage, why did the dedicated conductor emerge in the nineteenth century?
options: Orchestras grew larger and music more complex

questions.q: What does the passage say about the conductor's authority?
options: It was accepted by all musicians
It was resisted by some players
It was limited to small ensembles

questions.q: Some modern ensembles deliberately perform without a ________ altogether.
Composers such as Berlioz and Wagner wrote scores of ________ complexity.
Debates about whether a conductor is necessary still continue today.

listening.title: Joining a Community Choir
listening.script:
REGISTRAR: Good evening, you've reached the Riverside Community Choir. How can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to join. Could you tell me when you rehearse?

REGISTRAR: Of course. We meet every Thursday from seven until nine in the evening, at the Methodist Hall on Carlton Street.

CALLER: Lovely. Do I need any singing experience?

REGISTRAR: Not at all. We welcome complete beginners. We do ask new members to come fifteen minutes early on their first night to fill in a form.

CALLER: And is there a fee?

REGISTRAR: It's forty pounds for the whole term, which covers your sheet music. Students pay a reduced rate of twenty-five pounds.

CALLER: Great. What kind of music do you sing?

REGISTRAR: Mainly choral works, but this term we're focusing on folk songs for our spring concert in May.

CALLER: Perfect. My name's Daniel Pearce, that's P-E-A-R-C-E.

REGISTRAR: Thank you, Daniel. I'll add you to the list and see you Thursday.

reading.title: Why Songs Get Stuck in Our Heads
reading.passage:
Almost everyone has experienced the maddening phenomenon of a tune that refuses to leave the mind. Researchers call these involuntary musical images 'earworms', and they are among the most common forms of spontaneous mental activity. Surveys suggest that the great majority of people experience them regularly, and that the episodes can last anywhere from a few minutes to several hours.

What makes a song especially prone to becoming an earworm? Studies point to a combination of features. Such tunes tend to have a fast, upbeat tempo and a melodic shape that is simple enough to remember yet contains some unexpected interval or rhythm that catches the brain off guard. Crucially, repetition plays a central role. A catchy chorus heard repeatedly on the radio is far more likely to lodge itself in memory than a piece encountered only once.

The circumstances in which an earworm appears are also revealing. People often report that a song surfaces when their attention is loosely occupied, during routine tasks such as walking, showering or washing dishes. In such states the mind wanders freely, and fragments of recently heard music can rise unbidden into awareness. Stress and tiredness appear to increase the frequency of these episodes.

Far from being merely irritating, earworms may serve a purpose. Some psychologists argue that they reflect the brain's tendency to rehearse and consolidate musical memories. Others note that an unfinished melody creates a kind of cognitive itch; the mind keeps returning to it in an attempt to complete the pattern. Whatever their function, the most reliable way to dislodge a stubborn tune seems to be to engage in a moderately demanding activity, such as solving a puzzle, which occupies the very mental resources the earworm exploits.

questions.q: Most people experience earworms on a regular basis.
Earworms are more likely when a song has been heard many times.
Classical music produces earworms more often than pop music.
When do earworms most commonly appear, according to the passage?
options: When the mind is loosely occupied

questions.q: What is suggested as an effective way to get rid of an earworm?
Researchers refer to these involuntary tunes as ________.
An unfinished melody is described as creating a cognitive ________.
Stress and tiredness reduce how often earworms occur.

listening.title: Booking a Recording Studio
listening.script:
STAFF: Soundwave Studios, this is Megan speaking.

CLIENT: Hi Megan, I'd like to book a session to record some demos with my band.

STAFF: No worries. How many of you are there, and how long do you think you'll need?

CLIENT: There are four of us, and we're hoping for a full day, maybe six hours.

STAFF: We have two rooms. Studio A is bigger and suits full bands; it's eighty dollars an hour. Studio B is smaller, sixty an hour.

CLIENT: We'll take Studio A. Does that include an engineer?

STAFF: Yes, an engineer is included, but mixing afterwards is charged separately at fifty dollars per track.

CLIENT: Right. Could we book for Saturday the twelfth?

STAFF: Let me check. Saturday's fully booked, I'm afraid, but Sunday the thirteenth is free.

CLIENT: Sunday works. We'll start at ten in the morning.

STAFF: Brilliant. Can I take a name for the booking?

CLIENT: It's under Lucas Reid, R-E-I-D.

reading.title: The Lost Art of Whistled Languages
reading.passage:
In a handful of remote communities scattered across the globe, conversation can travel for kilometres without a single word being spoken aloud. Instead, speakers communicate through elaborate systems of whistling, encoding the sounds of their native tongue into piercing tones that carry far across valleys and ravines. The best known of these is Silbo Gomero, practised on the Canary island of La Gomera, though similar traditions exist in Turkey, Mexico and parts of West Africa.

These whistled languages are not separate codes invented from scratch. Rather, they are spoken languages transposed into whistle, preserving the rhythm, stress and tonal contours of ordinary speech. A skilled whistler effectively replaces vowels and consonants with variations in pitch and length, allowing fluent speakers to understand complex messages. On La Gomera, shepherds once used Silbo to relay news, summon help or announce the price of goods across the island's steep terrain, where shouting would have been useless.

The practical advantages are considerable. Sound travels through whistling far more efficiently than through ordinary speech, and a clear whistle can be understood at distances of up to several kilometres in favourable conditions. In mountainous landscapes lacking roads or telephones, this offered an ingenious solution to the problem of communication.

Yet most whistled languages have declined sharply, undermined by modern roads, mobile phones and the migration of young people to cities. Recognising the danger, authorities on La Gomera took the unusual step of making Silbo a compulsory subject in local primary schools, and in 2009 the practice was added to a list of safeguarded cultural traditions. Whether such measures can sustain a living language, rather than merely preserving it as a curiosity for visitors, remains an open question that linguists continue to debate.

questions.q: Whistled languages are entirely new codes unrelated to spoken languages.
Silbo Gomero is practised on an island in the Canaries.
Silbo is now also taught in schools outside La Gomera.
Why was whistling useful to shepherds on La Gomera?
options: It carried across steep terrain where shouting failed
It could not be overheard by strangers

questions.q: What has contributed to the decline of whistled languages?
options: The arrival of roads and mobile phones

questions.q: In favourable conditions a whistle can be understood up to several ________ away.
Silbo was added to a list of safeguarded cultural traditions in ________.
Linguists still disagree about whether these efforts can keep the language alive.

listening.title: A Lecture on Festival Logistics
listening.script:
LECTURER: Today I want to look at what it takes to stage a large outdoor music festival. People often imagine the bands are the hardest part to organise, but in fact the real challenge lies in logistics.

Consider the site itself. A typical major festival hosts around forty thousand people, and that crowd needs water, food, sanitation and somewhere safe to sleep. Planning usually begins a full eighteen months before the gates open.

Transport is the first headache. Organisers must coordinate with local councils to manage traffic, and many festivals now run dedicated shuttle buses from the nearest railway station to reduce congestion on country roads.

Then there is power. A festival of this scale can consume as much electricity as a small town, and increasingly organisers are turning to solar panels and biofuel generators to cut their carbon footprint.

Finally, safety. Each site requires a detailed medical plan, with a field hospital on standby and trained stewards positioned throughout the grounds. It is this invisible infrastructure, not the headline act, that ultimately determines whether a festival succeeds.

questions.q: What does the lecturer say is the real challenge of staging a festival?
A typical major festival hosts around ________ thousand people.
Planning usually begins ________ months before the gates open.
Many festivals run dedicated shuttle ________ from the nearest railway station.
Organisers increasingly use solar panels and biofuel ________ to cut emissions.
What does the lecturer say ultimately determines a festival's success?

speaking.q: Let's discuss music and society. Why do you think live music remains popular even though recordings are so easily available? In what ways can music help to bring a community together?

writing.q:

Format | 2005 | 2020
CDs | 62% | 9%
Downloads | 8% | 18%
Streaming | 0% | 65%
Vinyl | 30% | 8%

vocab.example: They use an elaborate system of whistles.
Whistlers encode speech into pitch.
A piercing whistle cut through the valley.
The sound carried across the deep ravine.
The language is transposed into whistle.

reading.title: The Economics of the Modern Musician
reading.passage:
The way musicians earn a living has been transformed almost beyond recognition over the past two decades. For much of the twentieth century, recorded music was the primary source of income for successful artists. Selling physical copies of albums generated substantial royalties, while live performances served largely as promotion for the latest release. The arrival of digital streaming has comprehensively reversed this relationship.

Streaming platforms now account for the overwhelming majority of recorded music consumption, yet the revenue they return to artists is notoriously meagre. A single play typically earns a tiny fraction of a penny, meaning that even a track streamed millions of times may yield only modest returns once record labels and other intermediaries have taken their share. For all but the most popular artists, recorded music has ceased to be a reliable livelihood.

As a consequence, the economic centre of gravity has shifted decisively towards live performance. Touring, once a promotional expense, has become the principal way many musicians make money, supplemented by merchandise sales and, increasingly, direct support from fans through subscription platforms. Some commentators argue that this represents a return to an older model, in which musicians were valued chiefly for their ability to perform in person.

This shift has not affected everyone equally. Established acts capable of filling large arenas can command enormous fees, while emerging artists struggle to cover the rising costs of touring. Critics warn that the new economy may inadvertently favour spectacle over substance, rewarding those who can stage elaborate shows rather than those who simply write fine music. Whether digital platforms will eventually develop fairer ways of compensating creators remains one of the most contested questions in the contemporary music industry.

questions.q: In the twentieth century, live shows mainly promoted album sales.
Streaming pays artists generously for each play.
Most musicians now earn more from merchandise than from touring.
What has become the main source of income for many musicians today?
What concern do critics raise about the new music economy?
options: It may reward spectacle over musical quality

questions.q: A single stream earns only a tiny fraction of a ________.
The economic centre of ________ has shifted towards live performance.
Established arena acts can charge very high fees.

listening.title: Discussing a Music Group Project
listening.script:
TUTOR: So, Priya, Marcus, how's the group presentation on the history of jazz coming along?

PRIYA: Pretty well. We've split it into three sections. I'm covering the early New Orleans period.

MARCUS: And I'm taking the swing era and the big bands of the nineteen thirties.

TUTOR: Good. Who's handling the more modern material?

PRIYA: That's Sophie, but she's worried she has too much to cover, from bebop right through to fusion.

TUTOR: I'd suggest she focuses on bebop and leaves fusion as a brief mention. How long is the presentation meant to be?

MARCUS: Twenty minutes, plus five for questions.

TUTOR: That's tight. Make sure you rehearse the timing. Are you using audio examples?

PRIYA: Yes, we've chosen four short clips, about thirty seconds each.

TUTOR: Perfect. Just check the speakers in room twelve beforehand. They've been unreliable lately. And remember to submit your slides by Friday.

questions.q: Priya is covering the early ________ period of jazz.
Marcus is taking the swing era and the big bands of the nineteen ________.
What does the tutor advise Sophie to do?
options: Focus on bebop and mention fusion briefly

reading.title: Music Therapy and the Brain
reading.passage:
The idea that music can heal is ancient, but only recently has science begun to explain how. Music therapy, now a recognised clinical discipline, uses musical activities to address physical, emotional and cognitive needs. Its practitioners work in settings ranging from hospitals and care homes to special schools, and a growing body of research suggests that its benefits are more than merely soothing.

One of the most striking findings concerns memory. Patients with advanced dementia, who may struggle to recognise close relatives, often retain the ability to recall songs from their youth in remarkable detail. Hearing familiar music can momentarily reconnect them with their past, sometimes prompting speech and emotional responses where other interventions have failed. Researchers believe this is because musical memories are stored across widely distributed brain regions, making them unusually resistant to the damage that erodes other forms of memory.

Music also appears to influence the body in measurable ways. Listening to slow, rhythmic pieces can lower heart rate and blood pressure, while in rehabilitation, rhythm is harnessed to help patients relearn movement. Stroke survivors, for instance, may regain the ability to walk more smoothly when they synchronise their steps to a steady beat, a technique known as rhythmic auditory stimulation.

Despite these encouraging results, music therapy faces obstacles. Rigorous trials are difficult to design, since the experience of music is highly personal and resistant to standardisation. Funding remains limited, and the discipline is sometimes dismissed as a pleasant extra rather than a serious treatment. Nevertheless, as understanding of the brain deepens, music therapy is steadily moving from the margins towards the mainstream of healthcare, supported by evidence that what delights the ear may also mend the mind.

questions.q: Music therapy is practised only in hospitals.
Dementia patients often remember songs from their youth even when they forget relatives.
Music therapy is now the most widely funded treatment in hospitals.
Why are musical memories thought to survive dementia so well?
options: They are stored in a single brain region
They are stored across many brain regions
They are formed later in life

questions.q: What is 'rhythmic auditory stimulation' used to help with?
Listening to slow, rhythmic music can lower heart rate and blood ________.
Rigorous ________ are hard to design because music is so personal.
Music therapy is sometimes dismissed as a pleasant extra rather than serious treatment.

listening.title: Radio Feature on a Piano Restorer
listening.script:
PRESENTER: Welcome back. My guest today is Harold Finch, who has spent over forty years restoring antique pianos. Harold, what first drew you to this craft?

HAROLD: Honestly, it was an accident. I trained as a furniture maker, but a neighbour asked me to repair an old upright, and I was hooked.

PRESENTER: How long does a full restoration take?

HAROLD: It varies enormously. A simple job might take three weeks, but a complete rebuild of a grand piano can take up to six months.

PRESENTER: And what's the hardest part?

HAROLD: The soundboard, without a doubt. It's the heart of the instrument, and replacing it requires real patience.

PRESENTER: Where do you source your materials?

HAROLD: The felt for the hammers comes from Germany, and I get my wire from a small supplier in Yorkshire.

PRESENTER: Finally, are you training anyone to continue the work?

HAROLD: Yes, my apprentice Tom has been with me for two years now. He's coming along beautifully.

Activity | Before | After

reading.title: The Quiet Revolution of the Cycle Lane
reading.passage:
When the city of Veldhaven first proposed a network of segregated cycle lanes in 1998, the plan met fierce resistance from local retailers, who feared that removing on-street parking would drive customers away. Two decades later, the same merchants are among the scheme's most vocal advocates. Surveys conducted in 2019 found that shops along the converted corridors reported, on average, a fourteen per cent rise in turnover, a figure that planners attribute to the steady stream of passing cyclists who stop more frequently than motorists.

The transformation did not happen overnight. Engineers discovered that simply painting lines on the road was insufficient; cyclists felt exposed alongside fast-moving traffic and largely ignored the markings. Only when physical barriers, raised kerbs and dedicated traffic signals were installed did usage climb sharply. By 2015, roughly a third of all trips within the inner ring were made by bicycle, a proportion that placed Veldhaven among the leading cycling cities of its region, though still behind the long-established Dutch benchmarks.

Critics caution that the model is not universally transferable. Veldhaven is compact and relatively flat, conditions that flatter any cycling initiative. In hillier or more sprawling settlements, the same investment might yield far smaller gains. Moreover, the scheme depended on sustained political will across several administrations, a luxury that many cities lack. Nevertheless, the underlying lesson appears robust: infrastructure shapes behaviour. People do not abandon their cars because they are lectured about emissions; they do so when an alternative becomes genuinely safer and more convenient than the journey they were making before.

questions.q: Local shopkeepers initially opposed the cycle lane proposal.
Cyclists readily used the lanes as soon as road markings were painted.
Veldhaven has overtaken Dutch cities in the proportion of cycling trips.
The 2019 survey recorded a decline in motor traffic on the converted corridors.
According to the passage, why did retailers eventually support the scheme?
What does the writer suggest is the main limitation of the Veldhaven model?
options: It is too expensive to maintain
It cannot be copied everywhere without adjustment

questions.q: Usage rose sharply only after physical barriers, raised kerbs and dedicated traffic ________ were added.
By 2015, around a ________ of inner-ring trips were made by bicycle.

listening.title: Joining the City Bike-Share Scheme

STAFF: Good morning, welcome to the CityRide bike-share desk. Are you here to register?
CUSTOMER: Yes, I'd like to sign up for a monthly membership, please.
STAFF: Of course. Can I take your full name?
CUSTOMER: It's Daniel Foster. That's F-O-S-T-E-R.
STAFF: Lovely. And a contact number?
CUSTOMER: It's 0-7-double-4-1, 6-2-9-0-3-8.
STAFF: Thank you. The standard monthly pass is fifteen pounds, which gives you unlimited rides up to ninety minutes each. Anything longer adds a small surcharge.
CUSTOMER: That's fine. Where can I pick the bikes up?
STAFF: There are docking stations across the centre. The nearest one to here is on Maple Street, just behind the library.
CUSTOMER: Great. And do I need a code to unlock them?
STAFF: You'll use the app, but if your phone runs out of battery, you can enter your four-digit PIN on the keypad instead.
CUSTOMER: Perfect. One last thing, is there a deposit?
STAFF: No deposit, but you should report any fault within twenty-four hours to avoid a charge.

reading.title: Rethinking the Multi-Storey Car Park
reading.passage:
For much of the twentieth century, the multi-storey car park was treated as an unavoidable feature of the modern city centre, a concrete monument to the assumption that prosperity and private motoring went hand in hand. Today, as car ownership stalls in many wealthy nations and online shopping erodes the foot traffic that once justified these structures, urban authorities are confronting an awkward question: what should be done with a building designed for a purpose that may be fading?

Demolition is rarely the obvious answer. The structures are expensive to tear down, and their robust frames, built to bear the weight of thousands of vehicles, lend themselves to reinvention. In the British city of Norchester, a disused car park has been converted into a mix of small workshops, a rooftop garden and an indoor sports hall, while retaining a single floor for parking. Architects argue that such adaptive reuse is far greener than starting afresh, since the carbon already embedded in the existing concrete is preserved rather than wasted.

Not every scheme has succeeded. Some conversions have struggled to attract tenants, partly because the deep floor plates and low ceilings typical of these buildings are ill-suited to housing. Daylight is scarce in the interior, and ventilation can be poor. Engineers point out that the very features that made the structures efficient for cars, gentle ramps and open sides, become obstacles when the goal is a comfortable place to live or work.

Still, the direction of travel seems clear. As planners increasingly question whether city centres should privilege the storage of stationary vehicles, the multi-storey car park is being quietly recast. Whether it becomes a vertical farm, a cultural venue or simply a smaller car park with room for something else, its dominance over the urban skyline appears to be drawing to a close.

questions.q: Multi-storey car parks were once seen as a normal part of city centres.
Demolishing these car parks is usually the cheapest option.
All conversion projects have found tenants easily.
The Norchester car park was the first in Britain to be converted.
Why do architects favour adaptive reuse over demolition?
options: It is faster to obtain permits
It preserves carbon already embedded in the concrete
It is required by national law

questions.q: What makes these buildings difficult to convert into housing?
options: Their deep floor plates and low ceilings

questions.q: The frames were built strong enough to bear the weight of thousands of ________.
At Norchester, a single ________ was kept for parking.

listening.title: Library Talk: A Heritage Walking Tour
listening.script: GUIDE: Hello everyone, and thanks for joining today's heritage walking tour of the old transport district. We'll begin here at the former tram depot, which opened back in 1908 and once housed over forty trams. The building you see behind me is now a museum, and entry is free on weekdays. From here, we'll walk down Canal Road to the original railway goods yard. It's about a ten-minute stroll, so do keep together. Along the way, please watch the kerbs, as some of the old cobblestones are uneven. At the goods yard, you'll see the restored signal box, which volunteers reopened last spring. The tour lasts roughly two hours in total, and we'll finish at the harbour, where there's a small cafe if you'd like refreshments afterwards. One quick reminder: photography is welcome everywhere except inside the signal box, where flash can damage the old paperwork on display. Right, if everyone's ready, let's set off.

reading.title: The Promise and Peril of On-Demand Transit
reading.passage:
Conventional public transport runs to a fixed timetable along a fixed route, an arrangement that works efficiently where demand is dense but falters in thinly populated suburbs, where half-empty buses trundle past empty stops. In response, a number of cities have begun experimenting with on-demand transit, a hybrid model in which small vehicles are summoned through a smartphone application and routed dynamically to gather several passengers travelling in roughly the same direction.

The appeal is intuitive. Passengers enjoy something close to the door-to-door convenience of a taxi at a fraction of the cost, while operators avoid running large vehicles along sparsely used corridors. Early pilots in the suburbs of Adelaide reported that ridership among residents who had previously never used public transport rose noticeably, and that some households felt able to manage with one car instead of two. Algorithms, refined over successive trials, grew steadily better at predicting where demand would arise and at minimising the detours that frustrate passengers.

Yet the economics remain stubbornly difficult. Because each vehicle carries fewer people than a full bus, the cost per passenger can be high, and several schemes have survived only with generous public subsidy. Critics warn that, without such support, fares would rise to a level that defeats the social purpose of the service, namely to reach those who cannot easily drive. There is also the awkward possibility that on-demand transit, if priced too cheaply, simply lures people away from walking and cycling rather than from their cars.

For now, most planners regard the technology as a complement to traditional networks rather than a replacement. It may prove most valuable not in the city centre, where buses and trains already perform well, but at the margins, ferrying passengers from their homes to the nearest station, where the fixed network takes over. Whether it can ever pay its own way, however, is a question that remains firmly open.

questions.q: Fixed-route public transport works well in densely populated areas.
On-demand vehicles follow a set timetable and route.
Some Adelaide households reduced the number of cars they owned.
On-demand transit has been adopted as standard across all of Australia.
What is identified as the main economic problem with on-demand transit?
options: The cost per passenger can be high

questions.q: According to critics, what risk arises if fares are set too low?
options: People may stop walking or cycling instead
Operators will run out of vehicles

questions.q: Most planners see the technology as a ________ to traditional networks rather than a replacement.
The service may be most useful at the ________, linking homes to the nearest station.

listening.title: Student Seminar: Designing a Bus Survey
listening.script:
TUTOR: Right, so for your group project you're surveying bus users about the new on-demand service. Let's plan the questions. Mia, where would you start?
MIA: I think we should first ask how often people travel, maybe daily, weekly, or rarely.
TUTOR: Good, frequency is a solid opener. What next?
MIA: Then their main reason for the trip, like work, study, or shopping.
TUTOR: Sensible. Liam, anything to add?
LIAM: We could ask how long they typically wait for a vehicle. The app claims an average of eight minutes.
TUTOR: Worth checking against reality. Just be careful not to lead them. Ask the wait time before you mention the eight-minute figure.
LIAM: Right, otherwise we bias the answer.
TUTOR: Exactly. And how will you collect responses?
MIA: We thought a paper form at the depot, plus an online version for people who'd rather reply later.
TUTOR: Good, two channels widens your sample. Aim for at least sixty completed forms by Friday so we have enough data to analyse.

reading.title: The Fifteen-Minute City and Its Discontents
reading.passage:
The idea is seductively simple. In a so-called fifteen-minute city, every resident should be able to reach the essentials of daily life, work, schooling, healthcare, shops and green space, within a short walk or cycle from home. Proponents argue that such proximity reduces dependence on cars, cuts emissions, and revives the kind of local community that decades of car-centred planning have hollowed out. Since the concept was popularised in Paris in the late 2010s, planners from Melbourne to Bogota have adopted its vocabulary.

The principle is hardly new. Before the rise of mass motoring, most settlements were necessarily compact, organised around the distance a person could comfortably walk. What is new is the deliberate attempt to engineer such proximity back into cities that have spent a century sprawling outward. This requires more than fine words; it demands genuine investment in local clinics, schools and shops, distributed evenly rather than concentrated in a single downtown core.

Here the difficulties begin. Critics on the political right have occasionally portrayed the concept as a sinister plot to confine citizens to their neighbourhoods, a misreading that planners find exasperating, since nothing prevents residents from travelling further. More serious objections come from those who note that desirable amenities tend to cluster in wealthier districts. Without careful policy, a city of fifteen-minute neighbourhoods could simply harden existing inequalities, granting the affluent a short stroll to a delicatessen while the poor still face long journeys to a distant supermarket.

The most thoughtful advocates accept this risk and argue that the model must be paired with deliberate efforts to spread services equitably. Proximity, they insist, is a means rather than an end. A neighbourhood that is walkable but offers nothing worth walking to has missed the point entirely. The measure of success, ultimately, is not how close the amenities sit on a map, but whether they meet the genuine needs of the people living around them.

questions.q: The fifteen-minute city concept was first popularised in Paris.
The idea of compact, walkable settlements is entirely new.
Planners agree with the claim that the concept aims to trap people in their neighbourhoods.
Paris has fully achieved the fifteen-minute city across all its districts.
What do thoughtful advocates say proximity should be regarded as?
options: A means rather than an end

questions.q: What serious risk do critics identify with the model?
Achieving proximity demands genuine investment in local clinics, schools and ________.
The true measure of success is whether amenities meet the genuine ________ of residents.

listening.title: Lecture: Measuring Urban Walkability
listening.script: LECTURER: Today I want to look at how researchers actually measure walkability, because it's more complex than it sounds. The first factor most studies use is density, that is, how many homes and jobs sit within a given area. Higher density usually means more destinations within reach. The second factor is connectivity, which describes how well the street network links places together. A grid of small blocks scores well; a maze of cul-de-sacs scores poorly. Third, we consider the mix of land uses. A street with homes, cafes and offices side by side is more walkable than one with housing alone. Now, here's a common mistake. People assume that adding sidewalks automatically creates a walkable area. It helps, of course, but a wide sidewalk leading to nowhere useful changes very little. The destination matters as much as the path. In our next session, we'll examine how cities like Portland have combined all three factors, and I'll ask you to score your own neighborhood using a simple checklist I'll hand out at the end.

questions.q: What is the first factor researchers use to measure walkability?
________ describes how well the street network links places together.
Which type of street layout scores well for connectivity?
The third factor is the mix of ________ uses.
What common mistake does the lecturer mention?
The example city the lecturer will examine next time is ________.

speaking.q: Let's talk about your neighbourhood. What can you reach easily on foot from where you live? Is there anything you wish were closer to your home?

writing.q:
Some people believe that cities should be planned so that residents can meet most of their daily needs within a short walk of their homes. Others argue that this is unrealistic and that good transport links matter more. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

Proximity reduces car dependence.
Proponents argue it revives community.
Car planning hollowed out communities.

reading.title: The Long Shadow of the Urban Motorway
reading.passage:
In the decades following the Second World War, engineers across the industrialised world set about driving motorways directly into the hearts of their cities. The logic seemed unassailable: faster roads would ease congestion, knit suburbs to the centre, and signal a confident, modern future. Whole neighbourhoods, frequently those inhabited by the poor and by minorities with little political voice, were cleared to make way for elevated concrete ribbons. For a time, the new roads delivered the promised speed.

The trouble was that they did not stay fast for long. A principle that traffic engineers now call induced demand had been quietly at work. When a road is widened or a new motorway opened, the additional capacity lowers the perceived cost of driving, and so more people choose to drive. Within a few years, the fresh lanes fill, congestion returns to its former level, and the city is left with the noise, pollution and severance of a vast structure that has solved nothing. The very remedy had multiplied the disease.

Recognition of this paradox has prompted a striking reversal. A growing number of cities have begun, at considerable expense, to tear down the urban motorways their predecessors built. The most celebrated example is the Cheonggyecheon in Seoul, where an elevated expressway was removed in the early 2000s and the buried stream beneath it brought back to the surface. Far from producing the gridlock that sceptics predicted, the demolition was followed by a fall in traffic, as drivers adjusted their habits, alongside a flourishing of the riverside as public space.

None of this means that motorways have no place. In the spaces between cities, where distances are long and alternatives few, they remain indispensable. The lesson is narrower but no less important: the assumption that a city can build its way out of congestion has proved, time and again, to be an illusion. Roads, it turns out, generate the very traffic they are meant to relieve.

questions.q: Post-war engineers believed urban motorways would reduce congestion.
The neighbourhoods cleared for motorways were often poor or home to minorities.
The new motorways remained fast permanently.
The Cheonggyecheon project cost more than any other motorway removal.
What does the principle of induced demand describe?
options: Roads becoming cheaper to build over time
Added road capacity attracting more drivers

questions.q: What happened after the Seoul expressway was demolished?
options: Traffic fell and the riverside flourished

questions.q: Between cities, where distances are long, motorways remain ________.
The writer concludes that roads generate the very ________ they are meant to relieve.

listening.title: Radio Interview: Removing a City Flyover
listening.script:
HOST: Welcome back. With me is Dr Hana Reeves, who has studied the removal of the old Castle Street flyover. Hana, why was it taken down?
REEVES: Well, it was reaching the end of its safe life. Repairing it would have cost around forty million pounds, which the council simply couldn't justify.
HOST: And many feared chaos when it closed.
REEVES: They did. The prediction was that journey times across the city would double. In fact, after about three months, most journeys had returned to normal, and some were quicker.
HOST: How is that possible?
REEVES: People adapt. Some shifted to the train, others changed their travel times, and a fair number of trips simply vanished, journeys people decided weren't worth making.
HOST: What replaced the flyover?
REEVES: A tree-lined boulevard with a cycle track and wider pavements. Footfall in the local shops has risen by roughly twenty per cent.
HOST: A success, then.
REEVES: On the whole, yes, though I'd stress that careful planning beforehand was essential. You can't just knock these things down and hope.

questions.q: Why was the flyover removed?
options: It was reaching the end of its safe life

questions.q: Repairing the flyover would have cost around ________ million pounds.
Critics predicted journey times would ________.

options: A tree-lined boulevard with a cycle track

questions.q: Footfall in local shops rose by roughly ________ per cent.

speaking.q:
Describe a change to roads or transport in your area that has affected how people travel. You should say:
- what the change was

and explain whether you think the change was a good idea.

Measure | Before | After

vocab.example: The logic seemed unassailable.
Roads would knit suburbs to the centre.

reading.title: The Hidden Lives of Cloud Seeds
reading.passage:
Every cloud, however vast and amorphous it may appear, owes its existence to particles too small to see with the naked eye. Water vapour in the atmosphere does not condense into droplets spontaneously; it requires a microscopic surface on which to gather. These surfaces, known as cloud condensation nuclei, may be grains of mineral dust lifted from arid plains, salt crystals flung skyward by breaking waves, or particulate matter emitted from industrial chimneys. Without them, the air could become heavily supersaturated with moisture and still produce no cloud at all.

The nature of these nuclei has a surprising influence on the weather. Clouds that form around a great many small particles tend to consist of numerous tiny droplets rather than a few large ones. Such clouds reflect sunlight more efficiently and are slower to release rain, because the minuscule droplets struggle to coalesce into drops heavy enough to fall. Researchers studying regions downwind of major cities have observed precisely this effect: pollution can, paradoxically, suppress rainfall while simultaneously brightening the clouds overhead.

Marine environments tell a different story. Over the open ocean, where industrial pollutants are scarce, a significant proportion of cloud nuclei originates from microscopic plankton. As these organisms metabolise, they release a sulphur compound that, once airborne, oxidises into particles capable of seeding clouds. Some scientists have proposed that this constitutes a self-regulating loop: when sunlight intensifies, plankton flourish, more nuclei are produced, and the resulting clouds shade the waters below. The strength of this feedback remains contested, and field measurements have so far proved difficult to reconcile with the elegant theory.

Understanding cloud nuclei is far from an academic curiosity. Climate models that aim to project future warming must somehow represent these particles, yet their behaviour spans scales from the molecular to the planetary. A small error in how a model treats them can translate into a large discrepancy in its forecast of global temperature. For this reason, the humble cloud seed has become one of the most stubborn sources of uncertainty in contemporary climate science.

questions.q: Water vapour will always form a cloud once the air becomes supersaturated.

listening.title: Booking a Weather-Station Tour
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, Hilltop Meteorological Centre, how can I help?

VISITOR: Saturday would be best. What time does it start?
RECEPTIONIST: The morning tour begins at half past ten and lasts about ninety minutes.
VISITOR: Lovely. And how much is it?
RECEPTIONIST: It's twelve pounds for adults, or eight if you're a student.
VISITOR: I'm a student, so eight pounds. Can I pay on the day?
RECEPTIONIST: You can, but we ask for a deposit of three pounds to secure the booking.
VISITOR: That's fine. Will I see the instruments outside?
RECEPTIONIST: Yes, the guide shows you the rain gauge and the anemometer, which measures wind speed. Do bring a waterproof jacket, as part of the tour is on the exposed roof.
VISITOR: Good to know. Can I take photographs?
RECEPTIONIST: Photographs are welcome everywhere except the forecasting room. May I take your name?
VISITOR: Yes, it's Daniel Pierce, P-I-E-R-C-E.
RECEPTIONIST: Perfect, you're booked for Saturday.

reading.title: Reading the Rings of Ancient Trees
reading.passage:
Long before instruments existed to record temperature or rainfall, the climate left its signature in the wood of living things. Each year a tree adds a fresh layer of growth beneath its bark, and the width of that layer reflects the conditions of the season in which it formed. A warm, wet summer typically produces a broad ring; a year of drought or bitter cold leaves a narrow one. By examining a sequence of rings, scientists can read a chronicle of past weather extending back centuries, and in some cases millennia.

The discipline that interprets these patterns is known as dendrochronology. Its practitioners do not rely on a single tree, for any individual specimen may be distorted by local accidents such as a fallen neighbour or an infestation of insects. Instead they cross-reference many trees from the same region, building a master sequence in which the idiosyncrasies of one specimen are cancelled out by the consensus of the rest. Where living trees overlap with timber from old buildings, and that timber overlaps in turn with wood preserved in bogs, the record can be extended far beyond the lifespan of any single tree.

Dendrochronology has uses well beyond the study of climate. Because the pattern of rings in a given region forms a kind of natural barcode, archaeologists can date the beams of a medieval roof or the planks of a buried ship with remarkable precision, often to the exact year of felling. Art historians, too, have turned to the technique to authenticate the wooden panels on which old masters painted, exposing forgeries whose timber was simply too young to be genuine.

The method is not infallible. Trees growing in tropical regions, where the seasons vary little, may form rings that are faint or absent altogether, frustrating efforts to build a reliable sequence. And while ring width captures the broad sweep of past climate, translating that width into a precise temperature requires careful calibration against the modern instrumental record. Even so, the patient counting of rings has given science one of its longest and most trusted windows onto the climates of the past.

questions.q: A narrow tree ring usually indicates a year of favourable growing conditions.
Scientists cross-reference many trees to reduce the influence of local accidents.
Dendrochronology was first developed by art historians.
Why is a single tree considered unreliable for building a record?
options: Its rings are always too faint to read
Local events may distort its growth

questions.q: How can the record be extended beyond one tree's lifespan?
options: By overlapping living trees with old timber and bog wood

questions.q: The discipline that interprets tree-ring patterns is called ________.

listening.title: A Tutorial on a Tree-Ring Project
listening.script:
TUTOR: So, Maya, you've chosen tree rings for your independent project. How's it going?
MAYA: Quite well, though I'm worried about the sample size.
TUTOR: A fair concern. How many trees have you cored so far?
MAYA: Fourteen. I was aiming for twenty.
TUTOR: Fourteen is a reasonable start, but try to reach at least eighteen before you draw conclusions.
MAYA: Right. The tricky part is measuring the ring widths accurately.
TUTOR: You're using the microscope in the lab, I hope, not a ruler.
MAYA: Yes, the one on the second floor. It reads to a hundredth of a millimetre.
TUTOR: Excellent. And remember to record the orientation of each core, because growth can differ on the sunny side.
MAYA: I've been noting that. Should I include the bog-wood samples too?
TUTOR: Only if you can date them confidently. Otherwise keep them separate.
MAYA: Understood. When is the draft due?
TUTOR: The fifteenth of March. And do book a slot to discuss your graphs beforehand.
MAYA: I'll email you for a time. Thank you.

reading.title: The Return of the Monsoon
reading.passage:
For roughly half the population of the planet, the rhythm of life is governed not by a calendar but by a wind. The monsoon, a seasonal reversal of the prevailing winds, brings to South Asia the rains on which agriculture, hydroelectric power and drinking water all depend. When the monsoon arrives on time and in the expected measure, harvests flourish and reservoirs fill. When it falters, the consequences ripple through entire economies.

The mechanism behind the monsoon is, in essence, a vast version of the sea breeze familiar to anyone who has stood on a beach. During the hot months, the land mass of the subcontinent warms far faster than the surrounding ocean. The heated air over the land rises, creating an area of low pressure that draws in moisture-laden air from the sea. As this humid air is forced upward over the terrain, it cools, and the moisture it carries falls as torrential rain. With the change of season the pattern reverses, and dry continental air flows outward toward the ocean.

Predicting the precise timing and strength of each year's monsoon has long defeated forecasters. The system is influenced by a tangle of distant factors, among them the temperature of the Pacific Ocean thousands of kilometres away and the extent of snow cover on the Himalayan plateau the previous winter. A warmer Pacific, associated with the phenomenon known as El Niño, has historically tended to weaken the monsoon, though the relationship is far from mechanical and occasionally breaks down without warning.

There is growing concern that a warming climate may render the monsoon more erratic. Some projections suggest that total rainfall could increase, since warmer air holds more moisture, yet that this rain may fall in fewer, more violent bursts separated by longer dry spells. Such a shift would be doubly cruel: intense downpours overwhelm drainage and trigger floods, while the lengthening gaps between them parch the fields. For the farmers who must decide when to sow, an unpredictable monsoon is in some ways worse than a consistently poor one.

questions.q: The monsoon affects only a small fraction of the world's population.
During the hot season, the land warms more quickly than the ocean.
El Niño always weakens the monsoon in a predictable way.
Most farmers in South Asia now use satellite apps to plan their sowing.
The monsoon mechanism is compared in the passage to
What change to the monsoon do some projections predict under a warming climate?
options: Less total rain but more even spread
More total rain falling in fewer violent bursts

questions.q: Heated air over the land rises, creating an area of low ________.

listening.title: A Radio Report on Flood Preparations
listening.script:
PRESENTER: With the wet season approaching, the council has announced new measures to protect the town. I'm joined by emergency coordinator Tom Hale. Tom, what's the headline?
TOM: Well, the main news is that we've raised the riverside levee by half a metre, which should hold back even a once-in-fifty-year flood.
PRESENTER: Reassuring. And what about residents themselves?
TOM: We're asking everyone in the low-lying eastern district to prepare an emergency kit. That means three days of water, a torch, and copies of important documents.
PRESENTER: How will people know when to evacuate?
TOM: There are two warnings. A siren means be ready; a continuous siren means leave immediately for the community hall on Bridge Street.
PRESENTER: Is the hall well stocked?
TOM: Yes, it can shelter two hundred people and has its own generator.
PRESENTER: And for those who can't drive?
TOM: A free shuttle bus runs from the library every thirty minutes during an alert.
PRESENTER: Excellent. Where can listeners find more detail?
TOM: On the council website, or call the hotline on five-five-oh-two.
PRESENTER: Thanks, Tom.

reading.title: Cities That Make Their Own Weather
reading.passage:
It has long been observed that a great city is, in a meteorological sense, an island. Surrounded by cooler countryside, the dense core of a metropolis can be several degrees warmer than the fields beyond its edge, a phenomenon known to climatologists as the urban heat island. The difference is most pronounced at night, when the brick, asphalt and concrete that absorbed the sun's energy by day release it slowly back into the air, keeping the streets stubbornly warm long after the surrounding land has cooled.

The causes are several and reinforce one another. Hard surfaces shed rainwater rapidly, leaving little moisture to evaporate and cool the air, as happens naturally over soil and vegetation. Tall buildings trap heat within deep street canyons and block the breezes that might otherwise carry it away. Add to this the warmth radiated by vehicles, air-conditioning units and the sheer concentration of human activity, and the result is a microclimate distinctly hotter than nature alone would produce.

The consequences are not merely a matter of comfort. During a heatwave, the elevated temperatures of a city centre can prove genuinely dangerous, particularly for the elderly and the unwell, and the persistent night-time warmth denies residents the relief that darkness would normally bring. Higher temperatures also drive up the demand for cooling, which in turn consumes more electricity and, where that electricity is generated by burning fuel, releases yet more heat and carbon into the atmosphere.

Fortunately, the heat island is not an immutable feature of city life. Planting trees along streets provides shade and restores the cooling effect of evaporation. Coating roofs in pale, reflective materials can bounce sunlight back to the sky rather than absorbing it. Some cities have gone further, weaving parks, ponds and green corridors into the urban fabric so that cooler air can circulate. None of these measures is a complete remedy on its own, but together they demonstrate that the weather of a city, unlike the weather of the wider world, lies substantially within human control.

questions.q: The urban heat island effect is usually strongest during the daytime.
Hard urban surfaces allow less water to evaporate than soil and vegetation do.
Reflective roof coatings are more expensive than planting street trees.
Why does a city stay warm at night?
options: The sun shines for longer over cities
Built surfaces slowly release heat absorbed during the day

questions.q: Which group is described as especially at risk during urban heatwaves?
What is the author's overall view of the heat island effect?
options: It can be substantially reduced by human action

listening.title: A Lecture on Cooling Cities
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today we'll look at how cities can cool themselves, and I'll focus on three practical strategies. The first, and probably the cheapest, is the reflective roof. By painting a dark roof white, a building can lower its surface temperature by as much as thirty degrees Celsius on a sunny day. That's a striking figure for so simple a change.

The second strategy is urban greenery. A mature street tree doesn't just shade the pavement; through the evaporation of water from its leaves it can cool the air around it noticeably. One study in our own city found that streets lined with trees were on average two degrees cooler than bare ones nearby.

The third, and most ambitious, is the use of water features. Ponds and fountains cool the air as the water evaporates, though they do consume a resource that may itself be scarce in summer. For this reason planners increasingly favour designs that recycle rainwater rather than drawing on the mains supply.

Now, none of these works in isolation, and the best results come from combining all three. For next week, please read the chapter on green roofs and bring one question to the seminar.
