reading.title: The Science of the Jet Stream
reading.passage:
High above the surface of the Earth, at the altitude where airliners cruise, ribbons of fast-moving air encircle the globe. These are the jet streams: narrow currents of wind that can exceed two hundred kilometres an hour, snaking from west to east across the middle latitudes. Though invisible from the ground, they exert an outsized influence on the weather experienced far below, steering the storms and shaping the patterns of warmth and cold that define a season.

The jet streams owe their existence to a simple imbalance. The tropics receive far more solar energy than the poles, and the atmosphere is forever attempting to redistribute this heat from where it is abundant to where it is scarce. Where warm tropical air meets cold polar air, a sharp temperature gradient forms, and it is along this boundary, sharpened by the rotation of the planet, that the jet stream flows. The greater the contrast in temperature, broadly speaking, the stronger and straighter the current.

This relationship lies at the heart of a debate that has occupied climate scientists in recent years. The Arctic is warming faster than the rest of the planet, which reduces the temperature difference between the pole and the tropics. Some researchers argue that a weaker gradient produces a slower, wavier jet stream, one prone to forming great loops that linger over a region for days. Such stalled patterns, they suggest, may explain certain recent episodes of prolonged heat, drought or flooding. The hypothesis is intriguing but remains the subject of vigorous disagreement, for the natural variability of the jet stream is large enough to mask any underlying trend.

What is not in dispute is the practical importance of these high-altitude winds. Aircraft flying eastward ride the jet stream to save fuel and time, while those heading west take pains to avoid it. Forecasters watch its meanders closely, knowing that a southward dip can usher in a cold snap and a northward bulge can bring an unseasonable spell of warmth. For a current of air no one can see, the jet stream leaves an unmistakable mark on daily life.

questions.q: Jet streams generally flow from east to west across the middle latitudes.
A larger temperature contrast tends to produce a stronger, straighter jet stream.
The idea that Arctic warming makes the jet stream wavier is widely accepted by all scientists.
Most commercial pilots are trained specifically in jet-stream navigation.
What fundamental cause of the jet stream does the passage give?
options: The uneven heating of the planet by the sun

questions.q: Why does natural variability complicate the Arctic-warming hypothesis?
options: It makes the jet stream disappear
It is large enough to hide any underlying trend

questions.q: Jet streams are narrow currents that can exceed two hundred kilometres an ________.
A southward dip in the jet stream can usher in a cold ________.

listening.title: A Seminar on Forecasting Careers
listening.script:
ADVISOR: Welcome, everyone. This short seminar is about careers in weather forecasting, and I'll keep it practical. Let's start with qualifications. Most forecasters hold a degree in physics, mathematics or meteorology itself. A master's is increasingly expected for research posts, though not for broadcasting.

Now, the work is more varied than people assume. Some forecasters specialise in aviation, advising pilots on the jet stream and turbulence. Others work in agriculture, helping farmers time their planting. A growing field is renewable energy, where forecasters predict wind and sunshine for power companies.

Let me mention pay briefly. A trainee typically starts on around twenty-six thousand pounds, rising to perhaps forty thousand with experience. Senior research roles can pay considerably more.

A word of advice: develop your coding skills. Modern forecasting relies heavily on computer models, and an ability to program is now almost as valuable as the science itself. If you want to read further, I recommend the textbook by Hartley, third edition, which our library holds several copies of. And do come to the open day on the twelfth of June, when you can meet working forecasters.

reading.title: The Science of the Second Wind
reading.passage:
Endurance athletes have long described a curious phenomenon during prolonged exertion: a sudden easing of distress after a period of acute discomfort, popularly known as the "second wind." For decades this experience was dismissed as anecdotal, but physiologists now regard it as a genuine, if imperfectly understood, adjustment of the body's energy systems. The transition appears to coincide with the moment when the cardiovascular system finally meets the oxygen demand that the working muscles have been imposing since exercise began.

At the onset of vigorous activity, the heart and lungs cannot instantly supply enough oxygen, so muscles rely heavily on anaerobic metabolism, which produces energy quickly but generates lactate and a sensation of heaviness. During this oxygen deficit phase, breathing feels laboured and the limbs grow leaden. After several minutes, however, blood flow redistributes, capillaries dilate, and aerobic metabolism takes over as the dominant fuel pathway. The unpleasant symptoms recede, and the athlete suddenly feels capable of continuing indefinitely. Some researchers argue that a rise in endorphin levels reinforces this sense of relief, although the evidence for that contribution remains contested.

Notably, the second wind is not guaranteed. It tends to appear most reliably in individuals who maintain a steady, sustainable pace rather than those who begin too aggressively. Trained athletes reach the aerobic threshold faster than novices because their cardiovascular systems respond more efficiently, which means they may experience the shift earlier and more predictably. Coaches sometimes exploit this knowledge by encouraging runners to push through the initial discomfort rather than slowing down, reasoning that relief lies just beyond the worst of it.

The phenomenon also has a psychological dimension. Athletes who expect a second wind appear more willing to tolerate early distress, and this expectation may itself influence whether the transition is perceived. Whether the mechanism is chiefly metabolic, hormonal, or partly a matter of belief, the experience remains one of the more compelling examples of the body's capacity to recalibrate under sustained strain.

questions.q: Physiologists once treated reports of the second wind as merely anecdotal.
Anaerobic metabolism supplies energy more slowly than aerobic metabolism.
The contribution of endorphins to the second wind has been firmly established.
The second wind occurs at the same point in every athlete's exercise session.
Sprinters experience the second wind more often than distance runners.
According to the passage, the second wind is most reliably experienced by athletes who
Why do trained athletes tend to reach the aerobic threshold sooner?
options: Their cardiovascular systems respond more efficiently
They rely on anaerobic metabolism longer

questions.q: During the oxygen deficit phase, muscles depend largely on ________ metabolism.

listening.title: Joining the University Running Club
listening.script:
STAFF: Good morning, you've reached the University Running Club desk. How can I help?
STUDENT: Hi, I'd like to sign up for the beginners' group, please.
STAFF: Lovely. The membership fee for the term is eighteen pounds, which covers all the coached sessions.
STUDENT: Great. When do the beginners meet?
STAFF: The beginners train on Tuesday and Thursday evenings, starting at half past six. We gather by the main sports pavilion.
STUDENT: And how long does each session last?
STAFF: About fifty minutes, including a warm-up and a cool-down. Could I take your name?
STUDENT: It's Daniel Foster. That's F-O-S-T-E-R.
STAFF: Thank you. And do you have any injuries we should know about?
STUDENT: I had a knee problem last year, but it's fully recovered now.
STAFF: Good to know. One more thing: we recommend bringing a reflective vest for the winter evenings, as some of the route runs alongside the river.
STUDENT: I'll order one. Is there anything else I need?
STAFF: Just a water bottle and proper trainers. We'll see you on Tuesday, Daniel.

reading.title: Altitude Training and the Limits of Adaptation
reading.passage:
For more than half a century, endurance athletes have travelled to mountain regions in the belief that training at altitude will improve their performance at sea level. The rationale rests on a well-documented physiological response: when the body is exposed to the thin air of high elevations, where oxygen is comparatively scarce, it gradually produces more red blood cells to compensate. A greater concentration of these cells theoretically enhances the blood's capacity to carry oxygen, which should translate into superior endurance once the athlete returns to lower ground.

The reality, however, has proved more complicated. Although the increase in red blood cell production is genuine, the benefit is frequently offset by a significant drawback. At altitude, athletes cannot train at the same intensity they manage at sea level, because the reduced oxygen availability limits how hard their muscles can work. Over an extended camp, this enforced reduction in workload can erode the very fitness the athlete hoped to build, leaving some competitors no better off, or occasionally worse, than when they arrived.

In response, sports scientists devised a more nuanced strategy popularly summarised as "live high, train low." Under this regime, athletes sleep and rest at elevation to stimulate red blood cell production, but descend to lower altitudes for their most demanding sessions, where the richer air permits full-intensity training. Studies suggest that this combination captures the haematological advantage without sacrificing training quality, though arranging such logistics is costly and not available to everyone.

Responses to altitude also vary enormously between individuals. Some athletes are classified as strong responders, producing abundant new red cells, while others gain almost nothing despite identical exposure. This variability has frustrated coaches seeking a universal formula and has led to growing interest in genetic and individual screening. The persistent appeal of the mountains, then, reflects not a guaranteed shortcut but a calculated gamble whose payoff depends heavily on how, where, and by whom it is pursued.

listening.title: A Lecture on Recovery After Competition
listening.script:
LECTURER: Right, let's continue our series on athletic recovery. Today I want to focus on what happens in the twenty-four hours after a major competition. The first priority is rehydration. During a hard event, an athlete can lose up to two litres of fluid, and replacing it quickly is essential. We generally advise drinking water with a small amount of added salt, because plain water alone is absorbed less effectively.

The second priority is refuelling. Muscles store energy as glycogen, and that supply is heavily depleted after intense effort. The window for replenishing it is widest in the first ninety minutes, so a carbohydrate-rich snack soon after finishing makes a real difference. Protein matters too, since it supports the repair of muscle fibres damaged during exertion.

Thirdly, sleep. This is the single most underrated recovery tool. During deep sleep the body releases growth hormone, which drives tissue repair. We recommend at least eight hours, and ideally a short nap as well. Finally, gentle movement the next day, often called active recovery, helps clear metabolic waste from the muscles. So remember the order: rehydrate, refuel, rest, and then move gently.

questions.q: An athlete can lose up to ________ litres of fluid during a hard event.
Why is salt added to the recovery water?
options: Because plain water is absorbed less effectively

reading.title: The Rise of Wearable Performance Trackers
reading.passage:
A decade ago, the idea that an ordinary recreational runner might wear a device monitoring heart rate, stride length, and oxygen saturation in real time would have seemed extravagant. Today such wearable trackers are commonplace, and their influence on how amateurs approach training has been profound. By converting bodily signals into streams of accessible data, these devices have democratised forms of measurement that were once confined to professional laboratories.

The appeal is straightforward. A runner can now see, minute by minute, whether they are working within an optimal range or pushing into territory that risks injury or burnout. Many trackers offer recovery scores, estimating how rested the body is and advising whether a hard session is advisable. For people who lack access to a coach, this feedback can provide a measure of guidance that was previously unavailable. Enthusiasts report feeling more motivated and more accountable when their efforts are recorded and quantified.

Yet specialists urge caution. The accuracy of consumer devices varies considerably, and readings taken at the wrist are often less reliable than those from clinical equipment. A figure presented with apparent precision may in fact carry a substantial margin of error, and users who treat such numbers as definitive can be misled. There is also concern that an excessive focus on data may diminish an athlete's ability to interpret their own bodily sensations, a skill that experienced competitors consider indispensable.

Perhaps the most subtle risk is psychological. A device that flags inadequate recovery every morning can breed anxiety rather than reassurance, and some users develop an unhealthy preoccupation with optimising every metric. The technology, in other words, is a tool whose value depends entirely on how thoughtfully it is used. Employed sensibly, it can sharpen training and prevent overexertion; relied upon blindly, it can distort the very judgement it was meant to support.

questions.q: Detailed physiological measurement was once mostly limited to professional laboratories.
Wrist-based readings are generally more reliable than clinical measurements.
Recovery scores attempt to estimate how rested the body is.
Wearable trackers are now more expensive than they were a decade ago.
Specialists believe consumer devices are always accurate.
What does the passage identify as the most subtle risk of wearable trackers?
According to the passage, the value of the technology depends mainly on
Specialists worry that focusing too much on data may diminish an athlete's ability to interpret their own bodily ________.

listening.title: Buying a Fitness Tracker at an Electronics Shop
listening.script:
CUSTOMER: Hi, I'm looking for a fitness tracker, but I'm not sure which one to get.
ASSISTANT: Sure, I can help with that. What features matter most to you?
CUSTOMER: Mainly heart rate and sleep tracking. And I run, so I'd like GPS.
ASSISTANT: In that case I'd recommend the Stride Pro. It has built-in GPS, heart-rate monitoring, and detailed sleep analysis.
CUSTOMER: How much is it?
ASSISTANT: It's normally a hundred and forty dollars, but it's on sale this week for a hundred and fifteen.
CUSTOMER: That's tempting. What about the battery?
ASSISTANT: The battery lasts about seven days on a single charge, though using GPS continuously reduces that.
CUSTOMER: And is it waterproof? I sometimes swim.
ASSISTANT: Yes, it's water-resistant up to fifty metres, so swimming is fine.
CUSTOMER: Great. Does it come with a warranty?
ASSISTANT: It comes with a two-year warranty, and you can extend it for an extra charge.
CUSTOMER: Perfect. I'll take the Stride Pro, please.

reading.title: The Forgotten Role of Sleep in Elite Sport
reading.passage:
When the public imagines the routines of elite athletes, attention naturally gravitates towards gruelling workouts, meticulously planned diets, and cutting-edge equipment. Sleep, by contrast, is rarely featured in this picture, and for much of the twentieth century it was treated as little more than the absence of training. Recent research, however, has overturned that assumption, establishing rest as a performance variable of the first importance, arguably as influential as any session in the gym.

The physiological case is compelling. During the deeper stages of sleep, the body undertakes the bulk of its tissue repair, consolidates the motor skills practised during waking hours, and regulates the hormones that govern appetite and stress. An athlete deprived of adequate sleep does not merely feel tired; their reaction times slow measurably, their risk of injury rises, and their capacity to learn new movements deteriorates. One frequently cited study found that basketball players who extended their nightly sleep improved both their sprint speed and their shooting accuracy.

Despite this evidence, sleep remains widely neglected within competitive sport, partly because the demands of the calendar conspire against it. International fixtures require travel across multiple time zones, disrupting the internal clock that governs the timing of rest. Evening competitions leave athletes too stimulated to fall asleep promptly, and early-morning training compresses the available window still further. The result is that many competitors operate in a state of chronic, low-level sleep debt without recognising the toll it exacts.

Forward-thinking organisations have begun to respond. Some teams now employ sleep specialists, schedule flights to minimise disruption to the body clock, and design accommodation to encourage deeper rest. Such measures remain far from universal, and a degree of cultural resistance persists, since rest still strikes some traditionalists as a sign of weakness rather than a foundation of excellence. Yet the direction of change is unmistakable, and sleep is steadily being reclaimed as an active component of training rather than its passive opposite.

questions.q: For much of the twentieth century, sleep was viewed mainly as the absence of training.
Sleep deprivation has no measurable effect on reaction times.
A study found that more sleep improved basketball players' shooting accuracy.
All competitive sports organisations now employ dedicated sleep specialists.
Sleep specialists earn higher salaries than team physiotherapists.
Why does the competition calendar work against adequate sleep?
options: Athletes prefer to train at night
Travel across time zones disrupts the body clock

questions.q: What attitude do some traditionalists hold towards rest, according to the passage?
options: They see it as a foundation of excellence
They regard it as a sign of weakness
They consider it more important than training
They believe it should be measured electronically

questions.q: During deeper sleep, the body consolidates the ________ skills practised while awake.

listening.title: Seminar Discussion on Sleep and Jet Lag
listening.script:
TUTOR: So today we're looking at how athletes cope with jet lag during international tours. Maya, you researched this. What did you find?
MAYA: Well, the main problem is that the body's internal clock takes time to adjust. A useful rule of thumb is that it takes roughly one day to adapt for each time zone crossed.
TUTOR: That's a striking figure. So a five-zone trip might need almost a week?
MAYA: Exactly. That's why teams now try to arrive early. One squad I read about flew in eight days before their first match.
TUTOR: Interesting. And what helps speed up the adjustment?
MAYA: Light exposure is the key tool. Getting bright daylight in the morning at the destination helps reset the clock. Some athletes also take small doses of melatonin to encourage sleep at the right time.
TUTOR: And diet?
MAYA: There's some evidence that adjusting meal times to the new zone helps too, but the research there is weaker.
TUTOR: Thank you, Maya. Next week we'll look at recovery nutrition.

options: To allow time for the body clock to adjust

reading.title: Why Underdogs Sometimes Win
reading.passage:
Few narratives in sport are as enduringly popular as the triumph of the underdog: the smaller team, the unfancied competitor, the side that defies the odds to overcome a manifestly superior opponent. Such victories are often attributed to luck, and chance undoubtedly plays a part. Yet researchers who study competition have identified several structural and psychological factors that make upsets considerably more probable than a naive assessment of talent alone would predict.

One factor concerns the nature of the contest itself. In sports decided by a single, low-scoring encounter, random variation has greater scope to influence the outcome than in formats where superior ability can assert itself repeatedly over many points or games. A football match may hinge on one deflected shot, whereas a tennis player of greater skill will usually prevail across the dozens of points that constitute a set. Consequently, knockout tournaments produce more shocks than leagues, simply because they offer the weaker party fewer opportunities to be exposed.

Psychology supplies a second explanation. The favourite carries the burden of expectation, and the fear of an embarrassing defeat can induce a cautious, constrained style of play that suppresses the very flair that made them favourites. The underdog, by contrast, has little to lose and may compete with a freedom that unsettles a more illustrious opponent. This asymmetry of pressure can partially neutralise an apparent gap in ability.

Finally, underdogs frequently adopt unconventional tactics precisely because conventional ones would lead to predictable defeat. A team unable to match its rivals physically may disrupt the established rhythm of a match, deny space, and force errors, converting its supposed weakness into a strategic asset. None of this guarantees success, and favourites still win the overwhelming majority of contests. But the persistent recurrence of upsets is not an affront to logic; it is, on closer inspection, a predictable consequence of how competition is structured and experienced.

questions.q: The passage claims that chance plays no part in underdog victories.
Knockout tournaments tend to produce more upsets than leagues.
Favourites win the majority of contests despite the frequency of upsets.
Underdog victories attract larger television audiences than expected results.
The fear of defeat can make a favourite play more cautiously.
Why does random variation have more influence in low-scoring contests?
options: Because superior ability has fewer chances to assert itself

questions.q: How do underdogs often turn a weakness into a strategic asset?
options: By adopting unconventional tactics that disrupt rhythm
By playing more cautiously than the favourite

questions.q: The favourite carries the burden of ________, which can constrain their play.

listening.title: A Talk on the Psychology of Competition
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone. Tonight I want to explore why pressure affects performers so differently, and what we can do about it. Let me start with a simple idea: pressure is not inherently bad. A moderate level of arousal actually sharpens focus and reaction time. The problem begins when arousal becomes excessive, because that's when fine motor control starts to break down.

This relationship is often drawn as an inverted U. Performance rises as pressure increases, reaches a peak, and then falls away as anxiety takes over. The skill, then, is finding your own optimal point on that curve. For a weightlifter, that point may be quite high; for a golfer attempting a delicate putt, it's considerably lower.

So how do athletes manage this? Three techniques dominate. The first is controlled breathing, which lowers the heart rate within seconds. The second is what psychologists call a pre-performance routine, a fixed sequence of actions that creates a sense of familiarity and control. The third is reframing, deliberately interpreting nerves as excitement rather than fear. Studies show that athletes who reframe in this way perform measurably better under pressure.

options: A sense of familiarity and control

reading.title: The Quiet Rise of the Four-Day Week
reading.passage:
For most of the twentieth century, the five-day working week was treated as a fixed feature of industrial life, as natural and unquestioned as the calendar itself. In recent years, however, a growing number of firms have begun to challenge that assumption, trialling a compressed schedule in which employees complete their duties in four days while retaining full pay. Advocates argue that the change reflects a long-overdue recognition that hours spent at a desk are a poor proxy for genuine productivity.

The most widely cited evidence comes from a coordinated pilot in which around seventy organisations, ranging from software developers to small charities, adopted the model for six months. Researchers reported that revenue remained broadly stable, while measures of employee burnout fell sharply. Perhaps more strikingly, the overwhelming majority of participating firms chose to continue the arrangement once the trial concluded, suggesting that the benefits were not merely a temporary novelty.

Sceptics, however, caution against drawing sweeping conclusions. They note that the firms volunteering for such schemes are unlikely to be representative; a struggling factory operating on thin margins may find it impossible to maintain output with a fifth of its working time removed. Critics also point out that some apparent gains stem simply from cutting unproductive meetings and tightening workflows, reforms that could be introduced without shortening the week at all.

What is rarely disputed is that the conversation itself marks a shift in how work is valued. Where earlier generations measured commitment in hours logged, a younger workforce increasingly judges employers by the flexibility they offer. Whether the four-day week becomes a mainstream norm or remains the preserve of a privileged minority of knowledge industries, it has already forced managers to ask a question that was once unthinkable: how much of the conventional week is genuinely necessary?

questions.q: The five-day week was rarely questioned during the twentieth century.
In the six-month pilot, company revenue increased substantially.
Most firms that took part in the pilot decided to keep the four-day model afterwards.
The pilot included companies based in more than one country.
According to sceptics, one weakness of the trial evidence is that
options: the firms involved were not typical of all businesses
the trial lasted only one month
revenue was not measured at all

questions.q: The writer suggests that some productivity gains may actually come from

reading.title: Automation and the Reshaping of Skilled Trades
reading.passage:
Public debate about automation tends to fixate on a familiar image: a factory floor where robotic arms replace assembly-line workers. Yet this picture is increasingly out of date. The technologies now spreading through workplaces are less about brute mechanical strength and more about software capable of analysing, predicting and recommending. As a result, the occupations most exposed are not always the manual ones that intuition might suggest.

Consider the skilled trades, such as electricians, plumbers and carpenters. For decades these jobs were assumed to be relatively safe, since they demand physical dexterity and the ability to improvise in cramped, unpredictable spaces, qualities that machines have struggled to replicate. Recent surveys suggest that workers in these fields remain among the least anxious about technological displacement, and demand for their services in many regions continues to outstrip supply.

Professional roles tell a more complicated story. Tasks that involve processing documents, drafting standard contracts or reviewing routine data, once the bread and butter of junior lawyers and accountants, can increasingly be performed by software in a fraction of the time. This does not necessarily mean fewer professionals overall, but it does alter the shape of careers, hollowing out the entry-level positions through which newcomers traditionally learned their craft.

Economists studying these shifts emphasise a distinction between substitution and augmentation. In some cases technology simply replaces a human task; in others it amplifies what a skilled person can accomplish, allowing them to focus on judgement, persuasion and the handling of exceptions. The trades may prove resilient precisely because so much of what they do resists codification. The deeper lesson, many argue, is that the value of work is migrating towards the messy, situational problems that no algorithm has yet learned to solve.

questions.q: The writer believes the factory-robot image accurately reflects modern automation.
Skilled trades were long assumed to be relatively protected from automation.
Demand for skilled trades exceeds supply in many regions.
Junior lawyers earn higher salaries than junior accountants.
According to the passage, the effect of software on professional work is to
options: reduce entry-level positions while changing career structures
increase the number of junior roles

questions.q: The distinction between substitution and augmentation refers to whether technology
options: replaces a task or enhances a person's abilities

questions.q: Skilled trades may prove resilient because much of their work resists ________.
Value in work is said to be migrating towards messy, ________ problems.

listening.title: A Careers Adviser on Reskilling
listening.script:
ADVISER: Thanks for coming in. So you're thinking about retraining, is that right?

CLIENT: Yes, I've worked in administration for years, but a lot of my tasks are being automated.

ADVISER: That's a common story at the moment. The good news is there are several funded programmes you can join. The most popular one runs for twelve weeks, part-time, so you can keep working.

CLIENT: Twelve weeks sounds manageable. What does it cover?

ADVISER: It focuses on data analysis and basic coding. There's no upfront cost; the fee of around nine hundred dollars is covered by a government grant.

CLIENT: That's a relief. Are the classes online or in person?

ADVISER: They're blended. Two evenings online and one Saturday morning on campus each week.

CLIENT: And do I get any kind of certificate?

ADVISER: Yes, you'll receive a nationally recognised qualification at the end. Around eighty per cent of graduates find relevant work within six months.

CLIENT: That's encouraging. When does the next intake start?

ADVISER: The next group begins on the fourteenth of March. I'd recommend applying by the end of February to secure a place.

CLIENT: Great, I'll get my application in this week.

reading.title: The Gig Economy and the Question of Security
reading.passage:
The phrase 'gig economy' once referred to the casual, project-based engagements of musicians and freelance writers. Today it describes a vast labour market mediated by smartphone applications, in which drivers, couriers and cleaners are summoned at a tap and paid by the task. Proponents celebrate the autonomy this offers: workers can, in theory, log on whenever they choose and answer to no fixed employer. For many, particularly those balancing study or caring responsibilities, this flexibility is genuinely liberating.

Yet the same arrangement that grants freedom also withholds protection. Because platform workers are usually classified as independent contractors rather than employees, they typically forgo the safety nets that traditional jobs provide: paid sick leave, holiday entitlement, a guaranteed minimum wage and contributions towards a pension. When illness strikes or an algorithm quietly reduces the flow of available work, the individual bears the full cost. Critics describe this as a transfer of risk from companies to the people who can least afford to absorb it.

The legal status of these workers has become a battleground in courts across several continents. In a number of high-profile rulings, judges have decided that the degree of control platforms exert over pay, ratings and working conditions is incompatible with genuine self-employment. Such decisions have forced some companies to extend at least limited benefits, though enforcement remains uneven and many firms have restructured their contracts to sidestep the obligations.

What makes the debate so intractable is that workers themselves are divided. Surveys repeatedly find that a substantial minority value the flexibility above all else and resist any change that might curtail it, while others would readily trade some autonomy for the stability of a conventional contract. Resolving this tension may ultimately require a new legal category altogether, one that recognises the reality of work that is neither fully employed nor truly independent.

questions.q: The term 'gig economy' originally applied to freelance creative workers.
Platform workers are usually classified as employees of the company.
All courts that have ruled on the matter reached the same conclusion.
Some companies have changed their contracts to avoid new obligations.
Critics argue that the gig model effectively
options: increases company profits through higher wages

questions.q: Why have some courts ruled against the platforms?
options: The platforms paid workers too much
The level of control resembled an employer relationship
Workers refused to use the apps

questions.q: Many gig workers are classified as independent ________ rather than employees.
Resolving the tension may require a new legal ________ altogether.

listening.title: Lecture: Trust in Platform Work
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today I want to look at how trust is built in platform-based work, where employer and worker may never meet face to face.

The first mechanism is the rating system. After each job, both sides leave a score, usually out of five. A driver whose average falls below four point six may be suspended, which shows how much weight these numbers carry.

The second mechanism is the algorithm that distributes tasks. Workers often complain that it is opaque; they cannot see why they receive certain jobs and not others. Our research suggests that this lack of transparency is the single biggest source of frustration, mentioned by over seventy per cent of respondents.

The third factor is community. Despite working alone, many couriers form informal networks, often through messaging groups, where they share advice and warn each other about problem customers. These groups can include hundreds of members in a single city.

Finally, consider reputation portability. At present, a worker's rating is locked to one platform; if they switch apps, they start again from zero. Several policy proposals would let workers carry their reputation with them, a reform many believe would rebalance power. We'll examine one such proposal after the break.

reading.title: Lifelong Learning in an Age of Shrinking Shelf Lives
reading.passage:
There was a time when education was conceived as a single, front-loaded investment: a person studied intensively in youth, acquired a qualification, and then drew on that store of knowledge for a working lifetime. That model rested on a tacit assumption, namely that the skills learned at twenty would remain broadly relevant at sixty. As the pace of technological change accelerates, this assumption looks ever more fragile, and the notion of lifelong learning has moved from pious slogan to practical necessity.

The core problem is what specialists call the shrinking 'shelf life' of skills. In fast-moving fields, particularly those touching software and data, techniques that were cutting-edge a decade ago may now be obsolete, while entirely new competencies emerge faster than universities can design courses to teach them. Workers therefore face the prospect of repeatedly returning to learning, not as a one-off detour but as a continuous companion to their careers.

Responding to this challenge is far from straightforward. Adults juggling jobs and families rarely have the luxury of pausing for full-time study, and the financial burden of repeated retraining can be considerable. Some governments have experimented with individual learning accounts, into which both the state and employers pay, giving citizens a personal budget to spend on accredited courses throughout their lives. Early results are mixed; uptake has often been disappointing, partly because workers struggle to identify which skills will actually prove valuable.

Perhaps the deepest shift is cultural rather than logistical. A system built around the idea that learning ends with graduation must be replaced by one in which curiosity and adaptability are themselves treated as core professional assets. Critics warn, however, that placing the entire responsibility on individuals risks letting employers off the hook. If the demand for constant reskilling is driven by corporate decisions, they argue, then companies, not just workers, should bear a fair share of the cost.

questions.q: The traditional model assumed that youthful learning would stay relevant for a whole career.
The 'shelf life' of skills is said to be lengthening over time.
Individual learning accounts are funded only by the government.
Uptake of learning accounts has been disappointing in some cases.
Individual learning accounts were first introduced in Scandinavia.
One reason workers underuse learning accounts is that they
options: cannot tell which skills will be valuable

The article says the deepest shift required is ________ rather than logistical.

listening.title: Two Students Discuss an Online Course
listening.script:
MAYA: Hey Tom, did you sign up for that online data course in the end?

TOM: I did, actually. I started last week. It's more demanding than I expected.

MAYA: How long does it run for?

TOM: Sixteen weeks, but it's self-paced, so I can go faster if I want.

MAYA: Nice. Is there a lot of coursework?

TOM: There's a quiz every week and one big project at the end. The project is worth half the final grade, so I can't ignore it.

MAYA: Fifty per cent, wow. What's the project about?

TOM: We have to analyze a real dataset and present our findings. I'm thinking of using transport data for my city.

MAYA: That sounds interesting. Is the course free?

TOM: The learning is free, but if you want the certificate it costs forty-nine dollars.

MAYA: That's reasonable. Do you get support if you're stuck?

TOM: Yes, there's a discussion forum, and a tutor answers questions every Tuesday in a live session.

MAYA: Maybe I'll join the next intake. When does it start?

TOM: The new cohort opens on the first of October. You should definitely sign up with me.

reading.title: Remote Work and the Geography of Opportunity
reading.passage:
Long before the recent surge in remote working, economists had observed a striking pattern: the most rewarding jobs were clustering ever more tightly in a handful of large cities. A young graduate seeking a career in technology, finance or design effectively had to relocate to one of these magnets, paying steep rents for the privilege of proximity to the right employers. This concentration enriched the cities concerned but drained talent and dynamism from the regions left behind.

The normalisation of remote work promised to disrupt this geography. If a software engineer could contribute from a small town as effectively as from a downtown office, the argument ran, then opportunity might at last be untethered from location. Smaller communities could retain their educated young people, housing pressure in the great cities might ease, and the long, exhausting commute could become a relic of a less enlightened age.

Reality has proved more nuanced. While remote work has undeniably allowed some professionals to leave expensive urban centres, the beneficiaries have often been those already established in their careers, with the reputations and networks to thrive at a distance. Newcomers, by contrast, frequently struggle without the informal mentoring and chance encounters that physical workplaces provide. There is also evidence that remote roles, being open to applicants anywhere, attract fiercer competition, and that some employers quietly pay less for them.

Moreover, the dispersal of workers has not always benefited struggling regions. Many who fled the cities chose pleasant commuter towns or coastal resorts rather than genuinely deprived areas, sometimes pushing up local house prices and displacing existing residents. The hope that remote work would automatically rebalance opportunity now seems naive. Whether it ultimately narrows or widens regional divides may depend less on the technology itself than on the policies, from transport to broadband, that determine where its benefits are allowed to land.

questions.q: The clustering of rewarding jobs in big cities began only after remote work became common.
Remote work was expected to weaken the link between jobs and location.
Established professionals have often benefited more from remote work than newcomers.
Newcomers may miss the informal mentoring found in physical workplaces.

options: attract more competition and sometimes lower pay

listening.title: Lecture: The Future of City Centres
listening.script:
LECTURER: In today's session I'll outline three scenarios for the future of city centres as remote work reshapes demand for office space.

The first scenario is decline. If a large share of workers stay home permanently, demand for offices falls, and older buildings may stand empty. In our regional survey, office occupancy was still only around sixty per cent of its pre-pandemic level.

The second scenario is conversion. Empty offices are turned into housing, which could help with the chronic shortage of homes. However, conversion is expensive; estimates suggest it costs roughly twice as much per square metre as a standard refurbishment.

The third scenario, which I find most plausible, is reinvention. Here the city centre survives not as a place to work nine to five, but as a destination for culture, dining and occasional collaboration. Footfall on weekends, interestingly, has already recovered to ninety-five per cent of former levels, even though weekday footfall lags well behind.

The key variable in all three scenarios is transport. Cheaper, faster public transport could draw people back; persistent delays could accelerate the flight to the suburbs. We'll model these effects in detail next week.

reading.title: The Silent Engine of the Oceans
reading.passage:
Beneath the apparent stillness of the open sea lies a vast circulatory system that quietly governs the climate of the entire planet. Known as the thermohaline circulation, this network of deep currents is driven not by wind but by differences in temperature and salinity. As warm surface water travels towards the poles, it gradually cools, becomes denser, and sinks into the abyss, displacing the water beneath it. This sinking sets in motion a slow, continuous conveyor belt that may take a single parcel of water a thousand years to complete its global journey.

The consequences of this circulation are far-reaching. By transporting heat from the tropics to higher latitudes, the system moderates temperatures that would otherwise be far more extreme. North-western Europe, for instance, enjoys winters that are notably milder than its latitude alone would suggest, largely because of the warmth delivered by these northward-flowing waters. Were the circulation to weaken, the regional effects could be pronounced, even as the planet as a whole continued to warm.

Scientists have grown increasingly concerned that the influx of fresh water from melting ice could disrupt this delicate balance. Fresh water is less dense than salt water, and a sufficient quantity poured into the northern seas might slow the sinking process that powers the entire conveyor. Although researchers disagree about how close such a threshold might be, most acknowledge that the circulation has weakened measurably over recent decades.

What makes the prospect so troubling is the difficulty of reversing it. Unlike a switch that can be flicked back on, the deep ocean responds to change over centuries rather than years. A circulation that slows today might not recover within any human timescale, leaving future generations to contend with consequences set in motion long before they were born.

questions.q: Thermohaline circulation is primarily powered by surface winds.
A single body of water may need around a thousand years to travel the full circuit.
The mild winters of north-western Europe are connected to warm northward-flowing water.
Scientists have reached firm agreement on exactly when the circulation will collapse.
The author states that melting ice has already shut down the circulation completely.
What makes weakening of the circulation especially worrying, according to the passage?
options: It can be reversed quickly with effort
It would cool the entire planet
It may take centuries to recover

questions.q: Fresh water is ________ dense than salt water.
The phrase 'silent engine' in the title most likely refers to the circulation's role in

STAFF: Lovely. We run two options: the morning Bay Tour and the afternoon Cliffs Tour. The Bay Tour is for beginners and costs eighteen pounds per person.
CALLER: And the Cliffs one?
STAFF: That's a bit more demanding, twenty-six pounds, and it lasts three hours.
CALLER: I'll take the Bay Tour, please. For two adults.
STAFF: Certainly. Can I take a name?
CALLER: It's Marsden. M-A-R-S-D-E-N.

reading.title: The Hidden Life of Coral Reefs
reading.passage:
Coral reefs occupy less than one per cent of the ocean floor, yet they shelter roughly a quarter of all marine species. This extraordinary concentration of life has earned them the nickname 'the rainforests of the sea'. The reefs themselves are built by tiny animals called polyps, which secrete a hard skeleton of calcium carbonate. Over thousands of years, the accumulated skeletons of countless generations form the vast limestone structures that we recognise as reefs.

What allows corals to flourish is a partnership with microscopic algae known as zooxanthellae, which live within the polyps' tissues. The algae harness sunlight to produce sugars, supplying the coral with most of its energy; in return, the coral offers shelter and the raw materials the algae need. This relationship is so productive that reefs can thrive in tropical waters that are otherwise remarkably poor in nutrients, a fact that long puzzled early naturalists.

The partnership, however, is finely tuned and easily upset. When water temperatures rise even slightly above the usual maximum, the corals expel their algae, losing both their colour and their principal food source. This phenomenon, known as bleaching, leaves the corals pale and vulnerable. If conditions return to normal quickly, the algae may recolonise the tissues and the coral can recover. Prolonged stress, however, can prove fatal, and a once-vibrant reef may be reduced to a graveyard of bare skeletons within weeks.

Efforts to protect reefs now extend beyond simply limiting local damage from fishing and pollution. Some researchers are cultivating heat-tolerant corals in nurseries, hoping to transplant them onto damaged reefs. Others argue that no amount of intervention will succeed unless the underlying warming of the oceans is itself addressed. What remains certain is that the fate of these ancient structures is bound ever more tightly to decisions made far from the water's edge.

options: Global ocean warming must also be tackled
Reefs cannot be helped at all

listening.title: A Talk on Marine Volunteering
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to our reef conservation volunteering programme. Let me run through how it works. The programme lasts four weeks, and volunteers spend most mornings in the water collecting data on coral health. In the afternoons, there are workshops on identifying species and recording results.

Now, accommodation is provided in shared cabins about ten minutes from the beach. Meals are included, but please tell us in advance about any dietary requirements. The registration fee is two hundred and forty dollars, which covers your training, equipment hire and a logbook.

You'll need to bring your own reef-safe sunscreen, as ordinary types can harm the coral. We also recommend a long-sleeved swimming top to protect against the sun. Snorkelling gear is supplied, but if you have your own mask you're welcome to bring it.

One more thing: before you start, you must complete an online safety module. It takes about an hour and finishes with a short quiz. You'll receive the link by email once your place is confirmed. Any questions, just ask me afterwards.

reading.title: The Problem of Ocean Plastics
reading.passage:
Every year, millions of tonnes of plastic make their way into the world's oceans, carried by rivers, blown from coastlines, or discarded directly from ships. Once at sea, this material does not simply vanish. Sunlight and the constant motion of the waves gradually break larger items into ever smaller fragments, until they form particles barely visible to the naked eye. These so-called microplastics now pervade marine environments from the surface to the deepest trenches.

The scale of the problem is difficult to grasp, in part because much of the plastic is invisible. Floating debris tends to gather in great rotating systems of currents called gyres, where it concentrates into loose patches stretching across enormous areas. Yet surveys suggest that the material visible at the surface represents only a small fraction of the total. The remainder is thought to sink, settling on the seabed or becoming lodged in sediments where it may persist for centuries.

The biological consequences are increasingly well documented. Seabirds and turtles mistake floating plastic for food, filling their stomachs with material they cannot digest. Smaller creatures ingest microplastics directly, and these particles can then pass up the food chain as larger animals consume the smaller. Researchers have detected such fragments in fish destined for human consumption, though the long-term effects on people remain uncertain.

Responses to the crisis have varied widely. Some governments have banned single-use items such as bags and straws, while others have invested in technologies designed to collect debris before it reaches the sea. Critics caution, however, that clean-up efforts, however well intentioned, can never keep pace with the volume entering the water. For them, the only durable solution lies in reducing production at source, a change that demands not merely new devices but a rethinking of how societies make and discard everyday goods.

questions.q: Plastic in the ocean breaks down into tiny fragments over time.
Most ocean plastic remains floating and visible at the surface.

options: They are harmless to all animals

listening.title: Lecture on a Beach Clean-up Study
listening.script:
LECTURER: Today I want to share the results of our year-long beach clean-up study along the eastern coast. Over twelve months, volunteers cleaned the same stretch of shoreline once a month and sorted everything they collected.

The most common item, perhaps unsurprisingly, was plastic bottles, which made up about thirty-five per cent of all waste by count. Next came food wrappers, followed by fishing line and lost equipment from boats. Interestingly, the amount of waste rose sharply in the summer, almost certainly because of increased tourism.

We also weighed the collected material. In total, volunteers removed nearly two thousand kilograms over the year. But here's the striking part: when we returned the following month, much of the lightest plastic had already drifted back in on the tide.

This tells us that local cleaning, while valuable for raising awareness, cannot solve the problem alone. Our recommendation is that clean-ups be paired with education programmes in nearby schools. We found that areas with active school programmes showed a measurable drop in litter over the study period.

reading.title: Desalination and the Quest for Fresh Water
reading.passage:
As populations expand and rainfall grows less predictable, many regions face the prospect of running short of fresh water. For nations with long coastlines, the ocean offers a tempting solution. Desalination, the process of removing salt from seawater, has been practised in some form for centuries, but only in recent decades has it become viable on an industrial scale. Today, thousands of plants supply drinking water to cities that would otherwise struggle to meet demand.

The dominant modern technique is reverse osmosis, in which seawater is forced under high pressure through membranes so fine that they allow water molecules to pass while blocking dissolved salts. The method is far more energy-efficient than the older approach of boiling water and collecting the vapour, yet it remains demanding in terms of power. In countries where electricity is generated by burning fossil fuels, this raises an uncomfortable paradox: securing water may worsen the very climate instability that made water scarce in the first place.

Energy is not the only concern. Every litre of fresh water produced leaves behind a quantity of concentrated salt, a thick brine that must be disposed of somewhere. Most plants return it to the sea, but if it is released carelessly it can sink and accumulate, raising local salinity to levels that harm marine life. Careful engineering can reduce this damage, though it adds to the expense.

For all its drawbacks, desalination is likely to expand. Falling costs and improvements in membrane design have brought it within reach of more countries each year. Whether it becomes a sustainable part of the water supply, however, may depend less on the technology itself than on the energy that powers it. A desalination plant running on renewable electricity tells a very different story from one fuelled by coal.

questions.q: Desalination only became practical on a large scale relatively recently.

options: The energy source that powers it

listening.title: Discussing a Water Project Assignment

TUTOR: So, Daniel, you wanted to talk about your project on regional water supply?

DANIEL: That makes sense. Should I include a case study?

TUTOR: The fourteenth of March. But I'd encourage you to submit a draft of your introduction by the end of February so I can give feedback.
DANIEL: I'll aim for that. Thank you.

reading.title: Mapping the Deep Sea Floor
reading.passage:
It is often remarked that we have better maps of the surface of Mars than of the floor of our own oceans. The comparison, though striking, contains a genuine truth. Light cannot penetrate more than a few hundred metres of seawater, and the immense pressures of the deep make direct exploration both difficult and costly. As a result, vast stretches of the seabed have never been examined in detail, and our picture of this hidden landscape remains remarkably incomplete.

The principal tool for charting the deep is sonar, which works by emitting pulses of sound and measuring the time they take to return after striking the bottom. Mounted on ships, modern systems can sweep a wide band of the seabed at once, building up a detailed image as the vessel moves. Yet even with such instruments, surveying the entire ocean is a colossal undertaking. A single ship covering its allotted area might take many years to complete the task, and the cost of keeping such vessels at sea is considerable.

Why does this matter? Accurate maps of the seabed serve purposes far beyond mere curiosity. They are essential for laying the cables that carry the world's internet traffic, for predicting how tsunamis will travel, and for understanding the habitats of creatures that live in the dark. Detailed knowledge of underwater terrain can even improve forecasts of ocean currents, which in turn feed into models of the global climate.

An ambitious international effort now aims to produce a complete map of the ocean floor within a generation. Progress depends partly on persuading commercial vessels and research ships to share the data they gather as they travel. If enough organisations cooperate, a task once thought impossibly vast may at last come within our grasp, revealing a world that has remained, until now, almost entirely unseen.

listening.title: Radio Feature on Ocean Mapping
listening.script:
PRESENTER: This week on Science Now, we're looking at the race to map the ocean floor. I'm joined by oceanographer Dr Lena Ford. Lena, how much of the seabed have we actually mapped in detail?
DR FORD: Surprisingly little. Until recently, only about twenty per cent had been mapped to modern standards. The rest is based on rough estimates from satellites.
PRESENTER: That's remarkable. So how do you go about filling the gaps?
DR FORD: The main method is sonar mounted on ships. But ships are expensive, so increasingly we ask merchant vessels to record data while they sail their normal routes.
PRESENTER: A clever use of existing journeys. What's the biggest challenge?
DR FORD: Honestly, it's coordination. Different countries use different formats, so combining the data is harder than collecting it.
PRESENTER: And the goal?
DR FORD: A complete map by twenty thirty. It's ambitious, but with cooperation it's achievable.
PRESENTER: Fascinating. Thank you, Dr Ford.

reading.title: The Promise of Self-Healing Concrete
reading.passage:
Concrete is the most widely used construction material on Earth, yet it suffers from an inherent flaw: it cracks. Even hairline fissures, invisible to the naked eye, allow water and corrosive agents to penetrate the structure, accelerating the deterioration of the steel reinforcement embedded within. Traditional repair is costly and disruptive, prompting researchers to ask whether concrete might be engineered to mend itself.

One of the most intriguing approaches relies on bacteria. Certain species, notably from the genus Bacillus, can survive in a dormant state for decades. When these spores are mixed into the concrete along with a calcium-based nutrient, they remain inactive until a crack forms and water seeps in. The moisture revives the bacteria, which then metabolise the nutrient and precipitate calcium carbonate, the same compound that constitutes limestone. This mineral gradually fills the crack, effectively sealing it before significant damage can occur.

Laboratory trials have been encouraging. In one study, cracks up to half a millimetre wide were sealed within three weeks, and the healed regions regained a substantial portion of the original compressive strength. Critically, the bacteria pose no threat to humans and remain viable for the lifespan of the structure, lying dormant until needed again.

Despite this promise, commercial adoption has been slow. The bacterial additive can double the cost of the concrete mix, a deterrent for budget-conscious contractors. Engineers are now experimenting with cheaper encapsulation methods and alternative nutrient sources to bring the price down. If these efforts succeed, self-healing concrete could dramatically extend the service life of bridges, tunnels and buildings, reducing both maintenance expenditure and the considerable carbon footprint associated with cement production.

questions.q: Hairline cracks in concrete are too small to allow water to enter.
Bacillus spores can remain dormant in concrete for many years.
The bacteria used in self-healing concrete were originally discovered in limestone caves.
What triggers the bacteria to become active?
options: The presence of water entering a crack
The application of an electric current

questions.q: What is the main obstacle to commercial use of self-healing concrete?
options: It cracks more easily than normal concrete

questions.q: The bacteria produce calcium ________, the compound that forms limestone, to seal cracks.

ASSISTANT: Good morning, Hartley Building Supplies, how can I help?
CUSTOMER: Hello, I'm renovating a patio and I need some advice on materials. Do you stock the self-sealing concrete I've read about?
ASSISTANT: We do carry a self-healing mix, yes. It comes in twenty-five kilogram bags, priced at thirty-two pounds each.
CUSTOMER: That's a bit more than ordinary concrete, isn't it?
ASSISTANT: It is, roughly double, but it lasts considerably longer, so many customers find it worthwhile for outdoor projects.
CUSTOMER: I see. How many bags would I need for a patio of about fifteen square metres?
ASSISTANT: For a standard depth, I'd recommend around eighteen bags. We can deliver them on Thursday if you order today.
CUSTOMER: Thursday works. And is there a delivery charge?
ASSISTANT: Delivery is free for orders over two hundred pounds, so you'll qualify.
CUSTOMER: Excellent. One last thing, how long before I can walk on it?
ASSISTANT: Allow at least forty-eight hours for it to cure properly before any heavy use.
CUSTOMER: Perfect. I'll place the order now. Thank you very much.

reading.title: Shape-Memory Alloys
reading.passage:
Among the more remarkable materials developed in the twentieth century are the so-called shape-memory alloys, metals that can return to a predetermined form after being deformed. The most famous of these is Nitinol, an alloy of nickel and titanium first investigated in the early 1960s at a United States naval laboratory. When a Nitinol component is bent out of shape and subsequently heated above a critical temperature, it springs back to its original geometry as if remembering how it ought to look.

This behaviour arises from a reversible transformation in the crystal structure of the metal. At lower temperatures the alloy adopts a flexible phase that can be readily reshaped; on heating, the atoms rearrange into a more rigid configuration that corresponds to the memorised form. Because the change is driven by temperature rather than by mechanical force, the recovery can exert a surprisingly strong pull, a property exploited in everything from spectacle frames to spacecraft.

The medical field has embraced these alloys with particular enthusiasm. Surgeons use Nitinol stents, tiny mesh tubes that are compressed for insertion into a blocked artery and then expand to their full diameter once warmed by body heat, propping the vessel open. Orthodontic wires made from the same material apply gentle, continuous pressure to reposition teeth, reducing the need for frequent adjustments.

Nevertheless, shape-memory alloys are not without limitations. They are relatively expensive to manufacture, and their performance can degrade after repeated cycles of deformation and recovery, a phenomenon known as fatigue. Researchers continue to refine the composition of these alloys, seeking formulations that combine durability with the seemingly magical ability to remember.

questions.q: Nitinol is composed of nickel and titanium.
Nitinol was first developed in a private commercial company.
Nitinol is more expensive than any other shape-memory alloy currently available.
What causes Nitinol to return to its original shape?
options: Being heated above a critical temperature

questions.q: How do Nitinol stents work inside the body?
options: They expand when warmed by body heat
They are inflated with a pump

questions.q: The gradual decline in performance after repeated cycles is known as ________.

listening.title: Student Consultation on a Lab Report
listening.script:
TUTOR: Come in, take a seat. You wanted to talk about your materials lab report?
STUDENT: Yes, the one on shape-memory alloys. I'm not sure I structured the results section correctly.
TUTOR: Let's have a look. How many samples did you test in total?
STUDENT: I tested twelve samples, heating each one to ninety degrees.
TUTOR: Good. And did you record the recovery percentage for each?
STUDENT: I did. The average recovery was about ninety-four per cent, which seemed quite high.
TUTOR: That's a reasonable figure for Nitinol. My main suggestion is to add a table comparing your results with the published values.
STUDENT: I hadn't thought of that. Where would I find those?
TUTOR: There's a useful reference in the library, section four hundred and twelve. The textbook by Henderson covers it well.
STUDENT: Great. And what about the word count? Mine is currently two thousand words.
TUTOR: The limit is two thousand five hundred, so you have room to expand the discussion. Aim to submit by Friday.
STUDENT: I will. Thanks so much for your help.

reading.title: Graphene: The Wonder Material
reading.passage:
When two physicists at the University of Manchester isolated a single layer of carbon atoms in 2004 using nothing more sophisticated than adhesive tape, they could hardly have anticipated the wave of excitement that would follow. The material they obtained, graphene, is a sheet just one atom thick in which carbon atoms are arranged in a honeycomb lattice. Despite its almost incomprehensible thinness, graphene is extraordinarily strong, conducts electricity better than copper, and is nearly transparent.

The combination of these properties has fuelled speculation about a vast range of applications. Engineers envisage flexible touchscreens that can be rolled up like paper, batteries that charge in seconds, and water-purification membranes capable of filtering out salt and contaminants with remarkable efficiency. Because graphene is composed entirely of carbon, an abundant element, enthusiasts have predicted that it could eventually become cheap to produce in bulk.

Reality, however, has proved more stubborn. Manufacturing graphene of consistent quality and in large quantities remains a formidable challenge. The tiny flakes produced by the original tape method are useless for industrial purposes, and the alternative techniques developed since tend to introduce defects or impurities that diminish the material's celebrated properties. Scaling up production without sacrificing quality has consumed a great deal of research effort and investment.

Even so, graphene has begun to find its way into niche products, from sports equipment to specialised sensors, and the pace of discovery shows no sign of slowing. Whether it ultimately transforms entire industries, as its champions hope, or settles into a more modest role, graphene has already reshaped how scientists think about what a material can be.

questions.q: Graphene was first isolated using highly advanced equipment.
Graphene conducts electricity better than copper.
The two Manchester physicists were awarded a Nobel Prize for their work.
What is the arrangement of carbon atoms in graphene called?
What is described as the main difficulty with graphene?
options: It is too weak for most uses
It is hard to produce in large quantities at consistent quality
It is made from a rare element

questions.q: Graphene is a sheet just one ________ thick.
Alternative production techniques often introduce defects or ________ that reduce graphene's properties.

listening.title: Lecture Extract: Materials for the Future
listening.script: LECTURER: Right, settling in to today's topic, which is the journey of graphene from laboratory curiosity to commercial product. As you'll recall, graphene was first isolated in two thousand and four. What I want to focus on today is the economics of production. A single gram of high-quality graphene can still cost several hundred pounds, which obviously limits its use. Now, there are three main production methods I'll outline. The first is mechanical exfoliation, essentially the tape method, which gives excellent quality but tiny quantities. The second is chemical vapour deposition, abbreviated to CVD, which produces large continuous sheets suitable for electronics. The third is liquid-phase exfoliation, the cheapest option, ideal for bulk applications like composites and coatings. For your assignment, due in week seven, I'd like you to compare two of these methods and assess their suitability for a specific industry. The recommended reading is chapter eight of the course text. Please don't leave it until the last minute, as the analysis takes time.

reading.title: Aerogels: Solids Made Mostly of Air
reading.passage:
Few materials seem to defy intuition as thoroughly as the aerogel. Often nicknamed 'frozen smoke' for its faint blue, almost ghostly appearance, an aerogel is a solid in which up to ninety-nine per cent of the volume consists of air trapped within a delicate skeleton of solid material. The result is a substance so light that a block large enough to fill a hand may weigh less than a feather, yet rigid enough to support objects many thousands of times its own mass.

The earliest aerogels were created in the 1930s by replacing the liquid in a gel with gas, a deceptively simple description of a process that requires great care to avoid collapsing the fragile internal structure. Most aerogels are made from silica, although versions based on carbon and even metal oxides now exist. What unites them is an extraordinary network of tiny pores, far smaller than the wavelength of visible light, which gives the material its translucent quality.

This porous architecture also makes aerogels exceptional insulators. Because heat struggles to travel through the labyrinth of minuscule cavities, a thin layer of aerogel can block warmth as effectively as a far thicker slab of conventional insulation. This property has attracted the attention of spacecraft designers, who have used aerogels to protect instruments from extreme temperatures and, famously, to capture particles of comet dust travelling at high velocity.

The principal drawback has long been fragility: traditional silica aerogels crumble under modest pressure. Recent advances in flexible and reinforced varieties, however, promise to widen their use into everyday settings such as building insulation and lightweight clothing, where their unmatched thermal performance could prove genuinely transformative.

questions.q: Aerogels can consist of up to ninety-nine per cent air by volume.
The first aerogels were produced in the 1930s.
Silica aerogels are more expensive to make than carbon aerogels.
Why are aerogels excellent insulators?
options: Heat travels poorly through their tiny cavities

reading.title: Biodegradable Plastics and the Limits of a Good Idea
reading.passage:
As public concern over plastic pollution has intensified, biodegradable plastics have been promoted as an elegant solution. In principle, these materials break down into harmless natural substances once discarded, sparing the environment the centuries of persistence associated with conventional plastics. Yet the reality is considerably more complicated than the marketing suggests, and a growing chorus of scientists urges caution before such products are embraced uncritically.

The term 'biodegradable' is itself misleading, for it implies a uniform behaviour that does not exist. Many so-called biodegradable plastics decompose only under highly specific conditions, typically the sustained heat and microbial activity found in industrial composting facilities. In a domestic compost heap, or worse, in the open ocean, these same materials may persist almost as stubbornly as ordinary plastic. A consumer who diligently buys biodegradable packaging may therefore achieve nothing if the local waste system lacks the means to process it.

There is a further complication. Biodegradable plastics are often manufactured from crops such as maize or sugar cane. While this reduces dependence on fossil fuels, it raises uncomfortable questions about diverting agricultural land from food production, particularly in regions where food security is already precarious. The environmental accounting is rarely as straightforward as a green label implies.

None of this means that biodegradable plastics are without merit. In carefully managed waste streams, and for particular applications such as agricultural mulching films that are designed to be ploughed into the soil, they can offer genuine benefits. The danger lies in treating them as a universal remedy that absolves consumers and manufacturers of the harder task of reducing plastic consumption in the first place. Technology, in this instance, is no substitute for changes in behaviour.

questions.q: All biodegradable plastics break down easily in any environment.

Biodegradable plastics made from maize are cheaper than those made from sugar cane.
Why might a consumer's choice of biodegradable packaging achieve nothing?
options: The local waste system cannot process it

options: Agricultural land may be diverted from food production

listening.title: Seminar Discussion on Sustainable Packaging
listening.script:
MODERATOR: Welcome to today's seminar on sustainable packaging. We have two panellists. Let's start with you, Priya.
PRIYA: Thanks. My research found that only about thirty per cent of biodegradable packaging actually reaches an industrial composter. The rest ends up in landfill, where it behaves much like ordinary plastic.
MODERATOR: That's a striking figure. Marcus, your perspective?
MARCUS: I'd add that consumer confusion is a major issue. In our survey of five hundred shoppers, nearly two-thirds believed biodegradable plastic would break down in a garden compost heap, which is rarely true.
MODERATOR: So the labelling needs reform?
MARCUS: Absolutely. We're recommending a clearer certification system, ideally a single national standard introduced by twenty twenty-eight.
PRIYA: I agree, though I'd stress that reducing overall consumption matters more than any label.
MODERATOR: A fair point. We'll open the floor for questions in about ten minutes, so please prepare them now.
MARCUS: And copies of our full report are available on the seminar website under the resources tab.

reading.title: The Hidden Cost of Light Pollution
reading.passage:
For most of human history, the night sky was a familiar presence, a canopy of stars that guided navigation and inspired mythology. Today, however, an estimated four-fifths of the world's population lives under skies brightened by artificial illumination, and roughly a third of humanity can no longer see the Milky Way from their homes. This phenomenon, known as light pollution, has expanded so rapidly that satellite measurements suggest the artificially lit area of the planet grows by around two per cent each year. What was once dismissed as a minor aesthetic nuisance is increasingly recognised by ecologists as a serious environmental disturbance.

The consequences for wildlife are particularly striking. Many species have evolved over millions of years to rely on the regular cycle of light and darkness to regulate their behaviour. Sea turtles, for instance, hatch on beaches and instinctively crawl towards the brightest horizon, which under natural conditions is the faint glow of the sea reflecting the night sky. Coastal development now floods these beaches with brighter artificial light, causing disoriented hatchlings to head inland, where they die from exhaustion or predation. Migratory birds, which navigate partly by starlight, are similarly drawn off course, sometimes circling illuminated buildings until they collapse.

Insects, too, suffer dramatically. The familiar sight of moths swarming around a streetlamp masks a quieter tragedy: trapped in the artificial glow, these insects neither feed nor reproduce, and many die before dawn. Because insects underpin food chains and pollinate countless plants, their decline has knock-on effects that ripple outward through entire ecosystems.

Encouragingly, light pollution is among the most reversible of environmental problems. Unlike carbon emissions, which linger in the atmosphere for decades, excess light vanishes the instant a lamp is switched off. Simple measures such as shielding fixtures so they point downwards, using warmer-toned bulbs, and dimming lights during the small hours can dramatically reduce the glow without compromising safety. A growing number of towns have embraced such reforms, and several regions have established 'dark sky reserves' where artificial lighting is tightly controlled. These efforts demonstrate that, with modest investment, communities can reclaim the night sky for both people and wildlife.

questions.q: Approximately one third of people worldwide cannot see the Milky Way from where they live.
Light pollution was immediately classified as a serious ecological threat when first observed.
Scientists have calculated the exact financial cost of light pollution to coastal economies.
Why do newly hatched sea turtles move inland in developed coastal areas?
options: Artificial light is brighter than the sea's glow
The sand is cooler away from the shore

questions.q: What point does the passage make about insects around streetlamps?
options: They are attracted because of warmth
Their swarming hides a serious population threat
They reproduce more successfully near light
They are harmless to local ecosystems

questions.q: Unlike carbon emissions, excess light disappears the moment a lamp is ________.
