reading.title: Restoring the World's Mangroves
reading.passage:
Mangroves are among the most productive and least appreciated ecosystems on Earth. These salt-tolerant trees thrive in the brackish waters where rivers meet the sea, anchoring their tangled roots in soft mud that few other plants can endure. For centuries, coastal communities regarded mangrove swamps as worthless wasteland, fit only to be cleared for shrimp farms, rice paddies or urban expansion. As a result, the planet lost more than a third of its mangrove forests during the twentieth century, much of it in tropical Asia.

Scientists now understand that this loss was profoundly short-sighted. Mangroves provide a remarkable array of services that benefit both nature and people. Their dense root systems act as nurseries for fish, shielding juveniles from larger predators until they are mature enough to venture into open water. The same roots trap sediment, gradually building up land and protecting coastlines from erosion. During storms, a healthy belt of mangroves can absorb the force of incoming waves, reducing the height of a storm surge by a significant margin and sparing communities behind them.

Perhaps most striking is the role mangroves play in storing carbon. Because the waterlogged soil in which they grow is starved of oxygen, organic matter decays extremely slowly. Carbon that would otherwise return to the atmosphere is instead locked away in the mud, sometimes for thousands of years. Hectare for hectare, mangroves can store several times more carbon than tropical rainforests, making their preservation a powerful tool against climate change.

These insights have sparked a wave of restoration projects, though success is far from guaranteed. Early efforts often failed because volunteers planted seedlings in unsuitable locations, choosing sites that were too deep or too exposed. More recent schemes take a subtler approach, restoring the natural flow of water so that mangroves can recolonise an area on their own. This method, which works with nature rather than against it, has proved both cheaper and more durable. Where it has been applied carefully, degraded coastlines have been transformed within a decade, offering hope that one of the planet's most valuable habitats can yet be saved.

questions.q: More than a third of the world's mangroves disappeared in the 1900s.
Mangrove roots provide protection for young fish from predators.
Mangrove wood is now the most valuable timber traded in tropical Asia.
Why does carbon remain trapped in mangrove soil for so long?
options: The soil is too cold for decay

questions.q: Why did many early restoration projects fail?
options: The trees were attacked by pests
Seedlings were planted in unsuitable sites

questions.q: A healthy belt of mangroves can reduce the height of a storm ________.

listening.title: Volunteering at a Wetland Reserve
listening.script:
COORDINATOR: Thanks for coming in. So you'd like to volunteer at the Riverbend Wetland Reserve?
VOLUNTEER: Yes, I've got most weekends free.
COORDINATOR: Brilliant. We run three main programs. The first is seedling planting, which happens on Saturday mornings from nine until noon.
VOLUNTEER: That suits me well.
COORDINATOR: The second is water quality monitoring. You'd collect samples and record readings. We train you to use the testing kits, so no experience needed.
VOLUNTEER: Sounds interesting. And the third?
COORDINATOR: Bird counting, every fortnight on a Sunday. We're tracking around forty species at the moment, and the data goes to a national database.
VOLUNTEER: I'd love to try the planting and the bird counting.
COORDINATOR: Great choices. One thing to note: the reserve can get muddy, so wear old clothes and bring a hat and sunscreen.
VOLUNTEER: Will do. Where exactly do we meet?
COORDINATOR: At the visitor centre on Kestrel Lane. Parking is free, and there's a bus, the number twelve, that stops right outside.
VOLUNTEER: Perfect. When can I start?
COORDINATOR: This Saturday, if you like. Just arrive a little before nine.

reading.title: The Return of the Beaver
reading.passage:
When the beaver vanished from much of Europe, hunted to local extinction for its fur and a glandular secretion once prized in medicine, few mourned its disappearance. The animal was widely regarded as a destructive pest, capable of felling trees and flooding farmland with its dams. For several centuries, rivers across the continent flowed without the engineering of this industrious rodent, and the landscape adjusted accordingly. Only recently have scientists begun to grasp how much was lost when the beaver departed.

The beaver is what ecologists call a keystone species: an organism whose influence on its environment far exceeds what its modest size might suggest. By building dams, beavers create ponds and wetlands that store water during dry spells and slow its passage during floods. These wetlands become havens for an astonishing diversity of life, from dragonflies and amphibians to wading birds and otters. In effect, a single family of beavers can reshape an entire valley, generating habitats that countless other creatures depend upon.

Reintroduction schemes, cautious at first, have spread across Europe over the past few decades. The results have frequently exceeded expectations. In several catchments, the dams built by reintroduced beavers have measurably improved water quality, filtering out sediment and pollutants before they reach rivers downstream. During heavy rainfall, the ponds hold back surges of water, reducing the risk of flooding in towns further along the valley. Such natural flood management can prove far cheaper than the concrete barriers that engineers traditionally favour.

Not everyone welcomes the beaver's comeback. Farmers whose fields border rivers sometimes find waterlogged land or blocked drainage channels, and conflicts over compensation can be heated. Conservationists argue, however, that with sensible management these tensions can be resolved, and that the broader benefits to water security and biodiversity are too valuable to ignore. The beaver, once dismissed as a nuisance, is increasingly seen as a partner in restoring the health of Europe's degraded rivers.

questions.q: The beaver was once hunted partly for a substance used in medicine.
A keystone species has an impact greater than its size would suggest.
Beaver fur is currently more valuable than it was in the past.
How do beaver dams help with water during dry periods?
options: They store water in ponds and wetlands

options: It is more attractive to tourists

listening.title: A Lecture on River Restoration
listening.script: LECTURER: Today I want to look at how we restore damaged rivers. For most of the last century, engineers straightened rivers to drain land quickly, but this caused serious problems. A straightened river flows faster, eroding its banks and carrying away valuable soil. It also offers little habitat for fish, since there are no quiet pools or shaded edges. So modern restoration often means reversing that process, allowing rivers to meander once more. One key technique is adding large pieces of dead wood to the channel. This may look untidy, but the wood slows the current, creates sheltered hollows, and traps gravel that fish use for spawning. Studies show fish numbers can rise by up to sixty per cent within three years of such work. Another approach is reconnecting a river to its floodplain, so that excess water can spread out harmlessly rather than rushing downstream. This reduces flood risk and recharges underground water supplies. Finally, removing old, redundant dams allows migrating fish such as salmon to reach their breeding grounds. One project in Scotland recorded salmon returning to a tributary within a single season of dam removal. The lesson is clear: a wilder, messier river is usually a healthier one.

reading.title: Cities Designed for Bees
reading.passage:
It might seem paradoxical, but some of the most promising refuges for declining pollinators are not remote nature reserves but busy cities. As industrial agriculture has spread across the countryside, vast monocultures of a single crop have replaced the patchwork of hedgerows, meadows and field margins that once sustained bees and butterflies. Drenched in pesticides and offering flowers for only a few weeks each year, much modern farmland has become a hostile environment for pollinators. Urban areas, by contrast, can offer a surprisingly rich and varied buffet.

The reason lies in the diversity of city landscapes. A single street may contain dozens of garden species, flowering at different times, alongside parks, allotments, cemeteries and the spontaneous vegetation of neglected corners. Because gardeners plant for colour throughout the seasons, urban pollinators often enjoy a longer and more reliable supply of nectar than their rural counterparts. Cities also tend to be a degree or two warmer than the surrounding land, extending the period in which insects can remain active.

Recognising this potential, a number of urban planners have begun to design with pollinators explicitly in mind. Strips of wildflowers are sown along roadsides and in roundabouts, where they require little maintenance and brighten otherwise drab spaces. Some cities have created continuous 'bee corridors', chains of flower-rich sites that allow insects to move safely across the urban landscape. Rooftops, long regarded as dead space, are being planted with hardy flowering plants that double as insulation for the buildings beneath.

The benefits extend well beyond the insects themselves. Pollinator-friendly planting tends to be cheaper to maintain than closely mown grass, since wildflower meadows need cutting only once or twice a year. Residents report that the colourful verges improve their sense of wellbeing, and schools use them to teach children about ecology. Critics caution that these initiatives, however welcome, cannot by themselves reverse the wider decline of insects, which is driven largely by what happens on farmland. Still, in demonstrating that human settlements need not be barren, the greening of cities offers both practical help and a powerful change of attitude.

questions.q: Modern farmland often provides flowers for only a short part of the year.
Cities tend to be slightly warmer than the surrounding countryside.

options: Gardeners plant for colour across the seasons

options: To let insects move safely across the city

questions.q: Wildflower meadows are cheaper to maintain because they need cutting only once or ________ a year.

listening.title: Planning a Community Garden
listening.script:
MIA: So, Tom, we finally got the council's approval for the community garden. Where do we begin?
TOM: Well, first we should decide on the layout. I think we need separate areas: vegetable beds, a wildflower patch, and a small seating space.
MIA: Agreed. The wildflower patch is important for the pollinators. How big should it be?
TOM: Maybe a quarter of the total plot. The council suggested at least twenty square meters.
MIA: Okay. And what about water? Carrying buckets sounds exhausting.
TOM: I've been thinking about that. We could install a rainwater tank that collects runoff from the shed roof. It holds about five hundred liters.
MIA: Perfect. That should cover dry spells. Now, who's going to maintain it all?
TOM: I've drawn up a rota. Each member takes one weekend a month. With twelve members signed up, the garden's covered all year.
MIA: Smart. When's our first work day?
TOM: I was thinking the fourteenth of April, a Saturday. We'll need spades, gloves, and seeds.
MIA: I'll bring the seeds. Let's send a message to everyone tonight.

reading.title: The Quiet Crisis in the Soil
reading.passage:
When we picture threatened environments, we usually imagine melting glaciers or vanishing rainforests. Few people think of the ground beneath their feet. Yet soil, that thin and unglamorous layer covering much of the land, is one of the planet's most vital and most endangered resources. A single teaspoon of healthy soil contains more living organisms than there are human beings on Earth, an invisible community of bacteria, fungi and tiny invertebrates that recycle nutrients and sustain the plants on which all terrestrial life depends.

This living foundation is being lost at an alarming rate. Intensive farming, with its heavy machinery and reliance on chemical fertilisers, strips the soil of organic matter and compacts it until water can no longer penetrate. Once exposed, bare earth is easily washed away by rain or blown off by wind. By some estimates, the world loses fertile topsoil far faster than natural processes can replace it, since the formation of a single centimetre of soil may take several centuries. The consequences are not confined to farmers: degraded soil holds less water, worsening both floods and droughts, and releases stored carbon into the atmosphere.

The encouraging news is that soil can recover, and often surprisingly quickly, when farmers change their methods. Practices grouped under the heading of regenerative agriculture aim to rebuild the soil rather than simply extract from it. Leaving crop residues on the surface protects the ground from erosion and feeds the organisms below. Planting 'cover crops' between harvests keeps living roots in the soil year-round, while reducing or eliminating ploughing avoids disturbing the delicate underground web of life. Grazing animals, if moved frequently, can even stimulate grasses to grow deeper roots.

What makes these approaches compelling is that they tend to benefit farmers as well as the environment. Healthier soil retains moisture, requires fewer expensive chemicals and can produce stable yields even in difficult years. As awareness grows, a movement once confined to a handful of pioneers is attracting mainstream attention. Restoring the soil, advocates argue, may prove one of the most overlooked yet achievable tools in the broader effort to stabilise the climate and secure the world's food supply.

questions.q: A teaspoon of healthy soil contains more organisms than there are people on Earth.
It can take several centuries to form a single centimetre of soil.
Regenerative agriculture was first developed by university scientists.
How does intensive farming damage soil structure?
options: By adding too much organic matter
By compacting it so water cannot penetrate

questions.q: What is the purpose of planting 'cover crops' between harvests?
options: To sell as an extra source of income
To keep living roots in the soil year-round

questions.q: Degraded soil holds less water, worsening both floods and ________.

According to the passage, regenerative methods usually harm farmers' profits.

listening.title: A Seminar on Sustainable Farming
listening.script: SPEAKER: Welcome to today's seminar on sustainable farming. I'll outline four principles that underpin healthy soil, and I'd encourage you to note them down. The first principle is minimal disturbance. Every time we plough a field, we break apart the fungal networks that bind the soil together, so the goal is to plough as little as possible. The second is keeping the soil covered at all times, whether with growing plants or with the residue of the previous crop. Bare soil, remember, is vulnerable soil. The third principle is diversity. A field that grows only one crop year after year exhausts particular nutrients, whereas rotating several crops keeps the soil balanced. Our trials showed yields improving by around fifteen per cent under rotation. The fourth principle is integrating livestock. When animals graze and are then moved on, their manure returns nutrients to the ground naturally. One farm in our study cut its fertiliser costs by half within two years. Finally, a word of caution: these methods require patience. The soil may take three to four seasons to respond fully, so don't expect overnight miracles. Are there any questions before we visit the demonstration plot?

reading.title: The Quiet Spread of Facial Recognition
reading.passage:
Facial recognition technology has migrated from research laboratories into the fabric of everyday life with remarkable speed. A decade ago, the ability to identify a stranger from a single photograph was largely confined to specialised security agencies. Today, comparable systems are embedded in smartphones, retail outlets and public transport networks, often operating without the explicit awareness of those being scanned. This expansion has been driven less by a single breakthrough than by the convergence of cheaper cameras, vast image databases and increasingly efficient machine-learning models.

Proponents argue that the technology offers tangible conveniences. Travellers can pass through airport gates without producing documents, and shoppers can authorise payments with a glance. Police forces in several countries have reported that automated matching shortens investigations that once took weeks. Yet the same characteristics that make the technology useful also make it troubling. Because a face cannot easily be changed or concealed, errors carry lasting consequences, and surveillance can be conducted at a distance without consent.

Researchers have repeatedly documented uneven accuracy across different demographic groups. A widely cited audit found that some commercial systems misidentified darker-skinned women far more often than lighter-skinned men, raising concerns that flawed tools might entrench existing inequalities rather than remove them. Vendors maintain that newer models have narrowed these gaps, but independent verification remains scarce, partly because companies guard their training data closely.

Regulators have responded unevenly. A handful of cities have banned public-sector use of the technology altogether, while national governments elsewhere have encouraged its deployment as a tool of economic competitiveness. The resulting patchwork means that a practice forbidden in one jurisdiction may be routine a short distance away. Whether a coherent international standard will emerge remains uncertain, but the pressure to establish one is mounting as the systems become ever more pervasive.

questions.q: Facial recognition systems are now sometimes used without people realising they are being scanned.
The rapid spread of the technology resulted mainly from one decisive scientific discovery.
Most travellers prefer facial recognition gates to traditional document checks.
According to the passage, why are errors in facial recognition especially serious?
options: Cameras are too expensive to replace
A face cannot easily be altered or hidden

questions.q: What does the passage say about vendors' claims of improved accuracy?
options: They have been confirmed by independent audits
They are difficult to verify because data is kept private
They apply only to airport systems

questions.q: A cited audit found that some systems misidentified darker-skinned ________ far more often than lighter-skinned men.
A few cities have ________ public-sector use of the technology completely.

listening.title: Reporting a Lost Travel Card
listening.script:
CLERK: Good afternoon, transport help desk. How can I help you today?

CALLER: Hi, I've lost my travel card and I'd like to report it before someone uses it.

CLERK: Of course. Can I take your full name, please?

CALLER: It's Daniel Forsythe. That's F-O-R-S-Y-T-H-E.

CLERK: Thank you. And the card number, if you have it?

CALLER: Yes, it's four six two, nine, zero one.

CLERK: Right, four six two nine zero one. When did you last use it?

CALLER: On Tuesday, around half past eight in the morning, at the Riverside station.

CLERK: I can block it immediately. A replacement costs twelve pounds, and it should arrive within five working days.

CALLER: That's fine. Can I keep the same balance? I think I had about thirty pounds on it.

CLERK: Yes, the balance transfers automatically once the old card is cancelled. I'll send a confirmation to your email. Could you spell that for me?

CALLER: It's d-forsythe, all one word, at mailbox dot com.

CLERK: Lovely. Your reference number is seven seven three one. Keep that safe in case you need to call us back.

reading.title: Gig Work and the Algorithm as Manager
reading.passage:
For millions of people, the boss is no longer a person but a piece of software. Ride-hailing drivers, food couriers and freelance designers increasingly receive their tasks, instructions and evaluations through digital platforms that allocate work according to opaque rules. This arrangement, frequently described as algorithmic management, promises flexibility while quietly transferring many of the burdens once shouldered by employers onto the workers themselves.

The appeal is genuine. Workers can, in principle, log on whenever they wish and decline assignments that do not suit them. For students, carers and those seeking supplementary income, such autonomy is valuable. Platforms also lower the barriers to entry: a person with a vehicle or a laptop can begin earning within days rather than navigating lengthy recruitment processes.

Closer inspection, however, reveals constraints that undercut this picture of freedom. Pay is often calculated by formulas that workers cannot see, and ratings supplied by customers can determine whether someone continues to receive offers at all. A driver who declines too many trips may find that the system quietly reduces the number of requests sent their way, a penalty that is never formally announced. Because the rules are concealed within proprietary code, challenging an unfair outcome is exceptionally difficult.

Several governments have begun to scrutinise these practices, debating whether platform workers should be reclassified as employees entitled to minimum wages and sick pay. Companies resist, warning that such obligations would raise prices and reduce the very flexibility workers value. The dispute exposes a deeper question about the future of labour: when management is automated and dispersed across millions of transactions, who is ultimately accountable for the conditions under which people work? The answer is likely to shape employment for a generation.

questions.q: The rules used by platforms to assign work are usually transparent to workers.
Platforms generally make it faster to start earning than traditional jobs do.
Most gig workers say they would prefer to be classified as employees.
What may happen to a driver who refuses too many trips?
options: They are formally warned in writing
The system may quietly send them fewer requests

questions.q: Why is it hard for workers to challenge unfair outcomes?
options: The rules are hidden in proprietary code

questions.q: The arrangement is often called ________ management.
Companies warn that new obligations would raise ________.

listening.script:
STAFF: Welcome to Harbour Hub co-working. Are you after a desk for the day or a longer membership?

VISITOR: Just a day to start with, thanks. Maybe a quiet spot, I've got calls.

STAFF: No worries. We've got the focus zone on level two. A day pass there is twenty-five dollars, and it includes coffee and the meeting pods.

VISITOR: That sounds good. Do I need to book the pods separately?

STAFF: For one or two people, no. For groups you book online, but there's no extra charge for members.

VISITOR: Great. What are the opening hours?

STAFF: We open at seven in the morning and close at nine in the evening, seven days a week.

VISITOR: Perfect. And the wifi password?

STAFF: It's printed on the card I'll give you, but it's harbour two thousand, all lowercase, no spaces.

VISITOR: Got it. Can I pay by card?

STAFF: Card or phone, both fine. I'll just need your email to send the receipt and the door code.

VISITOR: It's jordan dot lee at workmail dot net.

STAFF: Lovely. Your desk number is fourteen, just up the stairs and to your left.

Someone must be accountable for the harm.
The dispute went on for months.

reading.title: When Misinformation Travels Faster Than Truth
reading.passage:
The architecture of modern social platforms is engineered to maximise engagement, and engagement, it turns out, is not a neutral measure. Posts that provoke strong emotional reactions are rewarded with wider distribution, regardless of whether they are accurate. A celebrated study of millions of online messages found that false claims spread substantially faster and reached more people than verified information, not because of automated accounts, but because ordinary users found the falsehoods more novel and shared them more eagerly.

This dynamic poses a problem that cannot be solved by fact-checking alone. By the time a correction is issued, the original claim has often circulated for days, lodging itself in memory through sheer repetition. Psychologists describe a phenomenon in which people continue to rely on discredited information even after they have explicitly acknowledged that it was false, a stubborn effect that simple retractions rarely overcome.

Platforms have experimented with various remedies. Some attach warning labels to disputed posts; others reduce the visibility of content flagged by independent reviewers or prompt users to read an article before resharing it. Early evidence suggests that gentle interruptions of this kind can modestly reduce the spread of dubious material, though their long-term influence is harder to measure and may diminish as users grow accustomed to them.

Critics counter that the deeper issue lies in the incentive structures of the platforms themselves. As long as attention is the currency that generates revenue, they argue, any design that maximises attention will tend to amplify the sensational at the expense of the accurate. Reforming the underlying business model, rather than patching its symptoms, may be the only durable solution, yet it is also the one that companies have the least commercial reason to pursue.

questions.q: According to the study, false claims spread mainly because of automated accounts.
People sometimes continue to rely on information they know to be false.
Warning labels have completely stopped the spread of false posts.
Why does fact-checking alone fail to solve the problem?
options: By the time a correction appears, the claim has already spread widely

questions.q: What do critics identify as the deeper problem?
Users found false claims more ________ and shared them more eagerly.
Some platforms attach warning ________ to disputed posts.
Reforming the business model is something companies are strongly motivated to do.

listening.title: A Lecture on Digital Memory
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good morning. Today we're looking at how the internet reshapes the way we remember things. Now, decades ago, psychologists assumed memory was largely internal. But research increasingly suggests we offload information onto external tools, a process called transactive memory. In one well-known experiment, participants who expected to be able to look something up later remembered the facts themselves less well, but they remembered where to find them better. So the search engine becomes, in a sense, a partner in remembering.

This has practical consequences. Students who photograph a museum exhibit often recall it less vividly than those who simply observe it, a finding sometimes called the photo-taking impairment effect. The act of delegating the memory to the camera seems to reduce the attention we pay in the moment.

Now, this is not necessarily bad. Freeing our minds from storing trivial details may leave more capacity for analysis and creativity. But it does mean our knowledge becomes dependent on our devices. For your assignment, due on the fifteenth, I'd like you to keep a one-week log of every time you look something up rather than recalling it. We'll discuss the patterns next session.

questions.q: Offloading information onto external tools is called ________ memory.
What did participants who expected to look things up later do?
options: Remembered the facts less but recalled where to find them

questions.q: Photographing an exhibit can cause the photo-taking ________ effect.
What possible benefit of offloading memory does the lecturer mention?
options: More capacity for analysis and creativity

Platform | Always check | Sometimes | Never
Messaging apps | 18% | 47% | 35%
Video sites | 24% | 51% | 25%
Microblogs | 31% | 49% | 20%
News websites | 58% | 36% | 6%

reading.title: The Hidden Cost of the Cloud
reading.passage:
The language we use to describe digital storage is curiously weightless. We speak of saving files to the cloud, of streaming films from the ether, as though our data floated free of any physical foundation. In reality, every search, message and video is processed in vast warehouses crammed with servers that consume prodigious quantities of electricity and water. These data centres now account for a meaningful share of global power demand, and their footprint is expanding as artificial intelligence, which is especially energy-intensive, becomes embedded in everyday services.

The environmental implications are easy to overlook precisely because they are concealed. A user who deletes an unnecessary email saves a negligible amount of energy, but the aggregate behaviour of billions of users is far from trivial. Training a single large language model can emit as much carbon as several transatlantic flights, and the cooling systems that prevent servers from overheating draw heavily on local water supplies, occasionally straining communities already facing scarcity.

Operators are not indifferent to these pressures. Many of the largest companies have pledged to run on renewable energy and have invested in more efficient cooling and chip designs. Some locate their facilities in cold climates to reduce cooling costs, or recycle waste heat to warm nearby buildings. Yet efficiency gains have repeatedly been outpaced by surging demand, a pattern in which improvements paradoxically encourage greater overall consumption.

Whether digital growth can be reconciled with environmental limits remains an open question. Optimists point to rapid advances in clean energy and to the potential for technology itself to cut emissions elsewhere. Sceptics reply that the industry's appetite is effectively boundless, and that no amount of efficiency will help if the total volume of computation continues to climb without restraint. The debate, like the infrastructure it concerns, is only growing larger.

questions.q: Data centres use significant amounts of both electricity and water.
The environmental costs of digital storage are obvious to most users.
Governments have introduced taxes on data centre water use.
Why are efficiency improvements not solving the problem?
options: They are too expensive to implement

questions.q: What do some operators do with waste heat?
options: Use it to warm nearby buildings

questions.q: Training a single large language model can emit as much carbon as several transatlantic ________.

listening.title: A Talk on Sustainable Computing
listening.script:
PRESENTER: Thanks for coming. I want to spend the next few minutes on what we can actually do to make computing greener, both at home and at work. Let's start with the obvious one: hardware lasts longer than people think. The biggest carbon cost of a laptop is often its manufacture, not its use, so keeping a device for, say, five years instead of two makes a real difference.

Second, where your data lives matters. Providers powered by renewable energy can cut the footprint of the same task by more than half. So when you choose a service, it's worth asking what powers it.

Third, and this surprises people, the screen is a major drain. Lowering brightness and using dark mode can extend battery life noticeably on many devices.

Now, at the organisational level, we're trialling a scheme to switch off idle machines overnight. Early figures suggest we could cut office energy use by around eighteen per cent. If you'd like to join the pilot, sign up at the desk by Friday, and we'll send you a short guide. Any questions, my email is on the final slide.

reading.title: Designing Cities Around Data
reading.passage:
The phrase 'smart city' conjures images of seamless efficiency: traffic that flows because lights anticipate congestion, bins that signal when they are full, and energy grids that balance themselves in real time. Behind these conveniences lies an unprecedented effort to instrument the urban environment with sensors, cameras and connected devices, all feeding data into systems that promise to make cities cleaner, safer and more responsive. The vision is seductive, and billions have been invested in pursuit of it.

The gains can be concrete. In some pilot districts, intelligent traffic management has measurably cut journey times and emissions, while sensor-driven maintenance has allowed authorities to repair water pipes before they burst. Such successes demonstrate that careful, targeted use of data can improve services that residents genuinely rely upon, often at lower long-term cost than conventional approaches.

Yet enthusiasm has frequently outrun evidence. A number of flagship projects have stalled or been quietly abandoned, undone by costs that ballooned beyond forecasts and by technologies that proved less reliable than their vendors implied. More fundamentally, critics warn that the relentless collection of data about citizens' movements and habits creates infrastructures of surveillance that may persist long after the original purpose has faded. Who owns this data, who may access it, and how long it is retained are questions too often deferred until after the sensors are already in the ground.

The most thoughtful proponents now argue for a humbler conception of the smart city, one that treats technology as a tool in service of clearly defined public goals rather than as an end in itself. Under this view, the success of a city should be judged not by the sophistication of its systems but by whether ordinary residents find their daily lives demonstrably better. Technology, in other words, must remain answerable to the people it claims to serve.

questions.q: Some smart-city pilots have reduced both journey times and emissions.
Every flagship smart-city project has been a clear success.
Smart-city technology has been most widely adopted in coastal cities.
What caused some flagship projects to fail?
options: Costs that exceeded forecasts and unreliable technology

questions.q: What do the most thoughtful proponents now argue?
options: Technology should serve clearly defined public goals

questions.q: Sensor-driven maintenance let authorities repair water pipes before they ________.
Questions about data ownership are often ________ until after the sensors are installed.

listening.title: Seminar on Urban Sensors
listening.script:
TUTOR: Right, let's pick up where we left off on urban sensing. Last week we talked about what cities measure; today I want us to think about the trade-offs. So, take air-quality monitoring. Cheap sensors let a council put hundreds of units across a city instead of a handful of expensive stations. The catch is that cheap sensors drift, meaning their readings become inaccurate over time unless they're regularly calibrated.

Now, calibration costs money and labour, so there's a tension between coverage and accuracy. One city in our case study chose density over precision and accepted a margin of error of about ten per cent. Was that the right call? That's what I want you to debate.

Second issue: data retention. The same study found the council kept location data for two years, far longer than needed for the stated purpose. After public pressure, they cut it to ninety days.

For next week, read chapter seven and come ready to argue one side. And remember, your group presentation slots open for booking on Monday, so don't leave it late.

options: Their readings drift and become inaccurate

options: Read chapter seven and prepare to argue a side

reading.title: The Architecture of Habit
reading.passage:
Habits occupy a curious position in human psychology. Although we tend to credit our daily actions to conscious choice, researchers estimate that a substantial proportion of behaviour unfolds automatically, without deliberate intent. This automaticity is not a flaw but an efficiency: the brain conserves limited cognitive resources by delegating repetitive sequences to subcortical structures, freeing the conscious mind for novel problems.

The formation of a habit is often described as a loop comprising a cue, a routine, and a reward. A cue is any trigger that prompts the brain to initiate a behaviour; the routine is the behaviour itself; and the reward is the satisfaction that reinforces the loop. Over time, the brain begins to anticipate the reward as soon as the cue appears, which generates a craving that drives the behaviour forward. Crucially, once this circuit is established, the original reward becomes less important than the anticipatory craving it produced.

This insight has practical consequences. Because cues and rewards are difficult to eliminate entirely, behavioural scientists argue that the most reliable way to change an entrenched habit is not to suppress it but to substitute a new routine while keeping the same cue and reward. A person who reaches for a cigarette when stressed, for instance, might insert a brief walk in its place, preserving the relief the original behaviour delivered.

Not all habits are equally tractable, however. So-called keystone habits appear to exert disproportionate influence, setting off a cascade of secondary changes. Regular exercise, for example, has been linked to improvements in diet, sleep and even spending discipline, although the mechanisms remain debated. Whether such effects reflect genuine causation or merely the conscientious personalities of those who exercise is a question that continues to divide the field.

questions.q: Researchers believe that automatic behaviour represents a defect in human cognition.
Once a habit loop is established, the anticipatory craving can matter more than the reward itself.
Keystone habits have been shown to reduce stress more effectively than other habits.
According to the passage, the brain delegates repetitive behaviour to subcortical structures in order to
options: preserve cognitive resources for new problems

questions.q: The recommended strategy for changing an entrenched habit is to
options: replace the routine while retaining the cue and reward
ignore the craving until it disappears
reduce the size of the reward

questions.q: A habit loop is typically described as consisting of a cue, a routine and a ________.
Habits such as regular ________ are thought to trigger a cascade of secondary improvements.

listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, you've reached the University Sleep and Behaviour Lab. How can I help?

CALLER: Brilliant, thank you very much.

reading.title: The Limits of Memory
reading.passage:
Human memory is frequently imagined as a recording device that faithfully preserves experience for later replay. Decades of research, however, suggest a far less reassuring picture. Rather than storing intact copies of events, the brain reconstructs them each time they are recalled, assembling fragments of sensory detail, prior knowledge and present expectation into a plausible narrative. This reconstructive quality makes memory remarkably flexible, but also vulnerable to distortion.

One of the most striking demonstrations of this vulnerability concerns the false memory. In carefully controlled experiments, participants have been persuaded that they experienced childhood events that never occurred, from spilling punch at a wedding to becoming lost in a shopping centre. Merely encouraging a person to imagine such an episode, repeatedly and in vivid detail, can be enough to plant a conviction that the event was real. The implications for eyewitness testimony are considerable, since confidence in a memory bears little relation to its accuracy.

Memory is also shaped by the manner in which it is queried. In a classic line of research, witnesses who were asked how fast cars were going when they 'smashed' into each other reported higher speeds, and were more likely to recall broken glass, than those asked about cars that merely 'hit'. The phrasing of a single question had subtly rewritten the remembered scene.

Such findings need not be cause for despair. The same plasticity that exposes memory to error also underlies its usefulness, allowing us to update beliefs, integrate new information and extract general lessons from particular experiences. A perfectly literal memory, recording every detail without interpretation, would in fact be a burden, leaving us unable to distinguish what matters from what does not.

questions.q: The brain stores complete, unaltered copies of past events.
Researchers have convinced people that fictional childhood events actually happened to them.
False memories are more common in older adults than in younger adults.
The study involving the words 'smashed' and 'hit' demonstrated that
options: the wording of a question can alter what is remembered

options: is unrelated to whether it is correct

reading.title: The Bystander Effect Reconsidered
reading.passage:
Few ideas in social psychology have proved as durable, or as misunderstood, as the bystander effect. The classic formulation holds that the more witnesses there are to an emergency, the less likely any individual is to intervene. Responsibility, the theory proposes, becomes diffused across the crowd, so that each onlooker assumes someone else will act. For decades, introductory textbooks presented this as a settled and rather bleak truth about human nature.

The phenomenon was originally inspired by a notorious urban incident in which, according to early reports, dozens of neighbours allegedly failed to assist a woman under attack. Later investigation cast serious doubt on these accounts, revealing that several witnesses had in fact called for help and that the number of passive observers had been greatly exaggerated. The episode became a cautionary tale about how a compelling narrative can outrun the evidence supporting it.

More recent work has complicated the picture further. Analyses of real-world surveillance footage suggest that in genuine public conflicts, intervention is in fact the norm rather than the exception, with someone stepping in to help in the large majority of cases. The presence of others, it appears, can sometimes encourage assistance by providing reassurance that action is appropriate.

This does not mean the original research was worthless. Controlled laboratory studies do show that responsibility can be diffused under particular conditions, especially when an emergency is ambiguous and bystanders cannot see one another's reactions. The lesson is subtler than the textbook version: whether a crowd helps or hinders depends on the clarity of the situation and the social signals available to those within it.

questions.q: The classic bystander effect predicts that larger crowds make individual intervention less likely.

options: reassure individuals that helping is appropriate

questions.q: The theory claims that responsibility becomes ________ across the crowd.

VOLUNTEER: That's clever. How long is the course?

COORDINATOR: It runs over two Saturdays, from ten until four. The fee is forty pounds, but it's waived if you commit to volunteering afterwards.

VOLUNTEER: I'd be happy to volunteer.

VOLUNTEER: Got it. Where exactly is the venue?

COORDINATOR: The Beech Street community hall, room four.

reading.title: The Psychology of Procrastination
reading.passage:
Procrastination is commonly dismissed as a simple failure of willpower, a matter of laziness that discipline alone could remedy. Psychologists who study the behaviour, however, increasingly regard it as a problem of emotion rather than time management. People do not delay tasks because they are incapable of working; they delay because the prospect of a task arouses unpleasant feelings, such as anxiety, boredom or self-doubt, and avoidance offers a brief but seductive escape from those feelings.

This emotional account helps explain a paradox that has long puzzled observers: chronic procrastinators are frequently aware that their delay is irrational and self-defeating, yet they persist nonetheless. The relief obtained from postponement is immediate, whereas its costs are deferred and abstract. The brain, biased towards the present, reliably overvalues the small short-term reward of avoidance and discounts the larger long-term penalty.

Understanding procrastination as emotional regulation rather than poor scheduling points towards different remedies. Strategies that target feelings, such as practising self-compassion or breaking a daunting task into smaller, less threatening steps, tend to outperform stern self-criticism, which often deepens the distress that triggered avoidance in the first place. Curiously, studies suggest that forgiving oneself for past procrastination reduces the likelihood of procrastinating again.

None of this implies that procrastination is harmless. Persistent delay is associated with poorer health, lower earnings and heightened stress, and it can erode self-trust in corrosive ways. The point is rather that the most effective interventions address the underlying emotional discomfort, instead of simply exhorting people to try harder, an instruction that, for the chronic procrastinator, has usually already failed.

questions.q: Psychologists increasingly see procrastination as primarily a problem of managing emotions.
Chronic procrastinators are usually unaware that their delay is irrational.
Procrastination affects men more strongly than women.
According to the passage, avoidance of a task is appealing because it
options: provides immediate relief from unpleasant feelings

questions.q: Research on self-forgiveness suggests that it
options: lowers the chance of procrastinating again

questions.q: The brain tends to overvalue short-term rewards and ________ long-term penalties.
Breaking a task into smaller steps makes it feel less ________.
The writer regards procrastination as a harmless habit.

listening.title: A Talk on Beating Procrastination
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone, to today's session on managing procrastination. Before we begin, let me reassure you of one thing: procrastination is not a character flaw. It's largely about how we handle uncomfortable emotions. So instead of scolding yourself, we'll focus on practical tools.

The first tool is what I call the two-minute rule. If a task takes less than two minutes, do it immediately rather than adding it to a list. This clears small jobs that otherwise pile up and weigh on you.

The second tool is breaking large projects into chunks. Don't write a report; write the first paragraph. A smaller goal feels less threatening, and momentum builds naturally.

Third, schedule your hardest work for the time of day when your energy peaks. For most people, that's the morning, though night owls may differ.

Finally, be kind to yourself when you slip. Research shows that students who forgave themselves for delaying actually procrastinated less on future tasks. Self-criticism, by contrast, tends to make things worse. We'll practise each of these in the workshop after the break.

questions.q: The speaker says procrastination is mainly about

reading.title: The Paradox of Choice
reading.passage:
Few assumptions are as deeply embedded in modern economic life as the belief that more choice is always better. If one option is good, the reasoning runs, then twenty must be better still, since a wider range allows each person to find the alternative that suits them best. Psychological research over recent decades has gently but persistently challenged this intuition, suggesting that beyond a certain point, additional options can diminish rather than enhance well-being.

A frequently cited study illustrates the effect. Shoppers in a supermarket were offered samples from a display of jams; on some days the display held a modest selection, on others an extensive one. The larger array attracted more passing interest, yet shoppers who encountered it were considerably less likely to make a purchase than those who faced the smaller range. An abundance of options, it seemed, had paralysed rather than empowered them.

Several mechanisms may underlie this paradox. A greater number of alternatives raises the cognitive effort required to compare them, and it amplifies the regret that follows a decision, since the chooser becomes more aware of the attractive features of the options forgone. Even when people do choose, they report lower satisfaction, haunted by the suspicion that a better possibility lay just out of reach.

The practical implications are far-reaching, touching everything from retail design to public policy on pensions and healthcare. Yet the effect is not universal. It appears strongest among those who strive to make the optimal decision, and weakest among those content with an option that is merely good enough. For the latter group, an expanding menu of choices may pose no threat to contentment at all.

questions.q: Most modern economic thinking assumes that increasing choice generally benefits people.
In the jam study, the larger display led to more purchases than the smaller one.
The jam study was later repeated in several other countries.
According to the passage, having many alternatives can increase regret because the chooser
options: becomes aware of the appeal of rejected options

questions.q: The paradox of choice appears strongest among people who
options: are satisfied with a good-enough option
try to make the optimal decision

questions.q: An abundance of options seemed to have ________ rather than empowered the shoppers.

reading.title: The Standardisation of Time
reading.passage:
For most of recorded history, communities measured time locally. A town set its clocks by the moment the sun stood highest overhead, which meant that two settlements a hundred kilometres apart could differ by several minutes. This patchwork of local times caused little trouble while people travelled on foot or by horse, since the discrepancies were too small to notice over a slow journey. The arrangement only became untenable once a faster technology stitched distant places tightly together.

That technology was the railway. By the middle of the nineteenth century, trains ran fast enough that a passenger leaving one city and arriving in another could find the station clock disagreeing markedly with the timepiece in his pocket. More seriously, the proliferation of local times made it nearly impossible to compile reliable timetables. A single line passing through dozens of towns might, in theory, require dozens of separate columns of departure figures. Railway companies, unwilling to tolerate such chaos, began imposing a uniform 'railway time' along their routes, usually pegged to the time observed at a central observatory.

Britain adopted this practice comparatively early, with most public clocks synchronised by the 1850s. Other nations followed at varying speeds, often resisting what some regarded as an intrusion upon local custom. The decisive step came in 1884, when delegates from numerous countries met to divide the globe into standard zones, each nominally one hour apart and reckoned from a single agreed meridian. The conference did not settle every dispute, and a few governments declined to align themselves immediately, preferring idiosyncratic offsets that survived for decades.

What the episode illustrates is how a seemingly abstract convention can be driven by thoroughly practical pressures. Nobody reorganised the world's clocks out of a love of tidiness; they did so because commerce and transport demanded a shared framework. The modern traveller who resets a watch on landing rarely reflects that this small ritual is the residue of a vast nineteenth-century negotiation between technology and habit.

questions.q: Differences between local times rarely troubled travellers who journeyed slowly.
Railway companies found that local times complicated the creation of timetables.

options: practical demands of commerce and transport

reading.title: The Spice Routes of the Indian Ocean
reading.passage:
Long before European vessels rounded the southern tip of Africa, a dense web of maritime trade already crisscrossed the Indian Ocean. Merchants from the Arabian Peninsula, the Indian subcontinent, the Swahili coast and the islands of Southeast Asia exchanged goods across thousands of kilometres of open water. The system depended not on charts or compasses alone but on an intimate knowledge of the monsoon winds, which reversed direction with the seasons and allowed sailors to ride them outward and then home again.

Among the most coveted commodities were spices. Pepper, cinnamon, cloves and nutmeg were prized far beyond their value as flavourings; they served as medicines, preservatives and tokens of status. A handful of cloves, grown only on a few small islands, might pass through the hands of a dozen intermediaries before reaching a kitchen in a distant city, its price multiplying at every exchange. The merchants who controlled the points where routes converged accumulated considerable wealth, and the ports they ruled grew into cosmopolitan hubs where many languages were spoken.

This commerce carried more than cargo. Religions, scripts, culinary practices and architectural styles travelled the same lanes as the spices. Communities of traders settled far from home, married locally and gradually blended their customs with those of their hosts. The result was a remarkably interconnected zone in which ideas circulated almost as freely as goods, centuries before anyone spoke of a global economy.

When European powers eventually entered these waters, they did not discover an empty stage. Rather, they inserted themselves, often violently, into a sophisticated network that had functioned for generations. Their lasting innovation was not trade itself but the attempt to monopolise it by force, seizing key ports and demanding that commerce pass through channels they alone controlled. The older, more fluid system never entirely vanished, but the balance of power shifted decisively, and the open ocean became, for a time, a contested arena of empires.

questions.q: Indian Ocean trade existed before European ships reached the region.
Sailors relied on the seasonal reversal of the monsoon winds.
Spices were valued only for their use in cooking.
European powers found the Indian Ocean trade routes deserted.

options: attempting to monopolise trade by force

listening.title: A Lecture on Monsoon Sailing
listening.script:
LECTURER: Right, today we're looking at how early sailors used the monsoon winds. The key fact is timing. The southwest monsoon blows from roughly June to September, carrying ships from the Arabian coast towards India. Then, from November onwards, the winds reverse and the northeast monsoon brings them home. Miss the window, and you might be stranded in a foreign port for six months.

Now, a typical crossing from the Gulf to the Malabar coast took about four weeks under favourable winds. Ships carried fresh water for the voyage, and crews supplemented their diet by fishing. Navigation relied on the stars, the colour of the water, and even the flight of birds near land.

For your assignment, I want you to focus on one commodity. Pepper is the obvious choice, but consider cloves too, which came from much further east. Submit a two thousand word essay by the fifth of March. And please, cite at least eight sources, not the three I saw last term.

reading.title: The Printing Press and the Spread of Literacy
reading.passage:
The introduction of movable-type printing in mid-fifteenth-century Europe is often described as a revolution, yet its effects unfolded more gradually than the word implies. In the decades immediately following the first printed books, the great majority of the population remained unable to read. Printing did not create readers overnight; rather, it slowly altered the conditions under which reading could become widespread, lowering the cost of texts and multiplying the number of copies in circulation.

Before the press, books were copied by hand, a labour so painstaking that a single volume might take months to produce and command a price beyond the reach of all but the wealthy or the institutional. A printed book, by contrast, could be issued in hundreds of identical copies from a single setting of type. The economics of knowledge were transformed. Texts that had once existed in a handful of manuscripts could now be distributed across a continent, and a scholar's argument, once confined to a small circle, might be read and contested far from its origin.

The consequences extended well beyond scholarship. Cheaper books encouraged the production of works in everyday languages rather than Latin, which had long served as the medium of the learned. As more material appeared in vernacular tongues, reading became a plausible aspiration for artisans and merchants, not merely for clerics. Demand for literacy fed the establishment of schools, and rising literacy in turn fed demand for printed matter, in a self-reinforcing cycle that took generations to mature.

It would be a mistake, however, to credit the press alone. Printing flourished where social and economic conditions were already favourable: in towns with active commerce, a measure of disposable wealth, and authorities willing to tolerate the circulation of ideas. Where such conditions were absent, presses made little headway. The technology supplied a powerful instrument, but it was human appetite and opportunity that determined how, and how quickly, that instrument reshaped the world.

questions.q: Printing immediately turned most of the population into readers.
Hand-copied books were expensive and slow to produce.
Cheaper books encouraged publication in everyday languages.

The first printed book was a religious text.

options: the support of Latin scholars only

listening.title: Library Tour Briefing
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome to the University Rare Books Library. Before we begin, a few practical points. The tour today takes about forty-five minutes, and we'll visit three floors. Photography is allowed, but please switch off your flash, as bright light can damage old paper.

We'll start in the early printing gallery on the ground floor, which holds about three hundred volumes from before 1500. Many of these were printed in vernacular languages, not Latin, which was unusual for the period.

On the first floor you'll find the manuscript room. These hand-copied books predate printing entirely, and some took a scribe more than a year to complete. Please do not touch the displays; the oils on our skin harm the parchment.

Finally, on the second floor, there's a small workshop where we run binding demonstrations every Wednesday at two o'clock. If you'd like to attend, sign up at the front desk. Numbers are limited to twelve people per session. Now, please follow me, and keep your bags closed at all times.

reading.title: The Decline of the Bronze Age Cities
reading.passage:
Around three thousand years ago, a cluster of advanced civilisations that had flourished across the eastern Mediterranean and the Near East collapsed within a comparatively short span. Palaces that had stood for centuries were abandoned or burned, long-distance trade contracted sharply, and writing systems that depended on palace administration fell into disuse. For generations, scholars searched for a single, dramatic cause, but the evidence increasingly points to a tangle of overlapping pressures rather than one decisive blow.

The great cities of the age were tightly interdependent. Bronze itself, the metal that gave the period its name, required tin and copper that often came from distant sources, so the manufacture of tools and weapons relied on trade links spanning enormous distances. A palace economy of this kind was efficient but brittle: a disruption in one region could ripple outward, starving partners of materials they could not produce themselves. When several shocks arrived in close succession, the system had little slack with which to absorb them.

Among the suspected triggers were prolonged drought, which strained food supplies, and the movement of displaced peoples seeking new lands. Earthquakes may have damaged key centres, and internal unrest could have followed as ordinary people lost faith in rulers who failed to provide security or grain. None of these factors alone seems sufficient to explain so broad a collapse, but together they may have overwhelmed societies whose prosperity rested on fragile, far-flung connections.

What followed was not a return to savagery but a profound reorganisation. Some skills were lost, and certain regions entered a period of relative obscurity from which few records survive. Yet within a few centuries, new forms of society emerged, often simpler in structure but more resilient, and in some cases more inventive. The collapse, in other words, cleared ground as well as destroying it, reminding us that the fall of a civilisation is rarely the end of human history in a place, but a turbulent passage to something different.

questions.q: The Bronze Age cities collapsed gradually over many thousands of years.
Scholars now favour a combination of causes over a single one.

reading.title: Coffeehouses and the Public Sphere
reading.passage:
When coffee first became widely available in European cities in the seventeenth century, it arrived not merely as a beverage but as the focal point of a new kind of social space. The coffeehouse, unlike the tavern, served a drink that sharpened rather than dulled the mind, and it attracted a clientele eager to talk. Within decades, these establishments had multiplied across major cities, becoming venues where news was exchanged, business transacted and opinions tested against one another in open conversation.

What distinguished the coffeehouse from earlier gathering places was its relative openness. For the price of a single cup, a visitor of modest means could sit alongside merchants, writers and the occasional aristocrat, and could join discussions that ranged from commodity prices to the conduct of governments. Some houses specialised: one might be frequented chiefly by those interested in shipping, another by men of letters, a third by speculators in stocks. Out of these informal hubs grew institutions that would later become formal, including certain newspapers and even financial markets.

Not everyone welcomed the phenomenon. Authorities periodically viewed the coffeehouses with suspicion, fearing that the free circulation of opinion might breed dissent or sedition. Attempts were occasionally made to restrict or close them, though such measures generally proved unworkable and short-lived, so deeply had the habit taken root. The very feature that alarmed rulers, the open exchange of views among people of different ranks, was precisely what contemporaries found most valuable.

Historians have argued that the coffeehouse helped to foster what is sometimes called the public sphere: a domain, separate from both the household and the state, in which private individuals could come together to debate matters of common concern. Whether or not one accepts that grand interpretation, it is clear that these modest rooms, filled with conversation and the aroma of roasting beans, played a part in shaping habits of discussion and scrutiny that we still associate with open societies.

options: the open exchange of views among different ranks

listening.title: A Talk on Historical Cafes
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Good evening, everyone. Tonight I want to take you back to the early coffeehouses and explain why they mattered far beyond the drink they served. The first one in this city opened, by most accounts, in sixteen fifty, in a narrow lane near the harbour. Within just thirty years, there were more than eighty of them scattered across the centre.

Now, what made these places special? Three things. First, the price. A cup cost a single penny, which is why some called them penny universities. For that penny, you could sit and listen to debates for hours. Second, the mix of people. A dockworker might share a table with a banker. Third, the news. Many coffeehouses kept the latest pamphlets and gazettes, and some even employed a person to read the headlines aloud for those who couldn't read.

If you want to learn more, I've left a reading list at the back. The museum's own exhibition on this theme runs until the end of September, and entry is free on Tuesdays. Thank you, and do enjoy the rest of your evening.

reading.title: The Quiet Resurgence of Measles
reading.passage:
Measles was once regarded as a vanquished disease across much of the industrialised world. By the early 2000s, sustained vaccination campaigns had driven transmission so low that several nations formally declared the illness eliminated within their borders. Elimination, in epidemiological terms, does not mean the pathogen has been eradicated globally; it means that no continuous chain of domestic transmission persists for at least twelve months. The distinction matters, because imported cases can reignite outbreaks wherever population immunity has slipped below a critical threshold.

That threshold is unusually demanding for measles. The virus is among the most contagious agents known to medicine: a single infected person may transmit it to as many as eighteen susceptible contacts in an unvaccinated setting. To interrupt such efficient spread, roughly ninety-five per cent of a community must be immune, a figure considerably higher than that required for most other vaccine-preventable diseases. When coverage dips even modestly, the protective effect of herd immunity erodes and the virus finds renewed footholds, particularly among infants too young to be inoculated.

Recent years have witnessed a troubling reversal. Pockets of declining uptake, fuelled partly by misinformation circulating online and partly by disruptions to routine services during the pandemic, have allowed measles to re-establish itself in communities that had been free of it for years. Public health authorities have responded with targeted catch-up programmes, yet rebuilding confidence has proved slower than restoring logistics. Surveys suggest that hesitant parents are rarely swayed by statistics alone; trusted intermediaries, such as family physicians, tend to be more persuasive than mass-media appeals.

The consequences of complacency are not trivial. Although most patients recover, measles can precipitate pneumonia, encephalitis and, in rare instances, a fatal degenerative condition emerging years after the initial infection. It also suppresses the immune system for months, leaving survivors vulnerable to other pathogens they had previously fought off. For these reasons, epidemiologists caution that the disease should never be dismissed as a benign rite of childhood, and that maintaining high coverage is far cheaper than containing the outbreaks that inevitably follow its decline.

questions.q: Elimination of measles means the virus no longer exists anywhere in the world.
Measles requires a higher level of population immunity to control than most other preventable diseases.

Maintaining high vaccination coverage is described as more expensive than containing outbreaks.

listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, Maple Lane Surgery, how can I help?

CALLER: Hello, I'd like to book a flu jab, please.

RECEPTIONIST: Of course. Can I take your name?

CALLER: It's Daniel Hartley. That's H-A-R-T-L-E-Y.

RECEPTIONIST: Lovely. And your date of birth?

CALLER: The fourteenth of March, nineteen eighty-six.

RECEPTIONIST: Thank you. We're running clinics on Tuesdays and Saturdays this month. Which suits you better?

CALLER: Saturday would be ideal, I work weekdays.

RECEPTIONIST: We have a slot this Saturday at half past nine, or eleven o'clock.

CALLER: Let's say eleven o'clock.

RECEPTIONIST: Booked. Please arrive about ten minutes early to complete a short consent form. The appointment itself takes roughly fifteen minutes.

CALLER: Is there a charge?

RECEPTIONIST: For your age group it's free of charge, as you have a recorded asthma diagnosis.

CALLER: Right. And which entrance do I use?

RECEPTIONIST: Use the side entrance on Beech Road; the main reception is closed at weekends. Bring a form of photo identification if you can.

CALLER: Will do. Thank you very much.

RECEPTIONIST: You're welcome. See you Saturday.

Measles vaccination coverage (% of children)
Country | 2010 | 2022

vocab.example: The disease was once considered a vanquished threat in wealthy nations.
Health bodies hope one day to eradicate the virus entirely.
In epidemiological terms, elimination has a precise meaning.
Immunity must stay above a critical threshold to prevent outbreaks.
Measles is among the most contagious diseases known.

reading.title: Antibiotic Resistance and the Tragedy of the Commons
reading.passage:
When penicillin entered widespread use in the 1940s, it transformed medicine almost overnight, rendering once-lethal infections routinely curable. Yet the very triumph of antibiotics sowed the seeds of their decline. Every time such a drug is administered, it exerts selective pressure on bacterial populations: the susceptible organisms perish, while any that happen to carry resistant traits survive and multiply. Over successive generations, resistance accumulates, and a medicine that once worked reliably begins to falter.

The phenomenon is often described as a textbook tragedy of the commons. An individual prescriber, treating a particular patient, has a strong incentive to use the most effective drug available. The cost of that choice, however, is diffuse and deferred: it is borne not by the patient but by future patients across the wider population, who inherit a depleted arsenal. Because no single actor pays the full price of resistance, the collective resource of effective antibiotics is steadily overdrawn, much as a shared pasture is overgrazed when each herder pursues private gain.

The drivers are not confined to clinics. A substantial share of global antibiotic consumption occurs in agriculture, where the drugs are used not only to treat sick animals but, in some systems, to promote growth and pre-empt disease in crowded conditions. Such routine use creates vast reservoirs in which resistant strains can emerge and subsequently spread to humans through food, water and direct contact. Curbing this practice has proved politically delicate, since it implicates entire industries rather than individual prescribers.

Responses to the crisis fall into two broad camps. Stewardship initiatives aim to preserve existing drugs by tightening prescribing rules, improving diagnostics so that antibiotics are reserved for genuine bacterial infections, and educating both clinicians and the public. The alternative, developing wholly new classes of antibiotics, is hampered by economics: the drugs that would most benefit society are precisely those that should be used sparingly, leaving pharmaceutical firms with little prospect of recouping their investment. Several proposals therefore seek to decouple a developer's reward from the volume of sales, paying instead for the mere existence of a useful new agent. Whether such incentives will prove sufficient remains, for now, an open question.

questions.q: Penicillin had little immediate impact when it was first introduced.
Resistance arises because antibiotics allow organisms carrying resistant traits to survive and reproduce.
Most countries have already banned the use of antibiotics for promoting animal growth.
Why is antibiotic resistance compared to a tragedy of the commons?
options: Because the drugs are very cheap to produce
Because the cost of overuse falls on the wider population rather than the individual
Because herders and doctors cooperate closely

questions.q: What economic obstacle hampers the development of new antibiotics?
options: The drugs are too easy to manufacture
Effective new drugs should be used sparingly, limiting sales revenue
Patients refuse to take new medicines

questions.q: Each administration of an antibiotic exerts selective ________ on bacterial populations.
Some proposals aim to ________ a developer's reward from the volume of sales.
The passage states with certainty that new incentive schemes will solve the problem.

listening.title: A Lecture on Hospital Infection Control
listening.script:
LECTURER: Right, today we're looking at how hospitals limit the spread of resistant bacteria. The single most effective measure is also the simplest: hand hygiene. Studies repeatedly show that proper handwashing or alcohol gel reduces transmission dramatically, yet compliance among busy staff often falls below sixty per cent without active monitoring.

The second pillar is what we call antimicrobial stewardship. Each ward has a dedicated pharmacist who reviews prescriptions, asking whether an antibiotic is truly necessary and, if so, whether a narrower-spectrum drug would do. This single intervention has cut unnecessary prescribing by roughly a third in trial wards.

Third, isolation. Patients colonised with resistant organisms are placed in single rooms where possible, and staff wear gowns and gloves. The challenge here is capacity: when wards are full, isolation simply cannot be guaranteed.

Finally, surveillance. Laboratories report resistance patterns weekly, allowing managers to detect an emerging cluster before it becomes an outbreak. The data are only useful, though, if someone acts on them promptly. For your assignment, due on the fifteenth, choose one of these four measures and evaluate the evidence behind it.

reading.title: Mapping Disease Through the Sewers
reading.passage:
Long before laboratories could sequence a virus, the pioneering physician John Snow demonstrated that disease could be traced through the very infrastructure of a city. His celebrated investigation of a nineteenth-century cholera outbreak, which implicated a single contaminated water pump, established the principle that the movement of pathogens through shared systems can reveal patterns invisible to clinical observation alone. That insight has been resurrected, in startlingly sophisticated form, by the modern practice of wastewater surveillance.

The method rests on a simple biological fact: people shed traces of pathogens in their waste, often before they feel ill or seek testing. By sampling the influent at a treatment plant and analysing it for genetic fragments, scientists can estimate the prevalence of an infection across an entire catchment without testing a single individual. During recent respiratory pandemics, such monitoring frequently flagged rising transmission days ahead of clinical reports, furnishing authorities with a precious window in which to respond.

The technique carries notable advantages. It is comparatively inexpensive, it captures asymptomatic carriers who would never present at a clinic, and it sidesteps the biases that plague voluntary testing, where the worried and the wealthy are over-represented. Crucially, it aggregates data at the community level, so no individual can be identified from a sample, a feature that has largely insulated the practice from the privacy objections that dog other forms of health surveillance.

Yet the approach is not without limitations. Translating a concentration of genetic material into an actual number of infected people is fraught with uncertainty, since shedding rates vary between individuals and degrade unpredictably in transit. Rainfall can dilute samples, and populations served by a plant may fluctuate with commuting and tourism. Consequently, wastewater data are most reliable as an indicator of trends, telling us whether infection is rising or falling, rather than as a precise census. Used judiciously alongside conventional methods, however, the humble sewer has become an unexpectedly powerful sentinel, offering an early and impartial reading of a community's health.

questions.q: John Snow used genetic sequencing to identify the source of the cholera outbreak.
Wastewater surveillance can detect infections in people who show no symptoms.
Wastewater monitoring is now used in more countries than traditional clinical testing.
Why is wastewater surveillance largely free from privacy objections?
options: It is illegal to object to it
Data are aggregated so individuals cannot be identified
It does not involve any pathogens

questions.q: According to the passage, wastewater data are most reliable as:
options: A precise count of infected people
An indicator of whether infection is rising or falling
A method of identifying individual patients
A replacement for all clinical testing

questions.q: Scientists analyse the ________ at a treatment plant for genetic fragments.
The technique sidesteps the biases that affect ________ testing, where the worried are over-represented.

listening.title: Radio Interview: Tracking Outbreaks Early
listening.script:
HOST: Today I'm joined by Dr Priya Anand, an environmental epidemiologist. Priya, in plain terms, how does sewage tell us about an outbreak?

DR ANAND: Well, when people are infected, they shed fragments of the pathogen in their waste, sometimes a day or two before symptoms appear. We collect a sample at the treatment works, extract the genetic material, and measure how much is present.

HOST: And how quickly can you get a result?

DR ANAND: From sample to signal, usually within twenty-four hours. That speed is the real value; it can warn us before hospital admissions start climbing.

HOST: Is it expensive?

DR ANAND: Surprisingly not. A single plant can effectively monitor a population of two hundred thousand people for the cost of testing perhaps a few dozen individuals.

HOST: Any drawbacks?

DR ANAND: Heavy rain dilutes the signal, and we can't pinpoint exactly who is infected, only the broad trend. But for spotting a rising wave early, nothing else is quite as efficient.

HOST: Fascinating. Dr Anand, thank you for joining us.
