reading.title: The Placebo Effect and the Ethics of Deception
reading.passage:
Few phenomena in medicine are as paradoxical as the placebo effect. A pill containing nothing more than sugar, or an injection of inert saline, can nonetheless produce measurable improvements in a patient's condition, provided the patient believes it to be a genuine treatment. The effect is most pronounced in subjective complaints such as pain, fatigue and anxiety, but it has been documented across a surprising range of disorders. Far from being imaginary, these changes are accompanied by real physiological shifts, including the release of the body's own pain-relieving compounds.

For researchers, the placebo poses a methodological challenge that has shaped the entire architecture of modern trials. Because patients improve simply from the act of being treated, any new drug must be shown to outperform a placebo, not merely to make people better. The randomised controlled trial, in which neither patients nor those administering the treatment know who receives the active compound, was devised precisely to disentangle a drug's genuine pharmacological power from the powerful expectations it arouses.

The phenomenon raises an awkward ethical dilemma. If a sugar pill can relieve suffering, should physicians be permitted to prescribe one? The traditional objection is that doing so requires deception, which corrodes the trust on which the doctor-patient relationship depends. Yet a curious body of research suggests the effect may persist even when patients are openly told that their pills are inert, a finding that, if robust, could dissolve the ethical objection altogether.

The placebo also has a darker twin, the nocebo effect, in which negative expectations generate genuine harm. Patients warned of a drug's side effects frequently report experiencing them, even when given a dummy pill. This presents clinicians with a delicate balancing act: they are ethically obliged to disclose risks, yet the very act of disclosure may conjure the symptoms it describes. Navigating this tension, while neither deceiving patients nor needlessly alarming them, remains one of the subtler arts of clinical practice, and one for which no tidy formula yet exists.

questions.q: The placebo effect produces only imaginary changes with no physical basis.
The placebo effect is especially strong in subjective complaints such as pain and anxiety.
Most doctors today regularly prescribe placebos to their patients.
Why was the randomised controlled trial developed?
options: To reduce the cost of drug development
To separate a drug's real effect from patients' expectations
To eliminate the nocebo effect entirely
To allow patients to choose their treatment

questions.q: What finding might remove the main ethical objection to prescribing placebos?
options: That the effect can persist even when patients know the pill is inert
That placebos work better than real drugs

questions.q: The placebo's 'darker twin' is known as the ________ effect.

listening.title: Seminar Discussion on Trial Design
listening.script:
TUTOR: So, last week you read about placebo controls. Maya, why do we bother with them at all?

MAYA: Because patients often improve just from being treated, so without a placebo group we can't tell if the drug itself is doing anything.

TUTOR: Exactly. And what makes a trial double-blind?

MAYA: Neither the patients nor the staff giving the treatment know who's getting the real drug. It stops their expectations from skewing the results.

TUTOR: Good. Tom, what's a common problem with placebo trials?

TOM: Recruitment, mostly. Some patients won't join if there's a chance they'll get a dummy pill instead of treatment.

TUTOR: Right, and that can bias your sample. Now, there's an ethical wrinkle too. When is it unacceptable to use a placebo?

MAYA: When an effective treatment already exists. Withholding it could harm people, so you'd compare against the standard drug instead.

TUTOR: Precisely. For next week, I'd like a five-hundred-word critique of one published trial. Focus on whether the control group was appropriate. Submit it through the portal by Thursday.

options: To tell whether the drug itself has an effect

reading.title: Rethinking the Hospital Ward
reading.passage:
For more than a century, the open hospital ward, in which a dozen or more patients shared a single large room, was the unquestioned norm. Conceived in an era that prized economy and ease of observation, it allowed a small number of nurses to oversee many patients at once and concentrated scarce equipment in one place. Yet as understanding of infection and recovery has deepened, the merits of this venerable arrangement have come under sustained scrutiny, and a quiet revolution in ward design has followed.

The case against open wards rests on several pillars. Chief among them is infection: shared air and close proximity facilitate the transmission of resistant organisms, and a single colonised patient can seed a cluster that spreads down the row of beds. Noise is a second concern. Wards are notoriously restless places, where the cumulative din of monitors, conversations and nocturnal admissions fragments the sleep on which healing partly depends. Privacy, too, is compromised, with sensitive conversations conducted behind nothing more substantial than a thin curtain.

In response, many newly built hospitals have embraced the single-occupancy room. Advocates point to lower infection rates, quieter nights, and a dignity that the open ward cannot match. Some studies have even associated private rooms with shorter stays and reduced medication errors, since staff are less likely to confuse one patient with another. The model is not, however, without cost. Single rooms consume more floor space and are more expensive to build and staff, and a patient alone behind a closed door may go unobserved for longer should they suddenly deteriorate.

This last point has prompted considerable debate. Critics warn that isolation can breed loneliness, particularly among elderly patients for whom the bustle of a shared ward, for all its drawbacks, offered a measure of human company. Designers have sought a middle path, clustering single rooms around central nursing stations with generous sightlines, so that solitude need not mean neglect. Whether the additional expense is justified by the gains remains contested, but the trajectory is unmistakable: the cavernous open ward, once a symbol of modern medicine, is slowly passing into history.

questions.q: Open wards were originally designed partly to make patient observation easier.
Single rooms are cheaper to build than open wards.
Most hospitals built in the last decade now use single rooms exclusively.
What is described as the chief argument against open wards?
What concern do critics raise about single rooms?
options: They can cause loneliness, especially among the elderly

questions.q: Private rooms have been associated with shorter stays and fewer medication ________.

listening.title: Hospital Design Project Briefing
listening.script:
ARCHITECT: Thanks for coming in. This is the briefing for the new wing at Riverside Hospital. Our brief is to move away from the old open wards toward single rooms, but with smart clustering.

MANAGER: How many beds are we talking about?

ARCHITECT: The new wing will hold ninety beds in total, arranged as six pods of fifteen.

MANAGER: And each pod has its own nursing station?

ARCHITECT: Correct, positioned centrally so staff have clear lines of sight into every room. That addresses the main worry about patients being unobserved.

MANAGER: What about infection control?

ARCHITECT: Each room has its own bathroom, which is the single biggest factor in cutting cross-infection. We're also installing improved ventilation throughout.

MANAGER: It sounds expensive.

ARCHITECT: It is. The build cost is about fifteen per cent higher than a conventional ward, but the trust expects to recover that through shorter patient stays and fewer infections.

MANAGER: When does construction begin?

ARCHITECT: Groundwork starts in September, with completion targeted for two years later.

reading.title: The Quiet Revival of the Lecture
reading.passage:
For much of the past two decades, the traditional university lecture has been treated as an educational relic. Critics argued that the spectacle of a single academic addressing hundreds of passive listeners was inefficient, even regressive, when set against the promise of interactive seminars and digital platforms. Funding councils poured money into so-called active-learning spaces, and the lecture hall was widely expected to follow the chalkboard into obsolescence. Yet recent evidence suggests the obituary was premature.

A longitudinal study tracking undergraduates across eleven institutions found that students who attended well-structured lectures consistently outperformed peers in courses delivered entirely online. The advantage, researchers argued, lay not in the transmission of facts, which any recording can supply, but in the temporal discipline the lecture imposes. By compelling students to assemble at a fixed hour, the format counteracts the procrastination that erodes self-directed study. The presence of an expert thinking aloud, occasionally hesitating or revising, also models the very intellectual habits that examinations reward.

Proponents of digital learning counter that such findings reflect implementation rather than medium. A dull online module will indeed underperform a charismatic speaker, but a thoughtfully designed one need not. The genuine lesson, they suggest, is that no single format is inherently superior; what matters is the alignment between method and objective. A lecture excels at conveying the architecture of a discipline, whereas a workshop is better suited to rehearsing a skill.

What unsettles many administrators is the cost calculus. Lectures are comparatively cheap to scale, which is precisely why reformers grew suspicious of them: efficiency, they feared, was being mistaken for quality. The revival now under way is therefore tempered by caution. Few advocate a wholesale return to the podium. Instead, institutions are experimenting with hybrid models in which a compelling lecture seeds a question that subsequent small-group sessions are designed to resolve. The lecture, in this reimagining, is not an endpoint but an overture.

questions.q: Funding councils invested heavily in active-learning environments in the belief that lectures were outdated.
The longitudinal study covered students at more than a dozen institutions.
The study found that lectures were superior because they delivered facts that recordings could not.
The longitudinal study measured students' satisfaction with their courses.
According to advocates of digital learning, the main reason some online modules underperform is that
options: the medium itself is inferior to face-to-face teaching
they are poorly designed rather than digital in nature
they cannot convey the structure of a discipline

questions.q: Why did reformers become suspicious of lectures' low cost?
options: They believed cheap methods could not be scaled
They feared affordability was being confused with educational quality
They wanted to protect the jobs of seminar tutors
They thought students preferred expensive teaching

questions.q: The lecture's chief benefit, researchers claimed, was the ________ discipline it imposed by requiring attendance at a set time.

listening.title: Enrolling in the University Library Workshop
listening.script:
LIBRARIAN: Good morning, you've reached the library induction desk. How can I help?

STUDENT: Hi, I'd like to sign up for the research-skills workshop, please.

LIBRARIAN: Of course. We run two strands. The introductory session is on Tuesday the fourteenth, and the advanced one, on referencing software, is the following Thursday.

STUDENT: I think the advanced one suits me. What time does it start?

LIBRARIAN: It begins at half past two and runs for ninety minutes, in Room 4 on the second floor.

STUDENT: Perfect. Is there a fee?

LIBRARIAN: For enrolled students it's free, but you do need to bring your own laptop, as we won't be supplying machines.

STUDENT: Noted. And could I get the reading beforehand?

LIBRARIAN: Certainly. I'll email you a short guide. It covers about fifteen pages, mostly on managing citations. Can I take your student number?

STUDENT: It's seven-four-two-zero-six.

LIBRARIAN: Lovely. One last thing: places are capped at twenty, so I'd confirm soon. Shall I reserve you a seat?

STUDENT: Yes, please do.

reading.title: Why Bilingual Children Pause
reading.passage:
Parents of bilingual children often notice a curious phenomenon: in the early years, their offspring may begin to speak slightly later than monolingual peers, and may pepper their sentences with words from both languages. For decades this prompted anxiety, and not a little misguided advice. Well-meaning teachers urged families to abandon the home language in favour of the dominant tongue, convinced that two languages would overload a developing mind. That conviction, it turns out, rested on remarkably thin evidence.

Contemporary research paints a strikingly different picture. The apparent delay is typically modest and transient; by school age, most bilingual children match their monolingual counterparts on vocabulary when both languages are counted together. The mixing of languages, far from signalling confusion, reflects a sophisticated awareness of which words communicate best with which listeners. Children as young as two will switch languages depending on who enters the room, an act of social calibration that monolingual development simply does not require.

The deeper interest, however, lies in what bilingualism appears to cultivate beyond language itself. Managing two systems demands that the brain continually suppress one while deploying the other, exercising a faculty psychologists call executive control. Tasks that measure the ability to ignore distraction and shift attention tend to show a small but consistent bilingual advantage. Some scientists have speculated that this lifelong mental workout may even delay the onset of certain age-related cognitive declines, though that claim remains contested and far from settled.

None of this implies that raising a bilingual child is effortless. Exposure must be sustained and meaningful; a language heard only occasionally rarely takes root. Nor does bilingualism guarantee academic success, a leap that enthusiastic commentators sometimes make. What the evidence does support is a quieter reassurance: the pause that worries parents is not a warning sign but, more often, the audible trace of two systems learning to coexist.

questions.q: Some teachers in the past advised families to give up the home language.
By school age, bilingual children usually exceed monolingual children in vocabulary when only one language is counted.
Two-year-olds may switch languages according to who is present.
The claim that bilingualism delays cognitive decline is regarded as firmly established.
Bilingual children in the study scored higher in mathematics than monolingual children.
The mixing of two languages by young children is described as a sign of

listening.title: A Tutorial on Choosing a Dissertation Topic
listening.script:
TUTOR: Come in, take a seat. So you wanted to talk through your dissertation topic?

STUDENT: Yes. I'm torn between two ideas and I can't decide.

TUTOR: Tell me the first.

STUDENT: Language learning in early childhood, specifically bilingual development.

TUTOR: A rich area, though crowded. You'd need a narrow angle. And the second?

STUDENT: The effect of homework on teenage stress levels.

TUTOR: More original, I'd say, but harder to gather data on. Have you thought about how you'd collect it?

STUDENT: A questionnaire, maybe, distributed at a local school.

TUTOR: Sensible. You'd need ethics approval first, which takes about three weeks, so factor that in. My advice: go with the homework study, but interview a small group as well as surveying them. Mixed methods will strengthen it.

STUDENT: That makes sense. When is the proposal due?

TUTOR: The deadline is the ninth of March. Submit a draft to me a week earlier and I'll give you feedback.

STUDENT: Thank you, that's really helpful.

reading.title: The Myth of the Learning Style
reading.passage:
Few ideas have proved as resilient in education as the notion of the learning style. The theory is intuitively appealing: some people, it holds, are visual learners who absorb information best through diagrams; others are auditory, thriving on the spoken word; still others are kinaesthetic, needing to move and manipulate. Tailor the teaching to the style, the argument runs, and learning will flourish. Surveys consistently find that the overwhelming majority of teachers believe this, and many design lessons accordingly.

The difficulty is that the evidence has steadfastly refused to cooperate. To validate the theory, researchers would need to show what is technically called a crossover interaction: visual learners should outperform when taught visually and underperform when taught aurally, and vice versa. Despite numerous well-controlled experiments, this pattern has almost never appeared. Students may express a clear preference for one mode, but matching instruction to that preference yields no measurable gain in actual learning.

Why, then, does the myth persist? Part of the answer lies in its harmlessness on the surface. Encouraging teachers to present material in several formats is rarely bad practice; a topic explained through both a diagram and a narrative is often clearer than one explained through either alone. The error is not variety but the false belief that each learner possesses a fixed channel through which knowledge must flow. The most effective format usually depends on the material, not the person: one learns geography through maps and poetry through sound, regardless of temperament.

There is also a subtler cost. When a struggling student is labelled a particular type of learner, the label can become an excuse, both for the pupil who concludes that a subject is simply not for them and for the system that stops searching for better explanations. Dismantling the myth, its critics insist, is not about discouraging varied teaching but about restoring responsibility for learning to where it belongs: in effort, feedback, and the difficulty of the ideas themselves.

questions.q: Most teachers, according to surveys, accept the idea of learning styles.
Experiments have frequently confirmed the crossover interaction predicted by the theory.
Students are unable to state any preference for how they receive information.
Learning-style questionnaires are now banned in most schools.
What does the passage identify as the genuine error in learning-style theory?
options: the belief that each person has one fixed learning channel
explaining a topic with both a diagram and a narrative

questions.q: According to the passage, the best format for learning usually depends on
To validate the theory, researchers would need to demonstrate a so-called crossover ________.

listening.title: Lecture: Designing Better Study Habits
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today I want to dismantle a few comfortable myths about studying. The first is that re-reading your notes is effective. It feels productive, because the material grows familiar, but familiarity is not the same as recall. A far better technique is retrieval practice: closing the book and forcing yourself to remember.

The second myth is that long, uninterrupted sessions are best. In fact, spacing your study across several days, what we call distributed practice, produces far stronger memory than cramming, even when the total time is identical.

Third, many of you believe that you study best with background music. The research is mixed, but for tasks demanding concentration, silence usually wins. If you must have sound, choose something without lyrics.

Finally, a practical tip: after each study block, write three questions you could be asked in the exam. This turns passive reading into active rehearsal. Try it for one week and compare your confidence. I promise the difference will surprise you.

reading.title: The Apprenticeship Renaissance
reading.passage:
For a generation, the path through formal education seemed to point in only one direction: upward, towards a university degree. Vocational training, by contrast, carried an unmistakable stigma, regarded as the route taken by those who could not, or would not, pursue academic study. Across many wealthy economies, governments quietly disinvested from technical colleges while expanding access to higher education, in the conviction that a degree was the surest passport to prosperity. The assumption is now being reconsidered.

Several forces have converged to rehabilitate the apprenticeship. The first is economic. As graduate numbers swelled, the wage premium that a degree once guaranteed began, in some fields, to erode. Employers reported a paradoxical shortage: an abundance of graduates alongside a scarcity of workers with the practical competencies that modern industry demands. Skilled technicians, it emerged, were both harder to find and, increasingly, better paid than many holders of unremarkable degrees.

The second force is pedagogical. Researchers studying how expertise develops have grown sceptical of the sharp divide between knowing and doing. Much of what an expert knows, they argue, is tacit, acquired through repeated practice under the eye of a more experienced practitioner and resistant to capture in a textbook. The apprenticeship, in which a novice gradually assumes responsibility while a mentor recedes, embodies this insight almost perfectly. It is, in a sense, a very old answer to a very modern question about how skills are best transmitted.

None of this amounts to a wholesale rejection of universities, which remain indispensable for many professions and for the cultivation of inquiry for its own sake. The more nuanced lesson is that prestige and effectiveness are not the same thing. A society that channels every capable young person towards the same destination wastes both talent and opportunity. The renaissance of the apprenticeship is, at heart, a belated acknowledgement that there is more than one way to become accomplished, and that the older route was never as inferior as fashion had decreed.

questions.q: Many governments reduced funding for technical colleges while widening access to universities.
The wage advantage of a degree has increased in every field.
Employers reported having too few graduates available to them.

options: too few graduates but plenty of technicians
many graduates but a lack of practically skilled workers

questions.q: Why do researchers think apprenticeships suit how expertise develops?
options: because expertise can be fully written in textbooks
because much expert knowledge is tacit and gained through practice

questions.q: Much of what an expert knows is described as ________ knowledge, resistant to capture in a textbook.

listening.title: Information Session on an Engineering Apprenticeship
listening.script:
ADVISER: Welcome, everyone. I'll run through how our engineering apprenticeship works, then take questions. The programme lasts four years, combining paid work with one day a week at college.

Throughout, you'll be assigned a workplace mentor who oversees your progress and signs off each stage. Your starting salary is eighteen thousand pounds, rising each year as your responsibilities grow.

Applications open on the first of September and close six weeks later. We ask for two references and a short personal statement, no longer than five hundred words. There's no examination, but shortlisted candidates attend a practical assessment day.

A common question is whether the apprenticeship leads to a qualification. It does: on completion you receive a nationally recognised diploma, equivalent in standing to a foundation degree, and many of our apprentices go on to chartered status later.

Finally, places are limited. Last year we received over three hundred applications for forty positions, so do prepare your statement carefully. Right, who has a question?

reading.title: Rethinking the Open-Plan Classroom
reading.passage:
In the late twentieth century, a wave of educational architecture swept through schools in several countries. Walls came down, and the conventional classroom, that enclosed box presided over by a single teacher, was replaced by sprawling open-plan spaces in which dozens of children, and several teachers, worked side by side. The vision was generous: collaboration would flourish, teaching would become a shared craft, and the rigid hierarchies of the old schoolroom would dissolve. Decades on, the verdict is decidedly mixed.

The difficulty, predictably, was noise. Sound generated by one group readily bled into the next, and the cumulative din proved especially punishing for children who struggle to filter out distraction. Studies measuring acoustic conditions found that comprehension of spoken instruction fell sharply once background noise exceeded a certain threshold, an effect most pronounced among the youngest pupils and those learning in a second language. The very openness intended to foster connection was, for some, a barrier to the most basic act of listening.

Teachers, too, reported strain. Coordinating lessons in a shared space demanded a level of choreography that few had been trained for, and the constant visibility, while sometimes energising, could feel relentless. Some thrived on the collegiality; others longed for the autonomy of a room with a door. Crucially, the spaces themselves often went unmodified for years, so that staff inherited an architecture designed around a pedagogy they did not necessarily share.

The contemporary response has been neither nostalgic retreat nor stubborn persistence, but flexibility. The most successful designs now favour spaces that can be reconfigured: movable partitions that close for focused work and open for collaboration, acoustically treated surfaces, and quiet enclaves for pupils who need them. The lesson, architects increasingly concede, is humbling. A building cannot by itself manufacture a style of teaching; at best it can make a chosen approach easier, and at worst it can quietly sabotage one. The classroom of the future, it seems, will be defined less by walls than by the ease with which they can be moved.

questions.q: Open-plan schools were introduced with the aim of encouraging collaboration.
Background noise had the same effect on pupils of all ages.
Every teacher disliked working in the open-plan spaces.
Open-plan schools were more expensive to build than conventional ones.
What did acoustic studies find?
options: comprehension dropped once noise passed a threshold
older pupils were most affected by noise

questions.q: Why did some teachers struggle in shared spaces?
options: they were given too few resources
coordinating lessons required skills they had not been trained in

questions.q: The most successful modern designs favour spaces that can be ________, using movable partitions.
Architects concede that a building can, at worst, quietly ________ a chosen approach to teaching.

listening.title: Lecture: The Acoustics of Learning Spaces
listening.script:
LECTURER: Today we'll look at why sound matters so much in classrooms, a factor architects long overlooked. Let's start with a simple measure: reverberation time, the number of seconds a sound takes to fade away after its source stops. In an ideal primary classroom, this should be around point four seconds. Many older rooms measure double that, which blurs speech badly.

The second concept is the signal-to-noise ratio, the gap between the teacher's voice and background noise. For young children, that gap needs to be at least fifteen decibels. Below it, comprehension collapses, especially for second-language learners.

Now, the good news: solutions are often cheap. Carpeting, soft furnishings, and ceiling panels can cut reverberation dramatically. One school we studied reduced reverberation by half simply by adding acoustic tiles, and test scores in listening tasks rose by twelve per cent within a term.

The takeaway is straightforward: before blaming a child's concentration, measure the room. Next week we'll examine lighting.

questions.q: Reverberation time is the number of ________ a sound takes to fade after its source stops.

Which group is most affected when the signal-to-noise ratio is too low?
By adding acoustic tiles, one school raised listening test scores by ________ per cent.

reading.title: The Slow Death of the Architectural Ornament
reading.passage:
For most of recorded history, the surface of a building was treated as an invitation to elaboration. Carved foliage, painted friezes and intricate mouldings were not regarded as needless extravagance but as the visible proof of a builder's skill and a patron's standing. The cathedral, the palace and even the modest civic hall competed to display craftsmanship, and the ornament that covered them carried meanings that any educated observer could decode.

The twentieth century overturned this assumption with remarkable speed. In an essay that has since become notorious, the Austrian architect Adolf Loos argued that ornament was not merely outdated but morally suspect, a waste of labour that a rational society ought to outgrow. His polemic was deliberately provocative, yet it captured a mood already gathering force. As steel frames and reinforced concrete made it possible to raise tall, unadorned façades cheaply, the economic logic of decoration began to collapse. A plain wall was faster to build and easier to clean, and to many it now looked honest rather than impoverished.

The consequences were not confined to taste. Whole trades that had sustained generations of stonemasons, plasterers and woodcarvers withered within a few decades. Skills that had been transmitted through apprenticeship were no longer in demand, and the knowledge of how to execute them began to fade. By the time critics started to lament the monotony of the modern street, much of the craftsmanship needed to reverse the trend had already disappeared.

Recent years have seen a cautious reappraisal. Some architects now insist that the human eye craves texture and incident, and that the relentlessly smooth surfaces of mid-century modernism leave cities feeling cold. Yet reviving ornament is not simply a matter of will. Where decoration does reappear, it is frequently produced by computer-controlled cutting machines rather than by hand, a development that purists dismiss as a hollow imitation of the original tradition. Whether such methods can restore the richness of the past, or merely simulate its appearance, remains an open question that the profession has yet to resolve.

questions.q: Before the twentieth century, ornament on buildings was widely seen as a sign of skill and status.
Loos claimed that ornament was both old-fashioned and ethically questionable.
Loos's essay was the sole cause of the shift away from decoration.
The decline of decorative trades happened gradually over several centuries.
Loos personally regretted the loss of traditional craft skills late in his career.
What does the passage suggest was a practical advantage of plain walls?
options: They lasted longer than carved surfaces
They were cheaper and easier to maintain
They were considered more beautiful by everyone

questions.q: Why is the recent revival of ornament described as problematic?
options: Cities no longer have space for it
Much of the necessary hand-craft knowledge has been lost

questions.q: Critics of machine-made ornament regard it as a hollow ________ of the original tradition.

listening.title: Enquiring About a Design Studio Membership

RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, Maker's Yard design studio, how can I help?
CALLER: Hi, I'd like to ask about becoming a member so I can use the workshop.
RECEPTIONIST: Of course. We offer two plans. The standard membership is forty-five pounds a month, and the full plan, which includes the laser cutter, is sixty pounds.
CALLER: I think I'll need the laser cutter, so the full one. Do I have to pay for a whole year up front?
RECEPTIONIST: No, it's billed monthly, but the first payment includes a one-off joining fee of twenty pounds.
CALLER: All right. And what are the opening hours?
RECEPTIONIST: Weekdays we're open from nine until eight, and on Saturdays we close at five. We're shut on Sundays.
CALLER: Great. Is there an induction I need to do?
RECEPTIONIST: Yes, a two-hour safety session before you use any of the machines. The next one is on Thursday the twelfth.
CALLER: Perfect. Could you put my name down? It's Harlow, H-A-R-L-O-W.
RECEPTIONIST: Got it. We'll send confirmation to your email.

reading.title: Colour and the Limits of the Page
reading.passage:
When a graphic designer chooses a colour on a computer screen, they are working with light. Every shade is produced by mixing red, green and blue illumination, a system capable of generating millions of vivid combinations. The trouble begins when that same design must be transferred onto paper, where colour is created not by emitting light but by absorbing it. Printed inks reflect only part of the spectrum, and the range of hues they can achieve is considerably narrower than what a glowing screen can display.

The gap between these two worlds is known in the trade as the difference in gamut. A brilliant turquoise that looks luminous on a monitor may print as a flat, slightly muddy blue, because no combination of standard inks can reproduce its intensity. Designers who fail to anticipate this are frequently dismayed when proofs arrive, and inexperienced clients may wrongly conclude that the printer has made an error. In reality, the limitation is physical, not a matter of carelessness.

Professionals manage the problem through a process called colour management. Before a job is sent to press, the digital file is adjusted so that on-screen colours are translated into the closest values the printing process can actually deliver. This requires carefully calibrated equipment and reference charts, and it depends on the designer accepting that some compromise is unavoidable. The aim is not perfect fidelity, which is impossible, but a result that preserves the intended mood and relationships between colours.

Interestingly, the constraints of print have sometimes been embraced rather than resisted. Certain designers deliberately restrict themselves to a small, reliable palette, arguing that limitation sharpens rather than weakens a composition. A poster that uses only two well-chosen inks can be more striking than one that attempts to dazzle with every available hue. The history of design offers many examples in which scarcity of means, far from being a handicap, became the very source of a work's distinctive character.

questions.q: Screens create colour by emitting light, whereas printed pages create it by reflecting light.
Printed inks can reproduce a wider range of colours than computer screens.
Inexperienced clients sometimes blame the printer for colour differences that are actually physical limitations.
Colour management aims to achieve a perfect match between screen and print.
Most professional designers prefer working in print to working on screen.
What does the term 'gamut' refer to in the passage?
options: The range of colours a medium can produce
The speed of a printing press

questions.q: What point is made about designers who use a limited palette?
options: They believe restriction can strengthen a design

questions.q: Colour management depends on carefully ________ equipment and reference charts.

listening.title: A Lecture on Early Poster Design
listening.script: LECTURER: Right, today we're looking at the rise of the printed poster in the late nineteenth century. Now, before colour lithography became cheap, posters were mostly text, often crammed with tiny lettering. What changed everything was a technique that let artists draw directly onto stone, which meant images could be reproduced quickly and in large numbers. The first figure I want you to note is Jules Chéret, sometimes called the father of the modern poster. By the eighteen-nineties his workshop in Paris was producing thousands of designs a year. Chéret's great innovation was simplicity. Instead of cluttering the page, he used just three or four bold colours and a single dominant figure, usually a cheerful woman, to catch the eye from across a busy street. Remember, a poster has only a couple of seconds to communicate. His followers took this further. They reduced the message to a striking image and a few words, a principle that still governs advertising today. For next week, please read chapter four and bring one poster you find effective.

reading.title: The Quiet Revolution of the Sans-Serif
reading.passage:
Few people pause to consider the shapes of the letters they read every day, yet the design of typefaces has a long and surprisingly contentious history. For centuries, printed text in the West was dominated by serif fonts, recognisable by the small projecting strokes at the ends of their letters. These features were inherited from the chisel marks of Roman inscriptions and the flourishes of handwriting, and to early printers they seemed simply the natural way that letters ought to look.

The sans-serif, stripped of these decorative endings, emerged comparatively late. When such letterforms first appeared in the early nineteenth century, many readers found them crude, even ugly, and they were initially confined to advertising and signage, where boldness mattered more than elegance. It took the upheavals of the early twentieth century, and especially the influence of designers associated with the German Bauhaus, to recast the sans-serif as something modern and principled. To these reformers, the clean lines of a font without ornament expressed the spirit of a rational, machine-made age.

Debate has long surrounded the question of which style is easier to read. A common assumption holds that serifs guide the eye smoothly along a line of text, making them preferable for long passages, while sans-serif faces are clearer at small sizes or on screens. Yet rigorous studies have repeatedly failed to establish a decisive advantage for either. Much of what readers perceive as legibility appears to stem from familiarity: people simply read most fluently the styles they encounter most often.

The rise of digital screens has complicated the picture further. Early displays rendered fine serifs poorly, so sans-serif faces became the default for websites and software, accustoming a generation to their appearance. As screen resolution has improved, serifs have returned to digital text, but the older preference lingers. The story of the typeface is thus not a steady march towards greater clarity, but a series of reversals shaped as much by technology and fashion as by any objective measure of how well we read.

questions.q: Serif fonts dominated Western printing for hundreds of years before sans-serif faces became common.
When sans-serif letterforms first appeared, they were immediately praised for their elegance.
Bauhaus-associated designers helped present the sans-serif as modern and principled.
Studies have clearly proved that serif fonts are easier to read than sans-serif fonts.
The most widely used typeface on the internet today is a serif font.
According to the passage, what largely explains why a font seems legible to a reader?
options: The size of the printed page
How familiar the reader is with the style
The colour of the ink used

questions.q: Why did sans-serif faces become the default on early digital screens?
options: Early displays rendered fine serifs poorly
Serif fonts were banned from software

questions.q: The story of the typeface is described as a series of ________ shaped by technology and fashion.

listening.title: Choosing Fonts for a University Project
listening.script:
TUTOR: So, you wanted some advice on the typography for your final report?
STUDENT: Yes. I'm not sure whether to use a serif or sans-serif font for the body text.
TUTOR: For a long printed document, most people still go with a serif for the main text. It tends to feel more comfortable over many pages.
STUDENT: Right. And for the headings?
TUTOR: A sans-serif works well there. The contrast helps the reader navigate. Just don't use more than two fonts in total.
STUDENT: Okay, two fonts maximum. What about the size?
TUTOR: Eleven point for the body is sensible, perhaps twelve if your tutor's eyesight isn't what it was. And make sure your line spacing is at least one point five.
STUDENT: Got it. One last thing, should I justify the text?
TUTOR: I'd avoid full justification, actually. It can create ugly gaps. Left-aligned is safer. And submit it as a PDF, not a Word file, so the fonts don't shift.

reading.title: Designing for the Senses: The Rise of Tactile Architecture
reading.passage:
Architecture is usually discussed as though it were addressed solely to the eye. Photographs in magazines reduce a building to a flattering image, and the awards that shape professional reputations are frequently decided on the basis of such pictures. Yet a growing number of practitioners argue that this visual bias has impoverished the way buildings are conceived, neglecting the fact that we experience space through the whole body, not merely by looking at it.

The most influential statement of this view came from the Finnish architect Juhani Pallasmaa, who maintained that the dominance of sight has produced buildings that look impressive but feel sterile. A space, he argued, communicates through the coolness of a stone floor underfoot, the muffled acoustics of a carpeted room, even the faint scent of timber. These sensations register below the level of conscious attention, yet they powerfully shape whether a place feels welcoming or hostile. A building that ignores them may photograph beautifully while remaining unpleasant to inhabit.

Designing for the other senses, however, is far harder than designing for the eye. The visual qualities of a proposal can be conveyed in drawings and shown to a client before a single brick is laid; the way a corridor will sound, or how a handrail will feel in winter, can scarcely be represented in advance. As a result, such considerations are easily sacrificed when budgets tighten, since their absence is difficult to notice on paper and their presence is hard to justify to those holding the purse strings.

Some designers have nonetheless made sensory richness central to their work. Materials are chosen for how they age and how they respond to touch; daylight is shaped to shift across a room through the day; surfaces are varied so that footsteps produce changing sounds. Critics of the movement caution that an obsession with atmosphere can become self-indulgent, distracting from more pressing concerns such as cost and accessibility. Its defenders reply that buildings are made for people to live in, and that a structure which neglects the body has failed at its most basic task, however striking it may appear in a photograph.

questions.q: The reputations of architects are often influenced by photographs of their buildings.
Pallasmaa argued that visually impressive buildings can feel sterile.
Sensory qualities such as sound and touch are easy to represent in advance drawings.
Sensory considerations are often the first thing cut when budgets are reduced.
Pallasmaa designed several award-winning buildings himself.
Why are non-visual qualities easily sacrificed during a project?
options: Their absence is hard to notice on paper
They are illegal in many countries
They make buildings more expensive to photograph

questions.q: What warning do critics of sensory design give?
options: It can become self-indulgent and ignore practical concerns

questions.q: According to Pallasmaa, sensations such as a cool floor often register below the level of conscious ________.

listening.title: A Museum Audio Guide: The Sensory Gallery
listening.script: GUIDE: Welcome to the Sensory Gallery, the newest wing of the museum. Unlike our other galleries, this space is designed to be touched. Please feel free to run your hands over the exhibits. The first section explores texture in architecture. In front of you are seven material samples, ranging from rough granite to polished bronze. Notice how the bronze feels warmer to the touch even though both are at room temperature. That's because metal conducts heat away from your skin more slowly than you might expect. Now, move toward the second section, where the lighting changes. We've installed windows that track the sun, so the quality of light here shifts throughout the day. Architects call this daylighting, and it's a key tool for shaping mood. Our final section is about sound. Step onto the wooden platform and listen to how your footsteps echo, then onto the cork floor beside it, where the same steps fall silent. The contrast shows how flooring alone can transform the feeling of a room. The gallery closes at four thirty today.

reading.title: The Contested Idea of Restoration
reading.passage:
When an ancient building falls into decay, the decision about how to treat it is rarely as straightforward as it first appears. To the casual visitor, restoration seems an obvious good: a crumbling ruin is patched, repainted and returned to something like its former glory. Among specialists, however, the very concept has been the subject of fierce and long-running disagreement, touching on deep questions about authenticity and the proper relationship between the present and the past.

In the nineteenth century, two opposing positions crystallised. The French architect Eugène Viollet-le-Duc championed an ambitious approach, holding that a restorer should return a building to a state of completeness it may never actually have possessed, correcting the irregularities of earlier builders to realise the design as it ideally ought to have been. His critics were appalled. The English writer John Ruskin denounced such intervention as a destructive falsification, arguing that the marks left by centuries of weathering were precisely what made an old building valuable. For Ruskin, to scrape away that history was to destroy the only thing worth preserving.

This dispute was never fully settled, but the twentieth century saw the gradual triumph of a more cautious philosophy. International agreements began to insist that any new work should be clearly distinguishable from the original fabric, so that future observers would not be deceived about what was authentic and what was a later addition. Repairs should be reversible where possible, and intervention kept to the minimum necessary to halt further decay. The aim shifted from re-creation towards conservation: keeping what survives rather than imagining what once was.

Even this consensus, however, conceals continuing tensions. A war-damaged city may understandably wish to rebuild a beloved monument exactly as it stood, treating faithful reconstruction as an act of cultural recovery rather than deception. Tourism, too, exerts a quiet pressure, since visitors are often drawn to a polished, legible past rather than to ambiguous fragments. The modern restorer must therefore weigh competing claims that cannot all be satisfied at once, and no formula can dissolve the underlying conflict between honouring a building's history and meeting the expectations of those who encounter it today.

questions.q: To most ordinary visitors, restoring a decayed building seems clearly beneficial.
Viollet-le-Duc believed a restorer should recreate an idealised version of a building's design.
Ruskin supported the practice of removing signs of weathering from old buildings.
Twentieth-century agreements wanted new repairs to be clearly distinguishable from original material.
Viollet-le-Duc and Ruskin met in person to debate their disagreement.
What shift in aim does the passage describe over the twentieth century?
options: From minimal repair towards full rebuilding

questions.q: Why might a war-damaged city rebuild a monument exactly as it was?
options: As an act of cultural recovery

questions.q: Modern guidelines suggest that repairs should ideally be ________ where possible.

listening.title: A Discussion About a Heritage Restoration Project
listening.script:
PRESENTER: Today I'm joined by Dr Elena Voss, who's overseeing the restoration of the old market hall. Elena, where do you begin with a project like this?
VOSS: The first stage is always documentation. Before we touch anything, we record the entire structure in detail, often with laser scanning, so we have a permanent reference.
PRESENTER: And how do you decide what to repair and what to leave?
VOSS: Our guiding principle is minimal intervention. We only act where the building is genuinely at risk, for example where water is getting into the roof timbers.
PRESENTER: I understand you're using a special lime mortar rather than modern cement.
VOSS: That's right. Cement is too rigid and actually traps moisture, which damages old stone. Lime mortar lets the walls breathe.
PRESENTER: When will the hall reopen?
VOSS: We're aiming for spring, though heritage work is notoriously unpredictable. The total budget, by the way, is three point two million pounds.
PRESENTER: A significant sum. Thank you, Elena.

reading.title: The Hidden Economics of Loyalty Programmes
reading.passage:
Customer loyalty schemes — the points cards, tiered memberships and cashback offers that fill modern wallets — are often presented as a generous reward for repeat custom. Yet from a commercial standpoint, their primary purpose is rarely benevolence. They are sophisticated instruments designed to alter behaviour, gather data, and entrench a relationship between buyer and seller that benefits the latter at least as much as the former.

The behavioural logic is well understood. By converting purchases into accumulating points, retailers exploit what psychologists call the endowment effect: once consumers feel they have built up a balance, abandoning the scheme feels like a tangible loss rather than a neutral choice. This artificial sense of ownership raises the cost of switching to a competitor, even when a rival offers objectively better prices. The points themselves cost the firm little, since a substantial proportion are never redeemed at all.

Arguably more valuable than the lock-in, however, is the information harvested. Each transaction recorded against a membership number reveals not merely what a customer buys, but when, how often, and in what combinations. Aggregated across millions of accounts, this data allows firms to forecast demand, tailor promotions to individuals, and identify the precise discounts needed to nudge a hesitant shopper. The currency a customer earns in points, critics contend, is dwarfed by the currency they surrender in personal information.

None of this means loyalty programmes are worthless to consumers. For disciplined shoppers who would have made the purchases regardless, the rewards represent a genuine, if modest, saving. The difficulty arises when the scheme induces spending that would not otherwise have occurred, or when convenience discourages the comparison of alternatives. Whether a programme ultimately serves the shopper or the shop depends less on the headline rewards than on how the consumer chooses to use it.

questions.q: The writer believes the main aim of loyalty schemes is to reward customers generously.
How does the endowment effect benefit retailers?
options: it lowers the price of every product
it makes leaving a scheme feel like a loss, raising switching costs
it guarantees points are always redeemed

questions.q: A large share of loyalty points are never redeemed.
The writer suggests the data collected is arguably more valuable to firms than the customer ________.
Loyalty programmes are described as completely worthless to all consumers.
Loyalty schemes have been shown to increase the average household's overall debt.
According to the final paragraph, whether a programme benefits the shopper depends mainly on
options: the size of the headline rewards
how the consumer chooses to use it
the number of competitors in the market
the price of the membership card

questions.q: Transaction records reveal not only what a customer buys but when, how often, and in what ________.

listening.title: Enquiry: Joining a Business Co-working Space
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, Bridgeway Co-working, how can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I'm a freelance consultant and I'm looking for a desk. Could you tell me about your plans?

RECEPTIONIST: Of course. We offer three tiers. The flexible plan is forty pounds a week and gives you a hot desk in the open area. The dedicated plan is one hundred and sixty pounds a month for your own fixed desk. And the private office starts at four hundred pounds a month.

CALLER: The dedicated plan sounds right. Does that include meeting rooms?

RECEPTIONIST: You get eight hours of meeting-room time included each month. After that it's twelve pounds an hour.

CALLER: And the internet?

RECEPTIONIST: Fibre broadband is included, along with unlimited coffee and access from seven in the morning until ten at night.

CALLER: Perfect. How do I sign up?

RECEPTIONIST: Just bring some photo identification and a deposit equal to one month's fee. We can have you set up the same day.

CALLER: Great, I'll come in on Thursday.

reading.title: Why Some Cities Become Economic Hubs
reading.passage:
Why do a handful of cities come to dominate the economic life of entire nations, while others, often equally well placed on the map, remain peripheral? Economists have long puzzled over this uneven distribution of prosperity, and the explanations that satisfy them have shifted over time. Early accounts leaned heavily on geography — proximity to ports, rivers or mineral deposits. Such factors mattered, certainly, but they cannot account for cities that flourished far from any obvious natural advantage.

A more persuasive line of reasoning emphasises what are termed agglomeration economies: the self-reinforcing benefits that arise when firms and skilled workers cluster together. When many companies in related fields locate in one place, a deep pool of specialised labour forms. Workers can move between employers without relocating, and firms can fill vacancies quickly. Ideas, too, circulate more freely, as employees exchange knowledge formally and informally. Each advantage attracts further firms and workers, and the cluster grows denser still.

This dynamic helps explain why economic leadership, once established, proves so durable. A city that gains an early lead accumulates advantages that are difficult for rivals to replicate, however generous the incentives those rivals offer. Newcomers face the daunting task of building from scratch the dense networks that the leading city already possesses. The result is a tendency towards concentration that markets, left to themselves, do little to reverse.

Yet dominance is not permanent. Costs rise as cities grow congested; housing becomes unaffordable, and the very density that once spurred innovation can tip into gridlock. Improvements in communication may also loosen the grip of geography, allowing some activities to disperse. Whether today's leading hubs retain their position will depend on whether the forces pulling firms together continue to outweigh the mounting pressures pushing them apart.

questions.q: What limitation does the writer identify in geographical explanations?
options: geography has never mattered to cities
they cannot explain cities that thrived without natural advantages
ports and rivers are economically irrelevant
they only apply to small towns

questions.q: The self-reinforcing benefits of firms and workers clustering together are called ________ economies.
When related firms cluster, a deep pool of specialised labour tends to form.
Once a city gains an economic lead, that lead is easily overturned by rivals offering incentives.
Most leading economic hubs were founded by government planners.
According to the final paragraph, what could weaken a hub's dominance?
options: the elimination of all communication technology
rising costs, congestion and improved communication
an increase in the pool of skilled labour
the arrival of more related firms

questions.q: The writer says markets left to themselves do little to reverse the tendency towards ________.

listening.title: Tutorial: Preparing a Small Business Plan
listening.script:
TUTOR: So you're putting together a business plan for the start-up module. Let me walk you through the sections we expect.

STUDENT: Thanks. I wasn't sure where to begin.

TUTOR: Start with the executive summary — that's a single page summarising the whole plan. Write it last, even though it appears first. Then comes the market analysis, where you describe your customers and competitors. The most common mistake here is being too vague, so use real figures wherever you can.

STUDENT: How long should the financial section be?

TUTOR: For this assignment, three years of projections is plenty. We want a cash-flow forecast, not a full set of accounts. The key thing assessors look for is whether your assumptions are realistic.

STUDENT: And presentation?

TUTOR: Keep it to twenty pages maximum. Anything longer suggests you can't prioritise. Submit it as a single PDF by the ninth of March, and remember the late penalty is five per cent per day.

STUDENT: Got it. I'll aim to finish a week early.

TUTOR: Sensible. Come to my office hours on Tuesday if you get stuck.

reading.title: The Paradox of Choice in Markets
reading.passage:
It is an article of faith in much economic thinking that more choice is always better for the consumer. Greater variety, the argument runs, allows each individual to find precisely the product that suits their preferences, while the competition between sellers drives down prices and spurs innovation. For most of the twentieth century, this proposition went largely unchallenged. In recent decades, however, a body of research has complicated the picture, suggesting that the relationship between choice and welfare is far from straightforward.

The difficulty is that evaluating options is itself costly. Every additional product a shopper must consider consumes time and mental effort, and beyond a certain point these costs begin to outweigh the benefits of variety. Faced with dozens of nearly identical alternatives, consumers frequently report feeling anxious rather than empowered, and a significant proportion abandon the decision altogether. Paradoxically, an abundance of options can leave people less likely to buy anything at all.

Firms have not been slow to exploit this. Where comparison is genuinely difficult, sellers gain room to obscure the true cost of their products. Mobile-phone tariffs, energy contracts and insurance policies are often deliberately structured so that no two offers can be compared on identical terms. The headline price advertised may bear little resemblance to what a customer eventually pays once usage patterns and hidden conditions are taken into account. Complexity, in such markets, is not an accident but a strategy.

This does not amount to an argument for restricting choice. Variety remains valuable, and few would wish to return to an era of uniform products. The lesson, rather, is that choice and the ability to choose well are not the same thing. Markets serve consumers best not when they offer the largest number of options, but when they present those options in a form that ordinary people can actually understand and compare.

questions.q: The view that more choice is always better went largely unchallenged for most of the twentieth century.
Why can an abundance of options reduce purchasing?
options: because evaluating many options is costly and can cause people to give up
because variety is illegal in some markets
because consumers always prefer one brand

questions.q: Faced with many similar alternatives, consumers often feel anxious rather than ________.
The writer says complexity in some markets is a deliberate strategy rather than an accident.
Government regulators have successfully simplified mobile-phone tariffs in most countries.
What is the writer's overall conclusion?
options: uniform products are preferable to variety
markets serve consumers best when options can be understood and compared
the headline price is always accurate

questions.q: The writer distinguishes between choice and the ability to choose ________.
The writer argues for a return to an era of uniform products.

listening.title: Radio Feature: Behavioural Pricing
listening.script:
PRESENTER: Welcome back. Today we're looking at how retailers set prices to influence what we buy — a field sometimes called behavioural pricing. My guest is economist Dr Reyes.

REYES: Thanks for having me. The first thing to understand is that prices are signals, not just numbers. Take the classic ninety-nine ending. A jacket priced at forty-nine dollars feels meaningfully cheaper than fifty, even though the gap is a single cent. We read the left-hand digit first, and that anchors our perception.

PRESENTER: So it's about how we process the figure.

REYES: Exactly. Another technique is the decoy. Suppose a shop sells a small coffee for two dollars and a large for four. Adding a medium at three-fifty makes the large look like good value, even though almost nobody buys the medium. Its real job is to shift choices.

PRESENTER: Clever. Can consumers protect themselves?

REYES: To a degree. The best defence is to decide what you actually need before you see the prices. Once you know your budget, these tricks lose most of their power.

questions.q: The field of setting prices to influence buying is called ________ pricing.
Why does a price ending in ninety-nine feel cheaper?
options: the product is actually cheaper to make
we read the left-hand digit first, which anchors perception
shops are legally required to use it

questions.q: Dr Reyes says prices are ________, not just numbers.
What is the real purpose of the medium-sized coffee in the example?
options: to make the large look like good value
to lower the price of the small

questions.q: Almost nobody actually buys the decoy option.
The best defence is to decide what you need before you see the ________.

speaking.q: Let's discuss consumer choice. Some people say that having a huge range of products makes life better, while others feel it makes decisions more stressful. What is your view? Do you think businesses sometimes make choices deliberately confusing? How might consumers be protected? Develop your answer with reasons and examples.

Paradoxically, more options can mean fewer sales.

reading.title: Rethinking Economic Growth
reading.passage:
For the better part of a century, the health of an economy has been judged primarily by a single figure: gross domestic product, or GDP, which measures the total value of goods and services a country produces in a year. Politicians compete to raise it, commentators anxiously track its quarterly movements, and a country whose GDP is expanding is routinely described as succeeding. Yet a growing chorus of economists now questions whether this familiar yardstick measures the right things — or whether, in some respects, it measures the wrong ones.

The criticisms are varied but related. GDP counts all market activity as positive, drawing no distinction between spending that genuinely improves lives and spending that merely repairs damage. The costs of cleaning up an oil spill, treating preventable illness, or rebuilding after a flood all register as growth, even though they reflect harm rather than progress. At the same time, much that people value most — unpaid care, leisure, a clean environment, the depletion of natural resources — falls outside its scope entirely.

These blind spots are not merely technical. Because GDP is treated as a proxy for national wellbeing, the things it ignores tend to be undervalued in policy. A government anxious to boost the figure may favour activities that show up in the accounts over those, often more beneficial, that do not. Critics argue that what gets measured gets managed, and that a flawed measure quietly distorts the priorities of an entire society.

Proposals for reform are plentiful. Some advocate supplementing GDP with indicators of health, education and environmental quality; others would replace it with broader measures of wellbeing altogether. None has yet displaced the incumbent, whose great virtue — a single, comparable number — is also the source of its limitations. Whether a richer set of measures can ever command the same authority remains, for now, an open question.

questions.q: GDP measures the total value of goods and ________ a country produces in a year.
What objection does the writer raise about how GDP counts spending?
options: it treats spending that repairs damage the same as spending that improves lives

questions.q: Unpaid care and a clean environment are fully captured within GDP.
Because GDP is treated as a proxy for wellbeing, the things it ignores tend to be undervalued in policy.
The writer states that most economists now agree on a single replacement for GDP.
The first nation to officially abandon GDP was a small Himalayan kingdom.
According to the writer, why has no alternative yet replaced GDP?
options: GDP's single comparable number is hard to match in authority

questions.q: Critics argue that what gets measured gets ________.

listening.title: Lecture: Measuring National Wellbeing
listening.script:
LECTURER: This week we turn to the problem of measuring how well a country is really doing. As you'll recall, GDP has dominated the field for decades, but today I want to introduce some alternatives that economists and statisticians have devised.

The first is the Human Development Index, or HDI. Rather than looking only at income, it combines three dimensions: life expectancy, years of schooling, and income per person. A country can be wealthy yet score poorly if its people are poorly educated or short-lived. The HDI was introduced in nineteen ninety, and it remains widely cited today.

A second approach focuses directly on what people report about their own lives — so-called subjective wellbeing. Researchers simply ask large samples how satisfied they feel, typically on a scale from zero to ten. Critics worry that such answers are influenced by temporary moods, but over large populations the results turn out to be surprisingly stable.

Finally, some statisticians attempt to put a monetary value on the environment, treating clean air and forests as natural capital that can be depleted. The aim is to ensure that running down these assets shows up as a cost, not as growth. We'll examine each of these in detail next week.

questions.q: Which three dimensions does the HDI combine?
options: life expectancy, years of schooling and income per person

questions.q: The Human Development Index was introduced in ________.
Subjective wellbeing surveys typically use a scale from zero to ________.
Over large populations, subjective wellbeing results turn out to be surprisingly stable.
Some statisticians treat clean air and forests as natural ________.
What is the aim of valuing the environment as natural capital?
options: to ensure depleting these assets shows up as a cost

speaking.q: Let's talk about your country's economy and daily life. Do you think people in your country feel financially comfortable? What do most people spend their money on? Has the cost of living changed recently where you live? Give reasons for your answers.

writing.q:
The table below shows selected wellbeing and economic indicators for four countries in 2024. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Country | GDP per person (US$) | Life satisfaction (0-10) | Average years of schooling
Country W | 62,000 | 7.4 | 13.2
Country X | 18,000 | 6.1 | 9.8
Country Y | 45,000 | 7.6 | 12.5
Country Z | 9,000 | 5.3 | 7.1

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: GDP is a familiar yardstick of success.
A growing chorus of economists disagrees.
GDP is used as a proxy for wellbeing.
The depletion of resources is ignored.
Repair costs register as growth.
A flawed measure distorts priorities.
Some would supplement GDP with health data.
No measure has displaced the incumbent.
No alternative has displaced GDP.

Economists have devised alternatives.
Natural capital can be depleted.

reading.title: The Quiet Revolution in Global Trade
reading.passage:
When people picture international trade, they tend to imagine giant container ships laden with finished goods — cars, televisions, clothing — crossing the oceans between distant nations. This image, though not wrong, is increasingly out of date. Over recent decades the character of global commerce has been transformed by a development that attracts far less attention than tariffs or trade wars: the fragmentation of production into intricate, cross-border supply chains.

The essential point is that a modern product is rarely made in any single country. A smartphone may be designed in one nation, assembled in another from components manufactured in a dozen more, and sold worldwide. What crosses borders, increasingly, is not finished goods but tasks — particular stages in a long chain of production, each performed wherever it can be done most cheaply or skilfully. Statistics that record only the value of final exports can therefore be deeply misleading, attributing the full worth of a product to the country that merely assembled it.

This reorganisation has brought undeniable gains. By allowing each stage to be located optimally, it has lowered costs, spread manufacturing expertise to developing economies, and given consumers access to cheaper, more sophisticated goods. Countries once shut out of global markets have found a foothold by specialising in a single link of the chain rather than attempting to build an entire industry from nothing.

Yet the same interdependence carries risks that recent disruptions have laid bare. A flood, a factory fire or a political dispute in one country can halt production thousands of miles away, as firms that hold minimal stock discover that a missing component can idle an entire assembly line. Some businesses have responded by shortening their chains or holding larger reserves, sacrificing a measure of efficiency for resilience. Whether this marks a lasting reversal, or a temporary adjustment, is not yet clear.

questions.q: What development does the writer say has transformed global trade?
options: the fragmentation of production into cross-border supply chains

questions.q: The writer says what increasingly crosses borders is not finished goods but ________.
Statistics recording only final exports can be misleading.
Some developing countries have entered global markets by specialising in a single link of the chain.
The writer states that supply-chain disruptions have permanently ended global trade growth.
The author predicts that most manufacturing will return to a single country within ten years.
How have some businesses responded to recent disruptions?
options: by extending their chains across more countries
by shortening chains or holding larger reserves, trading efficiency for resilience
by holding no stock at all

questions.q: Firms that hold minimal stock find that a missing component can idle an entire ________ line.

listening.title: Discussion: Managing Supply-Chain Risk
listening.script:
MODERATOR: Welcome to the panel. Today we're discussing how companies are rethinking their supply chains. Priya, you advise manufacturers. What's changed?

PRIYA: For years the goal was pure efficiency — keep stock as low as possible, source from wherever was cheapest. That worked beautifully until the disruptions of the past few years. Suddenly firms with no buffer found themselves unable to deliver.

MODERATOR: So what are they doing now?

PRIYA: Three things, mainly. First, diversifying suppliers, so no single factory is a point of failure. Second, holding more inventory as a cushion. And third, moving some production closer to home — what's often called nearshoring.

MODERATOR: Doesn't all that cost more?

PRIYA: It does. The estimate I usually quote is that resilience adds around eight per cent to costs. But many boards now accept that as the price of reliability. The cheapest chain is no longer seen as the best chain.

MODERATOR: Fascinating. We'll take questions after the break.

questions.q: What was the main goal of supply chains for years, according to Priya?
Moving some production closer to home is often called ________.
Diversifying suppliers means no single factory is a point of failure.
Priya estimates that resilience adds around ________ per cent to costs.
What attitude do many company boards now take?
options: the cheapest chain is always best
they accept higher costs as the price of reliability
they refuse to hold any inventory

questions.q: Priya says holding more inventory acts as a cushion.

speaking.q: Describe a product you bought that was made in another country. You should say: what the product was; where it was made or where its parts came from; why you chose it; and explain how you feel about buying goods produced abroad.

writing.q: Many companies now produce goods using complex international supply chains spread across many countries. Some people believe this benefits everyone, while others think it makes economies too dependent on one another. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.

The fragmentation of production reshaped trade.
Intricate supply chains span many borders.
A phone is built from many components.

reading.title: The Hunt for Planet Nine
reading.passage:
For more than a century, astronomers have suspected that our solar system might harbour an undiscovered world far beyond the orbit of Neptune. The most recent version of this idea, often referred to as Planet Nine, emerged not from a direct sighting but from a pattern that seemed too orderly to be the product of chance.

The clue lies in a group of distant icy bodies in the Kuiper Belt. Several of these objects follow elongated orbits that are clustered together and tilted in the same direction. According to the researchers who first highlighted the anomaly, such an arrangement is unlikely to have arisen randomly. Instead, they argue, the gravitational influence of a hidden planet, perhaps five to ten times the mass of Earth, could be shepherding these bodies into their peculiar alignment.

Critics, however, urge caution. The number of objects studied is small, and observations of such faint, remote bodies are notoriously prone to bias. Telescopes tend to detect objects in the regions where astronomers happen to be looking, which may create the illusion of clustering where none truly exists. Until a larger and more representative sample is gathered, they maintain, the case remains unproven.

Resolving the dispute will require either a direct detection or a definitive demonstration that the apparent pattern is a statistical artefact. New survey telescopes, capable of scanning vast swathes of sky each night, are expected to settle the matter within the coming decade. Whether they reveal a genuine ninth planet or quietly dissolve the anomaly, the search itself has already sharpened our understanding of the solar system's distant frontier.

questions.q: Planet Nine was first identified through a direct telescopic sighting.
Some distant Kuiper Belt objects have orbits that are clustered and tilted in a similar way.
Why do critics urge caution about the Planet Nine hypothesis?
options: The proposed planet is too small to detect
The sample of objects is small and observations may be biased

questions.q: Researchers estimate the hidden planet could be five to ten times the mass of ________.
The researchers who proposed Planet Nine have calculated its exact surface temperature.
New survey telescopes are expected to settle the question within the coming ________.
What does the writer conclude about the search?
options: It has been a complete waste of resources
It has already improved understanding of the distant solar system
It has definitively proven Planet Nine exists
It will never produce any useful result

listening.title: Booking a Planetarium Visit
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, Riverside Planetarium, how can I help?

VISITOR: Hello, I'd like to book tickets for the evening star show this Saturday.

RECEPTIONIST: Of course. The Saturday show starts at half past seven. How many tickets would you like?

VISITOR: Four, please. Two adults and two children.

RECEPTIONIST: Right. Adult tickets are twelve pounds each and child tickets are seven pounds, so that comes to thirty-eight pounds in total.

VISITOR: That's fine. Is there anything we should know before we arrive?

RECEPTIONIST: Yes, please arrive at least fifteen minutes early, because the doors close once the show begins and latecomers can't be admitted. The show lasts about fifty minutes.

VISITOR: Understood. And is there parking on site?

RECEPTIONIST: There's a free car park behind the building, but it's small, so on busy nights it fills up quickly. Could I take a name for the booking?

VISITOR: Yes, it's under Harrington. That's H-A-double R-I-N-G-T-O-N.

RECEPTIONIST: Lovely. I'll email your confirmation now. Enjoy the show.
