reading.title: How Stars Forge the Elements
reading.passage:
Almost every atom in the human body was once manufactured inside a star. The carbon in our cells, the calcium in our bones, and the iron in our blood were all produced by nuclear reactions that took place long before the Sun and Earth came into existence. Understanding how stars create these elements has become one of the great achievements of modern astrophysics.

In the cores of stars, immense pressure and temperature force hydrogen nuclei to fuse together, releasing energy and producing helium. As a star ages and its hydrogen supply dwindles, it begins to fuse helium into heavier elements such as carbon and oxygen. The most massive stars can continue this process through successive stages, building up progressively heavier nuclei until they reach iron, which is the point at which fusion ceases to yield energy.

Elements heavier than iron require even more extreme conditions, and these are supplied by catastrophic events. When a massive star exhausts its fuel, its core collapses and the outer layers are blasted into space in a supernova. The energy unleashed in such explosions forges elements like gold and uranium and scatters them across interstellar space, where they may eventually be incorporated into new stars and planets.

This cosmic recycling means that each generation of stars is enriched by the debris of those that came before. The Sun, a relatively young star, formed from material already seeded with heavy elements produced by earlier generations. In a very real sense, then, the diversity of matter that makes life possible is the inheritance of countless stars that lived and died across billions of years.

questions.q: In the cores of stars, hydrogen nuclei fuse to produce ________.
Why does fusion stop yielding energy at iron?
options: Iron is too rare in stars
Iron is the point beyond which fusion no longer releases energy

questions.q: Elements heavier than iron are formed in events such as supernovae.
The Sun is described as a relatively young star.
The writer states that life exists on planets orbiting other stars.
When a massive star's core collapses, its outer layers are blasted into space in a ________.
What does 'cosmic recycling' refer to in the passage?
options: Each generation of stars being enriched by the debris of earlier ones
Planets returning material to the Sun

listening.title: Student Discussion: Astronomy Field Trip
listening.script:
TOM: Hi Priya, have you signed up for the astronomy field trip yet?

PRIYA: Not yet. Where are we actually going?

TOM: To the Mount Carter Observatory. It's about ninety minutes north of campus by coach.

PRIYA: Sounds good. What's the plan once we're there?

TOM: First there's a talk on how telescopes work, then a tour of the main dome, and after dark we get to do some observing ourselves.

PRIYA: Brilliant. Do we need to bring anything special?

TOM: Warm clothes, definitely. It gets really cold at the summit at night. And bring a notebook, because we have to record what we observe for the assessment.

PRIYA: Right. Is there a cost?

TOM: The trip itself is free, but you pay five dollars for the coach. Oh, and one important thing: don't bring a torch with a white light. It ruins everyone's night vision. A red light is fine.

PRIYA: Good to know. When do we have to sign up by?

TOM: By Friday. The list is on the noticeboard outside the lab.

PRIYA: Perfect, I'll add my name this afternoon.

What is the first activity at the observatory?
options: A talk on how telescopes work

questions.q: Students must record their observations for assessment.
Students must pay ________ dollars for the coach.

options: It runs out of battery quickly

reading.title: Listening to the Universe with Gravitational Waves
reading.passage:
For most of history, astronomy was an entirely visual science. Everything we knew about the cosmos came from light, whether the visible glow of stars or, later, the invisible radiation captured by radio and infrared instruments. In 2015, that changed dramatically when scientists detected gravitational waves for the first time, opening an entirely new way of observing the universe.

Gravitational waves are ripples in the fabric of space-time itself, predicted by Einstein a century earlier. They are produced by some of the most violent events in the cosmos, such as the collision of two black holes. As these waves spread outward, they stretch and compress space by an almost unimaginably tiny amount. Detecting them required instruments of extraordinary sensitivity, capable of measuring a change smaller than the width of a single atomic nucleus across a distance of several kilometres.

The significance of this achievement extends far beyond confirming a prediction. Because gravitational waves pass through matter almost undisturbed, they carry information that light cannot. Events hidden behind clouds of gas and dust, or occurring in regions that emit no light at all, can now be studied directly. In effect, astronomers have gained a new sense, allowing them to hear the universe rather than merely see it.

The field is still in its infancy, yet it has already reshaped our picture of the cosmos. Future detectors, including instruments based in space, promise far greater precision and may reveal events from the earliest moments after the Big Bang. What began as the faint signal of two distant black holes merging may ultimately allow us to probe regions of cosmic history that no telescope could ever reach.

questions.q: Before 2015, astronomy relied entirely on different forms of light.
What are gravitational waves described as?
Gravitational waves were predicted a century earlier by ________.
Gravitational waves are easily blocked by clouds of gas and dust.
Why are gravitational waves so valuable to astronomers?
options: They are cheaper to detect than light
They carry information that light cannot reach us with
They can only be seen by the naked eye

questions.q: The first detected gravitational waves came from two merging neutron stars.
Future detectors based in ________ may reveal events from soon after the Big Bang.
The writer suggests the field of gravitational-wave astronomy is already fully developed.

listening.title: Radio Feature: A Day in Mission Control
listening.script:
PRESENTER: Welcome back. Today I'm speaking with Dana Olsen, a flight controller at a satellite operations center. Dana, what does a typical shift involve?

DANA: Well, a shift lasts eight hours, and the most important part is monitoring the health of the spacecraft. We watch hundreds of data streams that tell us about temperature, power, and the position of each satellite.

PRESENTER: And if something goes wrong?

DANA: We have detailed procedures for almost every situation. If a satellite's battery starts overheating, for example, we can switch it to a safe mode within minutes. The key is staying calm and following the checklist.

PRESENTER: How many satellites do you look after?

DANA: My team currently manages twelve, though that number is growing every year.

PRESENTER: Is the job stressful?

DANA: It can be, especially during a launch. But honestly, the most challenging part isn't the emergencies. It's the long quiet stretches when you have to stay fully alert even though nothing is happening. Concentration is everything.

PRESENTER: Fascinating. Thank you, Dana.

questions.q: A typical shift lasts ________ hours.
What is the most important part of Dana's shift?
options: Monitoring the health of the spacecraft

questions.q: An overheating battery can be switched to safe mode within minutes.
Dana's team currently manages ________ satellites.
According to Dana, what is the most challenging part of the job?
options: Staying alert during long quiet periods

questions.q: Dana says that ________ is everything in the job.

speaking.q: Let's discuss scientific research and society. Some argue that scientific projects should only be funded if they have clear practical benefits, while others say that pure research with no obvious use is equally important. What is your view, and how should governments decide which research to support?

The table below shows the estimated annual budgets (in billions of US dollars) of four national space agencies in 2015 and 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

Agency | 2015 | 2023
Agency A | 18.0 | 25.0
Agency B | 5.2 | 7.8

These waves distort the fabric of space-time.

Detecting the signal required instruments of great sensitivity.
The waves pass through matter almost undisturbed.

reading.title: The Growing Problem of Space Debris
reading.passage:
Since the launch of the first artificial satellite in 1957, humanity has placed thousands of objects into orbit around the Earth. Many of these remain useful, providing communications, navigation, and weather data. Yet alongside the working satellites there now circulates an enormous quantity of debris: spent rocket stages, fragments of broken spacecraft, and even flecks of paint, all travelling at speeds great enough to cause serious damage.

The danger lies not in the size of individual fragments but in their velocity. An object only a centimetre across, moving at several kilometres per second, carries enough energy to disable a satellite or puncture the hull of a crewed vehicle. Because there is no air resistance in orbit to slow these fragments down, they can remain a threat for decades, or even centuries, depending on their altitude.

What alarms specialists most is the possibility of a chain reaction. If two large objects were to collide, they would shatter into thousands of smaller pieces, each capable of triggering further collisions. Some researchers warn that, beyond a certain density of debris, this cascade could become self-sustaining, rendering particular orbits unusable for generations. The scenario, first described in the late twentieth century, remains a subject of intense study.

Various solutions have been proposed, from nets and harpoons that capture defunct satellites to lasers that nudge fragments into the atmosphere, where they burn up harmlessly. None has yet been deployed on a large scale, and all face formidable technical and financial obstacles. In the meantime, the most practical measure may be prevention: designing satellites to remove themselves from busy orbits once their working life is over.

questions.q: The first artificial satellite was launched in ________.
According to the passage, why is even small debris dangerous?
options: It travels at very high velocity

questions.q: Debris in orbit is quickly slowed down by air resistance.
A collision between two large objects could produce thousands of smaller fragments.
Researchers fear a self-sustaining ________ of collisions could make some orbits unusable.
Laser systems for clearing debris have already been used on a large scale.
International law currently requires all countries to remove their old satellites.
What does the writer suggest is the most practical current measure?
options: Designing satellites to remove themselves from busy orbits
Ignoring the problem until technology improves

listening.title: Lecture: The Search for Life Beyond Earth
listening.script:
LECTURER: Today we'll examine how scientists search for signs of life beyond our own planet. It's worth stressing from the outset that this is a careful, evidence-based discipline, not a matter of speculation.

The first approach is to look for habitable conditions. Researchers focus on the so-called habitable zone, the region around a star where temperatures allow liquid water to exist on a planet's surface. Water, as far as we know, is essential for life.

The second approach is to analyse the atmospheres of distant planets. When a planet passes in front of its star, starlight filters through its atmosphere, and certain gases leave distinctive fingerprints in that light. Oxygen and methane together, for instance, would be hard to explain without some biological source.

A third strand of research listens for deliberate signals. For more than sixty years, astronomers have scanned the sky for radio transmissions that might indicate an intelligent civilisation. So far, nothing conclusive has been found.

What unites all three approaches is patience. Detecting life, if it exists, will almost certainly require repeated observations and very cautious interpretation. Extraordinary claims, as the saying goes, demand extraordinary evidence.

questions.q: What is the 'habitable zone'?
options: The region around a star where liquid water can exist
The edge of the solar system

questions.q: Water is considered ________ for life.
Gases in a planet's atmosphere can leave distinctive signs in starlight.
Astronomers have scanned for radio signals for more than ________ years.
What does the lecturer say unites all three approaches?
A conclusive intelligent radio signal has already been detected.

speaking.q: Let's talk about technology in everyday life. How often do you use satellite-based services such as GPS or weather apps? Do you find them reliable? Could you manage without them? Why or why not?

writing.q: Some people think that the exploration of space should be a joint effort shared by all countries, rather than carried out by individual nations or private companies. To what extent do you agree or disagree? Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Thousands of fragments circulate around the Earth.
Spent rocket stages add to the debris.
Even flecks of paint can be dangerous in orbit.
The fragment's high velocity makes it hazardous.
A small fragment could puncture the spacecraft's hull.

reading.title: Telescopes That Look Back in Time
reading.passage:
When we gaze at a distant galaxy, we are not seeing it as it is now but as it was when its light began its journey towards us. Because light travels at a finite speed, looking deeper into space is equivalent to looking further back in time. A galaxy whose light has taken ten billion years to reach Earth appears to us as it was ten billion years ago, long before our planet existed. This curious property has made the largest telescopes into powerful instruments for studying the history of the universe.

The further away an object is, the fainter and redder its light becomes by the time it arrives. As the universe expands, the wavelength of light stretches, shifting it towards the red end of the spectrum and, for the most distant objects, into the infrared. To capture this faint, stretched light, modern observatories rely on enormous mirrors and detectors cooled to extremely low temperatures, often situated in remote locations or even beyond the atmosphere altogether.

The rewards of this effort are considerable. By observing galaxies at different distances, astronomers can effectively watch cosmic evolution unfold, comparing young galaxies in the early universe with mature ones nearby. Such comparisons have revealed that galaxies were once smaller, more chaotic, and more prone to collisions than they are today.

Yet there are limits to how far back we can see. Before a certain point, the universe was filled with a dense fog that light could not penetrate. Peering beyond this barrier remains one of the central challenges of modern astronomy, and each new generation of telescopes pushes the boundary a little further, bringing the universe's earliest chapters slowly into view.

questions.q: Why does looking further into space mean looking back in time?
options: Light travels at a finite speed

questions.q: The light of very distant objects becomes fainter and redder before it reaches Earth.
As the universe expands, the wavelength of light is stretched towards the ________ end of the spectrum.
Modern detectors are sometimes cooled to very low temperatures.
What have comparisons of galaxies at different distances revealed?
options: Galaxies have always looked the same
Early galaxies were smaller and more chaotic than today's

questions.q: Scientists have now observed the very first galaxy ever formed.
Before a certain point, the early universe was filled with a dense ________ that light could not penetrate.
Each new generation of telescopes pushes the observational boundary further back.

listening.title: Lecture: Building the Next Great Observatory
listening.script:
LECTURER: This morning I want to walk you through the challenges of building a next-generation observatory, because the engineering is every bit as remarkable as the science.

Let's start with location. A great telescope needs dark skies, dry air, and high altitude. That's why so many are built on remote mountain tops, often more than three thousand metres above sea level, where the atmosphere is thin and stable.

Then there's the mirror. The primary mirror of a modern telescope can be over thirty metres across, far too large to cast as a single piece of glass. Instead, engineers build it from hundreds of smaller hexagonal segments, each aligned by computer to within a fraction of the width of a human hair.

Next, consider the atmosphere itself. Even on the clearest night, turbulence blurs the image of a star. To counter this, observatories use a technique called adaptive optics, in which a flexible mirror changes its shape hundreds of times a second to cancel out the distortion.

Finally, there's data. A single night of observing can generate enormous volumes of information, so powerful computing facilities are as essential as the telescope itself. Without them, the raw observations would be almost useless.

questions.q: Why are great telescopes often built on remote mountain tops?
options: They need dark skies, dry air, and high altitude
It is easier to transport materials

questions.q: Many observatories are built more than ________ metres above sea level.
A modern primary mirror is cast as a single piece of glass.
Large mirrors are built from hundreds of smaller ________ segments.
What does adaptive optics do?
options: Changes a flexible mirror's shape to cancel atmospheric distortion

questions.q: Powerful computing facilities are described as essential to the observatory.

speaking.q:
Describe a time when you saw something impressive in the natural world. You should say:
- what you saw
- where and when you saw it
- who you were with
and explain why it made such a strong impression on you.

writing.q:
The table below shows the number of operational telescopes of three types worldwide in 2000, 2010 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

Type | 2000 | 2010 | 2020
Ground-based optical | 320 | 410 | 520

Space-based | 8 | 19 | 34

reading.title: The Secret Communication of Forest Trees
reading.passage:
For most of the twentieth century, a forest was regarded by ecologists as little more than a crowd of individual trees, each competing fiercely with its neighbours for light, water and the limited nutrients held in the soil. The dominant image was one of relentless rivalry, in which the strongest specimens prospered while weaker ones were gradually shaded out and died. Recent research, however, has begun to complicate this picture in ways that few would have anticipated a generation ago.

Beneath the forest floor lies a dense and largely invisible web of fungal threads, known collectively as mycorrhizal networks. These fungi attach themselves to the roots of trees, drawing sugars from their hosts and, in return, supplying the trees with water and minerals that their roots alone could not efficiently obtain. What has astonished researchers is the discovery that a single network may connect dozens of trees of different species, allowing resources to pass not merely between fungus and tree but, apparently, from one tree to another.

Experiments using radioactive tracers have demonstrated that carbon can travel through these networks from trees standing in full sunlight to younger, shaded saplings struggling on the forest floor. Some scientists interpret this as a form of cooperation, suggesting that older trees may effectively subsidise the next generation. Others caution that the fungi, rather than the trees, may be directing the flow for their own benefit, and that what looks like generosity is simply a by-product of fungal self-interest.

The debate is far from settled, and sceptics rightly point out that vivid metaphors such as the 'wood-wide web' risk romanticising what may be ordinary biochemistry. Even so, the underlying finding is difficult to dismiss. Whatever the precise mechanism, forests appear to be far more interconnected than the older, competitive model allowed, and the boundary between one organism and another is less distinct than we once assumed.

questions.q: Twentieth-century ecologists generally viewed a forest as a collection of trees competing with one another.
The underground web of fungal threads is collectively known as ________ networks.
According to the passage, what do the fungi receive from the trees?
A single fungal network can link trees belonging to more than one species.
What did experiments with radioactive tracers show?
options: carbon moves from shaded saplings to sunlit trees
carbon can travel from sunlit trees to shaded saplings
fungi cannot transport carbon at all
saplings produce more carbon than mature trees

questions.q: The mycorrhizal networks were first discovered in tropical rainforests.
Some researchers warn that apparent generosity may actually be a by-product of fungal ________.
The writer concludes that the competitive model has been entirely disproved.

listening.title: Lecture: How Migratory Birds Navigate
listening.script:
LECTURER: Today I want to address one of the great puzzles of animal behaviour: how migratory birds find their way across thousands of kilometres, often returning to the very same nesting site year after year. For a long time it was assumed that they simply followed coastlines or rivers, but that explanation collapses once you consider birds that cross open ocean at night.

We now know that birds rely on several overlapping systems. The first is a kind of internal compass that detects the Earth's magnetic field. Remarkably, this appears to be linked to light-sensitive proteins in the eye, which means the birds may, in some sense, actually see the magnetic field as a pattern overlaid on their vision.

The second system uses the position of the sun by day and the stars by night. Young birds raised under an artificial sky have been shown to orient themselves according to whichever star pattern they were taught to treat as fixed. A third cue, less understood, is smell: certain species seem to build a kind of odour map of their territory.

What is striking is the redundancy. If one system fails, another takes over. This built-in backup explains why migration is so reliable, and why disrupting a single cue rarely stops a bird from reaching its destination.

questions.q: The old explanation collapses when considering birds that cross open ocean at ________.
The bird's magnetic compass appears to be linked to what?
Young birds reared under an artificial sky oriented according to the star pattern they were taught to treat as fixed.
A third navigational cue, which is less understood, is ________.
Why does the lecturer say migration is so reliable?
options: because birds only use one system
because the systems provide redundancy and back each other up
because coastlines are easy to follow
because birds never travel at night

questions.q: Disrupting a single cue usually prevents a bird from reaching its destination.

speaking.q: Let's talk about nature and the outdoors. Do you enjoy spending time in natural places such as forests or parks? How often do you get the chance to do so, and what do you usually do there? Has your interest in nature changed since you were a child?

Forest cover (% of land area)
Country | 1990 | 2005 | 2020

vocab.example: The trees were locked in relentless rivalry.
There is constant rivalry for light and water.
The strongest specimens prospered.
Few would have anticipated this finding.
A dense web of fungal threads lies below.
Roots alone could not efficiently obtain minerals.
The discovery astonished researchers.
Older trees may subsidise the next generation.
Generosity may be a by-product of self-interest.
Sceptics question the vivid metaphors.
The metaphor risks romanticising ordinary biochemistry.
The boundary is less distinct than we assumed.
The redundancy of the systems makes migration reliable.
Disrupting one cue rarely stops the bird.

reading.title: The Remarkable Resilience of Tardigrades
reading.passage:
Among the most extraordinary organisms on Earth is a creature barely half a millimetre long, plump and slow-moving, with eight stubby legs ending in tiny claws. Known formally as tardigrades and affectionately as 'water bears', these microscopic animals have captured the imagination of biologists for a reason that has nothing to do with their appearance: their almost unbelievable capacity to survive conditions that would kill virtually any other form of life.

When their environment dries out, tardigrades perform a trick that seems to defy the rules of biology. They expel almost all the water from their bodies, curl into a shrunken, barrel-shaped form, and effectively suspend their metabolism. In this state, called cryptobiosis, an individual may persist for years, perhaps decades, showing no detectable signs of life. Add a drop of water, and within hours it swells, stirs and crawls away as though nothing had happened.

The list of insults a tardigrade can endure in this dormant condition is genuinely staggering. They have been frozen to within a fraction of absolute zero and heated well above the boiling point of water. They tolerate pressures far greater than those at the deepest point of the ocean, and doses of radiation that would prove instantly lethal to a human. In one celebrated experiment, tardigrades were exposed to the vacuum and radiation of open space and a proportion of them survived, the first animals known to do so.

Quite how they achieve all this is still being unravelled. Researchers have identified proteins, unique to tardigrades, that appear to replace water inside cells and shield delicate structures as the body dries. Understanding these molecules is more than a curiosity. If their protective properties could be harnessed, they might one day allow vaccines and other fragile biological materials to be stored without refrigeration, a prospect of real value in regions where reliable cold storage is scarce.

questions.q: Tardigrades are affectionately known by the nickname 'water ________'.
Biologists are interested in tardigrades mainly because of their unusual appearance.
What happens to a tardigrade when its environment dries out?
options: it expels most of its water and suspends its metabolism
it grows larger and more active
it moves to find deeper water

questions.q: The dormant, water-free state that tardigrades enter is called ________.
Tardigrades were the first animals known to survive exposure to open space.
What practical application does the passage suggest for tardigrade proteins?
Tardigrades can survive doses of radiation that would be lethal to humans.
The protective proteins of tardigrades have already been used commercially in medicine.

listening.title: Talk: Restoring an Urban Wetland
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to the Riverside Wetland Reserve. Before we walk the trail, let me give you a little background on how this place came to be. Twenty years ago, this whole site was a disused industrial yard, with concrete drains channelling the creek straight out to sea. Almost no wildlife remained.

The restoration began in 2006. The first step was to remove the concrete and let the creek meander naturally again, which slowed the water and allowed sediment to settle. Volunteers then planted more than forty thousand native reeds and shrubs along the banks. These plants do something clever: their roots filter pollutants out of the water before it reaches the bay.

The results have exceeded everyone's expectations. We've recorded over ninety species of bird here, including several that had vanished from the city for decades. Frogs returned within three years, and they're a good sign, because frogs are highly sensitive to pollution.

Now, a quick note on today's walk. The full loop takes about ninety minutes. Please keep to the boardwalk, as the mud either side is deeper than it looks. And do bring out any binoculars you have, because the bird hide at the far end offers the best views in the reserve.

questions.q: Twenty years ago the site was a disused industrial yard.
The restoration of the wetland began in the year ________.
What do the roots of the planted reeds and shrubs do?
options: filter pollutants out of the water
speed up the flow of the creek

questions.q: More than ________ species of bird have been recorded at the reserve.
Frogs are mentioned as being highly sensitive to pollution.
What does the guide ask visitors to do during the walk?
options: walk across the mud for a closer view
stay away from the bird hide

speaking.q:
Describe a wild animal or plant that you find fascinating. You should say:

writing.q: Some people believe that the most effective way to protect endangered species is to keep them in zoos and breeding programmes. Others argue that resources should instead be spent on protecting natural habitats in the wild. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: Coral Reefs and the Puzzle of Bleaching
reading.passage:
A healthy coral reef is among the most biologically productive environments on the planet, supporting a quarter of all marine species despite occupying a tiny fraction of the ocean floor. Yet the brilliant colours that make such reefs so spectacular do not belong to the corals themselves. They are produced by microscopic algae that live embedded within the coral tissue, in a partnership so intimate that neither organism can readily flourish without the other.

The arrangement is mutually beneficial. The algae, sheltered inside the coral, photosynthesise and pass on a substantial share of the energy they capture, in some cases meeting the great majority of the coral's nutritional needs. In return, the coral provides a protected home and a steady supply of the compounds the algae require. It is this elegant exchange, refined over millions of years, that allows reefs to thrive in the clear but nutrient-poor waters of the tropics, where food is otherwise in short supply.

The relationship, however, is delicately balanced and easily upset. When the surrounding water grows too warm, even by a degree or two sustained over several weeks, the partnership breaks down. The stressed coral expels its algae, losing both its colour and its principal source of food. The result is the ghostly white phenomenon known as bleaching. A bleached coral is not yet dead, and if cooler conditions return quickly enough it may recover by taking up new algae. But if the stress is prolonged, the coral starves.

What troubles marine scientists is not bleaching itself, which has always occurred occasionally, but its rising frequency and scale. Severe events that once struck a given reef perhaps twice in a century are now recurring within years of one another, leaving too little time for recovery between them. Some researchers nonetheless draw cautious encouragement from the discovery that certain coral populations appear more heat-tolerant than others, raising the possibility that reefs may, given the chance, gradually adapt.

questions.q: Coral reefs occupy only a small fraction of the ocean floor.
Where do the colourful algae actually live?
options: on the surface of the water

questions.q: The algae provide energy by means of ________, passing much of it to the coral.
The coral-algae partnership allows reefs to thrive in nutrient-poor tropical waters.
What directly causes coral bleaching according to the passage?
options: the coral expelling its algae under heat stress
a sudden drop in water temperature

questions.q: A bleached coral is already dead and cannot recover under any circumstances.
Scientists are most troubled by the rising frequency and ________ of bleaching events.
Reefs damaged by bleaching can be fully restored within six months using artificial coral.

listening.title: Documentary Extract: The Return of the Beaver
listening.script:
NARRATOR: For centuries, the beaver was treated as little more than a nuisance, or as a source of fur, and across much of its range it was hunted almost to extinction. Today, however, ecologists are reintroducing this remarkable rodent quite deliberately, and the reason lies in what beavers do to the landscape.

A beaver is, in effect, a natural engineer. By felling trees and damming streams, it creates ponds and wetlands where once there was only a narrow channel. These new wetlands act like sponges, holding back water during heavy rain and releasing it slowly during dry spells. In catchments where beavers have returned, downstream flooding has measurably decreased.

The benefits extend further. The still water behind a beaver dam allows sediment and pollutants to settle, so the water leaving the system is noticeably cleaner. And the patchwork of pools and dead wood provides habitat for an astonishing variety of insects, fish, amphibians and birds. One survey found that species richness more than doubled within five years of a beaver's arrival.

There are, of course, conflicts. Flooded farmland and blocked culverts can frustrate landowners, and managing these tensions is now a central part of every reintroduction scheme. But the broad conclusion is clear: where we let beavers work, the wider ecosystem tends to benefit.

questions.q: The narrator describes the beaver as a natural ________.
How do beaver-created wetlands behave during heavy rain?
options: they hold back water like sponges
they have no effect on flooding

questions.q: Downstream flooding has decreased in catchments where beavers have returned.
Still water behind a dam lets sediment and ________ settle, making the water cleaner.
One survey found species richness more than doubled within ________ years of a beaver's arrival.
What is mentioned as a source of conflict with landowners?

speaking.q: Let's consider environmental responsibility more broadly. Some argue that protecting the natural world should be primarily the responsibility of governments, while others believe individuals must take the lead. Where do you think the main responsibility lies, and how can ordinary people be persuaded to change their behaviour for the sake of the environment?
